## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of Single Plate Coil Spring Clutch — Detailed Component Analysis

A single plate coil spring clutch consists of the following components arranged along the axis of rotation:

#### A) Driving Members (rotate with engine crankshaft)
1. **Flywheel** — A heavy cast-iron disc bolted to the crankshaft rear face. It has a machined friction surface on its forward face. It acts as the driving member and also stores rotational kinetic energy.
2. **Pressure Plate** — A cast-iron cover-plate that houses the coil springs. It presses the driven plate against the flywheel friction face.
3. **Clutch Cover (Housing)** — A pressed-steel cover bolted to the flywheel. It carries the fulcrum points, springs, and the pressure plate.

#### B) Driven Member (rotates with transmission input shaft)
4. **Driven Plate (Friction Disc)** — The single friction disc with a splined hub. It carries friction facings on both sides.

#### C) Operating Members (engage/disengage)
5. **Coil Springs (Helical Compression Springs)** — **9 to 15 in number**, equally spaced on a pitch circle on the pressure plate. Their function is to push the pressure plate forward against the driven plate with axial force **W**.
6. **Release (Throw-out) Levers** — Typically **3 levers**, pivoted on the clutch cover. They multiply the pedal force.
7. **Release Bearing (Pilot Bearing / Throw-out Bearing)** — A ball/race bearing that pushes the release levers axially when the clutch pedal is depressed.

#### D) Other Components
8. **Splined Hub** — Splines on the driven plate hub mate with the splines of the gearbox input shaft.
9. **Retracting Springs** — Light springs that pull back the release levers when the pedal is released.
10. **Cushion Springs** (optional) — Wave-shaped springs between the facings and the disc to absorb torsional vibrations.

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (Why & How)

**Step 1 — Engine running, foot OFF the clutch pedal (Engaged State):**
- The **coil springs** are in their **natural (free) length**, exerting maximum axial force on the pressure plate.
- Pressure plate clamps the **driven plate** against the **flywheel friction face**.
- Friction force = μ × N transmits engine torque to the transmission input shaft.
- **Why?** Because the springs are at maximum compression with no opposing force.

**Step 2 — Driver DEPRESSES the clutch pedal (Disengaging State):**
- Pedal linkage pushes the **release bearing** forward (towards the flywheel).
- Release bearing pushes the inner tips of the **3 release levers**.
- Levers pivot on the clutch cover, multiplying the force and pulling the pressure plate **backward** (away from the flywheel).
- Coil springs get **further compressed** — they now store the disengagement force.
- The driven plate is **released** from clamping → no friction → no torque transmission.

**Step 3 — Driver RELEASES the pedal gradually (Re-engagement):**
- Release bearing retracts; levers allow the **coil springs to decompress**.
- Pressure plate moves forward and progressively clamps the driven plate.
- Smooth power flow resumes due to gradual spring decompression.

---

### 2.3 Construction of Single Plate Diaphragm Spring Clutch — Detailed Component Analysis

The diaphragm spring clutch **replaces the coil springs + release levers combination** with a **single conical finger-type spring**.

#### Components (driving, driven, operating):

| S.No | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flywheel** | Same as coil-spring type — driving member |
| 2 | **Pressure Plate** | Cast-iron disc that clamps the driven plate |
| 3 | **Clutch Cover** | Bolted to flywheel; supports the diaphragm spring |
| 4 | **Diaphragm Spring** | A single conical spring with radial fingers — **does the dual job of (a) applying pressure AND (b) acting as a release lever** |
| 5 | **Driven Plate (Friction Disc)** | Same as before — single disc with facings |
| 6 | **Release (Throw-out) Bearing** | Acts on the **fingers (inner tips)** of the diaphragm spring |

---

### 2.4 Operational Logic — Diaphragm Spring (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diaphragm spring is the **heart** of this clutch. It is a thin, conical spring steel disc (≈ 2–3 mm thick) with radial fingers cut into it. Its cross-section looks like the letter **"Z"** or a **truncated cone with finger slits**.

**Step 1 — Engaged position:**
- The diaphragm spring's outer annular ring rests against the pressure plate.
- The spring's conical shape pushes the pressure plate **forward**, clamping the driven plate against the flywheel.
- The fingers (inner tips) lie in their natural position.

**Step 2 — Disengagement (driver depresses pedal):**
- The release bearing pushes the **inner finger tips of the diaphragm spring forward**.
- Because the spring is conical, this forward push at the tips causes the **outer ring to move backward** — it pivots about its mean diameter (the "spring fulcrum").
- The pressure plate retracts → clamping force removed → driven plate free.

**Step 3 — Self-Compensating Wear Feature (KTU Favourite Question):**
- As the friction facing wears out, the diaphragm spring **automatically compensates** because its operating range is on the **flat portion of its load-deflection curve**.
- This means clutch-pedal **effort remains nearly constant** throughout the clutch's life.
- **No periodic adjustment is required** (unlike coil-spring clutches where spring force reduces with wear).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments of PCAUT503.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Axial clamping force (single pair)** | $W = \dfrac{2T}{\mu \cdot D_m}$ | N; T = torque (N·m), D_m = mean friction diameter (m) |
| 2 | **Mean friction diameter** | $D_m = \dfrac{D_o + D_i}{2}$ | m; D_o = outer, D_i = inner diameter of friction face |
| 3 | **Total spring force = Total axial force** | $W_{total} = n \cdot W_s$ | N; n = number of springs, W_s = force per spring |
| 4 | **Force per spring** | $W_s = \dfrac{W_{total}}{n}$ | N |
| 5 | **Torque transmitted (uniform pressure theory)** | $T_u = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ | N·m |
| 6 | **Torque transmitted (uniform wear theory)** | $T_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R_o + R_i)$ | N·m; **Preferred for clutches** |
| 7 | **Ratio of uniform wear to uniform pressure torque** | $\dfrac{T_w}{T_u} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^2 + R_o R_i + R_i^2}{R_o^2 + R_i^2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Pressure on friction surface (uniform pressure)** | $p = \dfrac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ | Pa |
| 9 | **Pressure on friction surface (uniform wear)** | $p(r) = \dfrac{C}{r}$ with $C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)}$ | Pa; decreases with radius |
| 10 | **Maximum torque capacity (uniform wear)** | $T_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot p_{max} \cdot \pi \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ | N·m |
| 11 | **Lever mechanical advantage** | $M.A. = \dfrac{a}{b}$ | dimensionless; a = long arm, b = short arm of release lever |
| 12 | **Pedal effort (coil spring clutch)** | $F_{pedal} = \dfrac{n \cdot W_s}{M.A._{lever} \cdot M.A._{linkage}}$ | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two theories (KTU favourite):** Uniform Pressure theory assumes pressure is constant across the friction face — used for **new clutches**. Uniform Wear theory (proposed by **R.E. Newton**) assumes wear rate is uniform → pressure × radius = constant — used for **worn clutches**, and gives more realistic torque capacity.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coil spring clutches:** still used in **heavy commercial trucks** (Tata, Ashok Leyland), where high torque demands multiple robust springs.
- **Diaphragm spring clutches:** dominate **passenger car market** globally — Maruti Alto, Swift, Hyundai i10, Honda City — because of **self-compensation, lighter pedal effort, smaller axial length**, and **fewer moving parts** (1 spring vs. 12+ springs + 3 levers = **15+ parts eliminated**).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Single Plate Clutch Force & Pressure Distribution

For KTU 2024, examiners expect students to derive and compare **Uniform Pressure (UP)** and **Uniform Wear (UW)** distributions on the friction surface.

#### Setting up the problem:

Let a single friction plate be pressed against the flywheel with a total axial force **W** distributed over the annular friction surface between inner radius **R_i** and outer radius **R_o**.

##### CASE A — Uniform Pressure Theory (UPT)

**Assumption:** Pressure **p = constant** across the entire friction surface.

**Step 1 — Express total axial force W in terms of p:**

Consider a small elemental ring of radius r and width dr on the friction surface.
Area of element = dA = 2πr dr
Force on element = dW = p · dA = 2π p r dr

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi p r \, dr \\
  &= 2\pi p \left[ \dfrac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\
  &= \pi p (R_o^2 - R_i^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the uniform pressure is:

$$
p = \dfrac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute elemental friction torque:**

Elemental friction force on ring: dF = μ · dW = μ p · 2πr dr
Elemental torque about the clutch axis: dT = r · dF = 2π μ p r² dr

**Step 3 — Integrate to get total torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu p r^2 \, dr \\
    &= 2\pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{r^3}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu p (R_o^3 - R_i^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute p from Step 1:**

$$
T_u = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)} \cdot (R_o^3 - R_i^3)
$$

$$
\boxed{T_u = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}}
$$

---

##### CASE B — Uniform Wear Theory (UWT) — Newton’s Theory

**Assumption:** Wear rate is uniform across the contact surface, which means **p · r = constant = C**.

**Step 1 — Express pressure as a function of radius:**

$$
p(r) = \dfrac{C}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Total axial force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi r \cdot p(r) \, dr \\
  &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi r \cdot \dfrac{C}{r} \, dr \\
  &= 2\pi C \int_{R_i}^{R_o} dr \\
  &= 2\pi C (R_o - R_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Friction torque:**

Friction force on elemental ring: dF = μ · dW = μ · 2πr · (C/r) · dr = 2π μ C dr
Elemental torque: dT = r · dF = 2π μ C r dr

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu C r \, dr \\
    &= 2\pi \mu C \left[ \dfrac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\
    &= \pi \mu C (R_o^2 - R_i^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute C from Step 2:**

$$
T_w = \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)
$$

Note that R_o² − R_i² = (R_o − R_i)(R_o + R_i), so:

$$
\boxed{T_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (R_o + R_i)}
$$

---

##### CASE C — Comparison Ratio

Dividing T_w by T_u:

$$
\dfrac{T_w}{T_u} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (R_o + R_i)}{\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\dfrac{T_w}{T_u} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(R_o + R_i)(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}
$$

Using R_o² − R_i² = (R_o − R_i)(R_o + R_i), and R_o³ − R_i³ = (R_o − R_i)(R_o² + R_o R_i + R_i²):

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{T_w}{T_u} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(R_o^2 + R_o R_i + R_i^2)}{(R_o^2 + R_i^2)}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A single plate coil spring clutch has **9 coil springs**, each providing a force of **350 N**. The friction disc has an outer diameter of **240 mm** and inner diameter of **160 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **μ = 0.3**. Determine:
1. Total axial clamping force W.
2. Torque capacity using **Uniform Wear Theory**.
3. Torque capacity using **Uniform Pressure Theory**.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- n = 9 springs
- W_s = 350 N per spring
- D_o = 240 mm → R_o = 0.120 m
- D_i = 160 mm → R_i = 0.080 m
- μ = 0.3

**Step 1 — Total axial force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= n \cdot W_s \\
          &= 9 \times 350 \\
          &= 3150 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque by Uniform Wear Theory (preferred):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= \dfrac{1}{2} \mu W (R_o + R_i) \\
    &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3 \times 3150 \times (0.120 + 0.080) \\
    &= 0.15 \times 3150 \times 0.200 \\
    &= 94.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Torque by Uniform Pressure Theory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{0.3 \times 3150 \times (0.120^3 - 0.080^3)}{(0.120^2 - 0.080^2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Calculate numerator and denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o^3 - R_i^3 &= 0.001728 - 0.000512 = 0.001216 \text{ m}^3 \\
R_o^2 - R_i^2 &= 0.01440 - 0.00640 = 0.00800 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{0.3 \times 3150 \times 0.001216}{0.00800} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1.14912}{0.00800} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 143.64 \\
    &= 95.76 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** T_w = **94.5 N·m** < T_u = **95.76 N·m**, confirming that the uniform wear theory gives a slightly more conservative torque estimate (≈ 1.3 % lower here). For KTU board examination, **state both values and identify UW as the design value**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Single Plate Coil Spring Clutch

The Mermaid diagram below is adapted as a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping every component, its function, and the force-flow path during engagement/disengagement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Crankshaft] --> B[Flywheel - Driving Member]
    B --> C[Friction Contact Zone]
    C --> D[Driven Plate - Single Friction Disc]
    D --> E[Splined Hub]
    E --> F[Gearbox Input Shaft]
    C -.->|Friction transmits torque| F

    G[Coil Springs - 9 to 15 in number] -->|Axial force W| H[Pressure Plate]
    H -->|Clamps| C

    I[Clutch Pedal - Driver Input] -->|Pedal force Fp| J[Linkage and Pivot]
    J -->|Multiplied force| K[Release Bearing - Thrust Ball Bearing]
    K -->|Pushes inner tips| L[Release Levers - 3 levers]
    L -->|Retracts against spring force| H
    G -.->|Springs compressed - energy stored| H

    M[Retracting Springs - Light tension] -->|Reset to engaged state| L
    N[Clutch Cover - Bolted to flywheel] -->|Houses and supports| G
    N -->|Provides pivot fulcrum| L

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (→)** = engaged-state power flow path: Crankshaft → Flywheel → Friction Zone → Driven Plate → Splined Hub → Gearbox Input Shaft.
- **Dotted arrows (-.→)** = disengaged-state force flow: Pedal → Linkage → Release Bearing → Levers → Pressure Plate retraction.
- **Color coding:** Blue = engine-side driving components, Red = friction contact zone, Green = transmission-side driven components, Yellow = spring/pressure mechanism, Purple = pedal/release mechanism.

### 4.4 Force Flow Path Summary Table

| Stage | Coil Spring Clutch | Diaphragm Spring Clutch |
|---|---|---|
| Driver force | Pedal → linkage → release bearing → release lever (×3) → pressure plate retraction | Pedal → linkage → release bearing → finger tips of diaphragm spring → outer ring pivots back → pressure plate retraction |
| Number of springs | **9 – 15** helical coil springs | **1** conical diaphragm spring |
| Number of levers | **3** release levers | **0** (the diaphragm itself acts as the lever) |
| Total moving parts in actuation | **~ 15 +** | **~ 3 – 4** |
| Self-compensating for wear? | **No** — requires periodic adjustment | **Yes** — auto-compensates via flat portion of load-deflection curve |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCAUT503, Module 1]`
**List any six components of a single plate coil spring clutch and state the function of the clutch cover.**

**Model Answer (6 components + cover function):**

1. **Flywheel** — driving member bolted to crankshaft, provides friction surface.
2. **Pressure plate** — pushes driven plate axially against flywheel.
3. **Driven plate (friction disc)** — single friction disc with splined hub, transmits torque via friction.
4. **Coil springs (9–15)** — provide axial clamping force.
5. **Release levers (3)** — multiply pedal force and retract pressure plate.
6. **Release (throw-out) bearing** — receives pedal linkage force and pushes release levers.

**Function of clutch cover:** The clutch cover is a pressed-steel housing **bolted to the flywheel**. It carries the fulcrum pins for the release levers, houses the coil springs, and provides the structural frame within which the entire clutch assembly rotates as a single unit with the flywheel. **[3 marks]**

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCAUT503, Module 1]`
**What is a diaphragm spring in a clutch? Why is it preferred over coil springs in modern cars?**

**Model Answer:**

A **diaphragm spring** is a single, conical, thin spring-steel disc with **radial finger slits** cut into its inner periphery. It performs the dual function of (a) **applying axial clamping force** on the pressure plate and (b) **acting as the release lever** when its finger tips are pushed forward by the release bearing.

**Reasons for preference over coil springs:**
1. **Self-compensating for wear** — operates on the flat portion of its load-deflection curve, so pedal effort stays constant throughout clutch life. **No adjustment needed.**
2. **Fewer parts** — 1 spring replaces ~12 coil springs + 3 release levers + associated pins/links.
3. **Lighter pedal effort** — due to high mechanical advantage of the conical geometry.
4. **Shorter axial length** — reduces overall engine bay packaging size.
5. **Lower rotating mass** — improved NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
6. **Higher torque capacity in same envelope** due to favourable force distribution. **[3 marks]**

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PCAUT503]`

**a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **construction and working of a single plate coil spring clutch** used in passenger cars. **[7 marks]**

**b)** A single plate coil spring clutch has the following specifications:
- Outer diameter of friction face, D_o = 280 mm
- Inner diameter of friction face, D_i = 180 mm
- Number of coil springs, n = 12
- Force per spring, W_s = 400 N
- Coefficient of friction, μ = 0.30

**Compute:**
(i) Total axial clamping force.
(ii) Torque transmitted using **Uniform Wear Theory**.
(iii) Torque transmitted using **Uniform Pressure Theory**.
(iv) The ratio T_w / T_u. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Construction (label and describe each part):**

The single plate coil spring clutch consists of the following parts (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

| S.No. | Component | Material & Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flywheel** | Cast iron; bolted to crankshaft; rear face is precision-ground to form driving friction surface; also acts as part of engine rotating mass for smooth operation. |
| 2 | **Clutch Cover** | Pressed steel / cast aluminium; bolted to flywheel periphery; houses springs and lever pivots; rotates as a unit with flywheel. |
| 3 | **Pressure Plate** | Cast iron disc; held inside clutch cover; transmits spring force axially to driven plate. |
| 4 | **Coil Springs** | Helical compression springs (usually 9–15); fitted in pockets on pressure plate; push pressure plate axially with force W = n × W_s. |
| 5 | **Driven Plate** | Single disc with friction facings on both sides; hub splined to gearbox input shaft; carries torsional cushion springs between facings. |
| 6 | **Release Levers (3)** | Forged steel levers pivoted on cover fulcrum pins; multiply pedal force; pull pressure plate back when pedal is pressed. |
| 7 | **Release Bearing** | Ball / needle bearing; slides on guide sleeve; pushed by clutch linkage when pedal is depressed. |
| 8 | **Retracting Springs** | Tension springs that pull back the release levers to engaged position. |

**Working:**
- **Engaged state:** Coil springs in free state, press pressure plate → driven plate clamped against flywheel → torque transmitted via friction.
- **Disengaged state:** Driver depresses pedal → linkage pushes release bearing → bearing pushes inner tips of release levers → levers pivot on cover → pressure plate pulled back against spring force → clamping force removed → driven plate free → no torque transmission. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- D_o = 280 mm → R_o = 0.140 m
- D_i = 180 mm → R_i = 0.090 m
- n = 12 springs
- W_s = 400 N
- μ = 0.30

**(i) Total axial clamping force:**

$$
W_{total} = n \cdot W_s = 12 \times 400 = \boxed{4800 \text{ N}}
$$

**(ii) Torque by Uniform Wear Theory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R_o + R_i) \\
    &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.30 \times 4800 \times (0.140 + 0.090) \\
    &= 0.15 \times 4800 \times 0.230 \\
    &= \boxed{165.6 \text{ N·m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Torque by Uniform Pressure Theory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o^3 - R_i^3 &= 0.140^3 - 0.090^3 \\
              &= 0.002744 - 0.000729 \\
              &= 0.002015 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o^2 - R_i^2 &= 0.140^2 - 0.090^2 \\
              &= 0.01960 - 0.00810 \\
              &= 0.01150 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{0.30 \times 4800 \times 0.002015}{0.01150} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2.9016}{0.01150} \\
    &= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 252.31 \\
    &= \boxed{168.20 \text{ N·m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Ratio T_w / T_u:**

$$
\dfrac{T_w}{T_u} = \dfrac{165.6}{168.20} = \boxed{0.985 \;\;(\approx 98.5\%)}
$$

**Conclusion:** For this geometry, UW torque ≈ 98.5 % of UP torque — they are nearly equal because R_i/R_o is not too small. UW theory gives a slightly conservative (lower) design value, which is the **industry-preferred design approach** for clutches. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PCAUT503]`

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a single plate diaphragm spring clutch**. Highlight the dual role of the diaphragm spring. **[7 marks]**

**b)** Compare the **single plate coil spring clutch** and the **single plate diaphragm spring clutch** under the following heads:
(i) Number of springs
(ii) Self-compensation for wear
(iii) Pedal effort
(iv) Rotating mass
(v) Axial length
(vi) Maintenance
(vii) Cost **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**

| S.No. | Component | Description & Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flywheel** | Same as coil spring clutch — driving member with machined friction face. |
| 2 | **Clutch Cover** | Bolted to flywheel; supports the diaphragm spring at its mean (fulcrum) diameter; carries the pressure plate. |
| 3 | **Pressure Plate** | Single cast-iron disc pushed axially by the outer annular ring of the diaphragm spring. |
| 4 | **Diaphragm Spring** | **The heart of the clutch** — a thin conical spring-steel disc (~2–3 mm thick) with **12–16 radial fingers** cut into its inner periphery. The cross-section resembles a "Z" or a truncated cone. |
| 5 | **Driven Plate** | Same as coil spring clutch — single friction disc with splined hub. |
| 6 | **Release Bearing** | Pushes the inner finger tips of the diaphragm spring axially. |

**Dual role of diaphragm spring:**
- **Role 1 — Pressure provider:** The outer annular ring of the diaphragm spring pushes the pressure plate axially forward, clamping the driven plate against the flywheel.
- **Role 2 — Release lever:** When the release bearing pushes the **inner finger tips forward**, the conical spring pivots about its mean diameter, and the outer ring moves **backward**, retracting the pressure plate. **Thus, the diaphragm spring replaces both the coil springs AND the release levers** of the conventional clutch.

**Working:**
- **Engaged:** Outer ring of diaphragm pushes pressure plate forward → clamping force = W.
- **Disengaged:** Pedal depressed → release bearing pushes finger tips forward → outer ring pivots back → pressure plate retracts → clamping force removed.
- **Self-compensation:** As facings wear, diaphragm spring automatically moves to a new operating point on the flat (constant-load) portion of its load-deflection curve → pedal effort unchanged. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Single Plate Coil Spring Clutch | Single Plate Diaphragm Spring Clutch |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Number of springs** | **9 to 15** separate helical coil springs | **1** single diaphragm spring |
| (ii) | **Self-compensation for wear** | **No** — spring force reduces as facings wear; periodic manual adjustment of nuts required | **Yes** — operates on flat portion of load-deflection curve; auto-compensates |
| (iii) | **Pedal effort** | **Higher** (~ 150–250 N) due to lower mechanical advantage of release levers | **Lower** (~ 80–130 N) due to high M.A. of conical diaphragm geometry |
| (iv) | **Rotating mass** | Higher (more springs, levers, pins rotate with cover) | Lower — fewer components |
| (v) | **Axial length** | Larger | Smaller — compact |
| (vi) | **Maintenance** | Periodic adjustment required; individual spring replacement possible | **Almost zero maintenance**; no adjustment needed |
| (vii) | **Cost** | Lower initial cost, higher maintenance cost | Slightly higher initial cost, **lower life-cycle cost** |

**Conclusion:** Diaphragm spring clutches dominate modern passenger car applications because of **compactness, light pedal effort, self-compensation, lower mass, and zero adjustment**, despite marginally higher initial cost. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing "self-energizing" with "self-compensating"** — they are DIFFERENT concepts. *Self-compensating* = constant pedal effort despite wear (diaphragm feature). *Self-energizing* = friction torque helps increase the clamping force (not a single plate feature).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in numerical problems — marks are deducted silently for wrong units. Always write mm in brackets and **convert** before substitution.
> 3. **Using n = 9 springs but forgetting W_total = n × W_s** — examiners specifically test this in Part (a) of numericals.
> 4. **Not stating μ value in numerical** — if μ is missing, examiners assume μ = 0.3 only if explicitly mentioned in the question; otherwise show calculation with both theories.
> 5. **Writing "diaphragm spring replaces release levers"** — it **does not** simply replace them; it **performs their function along with the coil springs**. Phrase it carefully.
> 6. **Mixing up R_o and R_i in the formula T_w = (1/2) μ W (R_o + R_i)** — note the **sum, not difference**. R_o > R_i always.
> 7. **Not sketching** in 7-mark questions — **neat labelled sketch carries 2–3 marks** on its own. Always draw before writing the description.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **single plate clutch** uses **one friction disc** between flywheel and pressure plate.
- ✅ **Coil spring clutch** uses **9–15 helical springs + 3 release levers** to clamp and release the disc.
- ✅ **Diaphragm spring clutch** uses **one conical fingered spring** that doubles as both pressure provider AND release lever.
- ✅ **Pedal effort** in coil-spring type = **F_pedal = (n × W_s) / (Lever M.A. × Linkage M.A.)**.
- ✅ **Two design theories**: Uniform Pressure (new clutch) → T_u = (2/3) μ W (R_o³ − R_i³)/(R_o² − R_i²). Uniform Wear (Newton's theory, worn clutch) → T_w = (1/2) μ W (R_o + R_i).
- ✅ **Uniform Wear theory gives a more conservative (lower) torque estimate** → industry-preferred for design.
- ✅ **Mean friction diameter** D_m = (D_o + D_i)/2; mean radius R_m = (R_o + R_i)/2.
- ✅ **Standard friction coefficient** μ ≈ 0.25 to 0.35 for organic facings; always a **dimensionless** number.
- ✅ **Diaphragm spring is self-compensating** because it operates on the flat (nearly constant-force) portion of its load-deflection curve as the friction disc wears.
- ✅ **Diaphragm spring clutch requires no periodic adjustment** — a major KTU-favourite comparison point.
- ✅ **Pressure distribution**: Under UWT, p × r = constant → **pressure is HIGHEST at inner radius (R_i) and LOWEST at outer radius (R_o)**.
- ✅ **Maximum safe pressure** on friction surface = **0.07 to 0.15 MPa** for organic facings.
- ✅ **Modern passenger cars use diaphragm spring clutches**; **older trucks and heavy vehicles still use coil spring clutches** due to very high torque demands.
- ✅ Always draw **labelled sketch** in 7-mark construction questions — fetches 2–3 easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi Plate Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multi Plate Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multi-Plate Clutch** is a friction clutch that employs **more than two friction surfaces** (driving and driven plates) housed within a confined annular space to transmit engine torque to the transmission input shaft. It is formally classified under the category of **positive-contact friction clutches** and operates on the principle of frictional engagement between alternately-stacked driving and driven plates, where the axial clamping force is distributed over multiple contact interfaces to amplify torque capacity within a constrained radial envelope.

Mathematically, if **n** represents the number of friction pairs (contact surfaces), then for a single-plate clutch producing torque **T₁**, the multi-plate clutch transmits:

$$T_{multi} = n \cdot T_{single}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Multi-plate clutches are specifically studied under Module 1 because they form the cornerstone of modern automotive drivetrains, especially in **heavy commercial vehicles, racing applications, motorcycles, and limited-slip differentials**, where the demand for high torque transmission in a compact radial envelope cannot be met by single-plate designs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to hold a heavy rotating grinding wheel between your palms. With one pair of palms pressing against the wheel, your grip is limited. Now imagine **inserting multiple thin discs** between your palms and pressing them all together — the same squeezing force is now distributed over **many contact surfaces**, dramatically increasing the friction (and hence the torque you can transmit) without requiring any additional squeezing effort.

This is precisely the philosophy behind a multi-plate clutch:
- **Multiple friction plates** stacked alternately create many independent frictional interfaces.
- The **same axial spring force** is multiplied in its torque-transmitting effectiveness.
- The design allows the use of **smaller clutch diameter** for the **same torque** as a much larger single-plate clutch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Think of it as "force multiplication by surface multiplication." A single-plate clutch in a truck would need to be enormously large (often 400 mm+ in diameter) to handle peak torque. By introducing 5–7 plates, the same torque can be transmitted by a clutch assembly half the diameter — critical for **packaging efficiency** in modern vehicles.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics & Design Parameters

The following design parameters are used universally in KTU-aligned textbooks and KTU University Examinations:

- **Mean Effective Radius** ($R_m$): The radius at which the resultant friction force effectively acts.
- **Outer Radius (R)**: The outer edge of the friction lining.
- **Inner Radius (r)**: The inner edge of the friction lining.
- **Axial Operating Force (W)**: The total clamping force applied by the pressure plate springs.
- **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$)**: Typically **0.25 to 0.4** for organic linings; up to **0.5** for sintered bronze.
- **Maximum Allowable Pressure ($p_{max}$)**: Usually **0.7 to 1.5 MPa** for organic linings, **2.0 to 3.5 MPa** for ceramic/sintered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Reference Values (commonly used in KTU problems):**
> - $\mu = 0.1$ to $0.5$ (depends on lining material)
> - $p_{max} = 0.35$ to $1.05$ MPa (organic); up to $2.0$ MPa (ceramic)
> - Typical plate count: **3 to 7 pairs** in production clutches
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: Step-by-Step Logic

The working of a multi-plate clutch can be understood as a layered sequence of mechanical events:

**Step 1 — Plate Alternation (Sandwich Architecture)**
- Driving plates (with external splines engaging the **driving shaft / flywheel housing**) and driven plates (with internal splines engaging the **clutch hub / driven shaft**) are stacked alternately.
- Each driving plate carries the **friction lining**; driven plates are usually **bare steel** (or vice versa depending on design).

**Step 2 — Axial Clamping**
- When the clutch pedal is released, **diaphragm springs or coil springs** push the **pressure plate** axially.
- This clamps the entire plate pack together, generating **uniform contact pressure** across all frictional interfaces.

**Step 3 — Torque Transmission via Friction**
- Engine torque enters via the **driving member** (flywheel + driving plates).
- Friction at the contact surfaces drags the driven plates into rotation.
- Torque is delivered to the **driven shaft** (transmission input).

**Step 4 — Engagement Modulation (Slip → Lock)**
- During engagement, there is a controlled **slip phase** where kinetic energy is converted to heat.
- The plate stack must dissipate this heat without **thermal fade** or **warping**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Multi-Plate"?** The core engineering rationale is **torque density** — torque transmitted per unit clutch diameter. Multi-plate designs can deliver **3× to 5× the torque density** of equivalent single-plate designs, which is the central reason they dominate motorcycle, racing, and heavy-vehicle applications.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table thoroughly — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU University Exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|---|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Pressure distribution | $p = \text{constant}$ | $p \cdot r = \text{constant} = C$ |
| 2 | Maximum pressure location | Anywhere in $[r, R]$ | At inner radius $r$ |
| 3 | Axial force per pair | $W_1 = \pi p (R^2 - r^2)$ | $W_2 = 2\pi C (R - r) = \pi p_{max} r (R - r)$ |
| 4 | Torque per pair | $T_1 = \frac{2}{3} \mu W_1 \frac{(R^3 - r^3)}{(R^2 - r^2)}$ | $T_2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu W_2 (R + r)$ |
| 5 | Torque (multi-plate, n pairs) | $T = n \cdot T_1$ | $T = n \cdot T_2$ |
| 6 | Mean friction radius | $R_m = \frac{2}{3} \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$ | $R_m = \frac{R + r}{2}$ |
| 7 | Average pressure (intuition) | $p_{avg} = p_{max}$ | $p_{avg} = \frac{p_{max} \cdot r}{R}$ |

**Generalized Multi-Plate Compact Equations:**

$$T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m$$

$$W = n_{plates} \cdot W_{per\_pair}$$

$$\text{Number of contact surfaces } (n) = N_{driving} + N_{driven} - 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If the problem statement does not specify the wear theory, KTU examiners expect the student to **solve under BOTH theories** and present comparative results. This is a **5-mark differentiator** in University Exams.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-plate clutches are the **industry standard** in the following domains:

1. **Motorcycles (Wet Multi-Plate)**: Nearly every modern motorcycle uses a **wet multi-plate clutch** bathed in engine oil. This is because the radial envelope inside a motorcycle engine is severely constrained, demanding high torque density in a small diameter.
2. **Racing Cars (Dual or Triple Plate)**: Formula 1, WRC, and drag-racing vehicles use **3 or 4 plates** to handle extreme torque loads (often 700+ N·m) within a 180–240 mm diameter envelope.
3. **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (LCV/HCV Trucks)**: Multi-plate clutches allow the use of **smaller diameter diaphragms** while transmitting 2000+ N·m of diesel engine torque.
4. **Differential Clutches (Limited-Slip Differentials)**: Multi-plate packs are used as **differential lock clutches** in 4WD and LSD systems.
5. **Torque Converters & Automatic Transmissions**: The lock-up clutch and shift clutches inside modern automatics are predominantly multi-plate designs.

### 2.4 Comparison: Multi-Plate vs Single-Plate Clutch

| Feature | Single-Plate Clutch | Multi-Plate Clutch |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Number of contact surfaces | **1** | **n** (3 to 8 typically) |
| Diameter for same torque | Large | **Much smaller (1/√n approx.)** |
| Heat dissipation capacity | High (single large surface) | Lower per surface, but total is high |
| Smoothness of engagement | Better (single slip plane) | Poorer (multiple slip planes) |
| Cost & complexity | Lower | **Higher** |
| Typical applications | Passenger cars | **Motorcycles, trucks, racing, AT** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Torque Capacity Under Uniform Pressure Theory (Multi-Plate)

**Assumption (Uniform Pressure):** The contact pressure **p** is constant over the entire annular friction surface from radius **r** to **R**.

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental annular ring at radius $\rho$ of width $d\rho$.**

The area of this elementary ring is:
$$dA = 2\pi \rho \, d\rho$$

**Step 2 — Elemental normal (axial) force on this ring.**
$$dW = p \cdot dA = p \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from inner radius $r$ to outer radius $R$ to obtain total axial force per pair.**
$$W_1 = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi p \rho \, d\rho = 2\pi p \left[ \frac{\rho^2}{2} \right]_{r}^{R} = \pi p (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 4 — Elemental frictional (tangential) force on the ring.**
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dW = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho$$

**Step 5 — Elemental torque transmitted by this ring about the axis.**
$$dT_1 = \rho \cdot dF_f = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi \rho^2 \, d\rho$$

**Step 6 — Integrate to get the total torque per friction pair.**
$$T_1 = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu p \rho^2 \, d\rho = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \frac{\rho^3}{3} \right]_{r}^{R} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \mu p (R^3 - r^3)$$

**Step 7 — Eliminate $p$ using the relation from Step 3:**
From $W_1 = \pi p (R^2 - r^2)$, we get $p = \frac{W_1}{\pi (R^2 - r^2)}$.

Substituting:
$$T_1 = \frac{2}{3} \pi \mu \left[ \frac{W_1}{\pi (R^2 - r^2)} \right] (R^3 - r^3)$$

$$\boxed{T_1 = \frac{2}{3} \mu W_1 \cdot \frac{(R^3 - r^3)}{(R^2 - r^2)}}$$

**Step 8 — For a multi-plate clutch with $n$ friction pairs:**
$$\boxed{T_{multi,UP} = n \cdot \frac{2}{3} \mu W \cdot \frac{(R^3 - r^3)}{(R^2 - r^2)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Capacity Under Uniform Wear Theory (Multi-Plate)

**Assumption (Uniform Wear):** The product $p \cdot r$ is constant across the contact surface, equal to $C$ (this is based on the assumption that wear rate is proportional to frictional work, and the theory predicts $p_{max}$ at the inner radius $r$).

**Step 1 — Express pressure at any radius $\rho$:**
$$p = \frac{C}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Total axial force per pair:**
$$W_2 = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \rho \cdot p \, d\rho = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \rho \cdot \frac{C}{\rho} d\rho = 2\pi C \int_{r}^{R} d\rho = 2\pi C (R - r)$$

**Step 3 — Maximum pressure occurs at $\rho = r$ (innermost radius):**
$$p_{max} = \frac{C}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = p_{max} \cdot r$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back:**
$$W_2 = 2\pi (p_{max} \cdot r)(R - r) = 2\pi p_{max} r (R - r)$$

**Step 5 — Elemental frictional force on ring at $\rho$:**
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho = \mu \cdot \frac{C}{\rho} \cdot 2\pi \rho \, d\rho = 2\pi \mu C \, d\rho$$

**Step 6 — Elemental torque:**
$$dT_2 = \rho \cdot dF_f = 2\pi \mu C \rho \, d\rho$$

**Step 7 — Total torque per pair:**
$$T_2 = \int_{r}^{R} 2\pi \mu C \rho \, d\rho = 2\pi \mu C \left[ \frac{\rho^2}{2} \right]_{r}^{R} = \pi \mu C (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $C = p_{max} \cdot r$ and use Step 2 to express in terms of $W_2$:**
From $W_2 = 2\pi C (R - r)$, we have $C = \frac{W_2}{2\pi (R - r)}$.

$$T_2 = \pi \mu \cdot \frac{W_2}{2\pi (R - r)} \cdot (R^2 - r^2) = \frac{\mu W_2 (R^2 - r^2)}{2(R - r)} = \frac{\mu W_2 (R + r)}{2}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu W_2 (R + r)}$$

**Step 9 — For a multi-plate clutch with $n$ friction pairs:**
$$\boxed{T_{multi,UW} = n \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mu W (R + r)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU University Exam Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A multi-plate wet clutch has **6 friction surfaces**. The outer and inner radii of the contact area are **100 mm** and **50 mm**, respectively. The maximum intensity of pressure is limited to **0.7 MPa**. Assuming the coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$, determine:
> **(a)** The axial force required to engage the clutch.
> **(b)** The torque capacity of the clutch.

Take both uniform pressure and uniform wear theories into account.

**Given Data:**
- $n = 6$ (friction surfaces)
- $R = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $r = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $p_{max} = 0.7 \text{ MPa} = 0.7 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $\mu = 0.3$

---

**Solution Part (a) — Axial Force:**

**Case 1: Uniform Pressure Theory**

For uniform pressure, $p$ is constant, and total axial force per pair:
$$W_1 = \pi p_{max} (R^2 - r^2)$$

Computing $R^2 - r^2$:
$$R^2 = (0.1)^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2, \quad r^2 = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R^2 - r^2 = 0.01 - 0.0025 = 0.0075 \text{ m}^2$$

Therefore:
$$W_1 = \pi \times 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.0075 = 16493.36 \text{ N per pair}$$

For 6 friction surfaces:
$$W_{total,UP} = 6 \times 16493.36 = \boxed{98960.16 \text{ N} \approx 98.96 \text{ kN}}$$

**Case 2: Uniform Wear Theory**

For uniform wear, axial force per pair:
$$W_2 = 2\pi p_{max} r (R - r)$$
$$W_2 = 2\pi \times 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.05 \times (0.1 - 0.05)$$
$$W_2 = 2\pi \times 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.05 \times 0.05$$
$$W_2 = 2\pi \times 0.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.0025 = 10995.57 \text{ N per pair}$$

For 6 friction surfaces:
$$W_{total,UW} = 6 \times 10995.57 = \boxed{65973.45 \text{ N} \approx 65.97 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**Solution Part (b) — Torque Capacity:**

**Case 1: Uniform Pressure Theory**

$$T_1 = \frac{2}{3} \mu W_1 \cdot \frac{(R^3 - r^3)}{(R^2 - r^2)}$$

Computing $R^3 - r^3$:
$$R^3 = (0.1)^3 = 0.001 \text{ m}^3, \quad r^3 = (0.05)^3 = 0.000125 \text{ m}^3$$
$$R^3 - r^3 = 0.001 - 0.000125 = 0.000875 \text{ m}^3$$

$$T_1 = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 16493.36 \times \frac{0.000875}{0.0075}$$

$$T_1 = 0.2 \times 16493.36 \times 0.11667 = \boxed{384.84 \text{ N·m per pair}}$$

For 6 pairs:
$$T_{multi,UP} = 6 \times 384.84 = \boxed{2309.04 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Case 2: Uniform Wear Theory**

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu W_2 (R + r)$$
$$T_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3 \times 10995.57 \times (0.1 + 0.05)$$
$$T_2 = 0.15 \times 10995.57 \times 0.15 = \boxed{247.40 \text{ N·m per pair}}$$

For 6 pairs:
$$T_{multi,UW} = 6 \times 247.40 = \boxed{1484.40 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

**Summary Table of Results:**

| Quantity | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| Force per pair ($W_1, W_2$) | 16,493.36 N | 10,995.57 N |
| **Total Axial Force** | **98.96 kN** | **65.97 kN** |
| Torque per pair | 384.84 N·m | 247.40 N·m |
| **Total Torque (multi-plate)** | **2309.04 N·m** | **1484.40 N·m** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Plate Clutch

The following block diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the major subsystems of a multi-plate clutch assembly. This is the **KTU-recommended approach** when physical cross-sectional drawings are not feasible in text-based notes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Flywheel] -->|Rotational Input| B[Driving Plate Pack]
    B -->|Friction Contact| C[Friction Lining Interface]
    C -->|Friction Contact| D[Driven Plate Pack]
    D -->|Rotational Output| E[Clutch Hub Driven Shaft]
    F[Diaphragm Spring / Coil Spring] -->|Axial Clamping Force W| B
    G[Release Fork and Bearing] -->|Pedal Actuation Force| F
    H[Clutch Housing] -->|Radial Constraint| B
    H -->|Radial Constraint| D
    I[Circulating Oil System] -->|Heat Dissipation and Lubrication| C
    J[Anti-Rattle Springs] -->|Damping and NVH Control| D
    B --> K{Engaged or Disengaged State}
    K -->|Engaged| L[Torque Transmitted Full]
    K -->|Disengaged| M[Torque Transmission Zero]
```

```mermaid

    sub2[Driven Member System]:::drn

    sub2 --- a2[Driven Plates and Hub on Transmission Input Shaft]

    sub4 --- a4[Oil Jets Splash Lubrication and Fins]
    sub5 --- a5[Bell Housing and Clutch Cover Assembly]

    sub3 -->|Axial Clamping Force| sub1
    sub4 -.->|Heat Removal| sub1
    sub4 -.->|Heat Removal| sub2
    sub5 -->|Radial Constraint and Sealing| sub1
    sub5 -->|Radial Constraint and Sealing| sub2

```mermaid

    L7[Driving Plate with Friction Lining]:::p3
    L8[Driven Plate Steel]:::p2
    L9[Driving Plate with Friction Lining]:::p3
    L10[Driven Plate Steel]:::p2

This gives **8 contact surfaces** (4 + 5 - 1 = 8), a typical motorcycle wet-clutch configuration.
