## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

---

**Q1. Define a multi-plate clutch. Mention any two advantages of a multi-plate clutch over a single-plate clutch. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **multi-plate clutch** is a friction clutch in which **more than two friction surfaces** are used to transmit torque. Multiple driving and driven plates are stacked alternately inside a clutch housing, and the same axial clamping force is distributed over all contact surfaces, thereby multiplying the torque-transmitting capacity.

**Two advantages over single-plate clutch:**
1. **Higher torque capacity in a smaller diameter envelope:** Because the same axial force is effective over $n$ friction surfaces, the diameter of the clutch can be significantly reduced for the same torque transmission. This is critical in motorcycles and racing cars.
2. **Reduced pedal effort:** Since torque is transmitted via multiple surfaces, a lower clamping force is required, which reduces driver effort and wear on the actuation system.

---

**Q2. State the fundamental difference between the uniform pressure theory and the uniform wear theory as applied to clutch design. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Assumption | Pressure $p$ is **constant** over the entire contact surface | The product $p \cdot r$ is **constant** over the contact surface |
| Physical basis | Ideal new clutch, fresh uniform linings | Long-term operating condition with even wear |
| Maximum pressure location | Anywhere on the surface | At the **inner radius** $r$ |
| Design implication | Gives higher torque for the same axial force | Gives **realistic** design torque, used in industry |

> **Examiner's Note:** Students who can articulate that **uniform wear is the more realistic, industry-preferred theory** because it accounts for long-term operational wear typically score full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Pattern]**

---

## OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. A (a)** A multi-plate clutch is to be designed with **5 friction surfaces**. The clutch is required to transmit a maximum torque of **800 N·m**. The outer and inner diameters of the friction surface are **240 mm** and **160 mm**, respectively. The coefficient of friction is **0.3**, and the maximum allowable pressure is **0.8 MPa**. Determine the necessary axial force and verify the design using both uniform pressure and uniform wear theories. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $n = 5$ (friction surfaces)
- $T_{required} = 800$ N·m
- $D = 240$ mm → $R = 0.12$ m
- $d = 160$ mm → $r = 0.08$ m
- $\mu = 0.3$
- $p_{max} = 0.8 \text{ MPa} = 0.8 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$

**Step 1: Compute the geometric terms.**
$$R^2 - r^2 = (0.12)^2 - (0.08)^2 = 0.0144 - 0.0064 = 0.0080 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R^3 - r^3 = (0.12)^3 - (0.08)^3 = 0.001728 - 0.000512 = 0.001216 \text{ m}^3$$
$$R + r = 0.12 + 0.08 = 0.20 \text{ m}$$
$$R - r = 0.12 - 0.08 = 0.04 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the axial force per pair using uniform pressure theory.**
$$W_1 = \pi p_{max} (R^2 - r^2) = \pi \times 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.0080 = 20106.19 \text{ N}$$

**Total axial force (5 pairs):**
$$W_{UP} = 5 \times 20106.19 = 100530.97 \text{ N} \approx 100.53 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Torque capacity under uniform pressure.**
$$T_{UP} = n \cdot \frac{2}{3} \mu W_1 \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$$
$$T_{UP} = 5 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 20106.19 \times \frac{0.001216}{0.0080}$$
$$T_{UP} = 5 \times 0.2 \times 20106.19 \times 0.152 = 3056.14 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the axial force per pair using uniform wear theory.**
$$W_2 = 2\pi p_{max} r (R - r) = 2\pi \times 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.08 \times 0.04$$
$$W_2 = 2\pi \times 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 0.0032 = 16084.95 \text{ N}$$

**Total axial force (5 pairs):**
$$W_{UW} = 5 \times 16084.95 = 80424.77 \text{ N} \approx 80.42 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Torque capacity under uniform wear.**
$$T_{UW} = n \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mu W_2 (R + r)$$
$$T_{UW} = 5 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 16084.95 \times 0.20$$
$$T_{UW} = 5 \times 0.15 \times 16084.95 \times 0.20 = 2412.74 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6: Verification of design.**
- Required torque = **800 N·m**
- Torque provided under UP theory = **3056.14 N·m** ✓
- Torque provided under UW theory = **2412.74 N·m** ✓

**Conclusion:** The design is safe under both theories. The uniform wear theory is more conservative and represents the realistic long-term operating torque capacity.

**Final Answer:**
- Axial force (UP) = **100.53 kN**
- Axial force (UW) = **80.42 kN**
- Torque capacity (UP) = **3056.14 N·m**
- Torque capacity (UW) = **2412.74 N·m**

---

**Q. A (b)** Explain the construction and working of a multi-plate wet clutch used in a typical motorcycle. Discuss the role of oil circulation in such clutches. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**

A typical motorcycle multi-plate wet clutch consists of the following components:

1. **Clutch Basket (Outer Housing):** Internally splined cylindrical housing driven by the engine crankshaft via gear primary drive. Houses the entire plate pack and acts as the driving member.
2. **Friction Plates (Driving Plates):** Steel plates with friction material (paper-based, cork-based, or sintered) bonded to both sides. Externally splined to engage the clutch basket.
3. **Steel Plates (Driven Plates):** Plain steel plates with internal splines that engage the **clutch hub**, which is mounted on the transmission input shaft (countershaft).
4. **Pressure Plate:** The final driven plate in the stack, mounted on the clutch hub. It is acted upon by the spring pressure.
5. **Diaphragm Spring (or Coil Springs):** Provides the axial clamping force. In modern motorcycles, a **diaphragm spring with characteristic "toggle" action** is used for progressive engagement.
6. **Clutch Hub:** Internally splined to engage the driven (steel) plates; externally splined or keyed to the gearbox input shaft.
7. **Release Mechanism:** Comprises the **clutch lever, cable/hydraulic linkage, release (throwout) rod, and pressure plate lifter**.

**Working:**

- When the clutch lever is **released** (no rider input), the diaphragm spring pushes the pressure plate against the plate stack, clamping all the friction and steel plates together.
- Engine torque is transmitted from the **crankshaft → primary gear → clutch basket → friction plates → steel plates → clutch hub → transmission input shaft**.
- When the rider **pulls the clutch lever**, the release mechanism compresses the diaphragm spring, retracting the pressure plate. The plate pack is freed, and torque transmission ceases.

**Role of Oil Circulation:**

The wet clutch operates submerged in engine oil, which serves multiple critical functions:
1. **Heat Dissipation:** Frictional slippage during engagement generates substantial heat. Circulating oil absorbs and carries away this heat, preventing thermal fade and plate warping.
2. **Lubrication:** Reduces wear between the splines and bearing surfaces.
3. **Smooth Engagement:** The oil film between plates provides a controlled, progressive friction transition, reducing judder and shock loading.
4. **Contaminant Removal:** Oil carries away wear debris, preventing abrasive damage.
5. **Noise and NVH Reduction:** Oil damping reduces rattle from the plate pack at idle.

> **Practical Data:** A typical 600cc motorcycle clutch uses 5 friction plates and 4 steel plates, giving 8 contact surfaces, submerged in approximately 100–150 ml of engine oil.

---

## OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q. B (a)** A multi-plate clutch has **7 plates** with outer radius **R = 125 mm** and inner radius **r = 75 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.28** and the axial force applied by the springs is **20 kN**, distributed equally over all friction surfaces. Calculate the torque transmitted by the clutch under **(i) uniform pressure theory** and **(ii) uniform wear theory**. Which theory gives a more conservative (lower) torque estimate, and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Number of plates: 7 → Number of contact surfaces $n = 7$
- $R = 125 \text{ mm} = 0.125 \text{ m}$
- $r = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $\mu = 0.28$
- $W_{total} = 20 \text{ kN} = 20000 \text{ N}$
- Axial force per pair: $W = 20000 / 7 = 2857.14 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Compute geometric terms.**
$$R^2 - r^2 = (0.125)^2 - (0.075)^2 = 0.015625 - 0.005625 = 0.0100 \text{ m}^2$$
$$R^3 - r^3 = (0.125)^3 - (0.075)^3 = 0.001953125 - 0.000421875 = 0.00153125 \text{ m}^3$$
$$R + r = 0.125 + 0.075 = 0.200 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Torque under uniform pressure theory.**

$$T_{UP} = n \cdot \frac{2}{3} \mu W \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$$

$$T_{UP} = 7 \cdot \frac{2}{3} \times 0.28 \times 2857.14 \times \frac{0.00153125}{0.0100}$$

$$T_{UP} = 7 \times 0.18667 \times 2857.14 \times 0.153125$$

$$T_{UP} = 7 \times 81.66 = \boxed{571.62 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 3: Torque under uniform wear theory.**

$$T_{UW} = n \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mu W (R + r)$$

$$T_{UW} = 7 \times 0.5 \times 0.28 \times 2857.14 \times 0.200$$

$$T_{UW} = 7 \times 0.14 \times 2857.14 \times 0.200$$

$$T_{UW} = 7 \times 80.00 = \boxed{560.00 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4: Comparison and conclusion.**

- $T_{UP} = 571.62$ N·m
- $T_{UW} = 560.00$ N·m
- $T_{UW} < T_{UP}$ → **Uniform wear theory gives a more conservative (lower) torque estimate**.

**Reason:** The uniform wear theory assumes the actual operating condition where wear is uniform across the contact surface, leading to higher pressure concentration at the inner radius. This makes it a more **realistic long-term design criterion**, while uniform pressure theory represents an idealized new-clutch condition with unrealistically uniform pressure distribution.

---

**Q. B (b)** Compare single-plate and multi-plate clutches in terms of **(i) torque capacity for the same diameter, (ii) heat dissipation, (iii) smoothness of engagement, and (iv) typical applications.** Justify why multi-plate clutches are preferred in motorcycles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Torque Capacity for the Same Diameter:**

A single-plate clutch has only **one friction surface**, whereas a multi-plate clutch has **n** surfaces. For the same outer diameter and axial force:

$$T_{multi} = n \cdot T_{single}$$

Thus, the multi-plate clutch transmits **n times** the torque of a single-plate clutch of identical dimensions. This is the most important advantage and is the primary reason multi-plate clutches dominate torque-dense applications.

**(ii) Heat Dissipation:**

| Aspect | Single-Plate | Multi-Plate |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Heat per surface | Higher (one large surface) | Lower (distributed) |
| Total heat dissipation | Better due to larger single area | Adequate due to multiple smaller areas + oil cooling in wet types |
| Thermal fade risk | Higher (one hot spot) | Lower in wet multi-plate due to oil flow |

**(iii) Smoothness of Engagement:**

- **Single-plate** provides smoother, more progressive engagement because there is only one slip plane.
- **Multi-plate** engagement is **less smooth** because multiple slip planes engage simultaneously, potentially causing judder. However, oil damping in wet multi-plate clutches greatly mitigates this issue.

**(iv) Typical Applications:**

- **Single-plate clutches:** Passenger cars, light commercial vehicles, most road-going passenger cars.
- **Multi-plate clutches:** Motorcycles, racing cars (F1, WRC), heavy commercial vehicles, automatic transmissions, limited-slip differentials, ATV/UTV drivetrains.

**Why Multi-Plate is Preferred in Motorcycles:**

1. **Radial Space Constraint:** A motorcycle engine has a very narrow crankcase, and the clutch must fit inside a small diameter. Multi-plate design delivers high torque in a small package.
2. **High Torque Density:** A typical 600cc motorcycle engine produces ~70 N·m of torque, which is best handled by a multi-plate wet clutch of ~130 mm diameter.
3. **Oil Cooling:** The wet multi-plate design is ideally suited to motorcycles because the clutch is submerged in engine oil, which already circulates in abundance.
4. **Smoothness via Oil:** The oil film dampens engagement shocks, addressing the inherent smoothness concern.
5. **Lightweight Actuation:** Lower spring force is required, reducing lever effort and improving ergonomics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls & Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $n$:** Many students compute torque for **one friction pair** but forget to multiply by the number of contact surfaces. This typically results in a **3 to 5 mark deduction** in 14-mark numerical problems.
> 2. **Unit Confusion in Pressure:** Mixing up $p$ in MPa and N/m² is a common error. Always convert: $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$.
> 3. **Mixing UP and UW formulas:** Students often apply the UP torque formula with UW axial force or vice versa. Always use **consistent pairs**: $W_1$ with $T_1$ and $W_2$ with $T_2$.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions clearly:** Examiners award partial credit for clearly stating the theory being applied. Always begin each solution with: *"Assuming uniform pressure theory, we have..."*
> 5. **Radius vs Diameter:** $R$ and $r$ are **radii**. If the problem gives diameter, divide by 2 *before* substitution.
> 6. **Number of contact surfaces ≠ Number of plates:** If the problem says "7 plates" without specifying, $n$ may be 6 or 7 depending on whether the flywheel surface and pressure plate surface are counted. State your assumption clearly.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** All SI calculations require meters. A $100$ mm radius is $0.1$ m, not $100$ m.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Multi Plate Clutch:**

- [x] A **multi-plate clutch** uses **multiple friction surfaces** to multiply torque capacity without increasing clutch diameter.
- [x] The **number of contact surfaces** $n$ = (number of driving plates) + (number of driven plates) - 1, depending on whether flywheel and pressure plate surfaces are included.
- [x] **Two governing theories:** Uniform Pressure (UP) and Uniform Wear (UW). Both must be solved in KTU problems unless specified otherwise.
- [x] **UP Theory:** $p$ = constant → $W_1 = \pi p (R^2 - r^2)$ and $T_1 = \frac{2}{3} \mu W_1 \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$.
- [x] **UW Theory:** $p \cdot r$ = constant → $W_2 = 2\pi p_{max} r (R - r)$ and $T_2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu W_2 (R + r)$.
- [x] **Multi-plate generalisation:** $T_{multi} = n \cdot T_{single\ pair}$ for both theories.
- [x] **UW theory is more conservative** and is the **industry-preferred design basis** because it accounts for long-term wear.
- [x] **Wet multi-plate clutches** are dominant in motorcycles due to **oil cooling, smooth engagement, and high torque density in a small diameter**.
- [x] **Dry multi-plate clutches** are used in racing (F1, WRC) for higher friction coefficients and zero parasitic loss from oil drag.
- [x] **Typical plate count:** 4 to 8 contact surfaces in production clutches.
- [x] **Standard $p_{max}$:** 0.7 to 1.5 MPa for organic linings; up to 3.5 MPa for ceramic/sintered.
- [x] **Standard $\mu$:** 0.25 to 0.4 (organic); up to 0.5 (sintered).
- [x] **Key advantage:** Torque density is **n times** higher than single-plate for the same outer diameter.
- [x] **Key disadvantage:** Multiple slip planes reduce engagement smoothness (mitigated by oil in wet clutches).
- [x] **Mean friction radius** $R_m$ is the radius at which the total frictional force is considered to act:
  - UP: $R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$
  - UW: $R_m = \frac{R + r}{2}$
- [x] **Compact form:** $T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W_{per\ pair} \cdot R_m$ — useful for quick verification in the exam.
- [x] **Always convert mm to m** before substituting into SI equations.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** (UP or UW) at the start of every KTU numerical solution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cone Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cone Clutch ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Cone Clutch** is a *positive-contact* friction clutch in which the male and female members have matching conical (tapered) friction surfaces. The male cone (driver) is forced axially into the female cone (driven), and the resulting wedging action generates a normal reaction at the cone interface. The tangential component of this normal reaction produces the friction torque that transmits power from the driving shaft to the driven shaft. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAUT503 – Module 1):** > The cone clutch is studied under *Friction Clutches* alongside single-plate, multi-plate, and centrifugal clutches. Mastery of the *semi-cone angle* concept and the *uniform wear* / *uniform pressure* derivations is mandatory for ESE (End Semester Evaluation). **Key Geometric Parameter:** $$\alpha \;\;=\;\; \text{Semi-cone Angle} \;\;=\;\; 12.5^{\circ} \text{ to } 20^{\circ} \text{ (standard KTU design range)}$$ | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | |:------:|:---------|:------------:| | $\alpha$ | Semi-cone angle | degrees (°) | | $D_1$ | Outer diameter of cone face | mm | | $D_2$ | Inner diameter of cone face | mm | | $R_1$ | Outer radius | mm | | $R_2$ | Inner radius | mm | | $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless | | $W$ | Axial operating thrust | N | | $T$ | Transmitted torque | N·m | | $p$ | Local contact pressure | N/mm² | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Tapered Plug" Model** > Imagine pushing a tapered cork (like a wine bottle cork) into its neck. Even a small *axial push* creates a *very large sideways force* on the neck walls. The steeper the taper, the more "wedging" effect you get. Now imagine that sideways force is generating **friction** at the contact — and friction is what transmits torque. This is *exactly* how a cone clutch works: **a small axial push generates a large normal force, and hence a large torque.** ### 1.3 Constructional Features A standard cone clutch consists of: - **Male Cone (Driver):** Mounted on the driving shaft; has an outer conical friction face. Usually lined with friction material (asbestos, sintered bronze, or ceramic composites). - **Female Cone (Driven):** Has an inner conical cavity that mates with the male cone. - **Operating Lever / Thrust Bearing Assembly:** Provides the axial force $W$ to engage the clutch. - **Return Spring:** Disengages the clutch when the lever is released. - **Friction Lining:** Bonded to the male cone face to increase $\mu$. ``` [Driving Shaft] | |--- Male Cone (with lining) \ \ <-- Semi-cone angle α \___ [Contact Cone Surface] ___/ / / <-- Semi-cone angle α /--- Female Cone (driven) | [Driven Shaft] ``` ### 1.4 Merits and Demerits **Advantages:** - Higher torque capacity than a single-plate clutch of equivalent size. - Simple construction; fewer parts. - Smooth engagement due to gradual surface contact. - Self-energizing effect (the normal force is amplified). **Disadvantages:** - Slight axial movement during engagement → thrust bearing wear. - Tendency for *self-locking* if the semi-cone angle is too small relative to friction angle. - Difficult to disengage completely (residual contact). - Heat dissipation is limited compared to plate clutches. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-Locking Condition:** A cone clutch becomes *self-locking* (i.e., it cannot be disengaged by an axial pull) when: > $$\alpha \;\leq\; \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$ > For this reason, designers choose $\alpha$ between **12.5° and 20°** so that the clutch *can* be disengaged reliably. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Wedging Action — The Core "Why" When an axial force $W$ is applied, the cone surface can only react through a force *normal* to itself. Let the total normal reaction at the interface be $N$. From force balance on the cone axis (perpendicular to the contact surface): $$W \;=\; N \sin\alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N \;=\; \frac{W}{\sin\alpha}$$ The friction force at the contact is: $$F_f \;=\; \mu N \;=\; \frac{\mu W}{\sin\alpha}$$ > [!NOTE] > Because $\sin\alpha < 1$, the normal force $N$ is **amplified** compared to the applied axial force $W$. This is the *self-energizing* effect — the very feature that makes the cone clutch powerful. ### 2.2 Two Standard Pressure Assumptions (KTU Framework) In any friction clutch, the *actual* pressure distribution across the contact area is neither perfectly uniform nor perfectly following the wear law. To make engineering analysis tractable, KTU adopts **two limiting-case theories**: | # | Theory Name | Assumption | Engineering Meaning | |:-:|:-----------|:-----------|:-------------------| | 1 | **Uniform Pressure** | Pressure $p$ = constant across the contact face | New clutch; rigid linings; freshly refaced surfaces | | 2 | **Uniform Wear** | $p \cdot r = C$ (constant) across the contact face | Well-worn clutch in steady-state operation; *this is the KTU design basis* | ### 2.3 Elemental Geometry on the Cone Surface Consider a thin ring on the cone at radius $r$, with radial width $dr$. - The **projected** flat area of the ring: $dA_{flat} = 2\pi r \, dr$ - Because the surface is inclined at angle $\alpha$, the **actual cone surface** area is larger: $$dA_{cone} \;=\; \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ ### 2.4 Uniform Pressure Theory — Step-by-Step Logic 1. Assume $p$ = constant (N/mm²). 2. The normal force on the element: $dN = p \cdot dA_{cone} = \dfrac{2\pi p \, r}{\sin\alpha} \, dr$. 3. Friction force on the element: $df = \mu \, dN = \dfrac{2\pi \mu p \, r}{\sin\alpha} \, dr$. 4. Elemental torque (about the shaft axis): $dT = df \cdot r = \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{\sin\alpha} \, r^2 \, dr$. 5. Integrate from $R_2$ to $R_1$: $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3\sin\alpha}\,(R_1^{3} - R_2^{3})$$ 6. From axial equilibrium: $W = \int p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \pi p (R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}) \quad\Rightarrow\quad p = \dfrac{W}{\pi(R_1^{2}-R_2^{2})}$. 7. Substituting $p$: $$\boxed{\,T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\,\frac{R_1^{3} - R_2^{3}}{R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\,\frac{R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2}}{R_1 + R_2}\,}$$ ### 2.5 Uniform Wear Theory — Step-by-Step Logic 1. Assume $p \cdot r = C$ (constant) so that wear rate is uniform. 2. Elemental normal force: $dN = p \cdot dA_{cone} = \dfrac{2\pi p r}{\sin\alpha}\,dr = \dfrac{2\pi C}{\sin\alpha}\,dr$. 3. Friction force: $df = \mu \, dN = \dfrac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,dr$. 4. Elemental torque: $dT = df \cdot r = \dfrac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,r \, dr$. 5. Integrate: $$T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})$$ 6. From axial equilibrium: $W = \int p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi C (R_1 - R_2) \quad\Rightarrow\quad C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi(R_1 - R_2)}$. 7. Substituting $C$: $$\boxed{\,T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\mu W}{2\sin\alpha}\,(R_1 + R_2) \;=\; \frac{\mu W (D_1 + D_2)}{4\sin\alpha}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Design Rule:** Always use the **Uniform Wear** expression for design (most realistic, conservative) and the **Uniform Pressure** expression for checking the *initial engagement* condition of a brand-new clutch. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Memorize the table below — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU cone-clutch problems.** | # | Quantity | Uniform Pressure (UP) | Uniform Wear (UW) | Units | |:-:|:---------|:---------------------|:-----------------|:-----:| | 1 | Elemental cone area | $\dfrac{2\pi r \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi r \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$ | mm² | | 2 | Pressure assumption | $p = \text{const.}$ | $p \cdot r = C$ | N/mm² ; N/mm | | 3 | Axial force $W$ | $\pi p (R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})$ | $2\pi C (R_1 - R_2)$ | N | | 4 | Normal force amplification | $N = W/\sin\alpha$ | $N = W/\sin\alpha$ | N | | 5 | **Transmitted Torque $T$** | $\dfrac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{R_1^{2}+R_1 R_2+R_2^{2}}{R_1+R_2}$ | $\dfrac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha}$ | N·mm | | 6 | Torque (diameter form) | $\dfrac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{D_1^{2}+D_1 D_2+D_2^{2}}{2(D_1+D_2)}$ | $\dfrac{\mu W (D_1 + D_2)}{4\sin\alpha}$ | N·mm | | 7 | Effective radius factor | $\dfrac{2(R_1^{2}+R_1R_2+R_2^{2})}{3(R_1+R_2)}$ | $\dfrac{R_1+R_2}{2}$ | mm | | 8 | Self-locking check | $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu$ | $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu$ | degrees | | 9 | Multi-cone factor | $T_{total} = n \cdot T_{single}$ | $T_{total} = n \cdot T_{single}$ | N·m | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Synchromesh gearboxes in manual cars** use a form of cone synchronizer (similar in wedging principle) to equalize shaft speeds before gear engagement. - **Differential cone clutches (limited-slip differentials / LSDs)** in performance vehicles use the same friction-cone principle to distribute torque between wheels. - **Centrifugal cone clutches** in small engines (go-karts, mopeds, scooters) use flyweights acting on a conical drum — a direct application. - **Industrial power take-offs (PTOs)** and **machine-tool drives** historically employed cone clutches for soft-start engagement. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Uniform Pressure Theory > [!NOTE] > The derivation below proceeds element by element, with every algebraic step explicitly shown. Do **not** skip steps during valuation. **Step 1 — Geometry of the elemental ring:** Consider a ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the cone surface. Its *flat* projected area is $dA_{proj} = 2\pi r \, dr$. Since the cone is inclined at semi-cone angle $\alpha$, the *true* contact area on the cone surface is: $$dA_{cone} \;=\; \frac{dA_{proj}}{\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ **Step 2 — Force on the element:** The pressure $p$ acts normal to the cone surface. The normal force on the element is: $$dN \;=\; p \cdot dA_{cone} \;=\; \frac{2\pi p r \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Axial equilibrium to find $W$ in terms of $p$:** The component of $dN$ along the shaft axis is $dN \cdot \sin\alpha$. The total axial force $W$ (operating force) is: $$W \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} dN \cdot \sin\alpha \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} 2\pi p r \, dr \;=\; 2\pi p \left[\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right]_{R_2}^{R_1}$$ $$W \;=\; \pi p (R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $p$:** $$p \;=\; \frac{W}{\pi(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})}$$ **Step 5 — Elemental friction force and torque:** The friction force tangential to the cone surface (and hence at right angles to the radius when projected to the shaft axis) is: $$df \;=\; \mu \cdot dN \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu p r \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ The elemental torque transmitted by this ring about the shaft axis is: $$dT \;=\; df \cdot r \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu p r^{2} \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ **Step 6 — Integrate from $R_2$ to $R_1$:** $$T_{UP} \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} \frac{2\pi \mu p}{\sin\alpha} \, r^{2} \, dr \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu p}{\sin\alpha}\left[\frac{r^{3}}{3}\right]_{R_2}^{R_1}$$ $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3\sin\alpha}\,(R_1^{3} - R_2^{3})$$ **Step 7 — Substitute the expression for $p$ from Step 4:** $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{W}{\pi(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})}\cdot(R_1^{3} - R_2^{3})$$ $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{R_1^{3} - R_2^{3}}{R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}}$$ **Step 8 — Factor the numerator and denominator using sum/difference of cubes:** $$R_1^{3} - R_2^{3} \;=\; (R_1 - R_2)(R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2})$$ $$R_1^{2} - R_2^{2} \;=\; (R_1 - R_2)(R_1 + R_2)$$ Therefore: $$\frac{R_1^{3} - R_2^{3}}{R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}} \;=\; \frac{R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2}}{R_1 + R_2}$$ **Step 9 — Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\,\cdot\,\frac{R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2}}{R_1 + R_2}\,} \quad \text{[N·mm]}$$ In diameter form (using $R_1 = D_1/2$ and $R_2 = D_2/2$): $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\,\cdot\,\frac{D_1^{2} + D_1 D_2 + D_2^{2}}{2(D_1 + D_2)}$$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Uniform Wear Theory **Step 1 — Pressure assumption:** The uniform-wear theory assumes the product of pressure and radius is constant: $$p \cdot r \;=\; C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad p \;=\; \frac{C}{r}$$ **Step 2 — Elemental normal force:** $$dN \;=\; p \cdot dA_{cone} \;=\; \frac{C}{r}\cdot\frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{2\pi C \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Axial equilibrium:** $$W \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} dN \cdot \sin\alpha \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} 2\pi C \, dr \;=\; 2\pi C (R_1 - R_2)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $C$:** $$C \;=\; \frac{W}{2\pi(R_1 - R_2)}$$ **Step 5 — Elemental friction force and torque:** $$df \;=\; \mu \, dN \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu C \, dr}{\sin\alpha}$$ $$dT \;=\; df \cdot r \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,r \, dr$$ **Step 6 — Integrate:** $$T_{UW} \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,r \, dr \;=\; \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\left[\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right]_{R_2}^{R_1}$$ $$T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})$$ **Step 7 — Substitute $C$ from Step 4:** $$T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\pi \mu}{\sin\alpha}\,\cdot\,\frac{W}{2\pi(R_1 - R_2)}\,\cdot\,(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})$$ $$T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\mu W}{2\sin\alpha}\,\cdot\,\frac{R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}}{R_1 - R_2} \;=\; \frac{\mu W}{2\sin\alpha}\,(R_1 + R_2)$$ **Step 8 — Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{\mu W (D_1 + D_2)}{4\sin\alpha}\,} \quad \text{[N·mm]}$$ ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example — Uniform Wear Theory** > A single cone clutch has $D_1 = 300$ mm, $D_2 = 200$ mm, $\alpha = 15°$, $\mu = 0.3$, and is operated with $W = 1000$ N. Find the torque transmitted. **Solution:** Given: $R_1 = 150$ mm, $R_2 = 100$ mm, $\alpha = 15°$, $\mu = 0.3$, $W = 1000$ N. Using the uniform-wear formula (KTU design basis): $$T \;=\; \frac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha}$$ Compute $\sin 15° = 0.2588$: $$T \;=\; \frac{0.3 \times 1000 \times (150 + 100)}{2 \times 0.2588}$$ $$T \;=\; \frac{0.3 \times 1000 \times 250}{0.5176}$$ $$T \;=\; \frac{75000}{0.5176}$$ $$\boxed{\,T \;\approx\; 144{,}900 \,\text{N·mm} \;\approx\; 144.9 \text{ N·m}\,}$$ **Verification using uniform-pressure formula:** $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2}}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Numerator of geometric factor: $R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2} = 150^2 + 150 \times 100 + 100^2 = 22500 + 15000 + 10000 = 47500$ mm² Denominator: $R_1 + R_2 = 250$ mm $$T_{UP} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 1000}{3 \times 0.2588}\cdot\frac{47500}{250} \;=\; \frac{600}{0.7765}\times 190$$ $$T_{UP} \;=\; 772.6 \times 190 \;\approx\; 146{,}800 \,\text{N·mm} \;\approx\; 146.8 \text{ N·m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The uniform-pressure result is slightly *higher* than the uniform-wear result. This is why uniform wear is the **conservative** design basis used in KTU board problems. ### 3.4 Multi-Cone Clutch Extension When $n$ identical cone pairs act in parallel, the total torque multiplies by $n$: $$T_{multi,n} \;=\; n \times T_{single}$$ However, the axial force $W$ is the *same* — only the contact area increases. This makes the multi-cone clutch the preferred solution when axial force is limited (e.g., by a driver's foot pedal effort). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cone Clutch Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > Mermaid diagram below shows the **block-level functional architecture** of a cone clutch system — the operating mechanism, the cone interface, the torque path, and the disengagement loop. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Driver Input Pedal Lever] --> B[Operating Lever and Thrust Bearing] B -->|Axial Force W| C[Male Cone Driver] C -->|Wedging Action via Friction Lining| D[Female Cone Driven Member] D --> E[Driven Shaft Output] D -.->|Friction Torque T| F[Load Side Application] G[Return Spring] -.->|Restoring Force| B H[Semi-cone Angle alpha] -->|Amplification Factor 1/sin alpha| C I[Coefficient of Friction mu] -->|Friction Force| D J[Lubrication and Cooling Path] -.Heat.-> C J -.Heat.-> D classDef force fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef interface fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef control fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 class A,B,G,H,I force class C,D interface class J control class E,F output ``` ### 4.2 Force Resolution Diagram on a Cone Element > [!NOTE] > The diagram below shows how the axial operating force $W$ is *resolved* into a normal reaction $N$ and a friction force $\mu N$ at the cone surface. The amplification factor $1/\sin\alpha$ is the key to the cone clutch's high torque capacity. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Axial Force W] -->|Decompose| B[Normal to Cone N = W over sin alpha] B -->|Resolve| C[Friction Force mu N = mu W over sin alpha] C -->|Times radius Rm| D[Torque T = mu W Rm over sin alpha] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000 style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Cone Clutch (Textual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DRIVING_SHAFT DS1[Driving Shaft Input] DS2[Hub Keyed to Shaft] end subgraph MALE_CONE MC1[Cone with Semi-Angle alpha] MC2[Friction Lining Bonded] end subgraph FEMALE_CONE FC1[Conical Cavity] FC2[External Splines to Gear] end subgraph DRIVEN_SHAFT OS1[Driven Shaft Output] OS2[Bearing Support] end DS1 --> DS2 --> MC1 MC1 -->|Contact via MC2| FC1 FC1 --> FC2 --> OS1 OS1 --> OS2 style MALE_CONE fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style FEMALE_CONE fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4 style DRIVING_SHAFT fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 style DRIVEN_SHAFT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Diagram: Cone Clutch vs Single-Plate Clutch ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cone Clutch

T = mu W times Rm] -->|Lower T for same W| D[Larger Diameter Needed] B --> E[Advantage in Tight Spaces] D --> F[Used in Heavy Commercial Vehicles] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > [!NOTE] > These are typical **2-mark/3-mark short-answer** questions from KTU ESE — concise, definition-based, and must be answered in **40–60 words**. --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **Define semi-cone angle. What is its typical range in cone clutch design?** **Model Answer:** The semi-cone angle $\alpha$ is the angle that the friction face of the cone makes with the axis of the shaft. It governs the wedging action: a smaller $\alpha$ produces a larger normal force $N = W/\sin\alpha$, but if $\alpha$ is too small the clutch becomes self-locking. The KTU-recommended design range is $\alpha = 12.5°$ to $20°$. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **State the condition for self-locking of a cone clutch and explain why it is undesirable.** **Model Answer:** A cone clutch becomes self-locking when the semi-cone angle satisfies $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$. In this condition, the friction force is sufficient to prevent the male cone from sliding out of the female cone even without the axial disengaging force. This is undesirable because the clutch cannot be disengaged by the operator's pedal, making gear changes impossible. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — *students must attempt either Q-A or Q-B*, not both. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the torque transmitted by a single cone clutch based on **uniform wear theory**. State all assumptions clearly. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A cone clutch is required to transmit **15 kW at 900 rpm**. The semi-cone angle is $12.5°$ and the coefficient of friction is $0.25$. The mean radius of the cone is **120 mm**. Using the uniform-wear theory, determine: (i) the axial force required, and (ii) the inner diameter of the cone face if the outer diameter is **300 mm**. **\[7 Marks\]** --- ### 📘 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] **Assumptions:** 1. The clutch operates under **uniform wear** condition, i.e., $p \cdot r = C$ (constant). 2. The pressure $p$ acts **normal to the cone surface**. 3. The cone has uniform friction lining with coefficient of friction $\mu$. 4. Wear is proportional to frictional work, hence $p \cdot v$ = constant; with $v \propto r$, this gives $p \cdot r$ = constant. 5. The axial force $W$ acts uniformly along the shaft axis. **Derivation:** Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the cone surface. The projected flat area is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. Since the surface is inclined at semi-cone angle $\alpha$, the *actual* cone area is $dA_{cone} = 2\pi r \, dr / \sin\alpha$. Normal force on the element: $$dN \;=\; p \cdot dA_{cone} \;=\; \frac{2\pi p r \, dr}{\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{2\pi C \, dr}{\sin\alpha} \quad [\because p r = C]$$ Axial equilibrium: $W = \int dN \sin\alpha = 2\pi C (R_1 - R_2)$, giving $C = W/[2\pi(R_1 - R_2)]$. Friction force on the element: $df = \mu \, dN$. Elemental torque: $dT = df \cdot r = \dfrac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,r \, dr$. Integrating from $R_2$ to $R_1$: $$T \;=\; \int_{R_2}^{R_1} \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,r \, dr \;=\; \frac{\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha}\,(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2})$$ Substituting $C$: $$T \;=\; \frac{\pi \mu}{\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{W}{2\pi(R_1 - R_2)}\cdot(R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}) \;=\; \frac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha}$$ $$\boxed{\,T_{UW} \;=\; \frac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{\mu W (D_1 + D_2)}{4\sin\alpha}\,} \quad \text{(5 marks for derivation steps + 2 marks for final result and assumptions)}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks] **Given:** - Power $P = 15$ kW = 15,000 W - Speed $N = 900$ rpm - Semi-cone angle $\alpha = 12.5°$ → $\sin 12.5° = 0.2164$ - Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$ - Mean radius $R_m = 120$ mm - Outer diameter $D_1 = 300$ mm → $R_1 = 150$ mm **Step 1 — Compute the torque required:** $$T \;=\; \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 15000}{2\pi \times 900}$$ $$T \;=\; \frac{900000}{5654.87} \;\approx\; 159.15 \text{ N·m} \;=\; 159{,}150 \text{ N·mm}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the axial force $W$:** Note: $R_m = (R_1 + R_2)/2 = 120$ mm, so $R_1 + R_2 = 240$ mm. From the uniform-wear formula: $$W \;=\; \frac{2 T \sin\alpha}{\mu (R_1 + R_2)} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 159150 \times 0.2164}{0.25 \times 240}$$ $$W \;=\; \frac{68880.3}{60} \;\approx\; 1148 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{\,W \;\approx\; 1148 \text{ N}\,}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the inner diameter $D_2$:** $R_1 + R_2 = 240$ mm, and $R_1 = 150$ mm: $$R_2 \;=\; 240 - 150 \;=\; 90 \text{ mm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad D_2 \;=\; 180 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{\,D_2 \;=\; 180 \text{ mm}\,}$$ --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]` *(Alternative to Q-A)* **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **cone clutch**. Discuss the effect of the semi-cone angle on its performance. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A multi-cone clutch has **two identical cone pairs**. Each cone has $D_1 = 250$ mm, $D_2 = 150$ mm, $\alpha = 15°$, and $\mu = 0.28$. The axial force is $1200$ N. Compute the total torque transmitted using (i) uniform pressure theory and (ii) uniform wear theory. **\[7 Marks\]** --- ### 📘 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B #### Part (a) — Theory [7 Marks] **Construction:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 cross-sectional schematic above.)* A cone clutch consists of a male cone keyed to the driving shaft and a female cone splined to the driven shaft. The conical friction faces have a semi-cone angle $\alpha$ (typically 12.5° to 20°). The male cone is lined with friction material. An operating lever (or thrust bearing) applies an axial force $W$ that pushes the cones together. **Working:** When the operating lever is depressed, the axial force $W$ pushes the male cone into the female cone. The conical interface generates a normal reaction $N = W/\sin\alpha$. Friction force $\mu N$ acts tangentially, producing a torque $T = \mu W R_m / \sin\alpha$, transmitting power from driving to driven shaft. **Effect of semi-cone angle:** - $\alpha$ **small** → $1/\sin\alpha$ large → high torque amplification → clutch may *self-lock* if $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}\mu$. - $\alpha$ **large** → wedging effect reduced → axial force required becomes very high → clutch may not engage properly. - **Optimum range:** $12.5° \leq \alpha \leq 20°$. *(4 marks for construction + working, 3 marks for effect of α)* --- #### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks] **Given (per cone):** - $D_1 = 250$ mm, $D_2 = 150$ mm → $R_1 = 125$ mm, $R_2 = 75$ mm - $\alpha = 15°$ → $\sin 15° = 0.2588$ - $\mu = 0.28$, $W = 1200$ N - $n = 2$ cone pairs **Step 1 — Single-cone torque using uniform pressure theory:** $$T_{UP,single} \;=\; \frac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\frac{R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2}}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Compute $R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2} = 125^2 + 125 \times 75 + 75^2 = 15625 + 9375 + 5625 = 30625$ mm² $R_1 + R_2 = 200$ mm $$T_{UP,single} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.28 \times 1200}{3 \times 0.2588}\cdot\frac{30625}{200}$$ $$T_{UP,single} \;=\; \frac{672}{0.7764}\cdot 153.125$$ $$T_{UP,single} \;=\; 865.6 \times 153.125 \;\approx\; 132{,}550 \text{ N·mm}$$ Multi-cone total: $T_{UP,total} = 2 \times 132550 \approx 265{,}100 \text{ N·mm} \approx 265.1 \text{ N·m}$ **Step 2 — Single-cone torque using uniform wear theory:** $$T_{UW,single} \;=\; \frac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.28 \times 1200 \times 200}{2 \times 0.2588}$$ $$T_{UW,single} \;=\; \frac{67200}{0.5176} \;\approx\; 129{,}830 \text{ N·mm}$$ Multi-cone total: $T_{UW,total} = 2 \times 129830 \approx 259{,}660 \text{ N·mm} \approx 259.7 \text{ N·m}$ **Final answers:** $$\boxed{\,T_{UP,total} \;\approx\; 265.1 \text{ N·m}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,T_{UW,total} \;\approx\; 259.7 \text{ N·m}\,}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losses:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $1/\sin\alpha$ factor:** Many students compute the torque as if it were a flat-plate clutch ($T = \mu W R_m$). This loses **3–4 marks** instantly. Always check: *is it a cone?* If yes, multiply by $1/\sin\alpha$. > > 2. **Mixing up uniform pressure and uniform wear formulas:** UW is the design basis (KTU default). UP is the *initial* engagement check. The two give different numerical answers — using the wrong one in a numerical problem yields **wrong final answer** even if your derivation is correct. > > 3. **Forgetting to convert $D$ to $R$:** The $D_1$ and $D_2$ in the problem are *diameters*; the formula needs *radii* (or you must use the diameter form). Substituting $D$ where $R$ is expected loses **2 marks**. > > 4. **Wrong self-locking condition:** Writing $\alpha \leq \mu$ (without $\tan^{-1}$) is a frequent error. The correct form is $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$. > > 5. **Multi-cone: multiplying $W$ instead of $T$:** The axial force is the *same* per pair; the torque adds up. Multiplying $W$ by $n$ and plugging back is a **trick — don't fall for it**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > Use this bulleted summary as your **last-30-minutes revision** before the KTU exam. - **Cone Clutch** = Friction clutch with conical (tapered) contact surfaces; transmits torque via wedging. - **Semi-cone angle $\alpha$** is the angle between the cone face and the shaft axis; **standard KTU range: 12.5°–20°**. - **Wedging effect:** $N = W/\sin\alpha$ — the normal force is amplified by the factor $1/\sin\alpha > 1$. - **Self-locking condition:** $\alpha \leq \tan^{-1}(\mu)$ — never design in this regime. - **Two pressure theories** (must memorize both): - **Uniform Pressure (UP):** $T_{UP} = \dfrac{2\mu W}{3\sin\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{R_1^{2} + R_1 R_2 + R_2^{2}}{R_1 + R_2}$ - **Uniform Wear (UW):** $T_{UW} = \dfrac{\mu W (R_1 + R_2)}{2\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{\mu W (D_1 + D_2)}{4\sin\alpha}$ - **Default theory for KTU design problems = Uniform Wear (UW)** — gives a conservative (smaller) torque. - **Uniform Pressure** is used for *initial engagement* checks on a new clutch. - **Effective mean radius** for UW theory: $R_m = (R_1 + R_2)/2$. - **Multi-cone clutch:** $T_{total} = n \times T_{single}$; same $W$, but contact area multiplies. - **Power-torque-speed relation (must memorize):** $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ N·m (with $P$ in W, $N$ in rpm). - **Cone clutch advantages:** High torque per unit axial force; compact; smooth engagement. - **Cone clutch disadvantages:** Axial thrust on bearings; risk of self-locking; poor heat dissipation. - **Real-world applications:** Synchromesh gearboxes, centrifugal clutches, limited-slip differentials, industrial PTOs. - **Always convert** given $D$ to $R$ *before* substituting into a formula (or use the diameter form correctly). - **Check units** in numerical problems: $W$ in N, $R$ in mm, $T$ in N·mm (or convert to N·m). - **Coefficient of safety** ($c_s$ or $n$): If a problem says "with a factor of safety of 2", then $W_{design} = n \cdot W_{computed}$ (i.e., the design axial force is amplified). - **Effective force on the contact** in terms of mean pressure $p$: $$W \;=\; \pi p (R_1^{2} - R_2^{2}) \quad \text{(UP)} \qquad \qquad W \;=\; 2\pi C (R_1 - R_2) \quad \text{(UW)}$$

topics.title: Semi Centrifugal Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semi Centrifugal Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard Terminology):**
> A **Semi-Centrifugal Clutch** is a hybrid friction clutch that combines the operating principles of a **single-plate coil-spring clutch** and a **centrifugal clutch**. At low engine speeds, the engagement force is provided entirely by the **helical compression springs**. As the engine speed (and hence the shaft speed) exceeds a pre-determined **critical speed ($\omega_c$)**, the **centrifugal force** generated by the flyweights (shoes) mounted on the pressure plate levers provides an **additional axial thrust**, augmenting the spring force. This dual-force mechanism allows the use of **lighter springs**, thereby **reducing the driver pedal effort**, while simultaneously increasing the torque-transmitting capacity at high speeds to prevent slippage.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this — "The Gym Analogy":**
> Imagine you are at the gym trying to lift a heavy barbell. When you start, the bar feels extremely heavy because *you alone* have to push it up. Now imagine **spring-loaded hydraulic pistons** on either side of the bar helping you push at the start (this is the **spring force** at low speeds). Once the bar is moving upward, gravity assists the lift (this is the **centrifugal force** at high speeds, where rotational inertia helps press the shoes outward). 
> 
> In a semi-centrifugal clutch, the **springs do the "low-effort" work at low RPM** so your foot (driver pedal) doesn't get tired in traffic, and the **centrifugal weights do the "extra" work at high RPM** so the clutch doesn't slip under heavy torque loads. 

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Angular velocity of shaft | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Mass of each flyweight shoe | $m$ | **kg** |
| Distance of shoe CG from axis | $r$ | **m** |
| Number of shoes | $n$ | (dimensionless) |
| Spring force | $P$ | **N** |
| Coefficient of friction | $\mu$ | (dimensionless) |
| Mean friction radius | $R_m$ | **m** |
| Shoe lever angle | $\alpha$ | **degrees/rad** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "semi" explicitly denotes that the centrifugal action is **partial** — it supplements, but does not fully replace, the spring force. The driver must always apply pedal effort to disengage the clutch.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: At Engine Idle / Low Speed (Below $\omega_c$)**
- The shoes (flyweights) are held **inward** by the **retractor springs** (small springs on each lever).
- The **centrifugal force** acting on the shoes is insufficient to overcome the retractor spring tension.
- **Only the main coil springs** press the pressure plate against the flywheel.
- Axial thrust: $W = P$

**Step 2: As Engine Speed Rises (Approaching $\omega_c$)**
- The centrifugal force ($F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$) begins to grow with $\omega^2$.
- At a pre-set **critical speed $\omega_c$**, the centrifugal force exactly balances the retractor spring force.
- Beyond this speed, the shoes begin to **fly outward**, pivoting on their lever arms.

**Step 3: At High Speed (Above $\omega_c$)**
- The shoes pivot outward, and their **axial component** is transmitted through the lever to **add** to the spring thrust.
- **Total axial thrust** = Spring force + Centrifugal component.
- $W = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$

**Step 4: Disengagement**
- The driver **depresses the pedal** → releases the thrust bearing → pulls the pressure plate away via the release levers.
- Since the centrifugal action can only *augment* engagement (never disengage it), the pedal effort is solely determined by the (lighter) main springs.

### 2.2 The "WHY" Behind the Design

- **Why not use a full centrifugal clutch?**  
  → A full centrifugal clutch engages automatically with no driver control, which is dangerous on inclines and unsuitable for stop-and-go traffic.
- **Why not use a heavy spring clutch?**  
  → A strong spring transmits high torque but causes driver fatigue. Semi-centrifugal design uses a **lighter spring** (low pedal effort) and lets **centrifugal force handle the high-RPM torque**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Description | Equation | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Centrifugal force on one shoe | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | N |
| 2 | Axial thrust at low speed ($< \omega_c$) | $W_{low} = P$ | N |
| 3 | Axial thrust at high speed ($> \omega_c$) | $W_{high} = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | N |
| 4 | Critical angular speed | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{n \cdot m \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)}}$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Torque capacity (uniform pressure) | $T = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$ | N·m |
| 6 | Torque capacity (uniform wear) | $T = \mu \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | N·m |
| 7 | Maximum pressure (uniform pressure) | $p_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2 \pi R_m b}$ | N/m² |
| 8 | Maximum pressure (uniform wear) | $p_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2 \pi R_i b}$ | N/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The semi-centrifugal clutch is widely used in **passenger cars, light commercial vehicles, and two-wheelers (scooters like Honda Activa)** where driver comfort and progressive engagement are critical. It eliminates clutch slippage during aggressive acceleration, improving clutch life.

### 2.4 Lever Geometry — The Role of $\tan(\alpha)$

The lever arm transmits the radial centrifugal force into an axial thrust component. From the geometry of the pivoted shoe:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{l_1}{l_2}$$

where:
- $l_1$ = radial distance of the shoe's CG from the pivot point
- $l_2$ = axial distance of the pressure plate contact from the pivot point

By varying the ratio $\dfrac{l_1}{l_2}$, the designer can control **how aggressively** the centrifugal force contributes to engagement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Axial Thrust at High Speed

We will rigorously derive the expression for the total axial thrust $W$ on the pressure plate when the shaft rotates at an angular velocity $\omega > \omega_c$.

**Given:**
- Mass of each shoe = $m$
- Number of shoes = $n$
- Distance of shoe's CG from shaft axis = $r$
- Main spring force = $P$
- Lever angle with shaft axis = $\alpha$

**Step 1: Radial Centrifugal Force on a Single Shoe**

When the shaft rotates, each shoe experiences a centrifugal force directed radially outward. From Newton's second law applied in the rotating frame:

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

**Step 2: Resolve the Centrifugal Force Along the Lever**

The lever is inclined at an angle $\alpha$ to the axis of rotation. Resolving $F_c$ along the lever axis and perpendicular to it:

$$F_{axial} = F_c \cdot \sin(\alpha) = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

$$F_{radial} = F_c \cdot \cos(\alpha) = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

**Step 3: Convert Lever Force to Thrust on Pressure Plate**

Using the principle of moments (the pivot acts as the fulcrum), the axial component is transmitted to the pressure plate. The mechanical advantage of the lever is:

$$MA = \frac{l_1}{l_2} = \tan(\alpha)$$

Therefore, the effective additional thrust on the pressure plate due to **one shoe** is:

$$W_{shoe} = F_{axial} \cdot \tan(\alpha) = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

Since $\sin(\alpha) \cdot \tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\sin^2(\alpha)}{\cos(\alpha)}$, and for a simplified engineering model we use the small-angle linearization that is standard in KTU treatment:

$$W_{shoe} \approx m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 4: Total Centrifugal Contribution from $n$ Shoes**

$$W_{centrifugal} = n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 5: Total Axial Thrust on Pressure Plate**

The spring force $P$ and the centrifugal contribution act in the **same direction** (both push the pressure plate against the flywheel):

$$W_{total} = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Speed $\omega_c$

The critical speed is defined as the speed at which the centrifugal force just **begins to assist** the engagement (i.e., the retractor spring is just balanced).

At $\omega = \omega_c$:

$$n \cdot m \cdot \omega_c^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha) = P_{retractor}$$

For a simplified KTU-level analysis (assuming the retractor spring force is built into the design constant and the main spring $P$ is referenced):

$$n \cdot m \cdot \omega_c^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha) = P$$

Rearranging:

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{P}{n \cdot m \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{P}{n \cdot m \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)}}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Behaviour Across Speed Ranges

| Speed Regime | Centrifugal Force State | Active Engagement Force | Engagement Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\omega = 0$ (Engine off) | $F_c = 0$ | $W = P$ | Engaged (parked) |
| $0 < \omega < \omega_c$ | $F_c < F_{retractor}$ | $W = P$ | Spring-only engagement |
| $\omega = \omega_c$ | $F_c = F_{retractor}$ | $W = P$ | Transition point |
| $\omega > \omega_c$ | $F_c > F_{retractor}$ | $W = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Spring + Centrifugal |
| $\omega \gg \omega_c$ (High RPM) | $F_c$ dominates | $W \approx n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Centrifugal-dominant |

### 3.4 Numerical Illustration (Solved Example Format)

**Problem:** A semi-centrifugal clutch has 4 shoes, each of mass 1.2 kg, with their CG at a radius of 0.12 m. The main spring exerts a force of 600 N. The lever angle $\alpha = 30°$. The friction surfaces have $\mu = 0.3$, outer radius $R_o = 0.15$ m, inner radius $R_i = 0.075$ m. The engine speed is 3000 rpm. Find the torque transmitted (assuming uniform wear).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert engine speed to rad/s.

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\tan(30°)$.

$$\tan(30°) = 0.5774$$

**Step 3:** Compute centrifugal contribution.

$$W_{centrifugal} = n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

$$W_{centrifugal} = 4 \times 1.2 \times (314.16)^2 \times 0.12 \times 0.5774$$

$$W_{centrifugal} = 4 \times 1.2 \times 98696 \times 0.12 \times 0.5774$$

$$W_{centrifugal} \approx 32,824 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Total axial thrust.

$$W_{total} = P + W_{centrifugal} = 600 + 32{,}824 = 33{,}424 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Torque (uniform wear).

$$T = \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$$

$$T = 0.3 \times 33{,}424 \times \frac{0.15 + 0.075}{2}$$

$$T = 0.3 \times 33{,}424 \times 0.1125$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1{,}128.06 \text{ N·m}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Semi-Centrifugal Clutch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["DRIVER PEDAL INPUT"] --> node2["RELEASE BEARING MOVES"]
    node2 --> node3{"CLUTCH STATE"}
    node3 -- DISENGAGED --> node4["PRESSURE PLATE RETRACTED"]
    node3 -- ENGAGED --> node5["SPEED CHECK BY ECU LOGIC"]
    node5 -- LOW RPM --> node6["MAIN SPRING FORCE P ACTS"]
    node5 -- HIGH RPM --> node7["CENTRIFUGAL FORCE FC ACTS"]
    node6 --> node8["TOTAL THRUST W EQUALS P"]
    node7 --> node9["TOTAL THRUST W EQUALS P PLUS n m omega squared r tan alpha"]
    node8 --> node10["FRICTION TORQUE GENERATED"]
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11["POWER TRANSMITTED TO GEARBOX"]
    node4 --> node12["NO POWER FLOW ENGINE IDLE"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Force Flow Diagram — Mechanics of Engagement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ENGINE CRANKSHAFT"] -->|rotates at omega| nodeB["FLYWHEEL"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CLUTCH PLATE"]
    nodeD["MAIN COIL SPRING FORCE P"] -->|always active| nodeE["PRESSURE PLATE"]
    nodeF["SHOE MASS m AT RADIUS r"] -->|creates Fc equals m omega squared r| nodeG["PIVOTED LEVER"]
    nodeG -->|axial component Fc tan alpha| nodeE
    nodeE -->|total W equals P plus n Fc tan alpha| nodeC
    nodeC -->|friction torque mu W Rm| nodeH["GEARBOX INPUT SHAFT"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Component Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["ENGINE SIDE"]
        nodeS1["FLYWHEEL"]
        nodeS2["CLUTCH FRICTION DISC"]
    end
    subgraph B["TRANSMISSION SIDE"]
        nodeS3["PRESSURE PLATE"]
        nodeS4["SHOE WITH FLYWEIGHT"]
        nodeS5["RETRACTOR SPRING"]
        nodeS6["MAIN COIL SPRING"]
    end
    nodeS1 --- nodeS2
    nodeS2 --- nodeS3
    nodeS3 --- nodeS4
    nodeS4 --- nodeS5
    nodeS3 --- nodeS6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Students:**
> - Engine Side = Crankshaft, Flywheel, Clutch Disc
> - Transmission Side = Pressure Plate, Shoes, Retractor Springs, Main Springs
> - The shoes are mounted on the pressure plate (which rotates with the crankshaft), not on a separate member.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is a semi-centrifugal clutch? State its main advantage over a coil-spring clutch.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A semi-centrifugal clutch is a friction clutch in which the pressure plate is pressed against the flywheel by the combined action of **spring force** and **centrifugal force** generated by flyweights at higher speeds.

**Main Advantage:** It uses **lighter springs** (since the centrifugal force supplements engagement at high RPM), which results in **lower driver pedal effort** and reduced driver fatigue — without compromising the torque transmission capacity.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantage with proper reasoning)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Superset]**
*Define critical speed in a semi-centrifugal clutch. Write its expression.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **critical speed $\omega_c$** of a semi-centrifugal clutch is the minimum angular speed of the shaft at which the centrifugal force acting on the shoes just **begins to assist** the engagement, i.e., it overcomes the retractor spring force.

The expression is:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{P}{n \cdot m \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)}}$$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for expression, 1 mark for explaining variables)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Modified for Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working** of a semi-centrifugal clutch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A semi-centrifugal clutch has **4 shoes**, each of mass **2 kg**, with the centre of gravity at a radius of **150 mm**. The **spring force is 800 N**, and the **angle of the shoe lever is 35°** with the shaft axis. The friction surfaces have an **outer radius of 200 mm** and an **inner radius of 100 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.3**. The engine runs at **2500 rpm**. Calculate the **total axial thrust** and the **torque transmitted** (assume uniform pressure theory). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

The semi-centrifugal clutch consists of the following parts:

1. **Flywheel** — mounted on the engine crankshaft, acts as one friction surface.
2. **Clutch Plate (Friction Disc)** — carries the friction lining on both sides, splined to the gearbox shaft.
3. **Pressure Plate** — bolted to the flywheel via **main coil springs**, provides the clamping force.
4. **Flyweights (Shoes)** — typically 3 or 4, mounted on levers pivoted on the pressure plate. They can slide radially outward as the shaft speed increases.
5. **Retractor Springs** — small springs holding the shoes in their inward position at low speeds.
6. **Release Levers** — used to disengage the clutch when the pedal is pressed.

**Working:**
- At **low speeds**, the shoes are held inward by retractor springs. Only the **main springs** press the pressure plate against the flywheel.
- As the speed rises above the **critical speed $\omega_c$**, the centrifugal force on the shoes overcomes the retractor springs. The shoes **fly outward**, pivoting on the levers, and their axial component adds to the spring thrust.
- The **total thrust** $W = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ increases with speed, ensuring no slip at high torque loads.

*(3 marks for components list, 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for working principle)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 4$, $m = 2$ kg, $r = 0.15$ m, $P = 800$ N
- $\alpha = 35°$, $R_o = 0.2$ m, $R_i = 0.1$ m, $\mu = 0.3$
- $N = 2500$ rpm

**Step 1:** Angular velocity.

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 2500}{60} = 261.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Centrifugal contribution.

$$W_c = n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

$$\tan(35°) = 0.7002$$

$$W_c = 4 \times 2 \times (261.80)^2 \times 0.15 \times 0.7002$$

$$W_c = 4 \times 2 \times 68539 \times 0.15 \times 0.7002$$

$$W_c \approx 57{,}590 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Total thrust.

$$W = P + W_c = 800 + 57{,}590 = 58{,}390 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Torque (uniform pressure).

$$T = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$

$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 0.008 - 0.001 = 0.007 \text{ m}^3$$

$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.04 - 0.01 = 0.03 \text{ m}^2$$

$$T = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.3 \times 58390 \times \frac{0.007}{0.03}$$

$$T = 0.2 \times 58390 \times 0.2333$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 2{,}725 \text{ N·m}}$$

*(1 mark for omega, 2 marks for thrust, 2 marks for torque formula, 2 marks for final answer)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **design procedure** of a semi-centrifugal clutch, including the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A semi-centrifugal clutch is to transmit **20 kW at 1500 rpm**. The **shoes are 4 in number**, each of mass **1.5 kg**, with their CG at a radius of **125 mm**. The lever angle is **30°**, and the coefficient of friction is **0.25**. Assuming **uniform wear**, design the friction surface dimensions (i.e., find $R_o$ and $R_i$). Assume $R_o = 2 R_i$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Determine the torque to be transmitted** $T$ from power $P$ and speed $N$:
$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N}$$

2. **Select the number of shoes** $n$ (typically 3 or 4).

3. **Assume the spring force** $P$ based on pedal effort (KTU typical: 200–800 N).

4. **Choose the critical speed** $\omega_c$ (usually $0.7 \omega_{max}$ to $0.8 \omega_{max}$).

5. **Compute the lever angle** $\alpha$ from the geometric constraint of the pressure plate.

6. **Calculate the total axial thrust** $W$ at maximum operating speed.

7. **Apply the friction torque equation** (uniform pressure or uniform wear) to solve for $R_o$ and $R_i$.

8. **Validate the maximum pressure** $p_{max}$ against allowable values (typically $0.1$ to $0.3$ MPa for asbestos-based linings).

**Assumptions:**
- Uniform pressure or uniform wear distribution across the contact surface.
- Rigid shoes, no deformation under centrifugal load.
- Small-angle approximation for the lever geometry.

*(3 marks for procedure, 2 marks for assumptions, 2 marks for equations used)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 20$ kW $= 20{,}000$ W, $N = 1500$ rpm
- $n = 4$, $m = 1.5$ kg, $r = 0.125$ m
- $\alpha = 30°$, $\mu = 0.25$, $R_o = 2 R_i$

**Step 1:** Compute torque.

$$T = \frac{20{,}000 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1500} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{9424.78} \approx 127.32 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute angular velocity.

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Centrifugal thrust contribution.

$$W_c = 4 \times 1.5 \times (157.08)^2 \times 0.125 \times \tan(30°)$$

$$W_c = 4 \times 1.5 \times 24{,}674 \times 0.125 \times 0.5774$$

$$W_c \approx 10{,}687 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Total thrust (assume $P = 400$ N as a design assumption).

$$W = 400 + 10{,}687 = 11{,}087 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Torque (uniform wear) with $R_o = 2 R_i$.

$$T = \mu \cdot W \cdot \frac{R_o + R_i}{2} = 0.25 \times 11{,}087 \times \frac{3 R_i}{2}$$

$$127.32 = 0.25 \times 11{,}087 \times 1.5 \times R_i$$

$$127.32 = 4157.6 \times R_i$$

$$\boxed{R_i \approx 0.0306 \text{ m} = 30.6 \text{ mm}}$$

$$R_o = 2 R_i = 61.2 \text{ mm}$$

*(1 mark torque, 2 marks Wc, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert **rpm to rad/s** before substituting into the centrifugal force formula. *Always* use $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **uniform pressure** and **uniform wear** torque equations. KTU papers frequently test this. Uniform pressure uses $\dfrac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$; uniform wear uses $\dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Treating the centrifugal force as *replacing* the spring force. Remember — it is a *supplement*, and the total thrust is the **sum** $W = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$.
> 4. **Mark Loss Zone:** Skipping units in the final answer or using **N/mm² instead of N/m²** for pressure. Always state units explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam Night:**

- **Definition:** Semi-centrifugal clutch = Spring clutch + Centrifugal clutch (hybrid).
- **Working threshold:** Centrifugal action begins only **after critical speed $\omega_c$**.
- **Total thrust formula:** $W = P + n \cdot m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)$.
- **Critical speed formula:** $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{n \cdot m \cdot r \cdot \tan(\alpha)}}$.
- **Low-speed behaviour:** $W = P$ (spring-only, no centrifugal assistance).
- **High-speed behaviour:** Centrifugal component **adds** to spring force.
- **Key components:** Flywheel, pressure plate, **shoes (flyweights)**, retractor springs, main coil springs, release levers.
- **Two pressure theories:**
  - *Uniform Pressure:* $T = \dfrac{2}{3} \mu W \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$, $p_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2 \pi R_m b}$
  - *Uniform Wear:* $T = \mu W \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$, $p_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2 \pi R_i b}$
- **Advantage:** Lighter springs → **low pedal effort** + **no slip at high RPM**.
- **Disadvantage:** More complex, slightly heavier, and more expensive than simple coil-spring clutches.
- **Application:** Passenger cars, scooters, light commercial vehicles.
- **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ HP} = 735.5 \text{ W}$, $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{\pi}{30} \text{ rad/s}$.
- **Always re-check:** $R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ is used for **uniform pressure** in the $\dfrac{2}{3}$ formula context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Clutch; Need and Objectives of Gear Box; Construction and Operation of Sliding Mesh
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Clutch; Need and Objectives of Gear Box; Construction and Operation of Sliding Mesh

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 CENTRIFUGAL CLUTCH — FORMAL DEFINITION

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Centrifugal Clutch** is an automatic friction clutch that engages progressively through the action of **centrifugal force** generated on weighted shoes (or fly-weights) mounted on the driving member. Engagement occurs without any driver intervention once the engine speed crosses a pre-set threshold (engagement speed).

**Main Functional Elements:**

| S.No | Element | Role |
|------|------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Driving Member / Spider | Rotates with engine crankshaft |
| 2 | Fly-weights / Shoes (2, 3, or 4) | Pivot outward under centrifugal force |
| 3 | Friction Lining / Drum | Stationary outer drum receives shoes |
| 4 | Return Springs | Pull shoes back inward when disengaged |
| 5 | Levers / Links | Amplify radial movement |
| 6 | Output Shaft | Receives torque from drum via friction |

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine spinning a wet umbrella. As you spin it faster, the droplets fly outward and stick to the rim — they "engage" with the outer edge because of centrifugal force. The faster the spin, the harder they press outward. A centrifugal clutch works the **exact same way**: the engine acts as your hand, the shoes are the water droplets, and the drum is the umbrella's rim. No spinning → no contact. Fast spinning → firm contact → torque transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** Most automotive centrifugal clutches engage between **1800–2400 RPM**, with rated engine speeds around **3000–3600 RPM**.

---

## 1.2 GEAR BOX — NEED AND OBJECTIVES

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Need Statement:**
> An internal combustion engine delivers its **peak torque** only within a narrow speed band (roughly 1500–3000 RPM for petrol, 1200–2200 RPM for diesel). However, a road vehicle requires a **vastly wider range of tractive effort and speed**, from creep-in-traffic to high-speed cruising. A transmission system is therefore mandatory to reconcile this mismatch.

### Why an Engine Alone Cannot Drive a Vehicle Directly:

- **Starting torque deficit:** An engine cannot deliver infinite torque at zero RPM to overcome inertia from rest.
- **Speed-range limitation:** Engine redline is far below vehicle's maximum speed requirement when direct-coupled.
- **Reverse motion:** A 4-stroke engine is unidirectional; reverse gear must be synthesized externally.
- **Efficiency optimization:** Engine must operate in its best BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) island under varying load.

### Objectives of a Gear Box (KTU High-Yield Points):

1. **Speed Reduction with Torque Multiplication** — Gear reduction increases torque proportionally.
2. **Variable Speed Ratio** — Multiple forward gears adapt to varying road loads.
3. **Reverse Gear Provision** — Introduces idler gear to reverse output rotation.
4. **Neutral Position** — Disconnects engine from wheels without stopping the engine.
5. **Direction Change at Wheels** — Differential handles this; gearbox feeds it appropriately.
6. **Engine-Flywheel Continuity** — Allows engine to run idle while vehicle is stationary.

---

## 1.3 SLIDING MESH GEAR BOX — FORMAL DEFINITION

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Sliding Mesh Gear Box** is the earliest and most elementary form of constant-mesh-plus-sliding dog-clutch gearbox, in which **gears mounted on splined shafts are physically slid axially** to engage with their corresponding mating gears on the layshaft (countershaft) to obtain the desired speed ratio.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of two parallel staircases with gear teeth. By sliding a "cog block" sideways along a track, you bring it into contact with whichever gear on the parallel track you want to lock with — like shifting a tape spool horizontally until it meshes with the right neighbouring wheel. Engagement is *metallic and direct*, hence noisy but simple.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU Favorite Question):** Sliding mesh gearboxes were standard in vehicles like the **Ford Model T (1908–1927)** and early **Mahindra Jeep CJ-500** models. They have been replaced in modern vehicles by **synchromesh gearboxes** but remain syllabus-mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 CENTRIFUGAL CLUTCH — OPERATIONAL THEORY

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic:

- **Step 1 — Idle State:** Engine is idling at low RPM. The spring tension (F_spring) exceeds the centrifugal force (F_centrifugal) on the shoes.
- **Step 2 — Sub-engagement:** As RPM rises, F_centrifugal = m·ω²·r grows as the square of angular velocity. At a critical speed **ω_e** (engagement speed), F_centrifugal = F_spring.
- **Step 3 — Progressive Engagement:** Above ω_e, shoes pivot outward and progressively press the friction lining against the drum.
- **Step 4 — Full Engagement:** The shoes press with a force W = (m·ω²·r) − F_spring. This normal force creates friction torque transmitted to the drum.
- **Step 5 — Slip Phase:** During engagement, slip occurs; energy dissipated = slip power = T·(ω₁ − ω₂).
- **Step 6 — Lock-up:** Once drum speed equals shoe speed, no relative slip → no wear, full torque transfer.

### Spring Design Consideration:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Engagement speed is governed by spring stiffness. **Soft springs** → low engagement speed (risky for hill-starts). **Stiff springs** → high engagement speed (causes jerky launch).

### The 'WHY' Behind Centrifugal Action:

Centrifugal force is a *pseudo-force* observed in the rotating reference frame of the shoes. In the inertial frame, the shoe is constrained to circular motion, and the radial reaction from the pivot plus the constraint of the drum provides the necessary centripetal force. When this constraint exceeds the spring's inward pull, the shoe is forced to press harder against the drum.

---

## 2.2 GEAR BOX — FUNDAMENTAL RELATIONSHIPS

### Step-by-Step Reduction Logic:

- **Step 1:** Let the input gear have N₁ teeth and the output gear have N₂ teeth.
- **Step 2:** The linear pitch at the pitch circle must be identical → π·m·N₁ = π·m·N₂ (where m is module). Hence the gears have the same module.
- **Step 3:** Velocity ratio `i = N₂/N₁ = ω₁/ω₂`.
- **Step 4:** Torque ratio (ideal gear, 100% efficient): **T₂/T₁ = i × η**, where η ≈ 0.97–0.98 for spur gears.

### Step-by-Step Compound Gear Train:

For multi-stage reductions, the overall ratio is the product of stage ratios:
$$i_{overall} = i_1 \times i_2 \times i_3 \times \dots \times i_n$$

### Reverse Gear Logic:

Adding an **idler gear** between driver and driven reverses the direction without changing the magnitude of speed ratio.

---

## 2.3 SLIDING MESH GEAR BOX — OPERATIONAL THEORY

### Step-by-Step Operation:

- **Step 1 — Neutral:** All sliding gears are positioned midway, no two gears on main and layshafts are in mesh.
- **Step 2 — 1st Gear Selection:** The largest gear on the main shaft is slid to mesh with the smallest gear on the layshaft → highest torque, lowest speed.
- **Step 3 — Dog Clutch Locking:** Once engaged, a dog clutch (square-toothed collar) locks the gear to the shaft for solid power transfer.
- **Step 4 — Upshift Sequence:** Sliding the next gear progressively reduces the ratio.
- **Step 5 — Reverse:** An idler gear slides in to reverse rotation direction.
- **Step 6 — Double Declutching Required:** To avoid clash of gear teeth at unequal speeds, the driver must: depress → neutralize → release at engine's RPM → depress → engage.

### Why Sliding Mesh is Noisy:

Engagement is achieved by **forcibly crashing gear teeth together**. The driver must synchronize the engine speed to the layshaft speed (which mirrors road speed) — a skill called **double declutching**. Failure to do so causes the dreaded "gear clash" metallic crash.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET

| Phenomenon / Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Notes |
|-----------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Centrifugal Force on a Shoe | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | m = mass of shoe (kg); ω = angular velocity (rad/s); r = shoe CG radius (m) | Valid when shoe is held at radius r |
| Engagement Speed (Threshold) | $\omega_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{K \cdot x}{m \cdot r}}$ | K = spring stiffness (N/m); x = spring preload deflection (m) | Threshold condition: F_c = F_s |
| Normal Reaction Force on Drum | $W = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r - K \cdot x$ | W = net contact force per shoe (N) | Valid only when $\omega > \omega_e$ |
| Friction Torque (uniform pressure) | $T = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m \cdot n$ | μ = friction coefficient; R_m = mean drum radius (m); n = number of shoes | Assumes shoe pivots at drum centre axis |
| Friction Torque (alternative form) | $T = \dfrac{\mu \cdot W \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}{2} \cdot n$ | R₁ = inner shoe radius; R₂ = outer shoe radius | Mean radius model |
| Gear Velocity Ratio | $i = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1 \cdot \eta}$ | N = number of teeth; η = efficiency | Spur gear assumption |
| Overall Reduction (Multi-stage) | $i_{overall} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} i_k$ | Product of stage ratios | Compound train |
| Module Consistency | $m = \dfrac{D}{N}$ | D = pitch diameter (mm); N = teeth | Meshing gears have same module |
| Reverse Gear Ratio Magnitude | $\vert i_{reverse} \vert = \dfrac{N_{driven}}{N_{driver}}$ | Idler does not affect magnitude | Direction reverses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiner expects you to **always** write the engagement condition first, then derive W, then plug W into the torque equation. Marks are split across these three logical blocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX
