## 3.1 DERIVATION: TORQUE TRANSMITTED BY A CENTRIFUGAL CLUTCH

### Given (Standard KTU Problem Data):
- Number of shoes, $n = 3$
- Mass of each shoe, $m = 0.8 \text{ kg}$
- Centrifugal radius of shoe CG, $r = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Spring stiffness, $K = 4.5 \text{ N/mm} = 4500 \text{ N/m}$
- Spring preload deflection, $x = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$
- Drum inner radius, $R_1 = 0.085 \text{ m}$
- Drum outer radius, $R_2 = 0.115 \text{ m}$
- Friction coefficient, $\mu = 0.35$
- Operating speed, $N = 3000 \text{ RPM}$

### Step 1: Convert Rotational Speed to Angular Velocity

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000}{60} = \frac{6000 \pi}{60} = 100 \pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega \approx 314.159 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Centrifugal Force on One Shoe

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

$$F_c = 0.8 \times (314.159)^2 \times 0.075$$

$$F_c = 0.8 \times 98695.99 \times 0.075$$

$$F_c = 5921.76 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Spring Restoring Force on One Shoe

$$F_s = K \cdot x$$

$$F_s = 4500 \times 0.004$$

$$F_s = 18.0 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Determine Net Normal Reaction Force on Drum (Per Shoe)

$$W = F_c - F_s = 5921.76 - 18.0$$

$$W = 5903.76 \text{ N per shoe}$$

### Step 5: Verify Engagement Condition is Satisfied

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\frac{K \cdot x}{m \cdot r}} = \sqrt{\frac{4500 \times 0.004}{0.8 \times 0.075}} = \sqrt{\frac{18}{0.06}} = \sqrt{300}$$

$$\omega_e \approx 17.32 \text{ rad/s} \approx 165.4 \text{ RPM}$$

Since operating $\omega = 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \gg \omega_e = 17.32 \text{ rad/s}$, the clutch is fully engaged.

### Step 6: Mean Drum Radius

$$R_m = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2} = \frac{0.085 + 0.115}{2} = \frac{0.200}{2} = 0.100 \text{ m}$$

### Step 7: Total Friction Torque Transmitted

$$T = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m \cdot n$$

$$T = 0.35 \times 5903.76 \times 0.100 \times 3$$

$$T = 0.35 \times 1771.128$$

$$T \approx 619.89 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $T \approx \mathbf{620 \text{ N·m}}$ transmitted by the centrifugal clutch at 3000 RPM.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION: VELOCITY RATIO OF A SLIDING MESH GEAR BOX

### Given:
- 1st Gear: Main shaft gear $N_1 = 14$ teeth, Layshaft gear $N_2 = 38$ teeth.
- 2nd Gear: Main shaft gear $N_3 = 20$ teeth, Layshaft gear $N_4 = 32$ teeth.
- 3rd Gear: Main shaft gear $N_5 = 25$ teeth, Layshaft gear $N_6 = 27$ teeth.
- 4th Gear: Main shaft gear $N_7 = 30$ teeth, Layshaft gear $N_8 = 22$ teeth.
- Engine speed at input = 3000 RPM. Layshaft to rear wheels via final drive ratio $i_f = 3.5$.

### Step 1: Velocity Ratio Definitions

For each forward gear:
$$i_k = \frac{\text{Layshaft gear teeth}}{\text{Main shaft gear teeth}} = \frac{N_{\text{layshaft}}}{N_{\text{main}}}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Individual Gear Ratios

$$\begin{aligned}
i_1 &= \frac{38}{14} \approx 2.714 \\
i_2 &= \frac{32}{20} = 1.600 \\
i_3 &= \frac{27}{25} = 1.080 \\
i_4 &= \frac{22}{30} \approx 0.733
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute Output Speed at the Layshaft

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{out,1} &= \frac{3000}{2.714} \approx 1105.4 \text{ RPM} \\
N_{out,2} &= \frac{3000}{1.600} = 1875.0 \text{ RPM} \\
N_{out,3} &= \frac{3000}{1.080} \approx 2777.8 \text{ RPM} \\
N_{out,4} &= \frac{3000}{0.733} \approx 4093.6 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute Wheel Speed (After Final Drive)

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{wheel,1} &= \frac{1105.4}{3.5} \approx 315.8 \text{ RPM} \\
N_{wheel,2} &= \frac{1875.0}{3.5} \approx 535.7 \text{ RPM} \\
N_{wheel,3} &= \frac{2777.8}{3.5} \approx 793.7 \text{ RPM} \\
N_{wheel,4} &= \frac{4093.6}{3.5} \approx 1169.6 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Speed Ratio Interpretation (1st is Highest Torque, 4th is Highest Speed)

| Gear | Velocity Ratio $i_k$ | Output Speed (RPM) | Wheel Speed (RPM) | Torque Multiplier (ideal) |
|------|----------------------|--------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1st  | 2.714                | 1105.4             | 315.8             | 2.714×                    |
| 2nd  | 1.600                | 1875.0             | 535.7             | 1.600×                    |
| 3rd  | 1.080                | 2777.8             | 793.7             | 1.080×                    |
| 4th  | 0.733                | 4093.6             | 1169.6            | 0.733× (overdrive)        |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A ratio < 1 (4th gear here) means output speed exceeds input speed — this is termed **overdrive**, used for highway cruising to reduce engine RPM and fuel consumption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 CENTRIFUGAL CLUTCH — FUNCTIONAL BLOCK ARCHITECTURE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Crankshaft Input] --> B[Driving Spider / Flange]
    B --> C[Shoes Pivot Mounted on Spider]
    C --> D{Engine Speed exceeds Threshold}
    D -- NO --> E[Return Springs Hold Shoes Inward]
    D -- YES --> F[Centrifugal Force Throws Shoes Outward]
    F --> G[Friction Lining Contacts Drum Inner Surface]
    G --> H[Frictional Torque Generated]
    H --> I[Torque Transmitted to Drum]
    I --> J[Output Shaft Drives Load]
    E --> K[Clutch Disengaged - Idle]
    J --> L[Load Driven at Engine Speed]
```

## 4.2 CENTRIFUGAL CLUTCH — RADIAL FORCE BALANCE SCHEMATIC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Rotation Axis] --> B[Shoe Pivoted at Radius r1]
    B --> C[Shoe Mass M extends to radius r2]
    C --> D[F_c = M x omega squared x r2 OUTWARD]
    C --> E[F_s = K x x INWARD]
    D --> F[Shoe Presses Drum at radius R]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Normal Force W = F_c minus F_s]
    G --> H[Friction Force mu x W Tangential]
    H --> I[Torque T = mu x W x R x n]
```

## 4.3 SLIDING MESH GEARBOX — POWER FLOW ARCHITECTURE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Flywheel] --> B[Clutch]
    B --> C[Main Shaft Input Pinion]
    C --> D1[Sliding Gear Pair 1st - Largest Reduction]
    C --> D2[Sliding Gear Pair 2nd]
    C --> D3[Sliding Gear Pair 3rd]
    C --> D4[Sliding Gear Pair 4th - Smallest Reduction]
    C --> E[Reverse Idler Gear]
    D1 --> F[Layshaft Countershaft]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Output Pinion on Layshaft]
    G --> H[Final Drive Crown Wheel and Pinion]
    H --> I[Differential]
    I --> J[Drive Shafts to Rear Wheels]
```

## 4.4 SLIDING MESH GEARBOX — STATE TRANSITION DIAGRAM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Neutral
    Neutral --> FirstGear: Slide Largest Gear Left to Mesh
    FirstGear --> SecondGear: Slide Mid Gear - Double Declutch
    SecondGear --> ThirdGear: Slide Smaller Gear - Double Declutch
    ThirdGear --> FourthGear: Slide Smallest Gear - Double Declutch
    FourthGear --> Reverse: Engage Idler Gear
    Reverse --> Neutral: Return Spring Centring
    FirstGear --> Neutral: Lift Selector
    SecondGear --> Neutral: Lift Selector
    ThirdGear --> Neutral: Lift Selector
    FourthGear --> Neutral: Lift Selector
```

## 4.5 GEAR BOX FUNCTIONAL ROLE WITHIN VEHICLE DRIVETRAIN

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Demand via Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Throttle Body Modulates Air-Fuel]
    B --> C[Engine Produces Torque T_e at Speed N_e]
    C --> D[Clutch Disengages or Engages Power]
    D --> E[Gear Box Selects Ratio]
    E --> F[Propeller Shaft Carries Torque]
    F --> G[Final Drive Multiplies Torque]
    G --> H[Differential Splits to Wheels]
    H --> I[Wheels Convert Torque to Tractive Force]
    I --> J[Vehicle Acceleration Achieved]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Explain the working principle of a centrifugal clutch with a neat sketch. State two of its typical applications.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Working Principle:**
A centrifugal clutch operates on the principle of **centrifugal force** acting radially outward on weighted shoes attached to the rotating driving member. As the engine speed increases beyond a threshold, the centrifugal force overcomes the spring preload, forcing the shoes outward against the friction-lined drum. The resulting frictional contact transmits torque to the output shaft.

**Operating Sequence:**

- At low RPM, springs hold shoes inward → no contact → clutch disengaged.
- At engagement speed $\omega_e$, centrifugal force equals spring force.
- Above $\omega_e$, shoes press drum → progressive engagement → torque transfer.

**Applications (Any Two):**

1. **Scooters and mopeds** (e.g., Honda Activa, TVS XL) — single-gear automatic transmission.
2. **Lawn mowers and go-karts** — simple automatic drive.
3. **Industrial conveyors and pumps** — smooth start-up without jerk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Principle (1), Sequence (1), Applications (1).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**List any six objectives of providing a gear box in an automobile.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Torque Multiplication** — Provides higher torque at wheels than engine delivers.
2. **Speed Reduction** — Reduces engine speed to suit vehicle requirements.
3. **Variable Speed Operation** — Allows driver to select optimum ratio for varying road conditions.
4. **Reverse Gear Provision** — Reverses direction of rotation without reversing engine.
5. **Neutral Position** — Disconnects engine from drivetrain while idling.
6. **Efficient Engine Operation** — Keeps engine in its best fuel economy band.
7. **Hill-Start Assistance** — 1st gear provides high torque to climb gradients.
8. **Overdrive Facility** — Top gear may provide speed greater than direct drive, reducing engine wear on highways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** 6 points × 0.5 marks each = 3 marks (Any six).

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal clutch has three shoes each of mass 1.2 kg pivoted at a radius of 60 mm. The centre of gravity of each shoe is at a radius of 120 mm. The springs have a stiffness of 20 N/mm and a pre-load deflection of 2 mm. The inner and outer radii of the friction lining are 130 mm and 160 mm respectively. The coefficient of friction is 0.30. Determine:
(i) The engine speed at which the clutch begins to engage.
(ii) The torque transmitted when the engine runs at 3600 RPM.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a centrifugal clutch. Compare its merits over a single-plate friction clutch.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- $n = 3$ shoes
- $m = 1.2 \text{ kg}$
- $r_{pivot} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- $r_{cg} = 0.120 \text{ m}$
- $K = 20 \text{ N/mm} = 20000 \text{ N/m}$
- $x = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$
- $R_1 = 0.130 \text{ m}$, $R_2 = 0.160 \text{ m}$
- $\mu = 0.30$
- $N = 3600 \text{ RPM}$

**(i) Engagement Speed:**

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\frac{K \cdot x}{m \cdot r_{cg}}}$$

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\frac{20000 \times 0.002}{1.2 \times 0.120}}$$

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\frac{40}{0.144}} = \sqrt{277.78}$$

$$\omega_e \approx 16.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_e = \frac{60 \cdot \omega_e}{2 \pi} = \frac{60 \times 16.667}{2 \pi} \approx \frac{1000}{6.2832}$$

$$N_e \approx 159.15 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Torque at 3600 RPM:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 3600}{60} = 120 \pi \approx 376.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Centrifugal force per shoe:**

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_{cg} = 1.2 \times (376.99)^2 \times 0.120$$

$$F_c = 1.2 \times 142121.3 \times 0.120 \approx 20465.5 \text{ N}$$

**Spring force per shoe:**

$$F_s = K \cdot x = 20000 \times 0.002 = 40 \text{ N}$$

**Net normal force per shoe:**

$$W = F_c - F_s = 20465.5 - 40 = 20425.5 \text{ N}$$

**Mean drum radius:**

$$R_m = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2} = \frac{0.130 + 0.160}{2} = 0.145 \text{ m}$$

**Total torque transmitted:**

$$T = \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m \cdot n = 0.30 \times 20425.5 \times 0.145 \times 3$$

$$T = 0.30 \times 8885.09 \approx 2665.53 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Results:** $N_e \approx 159.15 \text{ RPM}$; $T \approx \mathbf{2665.5 \text{ N·m}}$.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Construction (Neat Schematic Described):**

A centrifugal clutch consists of the following parts:

1. **Driving Spider / Flange:** Bolted to engine crankshaft; carries the shoes.
2. **Shoes (3 or 4):** Pivot-mounted on the spider via pins; carry friction lining on the outer curved surface.
3. **Friction Lining:** Asbestos or sintered bronze material bonded to shoe outer face.
4. **Drum:** Mounted on the output (driven) shaft; concentric with the spider.
5. **Return Springs:** Tension springs anchoring each shoe to the spider.
6. **Levers / Links (Optional):** For amplifying shoe radial movement.

**Working:**

- Below engagement speed, springs keep shoes retracted → no contact → no torque.
- Above engagement speed, centrifugal force overcomes spring force → shoes press drum → friction torque transmitted.
- As speed rises, contact pressure and torque capacity increase.
- At drum speed equal to shoe speed, no slip → no wear.

**Merits of Centrifugal Clutch over Single-Plate Friction Clutch:**

| Feature | Centrifugal Clutch | Single-Plate Friction Clutch |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Driver Effort | Zero (automatic) | High (foot pedal) |
| Engagement Smoothness| Very smooth, gradual | Depends on driver skill |
| Stalling Risk | None | High in inexperienced hands |
| Wear | Low (engages only above threshold) | Moderate (every start) |
| Cost | Higher (precision springs) | Lower |
| Application | Two-wheelers, karts | Four-wheeler manual cars |

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main Question Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the construction of a sliding mesh gear box. Describe its operation in all forward gears and reverse.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the need and objectives of a gear box in an automobile. A car has four forward gears with ratios 3.5:1, 2.2:1, 1.4:1 and 1.0:1, and a reverse ratio of 3.8:1. If the engine delivers maximum torque of 180 N·m at 2400 RPM and the final drive ratio is 4.1:1, find the maximum torque available at the rear wheels in each gear. Assume gear-box efficiency = 95% and differential efficiency = 92%.

---

### **Model Answer to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Construction:**

A sliding mesh gear box consists of:

1. **Main Shaft (Input Shaft):** Splined throughout its length; carries all forward gears (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th) and a reverse sliding gear.
2. **Layshaft (Countershaft):** Fixed gears mounted rigidly; one large gear for 1st, smaller for 4th.
3. **Dog Clutches:** Square-toothed collars that lock sliding gears to the main shaft once meshed.
4. **Selector Forks:** Move the gears axially along splines.
5. **Reverse Idler Gear:** Slides into position between main shaft reverse gear and layshaft reverse gear to reverse direction.
6. **Gear Shift Lever:** Driver-operated lever inside cabin.

**Operation:**

| Selector Position | Action | Resulting Ratio |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| Neutral | All gears midway; no mesh | No power transfer |
| 1st | Largest main-shaft gear meshes smallest layshaft gear | Highest torque, lowest speed |
| 2nd | Next gear slides in | Reduced torque, increased speed |
| 3rd | Smaller gear slides in | Further speed increase |
| 4th (Top) | Smallest main-shaft gear meshes largest layshaft gear | Direct or near-direct drive |
| Reverse | Idler gear inserted → direction reversed | Reverse motion achieved |

**Limitation:** Requires **double declutching** to synchronise engine and layshaft speeds, otherwise gear clash occurs.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Need & Objectives of Gear Box:**

*(Ref. SECTION 1.2 and SECTION 2.2 for full theory)*

**Given:**

- Gear ratios: $i_1 = 3.5$, $i_2 = 2.2$, $i_3 = 1.4$, $i_4 = 1.0$, $i_{rev} = 3.8$
- Final drive ratio: $i_f = 4.1$
- Engine max torque: $T_e = 180 \text{ N·m}$
- Gear box efficiency: $\eta_{gb} = 0.95$
- Differential efficiency: $\eta_{diff} = 0.92$

**Formula:**

$$T_{wheel} = T_e \times i_{gear} \times i_f \times \eta_{gb} \times \eta_{diff}$$

**Calculation for Each Gear:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 180 \times 3.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\
&= 180 \times 3.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.874 \\
&\approx 180 \times 12.539 \\
&\approx 2257.0 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 180 \times 2.2 \times 4.1 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\
&\approx 180 \times 7.881 \\
&\approx 1418.6 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= 180 \times 1.4 \times 4.1 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\
&\approx 180 \times 5.015 \\
&\approx 902.7 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= 180 \times 1.0 \times 4.1 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\
&\approx 180 \times 3.583 \\
&\approx 644.9 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{rev} &= 180 \times 3.8 \times 4.1 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\
&\approx 180 \times 13.616 \\
&\approx 2450.9 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Gear | Ratio | Wheel Torque (N·m) |
|------|-------|---------------------|
| 1st  | 3.5   | 2257.0              |
| 2nd  | 2.2   | 1418.6              |
| 3rd  | 1.4   | 902.7               |
| 4th  | 1.0   | 644.9               |
| Rev  | 3.8   | 2450.9              |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Reverse gear has the highest torque multiplication, which is why vehicles reverse slowly but powerfully — useful for extricating from mud or steep inclines.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Spring Force Subtraction:** Many students compute $W = m\omega^2 r$ only. Correct form is $W = m\omega^2 r - Kx$. Mark deduction: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Spring Stiffness:** K is typically given in N/mm; convert to N/m before using SI. Failing this gives wrong $\omega_e$ by factor of $\sqrt{1000}$.
>
> 3. **Mean Radius Mistake:** Using $R_2$ (outer radius) instead of $(R_1 + R_2)/2$ for friction torque. Examiner *will* deduct for this.
>
> 4. **Efficiency Multiplication Omission:** In torque-at-wheels problems, students often multiply ratios but **forget $\eta_{gb} \times \eta_{diff}$**. Cost: **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Confusing Engagement Speed with Operating Speed:** $\omega_e$ is the threshold; the *actual* operating $\omega$ may be 5–10× higher.
>
> 6. **Missing the Idler Gear Note:** In reverse gear derivations, omitting the statement "idler does not affect ratio magnitude" costs **0.5 marks**.
>
> 7. **Diagram Quality:** Sliding mesh gearbox diagram must clearly show *splines* and *selector forks*. Sketches without these features lose **1–2 marks** in construction-type questions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Centrifugal Clutch** engages automatically via centrifugal force; no driver input.
- [ ] **Engagement condition:** $m\omega^2 r = Kx$ defines the threshold angular velocity.
- [ ] **Net normal force:** $W = m\omega^2 r - Kx$ (for $\omega > \omega_e$).
- [ ] **Torque formula:** $T = \mu W R_m n$, where $R_m = (R_1 + R_2)/2$.
- [ ] **Number of shoes** typically 2, 3, or 4 (even numbers for balance).
- [ ] **Applications:** Scooters, mopeds, go-karts, lawn mowers.
- [ ] **Gear Box** reconciles engine's narrow torque band with vehicle's wide speed-torque demand.
- [ ] **Six objectives:** Torque multiplication, speed reduction, variable ratio, reverse, neutral, engine efficiency.
- [ ] **Velocity ratio:** $i = N_2/N_1 = \omega_1/\omega_2$.
- [ ] **Reverse gear** uses an idler; magnitude unchanged, direction reversed.
- [ ] **Sliding Mesh** uses splined main shaft and sliding dog clutches.
- [ ] **Sliding mesh requires double declutching** to avoid gear clash.
- [ ] **Drawbacks:** Noisy, harsh engagement, requires driver skill.
- [ ] **Modern replacement:** Synchromesh gearbox (eliminates double declutching).
- [ ] **Wheel torque** formula: $T_w = T_e \times i_{gear} \times i_{final} \times \eta_{gb} \times \eta_{diff}$.
- [ ] **Overdrive:** Gear ratio < 1 (output faster than input); used in 4th/5th gear highway cruising.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Mesh and Synchromesh Gearboxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constant Mesh and Synchromesh Gearboxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Constant Mesh Gearbox — Formal Definition

A **Constant Mesh Gearbox** is a type of automotive transmission in which all the pairs of gears (one on the input/main shaft and the corresponding one on the countershaft/layshaft) remain permanently in mesh with each other throughout the operation of the vehicle. Gear ratio changes are achieved not by sliding gears into and out of engagement (as in a sliding mesh gearbox), but by locking the desired gear pair to its respective shaft using **dog clutches** (sliding sleeves) coupled to splined shaft sections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Constant mesh gearboxes form the foundational architecture from which all modern synchromesh transmissions are derived. They are a mandatory topic under Module 1 of PCAUT503 — Automotive Transmission.

## 1.2 Synchromesh Gearbox — Formal Definition

A **Synchromesh Gearbox** is an advanced evolution of the constant mesh gearbox in which the speed of the gear to be engaged is automatically synchronized (matched) with the speed of the shaft on which it is to be locked, **before** the dog clutch teeth make contact. This synchronization is achieved through the action of a friction cone and a synchronizer ring (also called the **baulk ring** or **blocking ring**), which equalizes the rotational speeds prior to positive engagement, thereby eliminating gear clash and permitting smooth, clash-free shifting.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Friction Clutch of Gears

Imagine two rotating discs spinning at different speeds:

- **Without Synchromesh (Constant Mesh only):** It is like trying to push two spinning gears together with bare hands — they will grind, clash, and refuse to mesh cleanly. The driver must "double-declutch" (rev-match manually) to align speeds. This is exactly what old trucks required.

- **With Synchromesh:** It is like having a thin friction pad (the synchronizer ring) that automatically presses against both discs, slowing one and speeding the other until both rotate at exactly the same RPM. Once synchronized, the rigid dog teeth slide in **without any clash**. This is what your modern Maruti, Hyundai, or Tata car does effortlessly.

## 1.4 Key Physical Standards & Metrics

The following parameters govern the design and performance of both gearboxes:

- **Pressure Angle of Gear Teeth:** **20°** (full-depth involute, ISO standard)
- **Module (m):** Ranges from **2.5 mm to 5 mm** in passenger car gearboxes
- **Helix Angle (for helical gears):** Typically **15° to 25°**
- **Synchronizer Ring Material:** Brass or sintered bronze (high friction coefficient ≈ **0.10 – 0.15**)
- **Number of Gear Speeds:** Passenger cars — **5 or 6 forward + 1 reverse**; Commercial vehicles — up to **10 forward + 2 reverse**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The *only* moving part during a gear change in a constant mesh gearbox is the **dog clutch sleeve**. In a synchromesh gearbox, the sleeve still moves, but it is **mechanically blocked from engaging** until synchronization is complete — this is the genius of the synchromesh system.

## 1.5 Why These Gearboxes Matter in Automotive Engineering

| Parameter | Constant Mesh | Synchromesh |
|---|---|---|
| Gear engagement method | Manual dog clutch | Synchronized dog clutch |
| Driver skill required | High (double-declutching) | Low (single clutch operation) |
| Gear clash | Possible | Eliminated |
| Wear on dog teeth | High | Minimal |
| Modern application | Heavy commercial vehicles, tractors | Almost all modern passenger cars |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Constant Mesh Gearbox (Step-by-Step Logic)

A constant mesh gearbox consists of the following major assemblies:

1. **Input Shaft (Main Shaft / Clutch Shaft):** Driven directly by the engine via the friction clutch. Carries only **pinions** (small gears) rigidly attached to it.

2. **Countershaft (Layshaft):** A parallel shaft carrying gears that are **permanently meshed** with the input shaft pinions. These countershaft gears rotate freely on needle bearings and drive corresponding gears on the output shaft.

3. **Output Shaft (Main Shaft):** Coaxial with the input shaft (or arranged in a different layout in RWD vehicles). Carries the **sliding dog clutches** which can be moved laterally to lock any of the output shaft gears to it.

4. **Reverse Idler Gear:** A small intermediate gear that reverses the direction of rotation for the reverse gear.

### Operational Sequence for Engaging First Gear:

- **Step 1:** The driver depresses the clutch pedal, disconnecting engine power from the input shaft.
- **Step 2:** All input pinions and countershaft gears continue to rotate freely (they remain meshed, but do not transmit power yet).
- **Step 3:** The driver moves the gear lever, sliding the dog clutch sleeve on the output shaft into engagement with the corresponding dog teeth on the first-gear output gear.
- **Step 4:** The first-gear countershaft gear is now locked to the output shaft via the sleeve — power flows: **Input Pinion → Countershaft Gear → Output Gear (via sleeve) → Output Shaft → Propeller Shaft.**
- **Step 5:** The clutch is released gradually; the vehicle moves in first gear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the term "constant mesh"?** Because whether the vehicle is in neutral, 1st gear, or 4th gear, the gears on the input shaft and countershaft are ALWAYS physically touching and rotating together. Only the dog clutch sleeve position determines which gear is active.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Synchromesh Gearbox (Detailed Logic)

The synchromesh unit is the heart of the modern gearbox. It contains the following components:

1. **Synchronizer Hub:** Splined to the output shaft. Carries external splines that match the sleeve's internal splines.
2. **Synchronizer Sleeve:** Slides over the hub; has internal splines and external dog teeth at both ends.
3. **Synchronizer Ring (Baulk Ring):** Sits between the sleeve and the gear. Has internal friction cone teeth and external dog teeth.
4. **Struts (or Spring-Loaded Balls):** Located inside the hub; they push the synchronizer ring against the gear cone under spring action.
5. **Friction Cone Surface:** On the gear side; mates with the cone on the synchronizer ring to generate frictional synchronization torque.

### The Synchronization Process — Step by Step:

- **Step 1:** Driver moves gear lever → sleeve pushes synchronizer ring toward the gear.
- **Step 2:** The friction cone of the synchronizer ring contacts the friction cone of the gear. **Frictional torque** is generated.
- **Step 3:** This torque equalizes the rotational speed of the gear with the sleeve/hub/output shaft. (The gear is either sped up or slowed down until it matches the shaft speed.)
- **Step 4:** During synchronization, the chamfered teeth of the synchronizer ring press against the chamfered teeth of the gear, creating a **positive locking force** that **mechanically prevents the sleeve from advancing** further. This is called the **baulking action**.
- **Step 5:** Once speeds are matched, the chamfered teeth slide past each other without any resistance (no more blocking torque).
- **Step 6:** The sleeve now slides forward freely and locks the gear to the hub — full engagement, zero clash.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The baulk ring's chamfer angle (typically **6° to 8°**) is the design parameter that determines how much friction force is generated before the sleeve is blocked. A steeper chamfer = stronger blocking but slower synchronization.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are essential for solving Module 1 numerical problems on constant mesh and synchromesh gearboxes:

| Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ | Simple Gear Ratio (Driver gear : Driven gear) | Dimensionless; $T$ = teeth count |
| $i_{compound} = \dfrac{T_2 \times T_4}{T_1 \times T_3}$ | Compound Gear Train Ratio (two pairs in series) | Used in constant mesh countershaft gearing |
| $N_{out} = \dfrac{N_{in}}{i}$ | Output Speed | RPM |
| $T_{out} = T_{in} \times i \times \eta$ | Output Torque | Nm; $\eta$ = gear mesh efficiency (**0.97 – 0.98** per pair) |
| $P_{out} = T_{out} \times \omega_{out}$ | Output Power | Watts; $\omega$ in rad/s |
| $v_{pitch} = \dfrac{\pi \times m \times N}{60}$ | Pitch Line Velocity | m/s; $m$ = module (mm), $N$ = RPM |
| $\tau_{sync} = \mu \times F_n \times r_{cone}$ | Synchronizer Friction Torque | Nm; $\mu$ ≈ 0.10 (brass on steel) |
| $F_{blocking} = \dfrac{\tau_{sync}}{r_{mean}} \times \tan(\phi)$ | Blocking Force on Sleeve | N; $\phi$ = chamfer angle |
| $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{gear}^n$ | Overall Efficiency (n gear pairs) | $n$ = number of meshed pairs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for Numerical Solving:** Always convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ before substituting into power equations. KTU examiners frequently test this conversion in Part B numericals.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Constant mesh gearboxes are still preferred in:

- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCVs)** like Tata LPT trucks and Ashok Leyland buses — because the gear teeth are always engaged, there is no tooth-end impact, giving **higher fatigue life**.
- **Off-road and agricultural tractors** — where dirt, mud, and rough usage demand rugged, simple designs.

Synchromesh gearboxes dominate in:

- **Passenger cars (Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta, Toyota Innova)** — for effortless, clash-free shifting.
- **Light commercial vehicles (Tata Ace, Mahindra Bolero Pickup)**.
- **Racing and high-performance cars (Ferrari, Porsche PDK)** — though these use advanced dual-clutch systems, they retain the synchromesh principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall Gear Ratio of a 4-Speed Constant Mesh Gearbox

Consider a typical 4-speed RWD constant mesh gearbox with the following teeth configuration:

- **Input shaft pinion (always meshes with countershaft 1st gear):** $T_{in1} = 18$ teeth
- **Countershaft 1st gear:** $T_{c1} = 36$ teeth
- **Output shaft 1st gear (driven by countershaft 1st gear):** $T_{o1} = 30$ teeth
- **Countershaft gear that drives output 1st gear:** $T_{c1'} = 20$ teeth
- **Input shaft pinion for 4th gear:** $T_{in4} = 24$ teeth
- **Countershaft 4th gear:** $T_{c4} = 24$ teeth (1:1 ratio = direct drive)
- **Output 4th gear teeth:** $T_{o4} = 22$ teeth
- **Countershaft gear driving output 4th:** $T_{c4'} = 22$ teeth

### Derivation for 1st Gear Ratio:

The power flow path is:
**Input Pinion → Countershaft Gear → Countershaft Drive Pinion → Output 1st Gear**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{1st} &= \left(\dfrac{T_{c1}}{T_{in1}}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{T_{o1}}{T_{c1'}}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{1st} &= \left(\dfrac{36}{18}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{30}{20}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{1st} &= 2.0 \times 1.5 \\[6pt]
i_{1st} &= 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The countershaft rotates at 2.0× reduction from the input shaft, and the output shaft rotates at 1.5× reduction from the countershaft. The **overall reduction** from engine to output shaft in 1st gear is **3.0 : 1**.

### Derivation for 4th Gear (Direct Drive) Ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{4th} &= \left(\dfrac{T_{c4}}{T_{in4}}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{T_{o4}}{T_{c4'}}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{4th} &= \left(\dfrac{24}{24}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{22}{22}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{4th} &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \\[6pt]
i_{4th} &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** In 4th gear, the countershaft rotates at the same speed as the input shaft, and the output shaft gear meshes with a countershaft gear of the same size — so output shaft speed equals input shaft speed. This is the **direct drive** condition — most fuel-efficient.

### Derivation for Reverse Gear Ratio:

A reverse idler gear is introduced between the countershaft reverse gear and the output reverse gear. The idler reverses the direction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{reverse} &= \left(\dfrac{T_{c,rev}}{T_{in,rev}}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{T_{o,rev}}{T_{idler}}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{reverse} &= \left(\dfrac{36}{18}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{30}{15}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{reverse} &= 2.0 \times 2.0 \\[6pt]
i_{reverse} &= 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Reverse gear has a higher reduction than 1st gear — typical for low-speed maneuvering.

## 3.2 Derivation: Output Speed and Torque (Sample Numerical)

**Given:**
- Engine speed at the input shaft: $N_{in} = 3000$ RPM
- Input torque: $T_{in} = 120$ Nm
- 1st gear ratio: $i_{1st} = 3.0$
- Overall gear mesh efficiency: $\eta = 0.96$

### Step 1: Output Shaft Speed

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{out} &= \dfrac{N_{in}}{i_{1st}} \\[6pt]
N_{out} &= \dfrac{3000}{3.0} \\[6pt]
N_{out} &= 1000 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Output Torque

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= T_{in} \times i_{1st} \times \eta \\[6pt]
T_{out} &= 120 \times 3.0 \times 0.96 \\[6pt]
T_{out} &= 345.6 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Power Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= T_{in} \times \omega_{in} = 120 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} \\[6pt]
P_{in} &= 120 \times 314.159 = 37699 \text{ W} \approx 37.7 \text{ kW} \\[6pt]
P_{out} &= T_{out} \times \omega_{out} = 345.6 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} \\[6pt]
P_{out} &= 345.6 \times 104.72 = 36191 \text{ W} \approx 36.2 \text{ kW} \\[6pt]
\text{Power Loss} &= P_{in} - P_{out} = 37699 - 36191 = 1508 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **1508 W power loss** represents frictional losses at the gear mesh contacts and bearing drag.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Criterion | Constant Mesh (Dog Clutch) | Synchromesh |
|---|---|---|
| Shifting Effort | High (requires skill) | Very Low |
| Synchronization Mechanism | None — manual | Automatic (friction cone) |
| Wear on Dog Teeth | Significant | Minimal (pre-synchronized) |
| Cost of Manufacturing | Low | Moderate to High |
| Weight | Moderate | Slightly Higher |
| Heat Generation During Shift | High | Low (sync done by friction cone) |
| Suitability for Beginners | Poor | Excellent |
| Maximum Torque Capacity | Very High (no friction limit) | Limited by synchronizer cone thermal capacity |

## 3.4 Synchronizer Blocking Force Derivation

When the synchronizer ring chamfer contacts the gear chamfer, the friction force generates a torque that creates a **normal force** on the chamfer surface, which in turn **blocks the sleeve** from advancing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{axial} &= \text{Sleeve push force (from driver/actuator)} \\[6pt]
N_{chamfer} &= \dfrac{F_{axial}}{\sin(\phi)} \quad \text{(Normal reaction at chamfer)} \\[6pt]
F_{friction} &= \mu \times N_{chamfer} = \mu \times \dfrac{F_{axial}}{\sin(\phi)} \\[6pt]
\tau_{chamfer} &= F_{friction} \times r_{mean} = \mu \times F_{axial} \times \dfrac{r_{mean}}{\sin(\phi)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **chamfer torque** must be greater than the torque required to synchronize the gear inertia:

$$
\tau_{chamfer} \geq I_{gear} \times \alpha_{sync}
$$

Where:
- $I_{gear}$ = mass moment of inertia of the gear being synchronized (kg·m²)
- $\alpha_{sync}$ = angular acceleration required during synchronization (rad/s²)

This is the **fundamental design equation** for any synchromesh unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the working of a synchromesh gearbox with a neat sketch," always include the **baulk ring chamfer angle** in your answer — it is a high-frequency marking area worth **2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constant Mesh Gearbox Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENGINE FLYWHEEL] --> B[FRICTION CLUTCH]
    B --> C[INPUT SHAFT PINIONS]
    C --> D[COUNTERSHAFT GEARS PERMANENTLY MESHED]
    D --> E[OUTPUT SHAFT GEARS FREE TO ROTATE]
    E --> F[DOG CLUTCH SLEEVE SELECTED POSITION]
    F --> G[OUTPUT MAIN SHAFT LOCKED GEAR]
    G --> H[PROPELLER SHAFT]
    H --> I[DIFFERENTIAL AND FINAL DRIVE]
    I --> J[DRIVE WHEELS]
    K[REVERSE IDLER GEAR] -.INSERTED FOR REVERSE.-> D
    L[GEAR LEVER AND SHIFT FORK] --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Synchromesh Unit Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[GEAR LEVER ACTUATION] --> B[SYNCHRONIZER SLEEVE MOVES AXIALLY]
    B --> C[SYNCHRONIZER RING PUSHED AGAINST GEAR CONE]
    C --> D[FRICTIONAL CONTACT ESTABLISHED]
    D --> E[SPEED EQUALIZATION VIA FRICTION]
    E --> F{CHAMFER CONTACT}
    F -->|SPEED MISMATCH| G[BAULK RING BLOCKS SLEEVE]
    G --> D
    F -->|SPEED MATCHED| H[SLEEVE SLIDES FREELY]
    H --> I[POSITIVE DOG TOOTH ENGAGEMENT]
    I --> J[GEAR FULLY ENGAGED TO OUTPUT SHAFT]
```

## 4.3 Functional State Diagram — Gear Engagement States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEUTRAL
    NEUTRAL --> SYNC_IN_PROGRESS : Driver moves lever toward target gear
    SYNC_IN_PROGRESS --> BAULKED : Chamfer contact, speeds unequal
    BAULKED --> SYNC_IN_PROGRESS : Friction continues to equalize speeds
    SYNC_IN_PROGRESS --> ENGAGED : Speeds matched, sleeve advances freely
    ENGAGED --> NEUTRAL : Driver shifts to neutral or next gear
    ENGAGED --> [*] : Engine off
```

## 4.4 Power Flow Comparison — Direct Drive vs Reduction Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIRECT_DRIVE[FOURTH GEAR DIRECT DRIVE]
        P1[INPUT PINION] --- Q1[COUNTERSHAFT GEAR EQUAL SIZE]
        Q1 --- R1[OUTPUT GEAR LOCKED VIA SLEEVE]
    end
    subgraph REDUCTION_MODE[FIRST GEAR REDUCTION]
        P2[SMALL INPUT PINION] --- Q2[LARGE COUNTERSHAFT GEAR]
        Q2 --- R2[OUTPUT GEAR LOCKED VIA SLEEVE]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** In direct drive (4th gear), all meshing gears have equal tooth counts — no speed change occurs. In reduction mode (1st gear), a small pinion drives a large countershaft gear — speed reduces, torque multiplies. This is the core principle behind all automotive gear reduction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the term "constant mesh" as applied to automotive gearboxes. Why is it preferred over sliding mesh gearboxes in heavy commercial vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A constant mesh gearbox is one in which all the gears on the input shaft and countershaft remain **permanently in mesh** with each other, irrespective of the gear selected. Gear ratio changes are achieved by sliding dog clutches on the output shaft to lock the desired gear.

**Advantages over sliding mesh gearbox (for HCVs):**

1. **No gear tooth end impact** — Since gears never disengage, the teeth do not experience the hammering action present in sliding mesh boxes. This gives much **higher fatigue life**.
2. **Quieter operation** — Continuous mesh eliminates the loud clashing noise.
3. **Suitable for high-torque, heavy-load applications** — Typical of trucks and buses.
4. **Easier and faster gear shifts** — Only the small dog clutch sleeve moves, not the entire gear.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages — full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of a synchronizer ring (baulk ring) in a synchromesh gearbox? Briefly explain the baulking phenomenon.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **synchronizer ring (baulk ring)** is a brass or bronze ring located between the synchronizer sleeve and the gear to be engaged. Its functions are:

1. **To generate frictional torque** between the gear and the sleeve/hub, equalizing their rotational speeds before engagement.
2. **To block (baulk) the sleeve from advancing** until synchronization is complete, by virtue of the chamfered teeth contact producing a self-energizing locking force.

**Baulking Phenomenon:** When the chamfered teeth of the synchronizer ring contact the chamfered teeth of the gear, the friction force at the chamfer creates a normal reaction force that pushes the ring axially — but more importantly, the wedging action of the chamfers creates a **torque that opposes the synchronizing motion**, effectively preventing sleeve advancement until the speeds are equal. This is what makes synchromesh shifting **clash-free**.

*(Award 1.5 marks for function, 1.5 marks for explanation of baulking.)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **synchromesh gearbox**. Highlight the role of the synchronizer ring and the friction cone. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Construction:**

A synchromesh gearbox consists of the following key components:

1. **Input (clutch) shaft** with pinions rigidly fixed.
2. **Countershaft (layshaft)** with gears permanently meshed with input pinions.
3. **Output (main) shaft** carrying the synchronizer assemblies.
4. **Synchronizer assembly** for each gear, comprising:
   - **Synchronizer hub** — splined to the output shaft.
   - **Synchronizer sleeve** — slides on the hub, has external dog teeth at both ends.
   - **Synchronizer ring (baulk ring)** — made of brass; has internal friction cone and external dog teeth with chamfered leading edges.
   - **Spring-loaded struts (or balls)** — push the baulk ring toward the gear cone.
5. **Gear with friction cone** — each output shaft gear has a conical friction surface that mates with the synchronizer ring.

**Working (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1:** Driver depresses clutch and moves the gear lever.
- **Step 2:** The shift fork pushes the synchronizer sleeve toward the target gear.
- **Step 3:** The sleeve pushes the baulk ring, which contacts the gear's friction cone. Frictional torque is generated.
- **Step 4:** This torque accelerates or decelerates the gear to match the sleeve/hub/output shaft speed.
- **Step 5:** As long as speeds differ, the chamfer contact creates a **blocking torque** on the sleeve (baulking action).
- **Step 6:** Once speeds are equalized, the chamfers slide past freely, and the sleeve moves forward.
- **Step 7:** The sleeve's external dog teeth engage with the gear's dog teeth — positive engagement, no clash.
- **Step 8:** Clutch is released; power flows from input shaft to output shaft through the engaged gear.

**Role of the Synchronizer Ring:**
- Provides the **friction surface** for synchronization.
- Acts as a **mechanical block** via chamfer wedging until speeds match.
- Made of brass to provide **controlled friction** without galling the steel gear.

**Role of the Friction Cone:**
- Provides the **contact surface** for the synchronizer ring.
- The cone angle (typically **7° to 10°** included angle) determines the **self-energizing characteristic** of the synchronization.

*(Allocate 2 marks for construction, 3 marks for working, 1 mark for synchronizer ring role, 1 mark for friction cone role.)*

---

**b)** In a 4-speed constant mesh gearbox, the input shaft carries pinions with the following teeth: 18T (1st/2nd) and 22T (3rd/4th). The countershaft gears have: 36T (1st), 28T (2nd), 26T (3rd), 22T (4th). The output shaft gears (which mesh with countershaft gears) have: 30T (1st), 26T (2nd), 24T (3rd), 22T (4th). Countershaft pinions driving output gears have: 20T (1st), 20T (2nd), 22T (3rd), 22T (4th). The engine delivers **80 Nm at 2500 RPM**. Assume gear mesh efficiency = **0.97 per pair**. Calculate:

(i) Overall gear ratio in 2nd gear.
(ii) Output shaft speed in 2nd gear.
(iii) Output torque in 2nd gear.
(iv) Power loss due to friction in 2nd gear.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Given Data:**
- Input torque: $T_{in} = 80$ Nm
- Input speed: $N_{in} = 2500$ RPM
- For 2nd gear:
  - Input pinion: $T_{in,2nd} = 18$ teeth
  - Countershaft gear (meshed with input pinion): $T_{c,2nd} = 28$ teeth
  - Countershaft pinion (drives output gear): $T_{c,2nd'} = 20$ teeth
  - Output shaft gear: $T_{o,2nd} = 26$ teeth
- Number of meshed gear pairs: 2
- Efficiency per pair: $\eta = 0.97$

---

**(i) Overall Gear Ratio in 2nd Gear:**

The power flow is: Input Pinion → Countershaft 2nd Gear → Countershaft 2nd Pinion → Output 2nd Gear.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{2nd} &= \left(\dfrac{T_{c,2nd}}{T_{in,2nd}}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{T_{o,2nd}}{T_{c,2nd'}}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{2nd} &= \left(\dfrac{28}{18}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{26}{20}\right) \\[6pt]
i_{2nd} &= 1.5556 \times 1.30 \\[6pt]
i_{2nd} &= 2.0222
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Overall 2nd gear ratio ≈ 2.022 : 1** — *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(ii) Output Shaft Speed in 2nd Gear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{out} &= \dfrac{N_{in}}{i_{2nd}} \\[6pt]
N_{out} &= \dfrac{2500}{2.0222} \\[6pt]
N_{out} &\approx 1236.27 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Output speed ≈ 1236 RPM** — *(1 mark)*

---

**(iii) Output Torque in 2nd Gear:**

The overall efficiency for 2 meshed pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= \eta^2 = (0.97)^2 = 0.9409
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= T_{in} \times i_{2nd} \times \eta_{overall} \\[6pt]
T_{out} &= 80 \times 2.0222 \times 0.9409 \\[6pt]
T_{out} &\approx 152.21 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Output torque ≈ 152.21 Nm** — *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(iv) Power Loss in 2nd Gear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{in} &= \dfrac{2\pi \times 2500}{60} = 261.799 \text{ rad/s} \\[6pt]
\omega_{out} &= \dfrac{2\pi \times 1236.27}{60} = 129.470 \text{ rad/s} \\[6pt]
P_{in} &= T_{in} \times \omega_{in} = 80 \times 261.799 = 20943.95 \text{ W} \\[6pt]
P_{out} &= T_{out} \times \omega_{out} = 152.21 \times 129.470 = 19704.99 \text{ W} \\[6pt]
P_{loss} &= P_{in} - P_{out} = 20943.95 - 19704.99 \\[6pt]
P_{loss} &\approx 1238.96 \text{ W} \approx 1.24 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Power loss ≈ 1239 W (or 1.24 kW)** — *(3 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**a)** Compare the **construction, working, and shifting effort** of a constant mesh gearbox and a synchromesh gearbox. Use a comparison table and explain any **three key advantages** of the synchromesh design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Constant Mesh Gearbox | Synchromesh Gearbox |
|---|---|---|
| Gear engagement | Dog clutch sleeve | Synchronized dog clutch |
| Speed matching | None — manual by driver | Automatic via friction cone |
| Synchronizer ring | Absent | Present (brass baulk ring) |
| Friction cone | Absent | Present on each gear |
| Driver skill required | High (double-clutching needed) | Low (single push) |
| Shifting effort | High | Low |
| Wear on dog teeth | High | Very low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Weight | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Typical application | HCV, tractors, off-road | Passenger cars, LCV |

**Three Key Advantages of Synchromesh Design (3 marks):**

1. **Clash-free shifting:** The synchronizer ring ensures that gear speeds match before the dog teeth engage, eliminating the gear clash noise common in constant mesh boxes during inexperienced shifting.

2. **Reduced driver fatigue:** No need for double-declutching or precise rev-matching — the synchronization is automatic. This significantly reduces driver workload in city traffic conditions.

3. **Extended component life:** Because the dog teeth engage only when speeds are matched, there is minimal impact loading on the dog teeth, leading to longer service life and lower maintenance costs.

---

**b)** A passenger car engine develops **maximum torque of 150 Nm at 3500 RPM**. The 1st gear ratio of its synchromesh gearbox is **3.5 : 1**, with a final drive ratio of **4.1 : 1** and overall transmission efficiency of **0.90**. The wheel radius is **0.32 m**. Calculate:

(i) The torque available at the drive wheels.
(ii) The tractive force developed at the wheel-road interface.
(iii) The vehicle speed in km/h, assuming no slip.
(iv) The power being transmitted to the road.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Given Data:**
- Engine torque: $T_{engine} = 150$ Nm
- Engine speed: $N_{engine} = 3500$ RPM
- 1st gear ratio: $i_{gb} = 3.5$
- Final drive ratio: $i_{fd} = 4.1$
- Overall efficiency: $\eta = 0.90$
- Wheel radius: $r = 0.32$ m

**Overall drivetrain ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{total} &= i_{gb} \times i_{fd} = 3.5 \times 4.1 = 14.35
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(i) Torque Available at the Drive Wheels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= T_{engine} \times i_{total} \times \eta \\[6pt]
T_{wheel} &= 150 \times 14.35 \times 0.90 \\[6pt]
T_{wheel} &= 1937.25 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Wheel torque ≈ 1937.25 Nm** — *(2 marks)*

---

**(ii) Tractive Force at Wheel-Road Interface:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tractive} &= \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r} \\[6pt]
F_{tractive} &= \dfrac{1937.25}{0.32} \\[6pt]
F_{tractive} &= 6053.91 \text{ N} \approx 6.05 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Tractive force ≈ 6054 N (6.05 kN)** — *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(iii) Vehicle Speed:**

Output shaft speed (at the differential input):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{diff} &= \dfrac{N_{engine}}{i_{gb}} = \dfrac{3500}{3.5} = 1000 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wheel rotational speed (after final drive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{wheel} &= \dfrac{N_{diff}}{i_{fd}} = \dfrac{1000}{4.1} \approx 243.90 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wheel angular velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{wheel} &= \dfrac{2\pi \times 243.90}{60} \approx 25.54 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Vehicle speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \omega_{wheel} \times r = 25.54 \times 0.32 = 8.173 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
V &= 8.173 \times 3.6 = 29.42 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Vehicle speed ≈ 29.42 km/h** — *(2 marks)*

---

**(iv) Power Transmitted to the Road:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{road} &= F_{tractive} \times V \\[6pt]
P_{road} &= 6053.91 \times 8.173 \\[6pt]
P_{road} &\approx 49478 \text{ W} \approx 49.48 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via torque–speed method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{engine} &= T_{engine} \times \omega_{engine} = 150 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 3500}{60} = 54977.87 \text{ W} \\[6pt]
P_{road} &= P_{engine} \times \eta = 54977.87 \times 0.90 = 49480.08 \text{ W} \approx 49.48 \text{ kW} \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Power to road ≈ 49.48 kW** — *(1.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Many students mix RPM and rad/s in the same calculation. **Always convert RPM to rad/s before substituting into power equations**, or alternatively, use $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$.
> 2. **Efficiency Misapplication:** Forgetting to raise efficiency to the power of the number of meshed pairs. With $n$ mesh pairs, total efficiency is $\eta^n$, **not** $n \times \eta$.
> 3. **Compound Gear Train Errors:** Confusing which gear is the "driver" and which is the "driven" in each stage. **Remember:** the smaller gear (fewer teeth) is always the driver when reducing speed.
> 4. **Drawing the Synchronizer:** Most students draw the sleeve in engagement position without showing the **intermediate baulking state**. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for showing the baulking phenomenon.
> 5. **Direction Reversal in Reverse Gear:** Forgetting that the reverse idler **reverses** the direction of rotation — output shaft rotates opposite to input shaft in reverse.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Constant Mesh and Synchromesh Gearboxes**

- **Constant Mesh Definition:** All gear pairs remain meshed permanently; only dog clutch sleeves move to select ratios.
- **Synchromesh Definition:** A constant mesh gearbox with an automatic speed-matching mechanism using friction cones and synchronizer (baulk) rings.
- **Major Components of Constant Mesh:** Input shaft pinions, countershaft gears, output shaft gears, dog clutch sleeves, reverse idler, shift forks, gear lever.
- **Major Components of Synchromesh:** All constant mesh parts + synchronizer hub, sleeve, baulk ring, struts/springs, friction cone on gears.
- **Baulking Phenomenon:** Chamfered teeth contact produces self-energizing torque that blocks sleeve advancement until speeds match.
- **Synchronizer Ring Material:** Brass or sintered bronze — chosen for high friction coefficient and low wear against steel.
- **Chamfer Angle of Baulk Ring:** Typically **6° to 8°** — controls the magnitude of blocking force.
- **Direct Drive Gear:** Final/4th gear typically has 1:1 ratio — most fuel-efficient.
- **Reverse Gear:** Achieved by inserting an idler gear between countershaft and output shaft reverse gears — reverses direction.
- **Formula $i = N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1$:** Universal gear ratio formula applicable to all automotive transmissions.
- **Compound Gear Train Formula:** $i_{compound} = (T_2 \times T_4)/(T_1 \times T_3)$ — used for all multi-stage constant mesh countershaft gear trains.
- **Power Loss Location:** Mainly at gear mesh contacts due to sliding friction; approximately 2% to 4% per meshed pair.
- **Modern Trend:** Even synchromesh gearboxes are being replaced by **dual-clutch transmissions (DCT)** and **continuously variable transmissions (CVT)** in high-end vehicles, but the fundamental principle of synchromesh remains foundational to automotive engineering education.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a labeled diagram before answering any construction-based question — KTU examiners reward neat sketches with up to **2 marks**.
- **Numerical Tip:** Convert RPM to rad/s as $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ before any power calculation; verify using the dual method $P = F \times V$ for sanity check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CVT and DCT - Simple Problems on Determination of Gear Ratios for Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CVT and DCT - Simple Problems on Determination of Gear Ratios for Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transmission Gear Ratio — Formal Definition

In an automotive powertrain, the **Overall Gear Ratio (i_total)** of a vehicle is defined as the product of the transmission ratio and the final drive (differential) ratio. It represents the rotational speed reduction (and hence torque multiplication) between the engine crankshaft and the driving wheels.

$$i_{total} = i_{transmission} \times i_{final\ drive}$$

The corresponding wheel speed and vehicle speed relationships are governed by:

$$N_{wheel} = \frac{N_{engine}}{i_{total}} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

$$V = 0.377 \cdot r_{wheel} \cdot N_{wheel} \quad \text{[km/h]}$$

where $r_{wheel}$ is the effective rolling radius of the tire in **meters**, and the constant **0.377** arises from unit conversion ($2\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 10^{-3}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A student must be able to determine the gear ratios of **CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission)** and **DCT (Dual Clutch Transmission)** drivetrains using speed, torque, and tire data provided in the problem statement. This topic is a frequent 14-mark Part B question.

## 1.2 CVT vs DCT — Intuitive Overview

**Conceptual Analogy — Think of it like a bicycle:**

| Transmission Type | Bicycle Analogy | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **CVT** | A scooter with a *smoothly variable* belt drive | No discrete "gears" — the ratio slides continuously between a low (hill-climb) value and a high (cruising) value. |
| **DCT** | A racing bike with *two front chainrings pre-selected* | Two clutches work alternately — odd gears on one clutch, even gears on the other — so the next ratio is pre-engaged and swap is instant. |

### CVT — Core Definition

A **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** is a transmission in which the ratio between input (engine) and output (propeller shaft) speeds can be varied *continuously* within a defined range $[i_{CVT,min},\ i_{CVT,max}]$, typically realised using a **metal-pushing belt** and two variable-diameter pulleys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants for CVT Problems:** A typical automotive CVT has a ratio spread of about **2.4 to 7.0** (ratio span), and the lowest ratio (underdrive) provides maximum torque multiplication for launch.

### DCT — Core Definition

A **Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT)** is an automated manual transmission employing **two separate clutches** — one controlling the **odd gears (1, 3, 5, 7)** and the other controlling the **even gears (2, 4, 6, R)**. Each gear has a fixed, discrete ratio. Pre-selection of the next gear eliminates the torque interruption seen in conventional automatics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical DCT Gear Count:** Modern road-car DCTs are **7-speed** (VW DQ200/DQ500) or **8-speed** (Porsche PDK, ZF 8DT). Each gear ratio is *stepped* and forms a geometric progression for smooth acceleration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic for Gear-Ratio Problems

The KTU Board Examiner usually frames problems using the following logical skeleton. Master this flow:

**Step 1 — Extract vehicle parameters from the problem statement:**
- Engine speed range $[N_{eng,min}, N_{eng,max}]$
- Peak torque $T_{peak}$ and the engine RPM at which it occurs
- Tire effective radius $r$
- Final drive ratio $i_f$
- Transmission ratio (CVT range or DCT individual gear ratios)

**Step 2 — Convert wheel speed to vehicle speed (or vice versa):**
Use the canonical relation $V = 0.377 \, r \, N_{wheel}$, where $r$ is in **m** and $V$ is in **km/h**.

**Step 3 — Apply the overall gear ratio:**
For CVT, the overall ratio is a *function* of the CVT setting. For DCT, it is a *discrete set* — one value per gear.

**Step 4 — Compute the requested quantity:**
- Maximum vehicle speed (at $N_{eng,max}$ in the highest/overdrive ratio)
- Minimum vehicle speed (at $N_{eng,max}$ in the lowest/underdrive ratio)
- Tractive force at peak torque: $F_t = T_{peak} \cdot i_{total} / r$
- Engine speed at a given cruise speed (reverse calculation)

**Step 5 — Sanity-check using "why" logic:**
- A *higher* gear ratio → slower wheel, more torque, lower vehicle speed.
- A *lower* gear ratio (overdrive) → faster wheel, less torque, higher vehicle speed.
- Real vehicles rarely exceed **250 km/h**; if you get 400+ km/h, re-check units.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_{total} = i_{tr} \times i_f$ | Overall gear ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $N_w = N_e / i_{total}$ | Wheel speed from engine speed | rpm |
| 3 | $V = 0.377 \, r \, N_w$ | Vehicle speed from wheel speed | km/h, m, rpm |
| 4 | $V_{max} = 0.377 \, r \, \dfrac{N_{e,max}}{i_{tr,min} \cdot i_f}$ | Max vehicle speed (CVT overdrive) | km/h |
| 5 | $V_{min} = 0.377 \, r \, \dfrac{N_{e,max}}{i_{tr,max} \cdot i_f}$ | Min vehicle speed (CVT underdrive, at full throttle) | km/h |
| 6 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_{peak} \cdot i_{total}}{r}$ | Tractive force at wheel | N |
| 7 | $N_e = \dfrac{V}{0.377 \, r} \cdot i_{total}$ | Reverse — engine speed from cruise speed | rpm |
| 8 | $\sigma = i_{tr,max} / i_{tr,min}$ | Ratio span (CVT figure of merit) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $r_t = i_{n+1}/i_n$ | Step ratio between adjacent DCT gears | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation:** When writing $i_{tr,max}$ vs $i_{tr,min}$ in a CVT, **maximum ratio = underdrive (high torque, low speed)** and **minimum ratio = overdrive (low torque, high speed)**. Confusing these is the most common mistake in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These gear-ratio calculations are the **backbone of every vehicle calibration** done at an OEM:
- *Cruise fuel economy* depends on selecting the gear that places the engine at its **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) sweet spot** (typically 1500–2500 rpm for petrol, 1200–2000 rpm for diesel).
- *Gradeability* (climbing a 30 % incline) is computed by ensuring $F_t > mg\sin\theta + F_{rolling}$.
- *Top speed* is governed by $V_{max}$ — a mismatch between $i_f$ and $i_{tr,min}$ can leave the engine "running out of revs" before reaching the design top speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Vehicle-Speed Equation

Starting from the kinematic relation between wheel angular velocity and vehicle linear velocity:

$$v = \omega_w \cdot r_w$$

Converting $\omega_w$ in rad/s to $N_w$ in rpm: $\omega_w = 2\pi N_w / 60$. Substituting:

$$v = \frac{2\pi N_w}{60} \cdot r_w \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

Converting m/s → km/h by multiplying by **3.6**:

$$V = \frac{2\pi N_w}{60} \cdot r_w \cdot 3.6 = 0.37716 \, r_w \, N_w \approx 0.377 \, r_w \, N_w$$

This is the canonical **0.377 formula** every KTU answer must reference.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tractive Force Equation

Power balance at the wheel (neglecting drivetrain losses for the simplified KTU formulation):

$$P_{wheel} = T_w \cdot \omega_w = F_t \cdot v$$

Using $T_w = T_{engine} \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}$ and rearranging:

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{T_{engine} \cdot i_{total}}{r_w}$$

For a KTU "simple problem" we assume **100 % drivetrain efficiency** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — CVT Problem

> **Problem Statement:** A compact passenger car uses a metal-belt CVT with the following data:
> - Engine maximum speed: **6000 rpm**
> - Engine peak torque: **180 Nm at 4200 rpm**
> - Final drive ratio: **3.73**
> - Effective tire radius: **0.31 m**
> - CVT ratio range: **0.45 (overdrive) to 2.95 (underdrive)**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Maximum and minimum vehicle speeds at full throttle.
> (b) Maximum tractive force available at the wheels.

### Solution (a) — Speed Range

**Minimum vehicle speed** occurs when the CVT is in the *underdrive* position (max ratio) at peak engine speed:

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{total, max} &= i_{tr,max} \times i_f = 2.95 \times 3.73 = 11.0035 \\
N_w &= \frac{N_{e,max}}{i_{total,max}} = \frac{6000}{11.0035} = 545.29 \ \text{rpm} \\
V_{min} &= 0.377 \times 0.31 \times 545.29 = 63.74 \ \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum vehicle speed** occurs when the CVT is in the *overdrive* position (min ratio) at peak engine speed:

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{total, min} &= 0.45 \times 3.73 = 1.6785 \\
N_w &= \frac{6000}{1.6785} = 3574.89 \ \text{rpm} \\
V_{max} &= 0.377 \times 0.31 \times 3574.89 = 418.48 \ \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A *theoretical* $V_{max}$ of ~418 km/h is well above any real-world top speed; the practical limit is set by aerodynamics. For KTU answers, the *theoretical* value computed from the formula is acceptable.

### Solution (b) — Maximum Tractive Force

Maximum tractive force uses **peak engine torque (180 Nm at 4200 rpm)** and the **underdrive CVT ratio (2.95)** to maximise torque multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= T_{peak} \times i_{tr,max} \times i_f = 180 \times 2.95 \times 3.73 = 1980.63 \ \text{Nm} \\
F_{t,max} &= \frac{T_{wheel}}{r_w} = \frac{1980.63}{0.31} = 6389.13 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — DCT Problem

> **Problem Statement:** A sports sedan uses a **7-speed wet-clutch DCT** with the following gear ratios:

| Gear | Ratio $i_{tr}$ |
|---|---|
| 1st | 4.71 |
| 2nd | 3.14 |
| 3rd | 2.11 |
| 4th | 1.67 |
| 5th | 1.29 |
| 6th | 1.03 |
| 7th | 0.84 |

Additional data: $i_f = 3.46$, $r_w = 0.33$ m, engine speed range = **1200 to 6800 rpm**.

**Calculate:**
(a) Maximum vehicle speed in each gear.
(b) The engine speed when cruising at **120 km/h** in 5th gear — comment on its suitability.

### Solution (a) — Speed in Each Gear at 6800 rpm

Apply $V = 0.377 \cdot r_w \cdot N_w = 0.377 \cdot 0.33 \cdot \dfrac{6800}{i_{tr} \cdot i_f} = \dfrac{845.62}{i_{tr} \cdot 3.46}$.

| Gear | $i_{tr}$ | $N_w$ (rpm) | $V_{max}$ (km/h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.71 | 417.3 | **51.96** |
| 2 | 3.14 | 625.9 | **77.93** |
| 3 | 2.11 | 931.4 | **115.99** |
| 4 | 1.67 | 1176.7 | **146.53** |
| 5 | 1.29 | 1523.0 | **189.66** |
| 6 | 1.03 | 1907.7 | **237.55** |
| 7 | 0.84 | 2339.7 | **291.36** |

### Solution (b) — Engine Speed at 120 km/h in 5th Gear

Step 1 — Compute wheel speed at 120 km/h:

$$N_w = \frac{V}{0.377 \cdot r_w} = \frac{120}{0.377 \times 0.33} = 964.65 \ \text{rpm}$$

Step 2 — Apply the overall gear ratio for 5th gear ($i_{tr} = 1.29$, $i_f = 3.46$):

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{total,5} &= 1.29 \times 3.46 = 4.4634 \\
N_e &= N_w \times i_{total,5} = 964.65 \times 4.4634 = 4305.27 \ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** At ~4305 rpm, the engine is in its **upper-mid range** — well above the BSFC sweet spot (~2000 rpm for a petrol engine) but within the power band. **5th gear is therefore NOT ideal for fuel economy at 120 km/h**; the driver should upshift to **6th or 7th gear** for cruise optimisation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table for Quick Revision

| Quantity | CVT Formula | DCT Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Range of vehicle speed | Continuous, bounded by $V_{min}, V_{max}$ | Discrete, one value per gear at fixed $N_e$ |
| Ratio spread | $\sigma = i_{tr,max}/i_{tr,min}$ | $\sigma = i_{1st}/i_{last}$ |
| Cruise optimisation | Continuous — engine can sit at BSFC sweet spot for *any* speed | Stepped — must select discrete gear close to sweet spot |
| Maximum tractive force | At $i_{tr,max}$ (underdrive) | In **1st gear** |
| Number of clutches | 1 (forward + reverse) | 2 (odd / even) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CVT Powertrain

The diagram below maps the flow of mechanical power and control signals in a typical metal-belt CVT drivetrain, which is the standard schematic expected in a KTU 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eng[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] -->|N_e rpm| primary[PRIMARY PULLEY VARIABLE DIAMETER]
    primary -->|Belt Drive CVT ratio i_tr| secondary[SECONDARY PULLEY VARIABLE DIAMETER]
    secondary -->|N_p rpm| prop[PROPELLER SHAFT]
    prop --> dif[FINAL DRIVE DIFFERENTIAL i_f EQUALS 3.73]
    dif --> wheel[DRIVING WHEELS r equals 0.31 m]
    wheel -->|F_t Newton| road[ROAD SURFACE]
    
    ctrl[ECU TCU] -.->|Ratio Command| primary
    ctrl -.->|Ratio Command| secondary
    ctrl -.->|Throttle| eng
    
    style eng fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style primary fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style secondary fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dif fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style wheel fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ctrl fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The TCU (Transmission Control Unit) commands the pulleys to vary their effective diameters, which varies $i_{tr}$ continuously. The wheel block contains the effective tire radius $r$ — the parameter needed for the **0.377 formula**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DCT Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eng[ENGINE] --> inp[INPUT SHAFT]
    inp --> c1[CLUTCH 1 ODD GEARS 1 3 5 7]
    inp --> c2[CLUTCH 2 EVEN GEARS 2 4 6 R]
    
    c1 --> g1[GEAR 1 i EQUALS 4.71]
    c1 --> g3[GEAR 3 i EQUALS 2.11]
    c1 --> g5[GEAR 5 i EQUALS 1.29]
    c1 --> g7[GEAR 7 i EQUALS 0.84]
    
    c2 --> g2[GEAR 2 i EQUALS 3.14]
    c2 --> g4[GEAR 4 i EQUALS 1.67]
    c2 --> g6[GEAR 6 i EQUALS 1.03]
    c2 --> gR[GEAR R i EQUALS 4.37]
    
    g1 --> out[OUTPUT SHAFT]
    g3 --> out
    g5 --> out
    g7 --> out
    g2 --> out
    g4 --> out
    g6 --> out
    gR --> out
    
    out --> dif[FINAL DRIVE i_f EQUALS 3.46]
    dif --> wheel[WHEELS r equals 0.33 m]
    
    mecu[MECHATRONIC ECU] -.->|Hydraulic Pressure| c1
    mecu -.->|Hydraulic Pressure| c2
    mecu -.->|Shift Actuator| g1
    mecu -.->|Shift Actuator| g2

    mecu -.->|Shift Actuator| g4
```

```mermaid

    q1 -->|Max fuel economy| cvt1[USE CVT]
    q1 -->|Sporty driving fast shifts| dct1[USE DCT]
    q1 -->|Heavy duty trucks| mt[USE AMT OR MT]
    
    cvt1 --> cvtfeat[Continuous ratio, engine stays in BSFC sweet spot, no shift shock]
    dct1 --> dctfeat[Pre-selected gears, sub 200 ms shift, fixed ratio per gear]
    
    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
