<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "ratio spread" of a CVT. For a CVT with $i_{min} = 0.5$ and $i_{max} = 3.0$, calculate the ratio spread.**

**Model Answer:**
Ratio spread is defined as the ratio of the maximum transmission ratio to the minimum transmission ratio of a CVT. It quantifies the range of speed variation available.

$$\sigma = \frac{i_{max}}{i_{min}} = \frac{3.0}{0.5} = 6.0$$

A spread of **6.0** is typical of modern automotive CVTs.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is a DCT generally considered faster-shifting than a conventional automatic torque-converter transmission?**

**Model Answer:**
A DCT pre-selects the next gear on the alternate clutch while the current gear is still engaged. When an upshift is commanded, the off-going clutch disengages and the on-coming clutch engages simultaneously — resulting in a **shift time of 150–200 ms** compared to **500–800 ms** for a torque-converter automatic. There is also no torque-converter slip to overcome.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — CVT Focus

> A 1500 kg passenger car uses a **metal-belt CVT** with the following specifications:
>
> - Engine peak power: **110 kW at 6000 rpm**
> - Engine peak torque: **180 Nm at 3500 rpm**
> - Final drive ratio: **4.10**
> - Effective tire radius: **0.32 m**
> - CVT ratio range: **0.42 to 2.85**
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The maximum theoretical vehicle speed and the minimum vehicle speed at full throttle at peak engine speed. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The maximum tractive effort at the driving wheels at peak torque, and check whether the car can climb a 30 % gradient (assume rolling resistance coefficient $f = 0.015$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Maximum and minimum vehicle speed:**

Step 1 — Minimum speed (underdrive, ratio = 2.85):

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{total, max} &= 2.85 \times 4.10 = 11.685 \\
N_w &= \frac{6000}{11.685} = 513.46 \ \text{rpm} \\
V_{min} &= 0.377 \times 0.32 \times 513.46 = 61.97 \ \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — Maximum speed (overdrive, ratio = 0.42):

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{total, min} &= 0.42 \times 4.10 = 1.722 \\
N_w &= \frac{6000}{1.722} = 3484.32 \ \text{rpm} \\
V_{max} &= 0.377 \times 0.32 \times 3484.32 = 420.34 \ \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> **[7 marks: 3.5 for sub-part 1, 3.5 for sub-part 2, including all intermediate steps]**

**(b) Tractive effort and gradeability check:**

Step 1 — Maximum tractive force at peak torque:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= T_{peak} \times i_{tr,max} \times i_f = 180 \times 2.85 \times 4.10 = 2103.3 \ \text{Nm} \\
F_t &= \frac{T_{wheel}}{r_w} = \frac{2103.3}{0.32} = 6572.81 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2 — Resistance forces on a 30 % gradient ($\tan\theta = 0.30 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 16.7°$):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{grade} &= mg\sin\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(16.7°) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.2873 = 4228.07 \ \text{N} \\
F_{roll} &= mg f \cos\theta = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 0.9578 = 211.45 \ \text{N} \\
F_{total,resist} &= 4228.07 + 211.45 = 4439.52 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Comparison:

$$F_t = 6572.81 \ \text{N} \ > \ F_{resist} = 4439.52 \ \text{N} \quad \checkmark$$

Since $F_t > F_{resist}$, the car **CAN climb** the 30 % gradient.

Net climbing force surplus: $6572.81 - 4439.52 = 2133.29$ N.

> **[7 marks: 2 marks tractive force, 3 marks resistance calculation, 2 marks final verdict with comparison]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — DCT Focus

> A 6-speed DCT vehicle has the following data:
>
> | Parameter | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Engine speed range | 1000 to 6500 rpm |
> | Tire radius | 0.34 m |
> | Final drive ratio | 3.70 |
>
> | Gear | $i_{tr}$ |
> |---|---|
> | 1 | 4.20 |
> | 2 | 2.50 |
> | 3 | 1.65 |
> | 4 | 1.20 |
> | 5 | 0.92 |
> | 6 | 0.75 |
>
> **(a)** Determine the maximum vehicle speed attainable in **each gear**. Identify the gear that gives the closest match to **100 km/h cruising** (target engine speed ~2000 rpm for best fuel economy). **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** If the engine produces **220 Nm peak torque at 4000 rpm**, calculate the maximum tractive force in **1st gear** and **3rd gear**. Comment on the gear ratio progression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Maximum speed in each gear:**

Using $V_{max} = 0.377 \times 0.34 \times \dfrac{6500}{i_{tr} \times 3.70} = \dfrac{225.13}{i_{tr}}$:

| Gear | $i_{tr}$ | $V_{max}$ (km/h) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.20 | 53.60 |
| 2 | 2.50 | 90.05 |
| 3 | 1.65 | 136.45 |
| 4 | 1.20 | 187.61 |
| 5 | 0.92 | 244.71 |
| 6 | 0.75 | 300.18 |

**Best gear for 100 km/h cruise at 2000 rpm:**

Step 1 — Wheel speed at 100 km/h:

$$N_w = \frac{100}{0.377 \times 0.34} = 780.42 \ \text{rpm}$$

Step 2 — For target $N_e = 2000$ rpm, the required overall ratio:

$$i_{total} = \frac{N_e}{N_w} = \frac{2000}{780.42} = 2.563 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_{tr} = \frac{2.563}{3.70} = 0.6927$$

This lies between 5th gear (0.92) and 6th gear (0.75). Since **6th gear (0.75)** gives:

$$N_e = 780.42 \times 0.75 \times 3.70 = 2165.6 \ \text{rpm}$$

which is closest to 2000 rpm, **6th gear is the optimal choice** for 100 km/h cruise.

> **[7 marks: 3 marks for table, 2 marks for wheel speed calculation, 2 marks for optimal gear identification]**

**(b) Maximum tractive force in 1st and 3rd gears:**

**1st gear:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_w &= 220 \times 4.20 \times 3.70 = 3418.8 \ \text{Nm} \\
F_t &= \frac{3418.8}{0.34} = 10055.3 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**3rd gear:**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_w &= 220 \times 1.65 \times 3.70 = 1343.1 \ \text{Nm} \\
F_t &= \frac{1343.1}{0.34} = 3950.3 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on gear ratio progression:**
The step ratios between adjacent gears are:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{1 \to 2} &= 4.20/2.50 = 1.68 \\
r_{2 \to 3} &= 2.50/1.65 = 1.515 \\
r_{3 \to 4} &= 1.65/1.20 = 1.375 \\
r_{4 \to 5} &= 1.20/0.92 = 1.304 \\
r_{5 \to 6} &= 0.92/0.75 = 1.227
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **close-ratio geometric progression** with step ratios between 1.2 and 1.7 — typical of sports-sedan DCTs. The progression yields **smooth, evenly-spaced acceleration** without large engine-speed drops between shifts.

> **[7 marks: 3 marks for two tractive force calculations, 2 marks for step-ratio table, 2 marks for the comment]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently write $V$ in m/s instead of km/h, or use $r$ in cm instead of m. **Always write the units explicitly** before substituting into the 0.377 formula.
> 2. **Inverting the Ratio (–2 marks):** When asked "maximum vehicle speed", students occasionally use $i_{tr,max}$ instead of $i_{tr,min}$. Remember: **min ratio = max speed** (overdrive).
> 3. **Forgetting the Final Drive (–3 marks):** A shocking number of answers compute $N_w = N_e / i_{tr}$ without multiplying by $i_f$. Always draw the power-flow diagram first to confirm what is included in $i_{total}$.
> 4. **DCT Cruise Optimisation (–2 marks):** The KTU answer key expects the **engine RPM at the chosen cruise speed**, not just the choice of gear. Show the calculation $N_e = N_w \times i_{total}$ explicitly.
> 5. **No Final Statement:** End every numerical answer with a **bold one-line conclusion** (e.g., "*Therefore, 6th gear is optimal.*") — examiners award the final mark for this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Golden Equation:** $V = 0.377 \cdot r \cdot N_w$ — commit this to memory verbatim; it appears in **every** KTU transmission problem.
- **Overall Ratio:** $i_{total} = i_{transmission} \times i_{final\ drive}$ — the *only* formula that links engine RPM to wheel RPM.
- **CVT — Two Endpoints:** Underdrive ($i_{max}$) → launch / hill climb. Overdrive ($i_{min}$) → top speed / cruise.
- **DCT — Discrete Gears:** Each gear has a fixed $i_{tr}$. Always compute $V_{max}$ for each gear at $N_{e,max}$ to populate the speed-vs-gear table.
- **Tractive Force:** $F_t = T_{peak} \cdot i_{total} / r$ — independent of vehicle speed (in the simplified KTU model).
- **Cruise Optimisation Logic:** Choose the gear that places the engine closest to its **BSFC sweet spot** (~2000 rpm for petrol).
- **Common Constants to Memorise:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $0.377 = 2\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 10^{-3} / 1$, $\sin(16.7°) \approx 0.287$ for a 30 % gradient.
- **Two-clutch Rule for DCT:** Odd gears (1, 3, 5, 7, R) on Clutch 1; Even gears (2, 4, 6) on Clutch 2. Drawing this in the exam fetches an extra mark.
- **Ratio Spread ($\sigma$):** $\sigma_{CVT} = i_{max}/i_{min}$; a typical modern CVT achieves $\sigma = 6$ to $7$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Epicyclic Gearboxes and Application: Principle of Planetary Gear Trains - Ford T-Model Gearbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Epicyclic Gearboxes and Application: Principle of Planetary Gear Trains - Ford T-Model Gearbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Epicyyclic (Planetary) Gear Train — Formal KTU Definition

An **Epicyclic Gear Train** (also called a **Planetary Gear Train**) is a compound gear mechanism in which one or more gears (called **planet gears**) revolve around a central **sun gear** while simultaneously rotating about their own axes, all carried by a common rotating arm known as the **planet carrier (or arm)**. The outermost member with internal teeth is called the **ring gear** or **annulus**.

Mathematically, it is defined as a gear system where the axes of some gears move relative to a fixed frame, producing "epicyclic" (circle-upon-circle) motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Ford Model T (1908–1927) gearbox is the **classic industrial application** of a single-set simple planetary gear train. It is the most frequently asked question from this module in KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Components of a Simple Planetary Gear Set

A simple planetary gear train has four essential members:

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Sun Gear (S)** | Central gear with external teeth; usually the input or output. |
| 2 | **Planet Gears (P)** | 2 or 3 (or more) smaller gears that mesh with both sun and ring; rotate and revolve. |
| 3 | **Ring Gear / Annulus (R)** | Outer gear with **internal teeth**; meshes with planets. |
| 4 | **Planet Carrier / Arm (C)** | Holds the planet gear pins/shafts; rotates about the central axis. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Solar System**
> Imagine our Solar System. The **Sun** is the central body. The **Earth and other planets** revolve around the Sun (this is "revolution") while also spinning on their own axis (this is "rotation"). The entire system is held together by a conceptual "carrier arm."
>
> In a planetary gearbox:
> - The **Sun gear** = the Sun.
> - The **Planet gears** = the Earth (they both rotate and revolve).
> - The **Planet carrier** = the gravitational path that holds the planets.
> - The **Ring gear** = the outer "shell" of the solar system (the Oort cloud boundary).
>
> By **holding (fixing) any one of the four members** and **driving another**, we get different speed ratios — just like our Solar System would behave differently if the Sun were locked in place.

## 1.4 The Ford T-Model Gearbox — Application Context

The **Ford Model T** (introduced in **1908** by Henry Ford) used one of the **earliest mass-produced epicyclic gearboxes** in automobile history. It was a **two-speed transmission** (High and Low) plus Reverse, built around a **single simple planetary gear set**.

| Specification | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| Number of forward speeds | 2 (Low + High) |
| Reverse | Yes (via separate reverse gear) |
| Gear reduction in Low | **3.66 : 1 to 4 : 1** (typical) |
| Number of planet gears | 3 |
| Control mechanism | **Friction band** (low) + **multi-plate clutch** (high) |
| Input member | Engine flywheel / central shaft |
| Output member | Planet carrier (drives the rear axle) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Tooth Count for Ford T-Model:**
> Sun gear (T_s) = **18 teeth**, Planet (T_p) = **18 teeth**, Ring gear (T_r) = **72 teeth**
> Ratio check: $T_r = T_s + 2T_p = 18 + 2(18) = 72$ ✓
> Tooth ratio $\frac{T_r}{T_s} = \frac{72}{18} = 4$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Planetary Gear Motion

The key kinematic insight is that the **planet gears perform two simultaneous motions**:

1. **Rotation** — spinning about their own axis (relative to carrier).
2. **Revolution** — orbiting the sun gear axis (along with the carrier).

This dual motion is what creates the "epicyclic" (epi = upon, cyclic = circle) name — one circle traced upon another.

### Why use a Planetary Gear Train?
- **Compactness:** High torque density in a small package (concentric shafts).
- **Coaxial arrangement:** Input and output are on the **same axis** — ideal for vehicles.
- **Multiple ratios:** Different gear reductions by simply changing which member is held/braked.
- **High efficiency:** Typically **97–99% per stage** (all teeth in mesh share the load).
- **Low inertia:** Distributed mass reduces dynamic loads.

## 2.2 The Willis Equation (Fundamental Kinematic Formula)

The **Willis Equation** gives the speed relationship between Sun, Ring, and Carrier.

**Derivation logic:** Imagine "freezing" the carrier and adding a common rotational speed of $-N_c$ to every member. The gear train then behaves like an **ordinary (fixed-axis) gear train**, where the planet becomes a simple **idler** between sun and ring.

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = speed of sun gear (rpm)
- $N_r$ = speed of ring gear (rpm)
- $N_c$ = speed of planet carrier (rpm)
- $T_s$ = number of teeth on sun gear
- $T_r$ = number of teeth on ring gear
- The **negative sign** arises because the planet idler reverses direction between sun and ring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **+ve** = direction of input (clockwise, say).
> - **External mesh** → gears rotate in **opposite** directions.
> - **Internal mesh** (sun-planet and planet-ring) → planets rotate in **same** direction as ring relative to sun.
> - **Negative speed ratio** = **direction reversal** between input and output.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Condition | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **General Willis Equation** | $\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$ | Fundamental relationship for any simple planetary set. |
| 2 | **Ring fixed** ($N_r = 0$) | $\frac{N_s - N_c}{-N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$ → $N_c = \frac{N_s}{1 + T_r/T_s}$ | **Reduction drive** (low gear). |
| 3 | **Sun fixed** ($N_s = 0$) | $\frac{-N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$ → $N_c = \frac{N_r \cdot T_r}{T_r + T_s}$ | **Overdrive** (speed increase). |
| 4 | **Carrier fixed** ($N_c = 0$) | $\frac{N_s}{N_r} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$ | Becomes an **ordinary gear train** with reversal. |
| 5 | **Two members locked** | All three rotate as a single unit (1:1 direct drive). | **High gear / direct drive.** |
| 6 | **Tooth constraint** | $T_r = T_s + 2T_p$ | Geometric condition for assembly. |
| 7 | **Speed ratio (Low gear)** | $i = \frac{N_s}{N_c} = 1 + \frac{T_r}{T_s}$ | Ford T-Model in low: $i = 1 + 4 = 5$ (typical). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is used in **automatic transmissions (AT), CVTs, helicopter gearboxes, wind turbine gearboxes, and electric vehicle reducers**. Every modern automatic gearbox (e.g., Toyota, ZF, Aisin) uses 4 to 8 such planetary sets in series (Ravigneaux, Simpson, Lepelletier configurations).

## 2.4 Methods to Solve Planetary Gear Train Problems (KTU Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to know the following **three methods**:

1. **Kinematic (Tabular / Willis) Method** — Most common; uses the Willis equation directly.
2. **Algebraic Method** — Treats epicyclic motion as compound of rotation + revolution.
3. **Vector / Relative Velocity Method** — Uses velocity diagrams (rarely used in KTU but good for understanding).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Tabular (Willis) Method** is the **default expected method** in KTU university exams. Always show the table clearly with three rows: Actual speeds, Arm-fixed (relative) speeds, and the fixed-axis gear train ratio.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Willis Equation

We consider a simple planetary gear train with **Sun (S)**, **Planet (P)** as idler, **Ring (R)**, and **Carrier (C)**.

### Step 1 — The Concept of "Subtracting the Carrier Speed"

Let $N_s$, $N_p$, $N_r$, and $N_c$ be the absolute speeds of sun, planet, ring, and carrier respectively.

To convert this epicyclic system into an equivalent simple fixed-axis gear train, **add a speed of $-N_c$ to every member**. This effectively **freezes the carrier** in space.

### Step 2 — Compute Relative Speeds

$$\text{Relative speed of Sun} = N_s - N_c$$
$$\text{Relative speed of Planet} = N_p - N_c$$
$$\text{Relative speed of Ring} = N_r - N_c$$

Since the carrier is now stationary, the planet gear acts as a **simple idler** between sun and ring.

### Step 3 — Apply the Idler Gear Relation

For a simple train Sun → Planet (idler) → Ring:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_p - N_c} = -\frac{T_p}{T_s} \quad \text{(external mesh)}$$

$$\frac{N_p - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = +\frac{T_p}{T_r} \quad \text{(internal mesh — same direction)}$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate the Planet Speed $N_p - N_c$

Multiplying the two equations above:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_p - N_c} \times \frac{N_p - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = \left(-\frac{T_p}{T_s}\right) \times \left(+\frac{T_p}{T_r}\right)$$

The planet terms cancel out:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_p \cdot T_p}{T_s \cdot T_r} = -\frac{T_p^2}{T_s \cdot T_r}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Geometric Tooth Constraint

For the ring gear to be a proper annular gear meshing externally with the planets:

$$T_r = T_s + 2T_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_p = \frac{T_r - T_s}{2}$$

Substituting $T_p$:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{\left(\frac{T_r - T_s}{2}\right)^2}{T_s \cdot T_r} = -\frac{(T_r - T_s)^2}{4 T_s T_r}$$

**However**, the **standard textbook form** (and the one expected in KTU) is the simplified form obtained by noting that the planet is an **idler** and the **direction reversal** between sun and ring is captured by the single negative sign:

$$\boxed{\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This final, simple form is obtained by recognizing that the magnitude ratio of sun-to-ring rotations in the carrier-fixed frame equals the inverse of their tooth ratio (with a sign flip for direction reversal across the idler). Most KTU textbooks (and the official answer key) use **this simplified form directly.**

## 3.2 Worked Example: Ford T-Model Speed Ratio (Low Gear)

**Given:** $T_s = 18$, $T_p = 18$, $T_r = 72$, ring gear **fixed** (low gear), input to sun gear.

**Step 1:** Apply Willis equation with $N_r = 0$:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{0 - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s} = -\frac{72}{18} = -4$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{-N_c} = -4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s - N_c = 4N_c$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $N_c$:

$$N_s = 5N_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N_c}{N_s} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

**Step 4:** Speed ratio in low gear:

$$\text{Speed Ratio } i = \frac{N_s}{N_c} = 5 : 1 \text{ (reduction)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Ford T-Model** (typical service manuals), the actual ratio is closer to **3.66:1** because of additional reduction in the reverse gear set and the differential gear. For exam purposes, the **theoretical ratio using tooth count is 4:1** (if $T_r/T_s = 4$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each "Held Member" Affects the Train

| Held Member | Driven Member | Output Member | Function | Speed Ratio |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|----------|-------------|
| **Ring (R)** | Sun (S) | Carrier (C) | **Reduction (Low gear)** | $N_s/N_c = 1 + T_r/T_s$ |
| **Sun (S)** | Ring (R) | Carrier (C) | **Overdrive** | $N_r/N_c = 1 + T_s/T_r$ |
| **Carrier (C)** | Sun (S) | Ring (R) | **Reverse / speed-up** | $N_s/N_r = -T_r/T_s$ |
| **None locked (all free)** | — | — | No useful output (waste) | — |
| **Any two members locked** | One | The other | **Direct drive (1:1)** | $i = 1$ |
| **Ring + Sun locked** | Carrier | Carrier | Cannot transmit power | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ford T-Model Transmission

This diagram maps the functional flow of power and control in the Ford T-Model epicyclic gearbox.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Engine[/ ENGINE FLYWHEEL /] --> InputShaft[Input Shaft Connects to Sun Gear]
    SunGearNode[Sun Gear T s equals 18 teeth]
    PlanetGearNode[Planet Gears 3 nos T p equals 18 teeth]
    RingGearNode[Ring Gear T r equals 72 teeth]
    CarrierNode[Planet Carrier Output to Propshaft]
    LowBand[Low Band Friction Brake on Ring]
    HighClutch[High Clutch Multiplate Locks Sun and Carrier]
    ReverseLever[Reverse Lever Engages Reverse Idler]
    OutputPropshaft[/ OUTPUT TO REAR AXLE /]

    InputShaft --> SunGearNode
    SunGearNode -. external mesh .-> PlanetGearNode
    PlanetGearNode -. internal mesh .-> RingGearNode
    PlanetGearNode --> CarrierNode
    CarrierNode --> OutputPropshaft

    LowBand -. controls .-> RingGearNode
    HighClutch -. locks .-> SunGearNode
    HighClutch -. locks .-> CarrierNode
    ReverseLever -. engages .-> OutputPropshaft

    classDef input fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef gear fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#003566,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#FFB3C1,stroke:#C9184A,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000

    class Engine,InputShaft input
    class SunGearNode,PlanetGearNode,RingGearNode,CarrierNode gear
    class LowBand,HighClutch,ReverseLever control
    class OutputPropshaft output
```

## 4.2 Kinematic (Tabular) Solution — Method Visualized

| Step | Operation | Sun (S) | Planet (P) | Ring (R) | Carrier (C) |
|------|-----------|---------|------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Actual speeds** (unknown) | $N_s$ | $N_p$ | $N_r$ | $N_c$ |
| 2 | **Subtract carrier speed** ($x = -N_c$) | $N_s - N_c$ | $N_p - N_c$ | $N_r - N_c$ | 0 |
| 3 | **Apply simple gear ratio** (with idler) | — | — | — | — |

**Reading the table:**
- Row 1 has the unknown absolute speeds.
- Row 2 represents the carrier-fixed (relative) frame.
- In Row 2, the gear train behaves like an ordinary fixed-axis train, so:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$$

## 4.3 Power Flow Diagram for the Three Operating Modes

| Mode | Power Path | Held Member | Effective Ratio |
|------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Neutral** | No path (low band OFF, high clutch OFF) | None | — |
| **LOW** | Engine → Sun → Planets → Carrier → Propshaft | **Ring** (low band tight) | $i = 5:1$ reduction |
| **HIGH** | Engine → Sun = Carrier → Propshaft (locked together) | None (high clutch locks sun + carrier) | $i = 1:1$ direct |
| **REVERSE** | Engine → Sun → reverse idler → output shaft | Sun held via reverse band | Reversal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HIGH = Direct Drive in Ford T-Model?**
> When the high clutch engages, it **mechanically locks the sun gear and planet carrier together** (and also to the input shaft). Since ring gear is free to rotate, the entire unit rotates as one — this is the most efficient mode (1:1) and is used for cruising.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define an epicyclic gear train. List any four advantages of using it in automobile transmissions.**

**Model Answer:**

An epicyclic gear train is a compound gear system in which one or more gears (called planet gears) rotate about their own axes **and** also revolve around a central sun gear, all carried by a common arm called the planet carrier.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Coaxial input-output** — both shafts lie on the same axis, ideal for inline vehicle layout.
2. **Compact and lightweight** — high torque density (up to 3× smaller than parallel shaft gears).
3. **High efficiency** — typically 97–99% per meshing stage.
4. **Multiple ratios** without engaging/disengaging gears — done by simply braking different members (smooth shifts, ideal for automatic transmissions).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Explain the principle of operation of the Ford T-Model epicyclic gearbox. State the role of the friction band and multi-plate clutch.**

**Model Answer:**

The Ford T-Model gearbox uses a **simple planetary gear set** (1 sun, 3 planets, 1 ring, 1 carrier). The engine drives the **sun gear**; the **planet carrier** delivers power to the propshaft.

- **Friction Band (Low gear):** Wraps around the outside of the **ring gear drum**. When tightened, it **holds the ring gear stationary**, forcing the planets to "walk" around the sun and rotate the carrier slowly — this gives the **reduction ratio** (typically 4:1 or 5:1).

- **Multi-plate Clutch (High gear):** When engaged, it **locks the sun gear, planet carrier, and input shaft together** as one rigid unit. The whole assembly rotates as a single body at engine speed — this is the **direct drive (1:1)** used for normal cruising.

- **Reverse:** A separate **reverse idler gear** is engaged, which reverses the direction of rotation at the output.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### (a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working** of the Ford Model T epicyclic gearbox. Explain the function of the high-speed clutch and the low-speed band. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
The Ford T gearbox has a single simple planetary gear set housed in a cast-iron casing bolted to the engine flywheel housing. The assembly consists of:
- A **sun gear** on the input shaft (driven by the engine).
- **Three planet gears** mounted on pins carried by the **planet carrier**.
- A **ring gear (annulus)** with internal teeth, surrounded by an external drum.
- A **low-speed friction band** wrapped around the ring gear drum.
- A **multi-plate high-speed clutch** that can lock the sun gear to the planet carrier.
- A **reverse idler gear** mounted on a separate shaft engaged via the reverse lever.
- The **planet carrier** is splined to the output (propshaft).

**Working:**

**LOW GEAR:** The high-speed clutch is disengaged. The driver tightens the **friction band**, which holds the **ring gear stationary**. Engine torque enters via the **sun gear**, spins the planets on their own axes, and because the ring is fixed, the planets are forced to revolve around the sun. This revolves the **planet carrier** slowly. The reduction ratio is:

$$i_{low} = \frac{N_s}{N_c} = 1 + \frac{T_r}{T_s} = 1 + \frac{72}{18} = 5$$

So engine speed is reduced **5:1** at the propshaft — used for starting, hill climbing, and bad roads.

**HIGH GEAR:** The low band is released. The driver engages the **multi-plate clutch**, which rigidly locks the **sun gear, planet carrier, and input shaft** into a single rotating assembly. The whole unit (sun + planets + carrier) spins as one — giving a **1:1 direct drive**. This is the normal cruising mode with the highest efficiency.

**REVERSE:** The reverse lever slides the reverse idler into mesh, reversing the output direction.

---

#### (b) Using the **tabular (Willis) method**, derive the general kinematic equation for a simple epicyclic gear train. Hence determine the speed ratio of a planetary gearbox with $T_s = 20$, $T_p = 25$, $T_r = 70$ when the **ring gear is held fixed** and the **sun gear is the input**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabular Method:**

| Step | Sun (S) | Planet (P) | Ring (R) | Carrier (C) |
|------|---------|------------|----------|-------------|
| 1. Actual speeds | $N_s$ | $N_p$ | $N_r$ | $N_c$ |
| 2. Subtract $-N_c$ from all | $N_s - N_c$ | $N_p - N_c$ | $N_r - N_c$ | 0 |
| 3. Gear train ratio (with planet as idler) | — | — | — | — |

**Step 2 — Apply Simple Train Relation:**

In the carrier-fixed frame, the planet gear is a simple idler:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Application:**

Given: $T_s = 20$, $T_p = 25$, $T_r = 70$, $N_r = 0$ (ring fixed).

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{0 - N_c} = -\frac{70}{20} = -3.5$$

$$N_s - N_c = 3.5 \, N_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s = 4.5 \, N_c$$

$$\boxed{\text{Speed ratio } i = \frac{N_s}{N_c} = 4.5 : 1 \text{ (reduction)}}$$

Verification: Tooth constraint $T_r = T_s + 2T_p = 20 + 2(25) = 70$ ✓

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### (a) Discuss the **three methods** of finding the speed ratio of an epicyclic gear train. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Kinematic (Tabular / Willis) Method:**
- **Procedure:** Add $-N_c$ to all member speeds to "freeze" the carrier, then apply the simple gear train ratio (with planet as idler) to derive:

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$$

- **Advantage:** Most systematic; works for any complexity (multiple planets, compound trains).
- **Limitation:** Requires knowing which member is fixed/released.

**2. Algebraic (Relative Motion) Method:**
- **Procedure:** Treat the planet motion as a sum of (a) rotation about its own axis and (b) revolution with the carrier. Write two simultaneous equations for the gear mesh velocities at the contact points, then solve.
- **Advantage:** Provides direct physical insight into rotation vs. revolution.
- **Limitation:** Becomes cumbersome for compound planetary trains (e.g., Ravigneaux).

**3. Vector (Instantaneous Centre / Relative Velocity) Method:**
- **Procedure:** Plot a velocity diagram using the relative sliding velocities at the pitch points. Locate instantaneous centres using Kennedy's theorem.
- **Advantage:** Graphical clarity; visual for students.
- **Limitation:** Lower accuracy; difficult for multi-stage trains.

---

#### (b) In a planetary gear train, the sun gear has **20 teeth**, planet gear has **30 teeth**, and the ring gear has **80 teeth**. Determine the speed ratio for the following three cases:
**(i)** Ring gear is fixed
**(ii)** Sun gear is fixed
**(iii)** Carrier is fixed **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_s = 20$, $T_p = 30$, $T_r = 80$. Check: $T_r = T_s + 2T_p = 20 + 60 = 80$ ✓
Willis equation: $\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s} = -\frac{80}{20} = -4$

---

**Case (i): Ring gear fixed → $N_r = 0$**

$$\frac{N_s - N_c}{-N_c} = -4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s - N_c = 4N_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s = 5N_c$$

$$\boxed{i_1 = \frac{N_s}{N_c} = 5 : 1 \text{ (reduction)}}$$

---

**Case (ii): Sun gear fixed → $N_s = 0$**

$$\frac{0 - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -N_c = -4N_r + 4N_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3N_c = 4N_r$$

$$\frac{N_c}{N_r} = \frac{4}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{i_2 = \frac{N_r}{N_c} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 : 1 \text{ (overdrive)}}$$

---

**Case (iii): Carrier fixed → $N_c = 0$**

$$\frac{N_s}{N_r} = -4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{i_3 = \frac{N_s}{N_r} = -4 : 1 \text{ (reversal, behaves as fixed-axis train)}}$$

The negative sign indicates the output reverses direction relative to input.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the Willis equation. It is NOT $-\frac{T_s}{T_r}$. Always use $\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$.
> 2. **Mixing up** which member is held vs. which is input vs. which is output. Read the question carefully: "ring fixed, sun input" ≠ "ring input, sun fixed."
> 3. **Skipping the tooth constraint check** ($T_r = T_s + 2T_p$). KTU examiners often give inconsistent tooth numbers as a trick — if the constraint fails, the gear train cannot be assembled.
> 4. **Not specifying the sign** in the speed ratio answer. KTU expects you to state whether the output is in the same or opposite direction as the input.
> 5. **In the Ford T-Model question**, confusing "low gear" with "high gear" — Low = ring fixed (reduction); High = clutch locks sun + carrier (direct drive).
> 6. **Marks lost for missing the diagram** — A neat labeled sketch of the Ford T-Model gearbox is **mandatory** for full marks in Part B (typically 2 marks are reserved for the diagram).

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Epicyclic gear train** = compound gear system with planet gears that **rotate AND revolve** simultaneously.
- ✅ **Four members:** Sun, Planet, Ring (Annulus), Carrier (Arm).
- ✅ **Tooth constraint (assembly condition):** $T_r = T_s + 2T_p$.
- ✅ **Willis Equation (the master formula):** $\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$ — must be memorized.
- ✅ **Ford T-Model** uses **ONE** simple planetary set with $T_r / T_s = 4$ → Low gear ratio = **5:1**.
- ✅ **Low gear** = friction band tightens on **ring gear** → reduction.
- ✅ **High gear** = multi-plate clutch locks **sun + carrier** → 1:1 direct drive.
- ✅ **Reverse** = separate reverse idler engaged.
- ✅ **Tabular (Willis) method** is the **KTU-expected** solving method — always show the 3-row table.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Negative ratio means **direction reversal**; positive means **same direction**.
- ✅ **Two members locked together** → entire gear set rotates as a rigid body (direct drive, 1:1).
- ✅ **When carrier is held fixed**, the planetary set behaves as an **ordinary fixed-axis gear train** with the planet acting as a simple idler.
- ✅ Modern applications: **Automatic transmissions (Toyota, Honda), CVTs, EV reducers, helicopter gearboxes, robotics, wind turbines.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wilson Gear Box
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wilson Gear Box

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wilson Gear Box** is a constant-mesh, synchromesh automotive transmission that uses **epicyclic (planetary) gear trains** to obtain all forward speed ratios and an auxiliary sliding spur gear pair to obtain the reverse gear ratio. Designed by David E. Wilson, it provides **four forward speeds and one reverse speed** with a single countershaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A Wilson gear box is a synchromesh constant-mesh gear box in which the **forward gears are obtained through three epicyclic gear trains** mounted coaxially on the main shaft and countershaft, while **reverse gear is obtained through a simple sliding spur gear mesh**. It is classified as a *pre-selective epicyclic gear box* because the gear to be engaged is pre-selected and engaged synchronously through cone clutches.

## 1.2 Constructional Architecture

The Wilson gear box consists of the following main sub-assemblies:

1. **Main (output) shaft** — carries three internally-toothed **annulus (ring) gears**, the **synchromesh unit**, and the sliding dog clutch.
2. **Countershaft (layshaft)** — carries the three mating **sun gears**, driven by the input shaft through a constant-mesh pair.
3. **Common planet carrier** — carries all planet pinions of the three epicyclic trains; can be locked to the gearbox casing through a **brake band**.
4. **Reverse idler shaft** — carries an idler pinion for the sliding-mesh reverse gear.
5. **Synchromesh cones** — mounted between adjacent annulus gears on the main shaft for smooth, clash-free gear engagement.
6. **Selector forks** — operate the synchromesh sleeve and the reverse sliding gear.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Multi-Speed Planetary Coffee Grinder**
>
> Imagine a coffee grinder where instead of changing grinding discs by hand (sliding mesh), you have **three pre-arranged gear sets on a single spindle** (epicyclic), each capable of a different grind. You just **dial in** which set you want using a smooth clutch ring (synchromesh). This is exactly what the Wilson gearbox does — instead of sliding gears into mesh (which causes grinding/clash), the desired gear ratio is *already meshed* and waiting to be coupled through a friction synchroniser.
>
> The **reverse** gear, however, is used so infrequently that Wilson left it as a simple spur sliding-mesh — cheaper, simpler, and adequate for occasional use.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard no. of forward speeds:** **4**
> - **Standard no. of reverse speeds:** **1**
> - **Typical 1st-gear ratio range:** **3.0 – 4.0 : 1**
> - **Typical 4th-gear (top) ratio:** **1 : 1 (direct)**
> - **Module of gears (typical automotive):** **2.5 – 4 mm**
> - **Helix angle (typical):** **20° – 30°**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working Principle

The Wilson gearbox exploits the **inherent speed-reduction property of an epicyclic train** when its planet carrier is held stationary:

1. The **input shaft** drives the **countershaft** through a primary constant-mesh pinion.
2. The countershaft carries the **three sun gears** (S₁, S₂, S₃), one for each forward gear ratio.
3. Each sun gear meshes with its own set of **planet pinions** carried on the **common planet carrier**.
4. The planet pinions mesh with the **annulus gears** (R₁, R₂, R₃) mounted on the **main shaft**.
5. The **planet carrier is locked to the casing** by a brake band — this is the *epicyclic action trigger*.
6. With the carrier fixed, when the sun (input) rotates, the planets "walk" along the fixed carrier and force the annulus (output) to rotate at a **reduced speed**.
7. The **synchromesh unit** on the main shaft selects which annulus to couple to the main shaft, thereby selecting the gear ratio.

## 2.2 Why Epicyclic Gearing for Forward Gears?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Advantages of Epicyclic Trains in Wilson GB:**
>
> | Sl. No. | Advantage | Engineering Significance |
> |---------|-----------|-------------------------|
> | 1 | **Coaxial mounting** | All three gear trains share the same axis → extremely compact design |
> | 2 | **Constant mesh** | Gears never disengage → silent operation, no tooth-clash wear |
> | 3 | **High torque capacity** | Multiple planet pinions share the load → smaller gears carry more power |

## 2.3 Why Sliding Spur Gear for Reverse?

Reverse gear is engaged only briefly during parking/manoeuvring. Designing it as epicyclic would add cost and complexity for no real benefit. Hence, a **simple external sliding spur mesh** is used — economical and adequate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formulae for Wilson Gear Box and Epicyclic Trains**

| Equation / Term | Symbolic Form | Description | Units |
|-----------------|---------------|-------------|-------|
| Epicyclic Speed Ratio (carrier fixed) | $\dfrac{N_{annulus}}{N_{sun}} = \dfrac{T_{sun}}{T_{sun} + T_{ring}}$ | Output speed of annulus with respect to input sun | dimensionless |
| Epicyclic Ratio (simplified) | $i = \dfrac{T_{sun}}{T_{sun} + T_{ring}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{T_{ring}}{T_{sun}}}$ | Gear reduction ratio | dimensionless |
| Train Value (Tab) | $\text{Tab} = \dfrac{T_{ring}}{T_{sun}}$ | Ratio of teeth on ring to sun | dimensionless |
| Alternative form | $i = \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{Tab}}$ | Using Tab notation | dimensionless |
| Gear efficiency (epicyolic) | $\eta_{ep} \approx 0.97 - 0.98$ per stage | Power loss per epicyclic stage | dimensionless |
| Overall ratio | $i_{total} = i_{primary} \times i_{epicyclic}$ | Combined countershaft × epicyclic | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **In the formula table above, all absolute-value or ratio separators use LaTeX \vert or fraction slash — never the bare markdown pipe character — to preserve table syntax integrity.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Wilson Gear Boxes Are/Have Been Used:**
> - **Rover (UK) cars** — P4 series, P6 series (1960s)
> - **Standard Motor Company** — Standard 8, 10, Pennant models
> - **Triumph (UK)** — Herald, Vitesse, Spitfire
> - **Ford (UK)** — early Consul/Zephyr/Zodiac models
> - **Studebaker (USA)** — Champion, Commander
> - **Mahindra (India)** — early Jeep CJ variants
>
> **Modern relevance:** The Wilson gearbox's design philosophy (epicyclic forward + sliding reverse) is conceptually the **progenitor of modern automatic transmissions** (e.g., the Chrysler TorqueFlite and Ford C4 use multi-element epicyclic trains derived from this principle).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Epicyclic Speed Ratio (Wilson Gear Box Core Equation)

### Step 1 — Define the System

Consider a single epicyclic train of the Wilson gear box:

- Sun gear with $T_S$ teeth — driven by the countershaft at angular velocity $\omega_S$
- Planet gear with $T_P$ teeth — idler on common planet carrier
- Ring (annulus) gear with $T_R$ teeth — connected to the main shaft at angular velocity $\omega_R$
- Common planet carrier — **held stationary** at $\omega_C = 0$

### Step 2 — Apply Relative Velocity Method (Willis Equation)

For an epicyclic train, the **Willis equation** states:

$$
\frac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign?**
> In an internal meshing (sun-planet) followed by external meshing (planet-ring), the sun and ring rotate in **opposite senses** relative to the carrier — hence the negative sign.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Boundary Condition

Since the planet carrier is **held fixed** by the brake band:

$$
\omega_C = 0
$$

Substituting into Willis equation:

$$
\frac{\omega_S - 0}{\omega_R - 0} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}
$$

$$
\frac{\omega_S}{\omega_R} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Output-to-Input Ratio

Taking magnitudes (we are interested in speed magnitudes only):

$$
\frac{\omega_R}{\omega_S} = \frac{T_S}{T_R}
$$

However, this gives the ratio **without** considering that the planets walk around the *fixed* sun. The corrected relation (with carrier fixed, sun as input, ring as output) is obtained by inverting the conventional planetary relation:

$$
\frac{\omega_R}{\omega_S} = \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R}
$$

### Step 5 — Show the Algebraic Transition Clearly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } i_1 &= \frac{\omega_R}{\omega_S} \\[4pt]
i_1 &= \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R} \quad \text{(Wilson reduction ratio, carrier fixed)} \\[4pt]
i_1 &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{T_R}{T_S}} \quad \text{(introducing the Tab ratio)} \\[4pt]
\text{Define } \text{Tab} &= \frac{T_R}{T_S} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad i_1 &= \frac{1}{1 + \text{Tab}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - As $T_R \rightarrow 0$ (no ring gear teeth), $i_1 \rightarrow 1$ → annulus rotates at the same speed as sun.
> - As $T_R \rightarrow \infty$ (infinitely large ring), $i_1 \rightarrow 0$ → annulus nearly stationary.
> - For typical automotive $T_R \approx 2.5 \, T_S$ to $3.5 \, T_S$, the ratio $i_1$ lies between **0.22 and 0.29**, giving a reduction of **3.5:1 to 4.5:1** — perfect for first gear.

### Step 6 — Combine with Primary Countershaft Drive

The countershaft itself is driven by the input shaft through a primary pinion. If $i_p$ is the primary ratio, the overall gear-box ratio is:

$$
i_{overall} = i_p \times i_1
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{overall} &= \frac{T_{driving}}{T_{driven}} \times \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R} \\[4pt]
            &= \frac{T_{driving} \cdot T_S}{T_{driven} \cdot (T_S + T_R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** In a Wilson gear box, the countershaft sun gear has 18 teeth and the corresponding annulus on the main shaft has 54 teeth. The planet carrier is held fixed by the brake band. Calculate the speed ratio between countershaft and main shaft.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_S &= 18 \\[4pt]
T_R &= 54 \\[4pt]
i_1 &= \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{18}{18 + 54} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{18}{72} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.25 \\[4pt]
\text{Speed ratio (main : countershaft)} &= \frac{1}{i_1} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The main shaft rotates at **one-quarter** the countershaft speed → a **4:1 reduction**, suitable for **1st gear**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wilson vs. Other Gearboxes

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Analysis Table for KTU Module 2**

| Feature | Wilson GB | Sliding Mesh GB | Constant Mesh GB | Epicyclic GB (Full) |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Forward gears | Epicyclic | Sliding spur | Constant spur | All epicyclic |
| Reverse gear | Sliding spur | Sliding spur | Sliding spur | Epicyclic |
| Synchromesh | Yes | No | Optional | Yes |
| Number of shafts | 2 + 1 idler | 2 | 2 + 1 idler | Compact coaxial |
| Compactness | High | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Noise level | Low | High | Low | Very Low |
| Cost | Medium-High | Low | Medium | High |
| Maintenance | Skilled | Simple | Medium | Skilled |
| Used in | Passenger cars | Vintage cars | Commercial vehicles | AT, racing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The figure below maps the **functional flow of power** through the Wilson Gear Box, identifying every major sub-assembly and its role.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Output / Input Shaft] --> B[Primary Pinion on Input Shaft]
    B --> C[Primary Gear on Countershaft]
    C --> D1[Countershaft Sun Gear 1 - T_S1]
    C --> D2[Countershaft Sun Gear 2 - T_S2]
    C --> D3[Countershaft Sun Gear 3 - T_S3]
    D1 --> E1[Epicyclic Train 1 - Planet Pinions]
    D2 --> E2[Epicyclic Train 2 - Planet Pinions]
    D3 --> E3[Epicyclic Train 3 - Planet Pinions]
    E1 --> F1[Annulus 1 on Main Shaft - T_R1]
    E2 --> F2[Annulus 2 on Main Shaft - T_R2]
    E3 --> F3[Annulus 3 on Main Shaft - T_R3]
    PC[Common Planet Carrier] --> E1
    PC --> E2
    PC --> E3
    BB[Brake Band - Locks Carrier to Casing] --> PC
    F1 --> G[Synchromesh Sleeve on Main Shaft]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Main Output Shaft to Propeller Shaft]
    RS[Reverse Sliding Spur Gear] --> RI[Reverse Idler Gear]
    RI --> H
```

## 4.2 Power-Flow Summary for Each Gear (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **How Each Forward Gear is Engaged in the Wilson Gear Box**

| Gear | Brake Band | Synchromesh Couples | Power Path | Ratio Range |
|------|------------|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1st | Applied (carrier fixed) | Main shaft ↔ Annulus 1 | Input → Countershaft → Sun 1 → Planets → Annulus 1 → Main | 3.5 : 1 to 4.0 : 1 |
| 2nd | Applied (carrier fixed) | Main shaft ↔ Annulus 2 | Input → Countershaft → Sun 2 → Planets → Annulus 2 → Main | 2.0 : 1 to 2.5 : 1 |
| 3rd | Applied (carrier fixed) | Main shaft ↔ Annulus 3 | Input → Countershaft → Sun 3 → Planets → Annulus 3 → Main | 1.3 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 |
| 4th (Top) | Released (carrier free) | Direct dog clutch engagement | Input → Main shaft (1:1) | 1 : 1 |
| Reverse | — | Sliding spur engaged | Input → Sliding gear → Idler → Main shaft | 3.5 : 1 to 4.5 : 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> In **4th gear**, the brake band is **released**, allowing the planet carrier to **rotate freely** as a unit with the sun gear and annulus (locked together as a solid block) — the entire epicyclic train "free-wheels" at input speed. This is equivalent to a direct drive at **1:1 ratio** with zero power loss in the gear train.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Wilson gear box.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Construction:** The Wilson gear box consists of a main shaft carrying three annulus gears and a synchromesh unit, a countershaft carrying three sun gears, a common planet carrier with planet pinions held by a brake band, and a reverse idler shaft.
- **(1 mark)** **Working — Forward:** With the brake band applied (carrier fixed), the sun (input) drives the planet pinions which walk along the carrier and rotate the annulus (output) at a reduced speed. The synchromesh sleeve selects which annulus couples to the main shaft, choosing 1st, 2nd, or 3rd gear.
- **(1 mark)** **Working — Reverse & 4th:** Reverse is obtained by sliding a spur gear into mesh with a reverse idler; 4th (top) gear is direct drive obtained by releasing the brake band and engaging the dog clutch, giving 1:1 ratio.

### Question 2
**Q:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *Why is reverse gear in a Wilson gear box obtained by sliding mesh and not by epicyclic gearing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Reverse gear is engaged only briefly (parking, manoeuvring), so it does not require the smoothness or compactness of epicyclic construction.
- **(1 mark)** Using epicyclic gearing for reverse would add **unnecessary cost, weight, and complexity** (extra sun, planet set, annulus, brake/clutch hardware).
- **(1 mark)** A simple sliding spur mesh through an idler is **economically sufficient and mechanically adequate** for the low-duty reverse function.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` *(a)* Explain with a sketch the **constructional features** of a Wilson gear box and the role of each component. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the **speed ratio equation** for an epicyclic train with the planet carrier held stationary, and calculate the **overall gear reduction** if the countershaft sun has 20 teeth, the annulus has 60 teeth, and the primary pinion pair has a ratio of 1:1.4. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Constructional Features (7 marks)**

> **(1 mark each sub-point, total 7)**

1. **Input Shaft:** Receives power from the clutch; carries the primary driving pinion.
2. **Countershaft:** Driven by the primary pinion; carries three sun gears (one per forward gear pair) and a sliding spur gear for reverse.
3. **Main Shaft:** The output shaft; carries three annulus (ring) gears and the synchromesh unit (cones + sliding sleeve).
4. **Common Planet Carrier:** Holds the planet pinions of all three epicyclic trains; can be locked to the casing by a **brake band**.
5. **Synchromesh Unit:** Friction cones + sleeve to pre-synchronise and engage the selected annulus with the main shaft; ensures clash-free, smooth gear changes.
6. **Brake Band & Servo:** External contracting/expanding band operated by the gear lever linkage; locks/unlocks the planet carrier.
7. **Reverse Idler Shaft:** Carries an idler pinion that reverses the direction of rotation when the sliding spur gear engages it.

**Part (b) — Speed Ratio Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Willis Equation:} \quad \frac{\omega_S - \omega_C}{\omega_R - \omega_C} &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S} \\[6pt]
\text{With carrier fixed: } \omega_C &= 0 \\[6pt]
\therefore \frac{\omega_S}{\omega_R} &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S} \\[6pt]
\text{Taking magnitudes:} \quad \frac{\omega_R}{\omega_S} &= \frac{T_S}{T_R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Correction (1 mark):** With carrier fixed and sun as input, the correct Wilson relation is:
>
> $$\frac{\omega_R}{\omega_S} = \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_S &= 20, \quad T_R = 60, \quad i_p = \frac{1}{1.4} \\[4pt]
i_{epicyclic} &= \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R} = \frac{20}{20 + 60} = \frac{20}{80} = 0.25 \\[4pt]
i_{overall} &= i_p \times i_{epicyclic} = \frac{1}{1.4} \times 0.25 = 0.1786 \\[4pt]
\text{Speed reduction} &= \frac{1}{0.1786} \approx 5.6 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(a)* Compare the **Wilson gear box** with a **simple sliding-mesh gear box** in terms of construction, operation, advantages, and limitations. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With the help of a **schematic diagram**, explain how **four forward speeds and reverse** are obtained in a Wilson gear box, and state the function of the **brake band** in detail. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

> **(½ mark each for 14 comparison points, totalling 7)**

| Aspect | Wilson Gear Box | Sliding-Mesh Gear Box |
|--------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Forward gear engagement | Epicyclic (constant mesh) + synchromesh | Sliding spur gears into/out of mesh |
| Reverse gear engagement | Sliding spur through idler | Sliding spur through idler |
| Number of shafts | 2 (main + countershaft) + 1 idler | 2 (main + countershaft) + 1 idler |
| Gear clash on shifting | None (synchromesh) | Yes (dog-clash possible) |
| Noise | Quiet | Noisy during shifting |
| Wear on gear teeth | Minimal | High (sliding contact wear) |
| Synchroniser cones | Yes | No |
| Compactness | High (coaxial epicyclic) | Lower (larger axial length) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Maintenance | Skilled labour needed | Easy |
| Power transmission efficiency | High (~98%) | High (~98%) but rougher |
| Typical applications | Passenger cars (Rover, Triumph) | Vintage/light commercial vehicles |
| Shift effort | Low | High |
| Driver comfort | High | Low |

**Part (b) — Schematic + Brake Band Function (7 marks)**

**Schematic Description (3 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** above — it depicts the complete Wilson GB architecture with all sub-assemblies and power paths.

**Speed Selection Logic (2 marks):**

- **1st gear:** Brake band **applied** (carrier fixed); synchromesh couples Annulus 1 to main shaft.
- **2nd gear:** Brake band **applied**; synchromesh couples Annulus 2 to main shaft.
- **3rd gear:** Brake band **applied**; synchromesh couples Annulus 3 to main shaft.
- **4th gear:** Brake band **released** (carrier free-wheels); direct dog clutch engaged → 1:1.
- **Reverse:** Brake band **released**; sliding spur gear on countershaft engages the reverse idler.

**Brake Band Function (2 marks):**

The brake band is a **steel friction band lined with organic friction material** that contracts around a drum integral with the planet carrier. When applied (via the gear-shift servo), it **locks the planet carrier to the gearbox casing**, making it a *stationary reaction member*. This is the **essential condition** that activates the epicyclic reduction — without a fixed reaction member, the gear train would simply free-wheel and transmit no torque.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Wilson Gear Box Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in Willis equation** — students often write $\omega_S / \omega_R = T_R / T_S$ without the minus sign, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $\omega_R / \omega_S = T_S / T_R$** instead of the correct $T_S / (T_S + T_R)$ when the carrier is held fixed — this is the most common error and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing "carrier fixed" with "ring fixed"** — these give different ratios; always read the question carefully.
> 4. **Omitting the role of the brake band** — many students explain forward gears but forget to state *why* the carrier must be locked. Mentioning "reaction member" or "stationary element" is mandatory.
> 5. **Skipping the synchromesh explanation** — even in construction questions, you must briefly explain how clash-free engagement is achieved.
> 6. **Not labelling the diagram** — unlabelled sketches fetch 0 marks even if the drawing is correct. Label **every** gear, shaft, and the brake band.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wilson Gear Box (Module 2)**
>
> - **Inventor:** David E. Wilson (UK, 1930s).
> - **Type:** Constant-mesh synchromesh with epicyclic forward gears and sliding-mesh reverse.
> - **Speeds:** 4 forward + 1 reverse.
> - **Shafts:** Main shaft (output) + Countershaft (input) + Reverse idler shaft.
> - **Epicyclic trains:** 3 in number, sharing a **common planet carrier**.
> - **Sun gears:** Mounted on countershaft (driven by input).
> - **Ring (annulus) gears:** Mounted on main shaft (drive output).
> - **Planet carrier:** Can be locked by **brake band** (essential for epicyclic action).
> - **Synchromesh:** Uses friction cones + sliding sleeve for clash-free engagement.
> - **Core Formula:** $i_{epicyclic} = T_S / (T_S + T_R) = 1 / (1 + \text{Tab})$.
> - **4th gear (top):** Brake band **released**; entire epicyclic train free-wheels → **1:1 direct drive**.
> - **Reverse gear:** Sliding spur mesh through reverse idler; no epicyclic action.
> - **Willis equation:** $(\omega_S - \omega_C) / (\omega_R - \omega_C) = -T_R / T_S$.
> - **Typical first-gear ratio:** **3.5:1 to 4.0:1**.
> - **Typical top-gear ratio:** **1:1**.
> - **Efficiency per epicyclic stage:** **97–98%**.
> - **Used in:** Rover P4/P6, Triumph Herald/Spitfire, Standard Pennant, early Ford Consul, Studebaker Champion, Mahindra early Jeep CJ.
> - **Advantage over sliding-mesh GB:** Silent, no tooth-clash, compact, smooth synchromesh shifts.
> - **Limitation:** Higher manufacturing cost; reverse is still sliding-mesh (no synchromesh for reverse).
> - **Conceptual successor:** Modern automatic transmissions (Chrysler TorqueFlite, Ford C4) — multi-element epicyclic trains derived from Wilson's principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ELECTROMAGNETIC TRANSMISSION
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electromagnetic Transmission (EM Transmission)** in automotive engineering refers to a non-mechanical, friction-based or field-based torque transfer mechanism in which the driving and driven members are coupled through **controlled magnetic flux linkage** rather than through direct mechanical contact (gears) or hydraulic fluid shear (torque converters). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCAUT503 – Automotive Transmission*, electromagnetic transmission systems use the **force of attraction between an energized electromagnetic coil and a ferromagnetic armature**, or the **induced eddy currents** in a conductive rotor, to transmit rotational torque from the engine (prime mover) to the transmission input shaft.

The four major industrial/automotive classifications are:

1. **Electromagnetic (Solenoid-Actuated) Friction Clutches** – Single-plate and multi-plate dry clutches where engagement force is generated by an electromagnetic coil.
2. **Magnetic Powder (Ferrofluid) Clutches** – Use fine ferromagnetic particles suspended in a carrier fluid whose shear resistance increases under a magnetic field.
3. **Eddy-Current Clutches (Induction Couplings)** – Use Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction to generate torque without physical contact.
4. **Electromagnetic CVT (E-CVT)** and **Hybrid Synergy Drive Couplings** – Modern Toyota-style planetary e-CVT systems where a motor/generator (MG1, MG2) controls planetary gear ratios electro-magnetically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (Module 2):** Electromagnetic clutches are evaluated for *engagement principle, force/torque equations, time constant, heat dissipation, and slip control*. Memorize the **Bennet / Smythe equation for magnetic pull force** and the **torque-slip characteristic of eddy-current couplings**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain-English Explanation

Imagine you are holding two circular steel plates, one in each hand, separated by a 2 mm air gap. You cannot push torque from one plate to the other because they are not touching. Now, suppose you place a powerful **electromagnet** (a coil of copper wire) on one plate and connect it to a **DC battery** through a **rheostat (variable resistor)**. When you switch on the current, a magnetic field "leaps" across the air gap and pulls the second plate toward the first. If the second plate is rotating, the magnetic field will drag it along at almost the same speed as the first plate — just like an invisible, frictionless rubber band.

- **No mechanical wear** because there is no direct contact.
- **Torque transmission is proportional to the coil current** — like a volume knob for power.
- This is exactly how the **starter motor Bendix drive** engages, how **A/C compressor clutches** cycle, and how **hybrid vehicle MG1/MG2 motors** transmit power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world manifestation:** Every modern car's air-conditioning compressor uses a *single-plate electromagnetic clutch* controlled by the ECU. When you press the A/C button, the ECU sends current to the clutch coil; the magnetic field snaps the compressor pulley into contact with the rotating engine belt, and your cabin begins to cool.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Units Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $B$ | Magnetic flux density | **Tesla (T)** = **Weber/m² (Wb/m²)** |
| $\Phi$ | Magnetic flux | **Weber (Wb)** |
| $F_m$ | Magnetic pull / clutch engagement force | **Newton (N)** |
| $T$ | Transmitted torque | **Newton-metre (N·m)** |
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** |
| $\mu_r$ | Relative permeability of armature | **Dimensionless (typically 2000–6000 for soft steel)** |
| $A$ | Pole-face / armature cross-section area | **m²** |
| $I$ | Coil current | **Ampere (A)** |
| $N$ | Number of coil turns | **Turns** |
| $L$ | Coil inductance | **Henry (H)** |
| $R$ | Coil resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** |
| $\tau$ | Electrical time constant | **Second (s)** |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | **rad/s** |
| $s$ | Slip = $(N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | **Dimensionless** |
| $k$ | Coupling constant | **N·m / A** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of an Electromagnetic Clutch

An electromagnetic clutch is essentially a **controllable magnetic brake/coupling**. The torque-transfer path is:

$$\text{Driving member (rotor / flywheel)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Magnetic field (air gap)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Driven member (armature / pressure plate)}$$

The clutch consists of four fundamental sub-systems:

1. **Field member (rotor):** Carries the energizing coil. The coil is stationary relative to the housing but the outer pole faces rotate with the input shaft.
2. **Armature (driven plate):** A ferromagnetic disc (typically silicon steel) mounted on the output shaft via splines. Free to move axially but rotates with the shaft.
3. **Air gap:** The small axial gap (typically **0.3 – 1.0 mm**) between the rotor pole face and the armature. This gap determines the magnetic circuit reluctance and the **pull-in force**.
4. **Slip rings / brushes (or stationary brushless design):** Supplies DC excitation current to the rotating coil.

### 2.2 Operating Sequence (Engagement Cycle)

| Step | Event | Physical Phenomenon |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Coil de-energized | No flux → armature held away by **return springs** → **disengaged** |
| 2 | ECU applies DC voltage to coil | Current $I$ rises exponentially with time constant $\tau = L/R$ |
| 3 | Magnetic flux $\Phi$ builds in the iron circuit | Flux density $B$ in the air gap reaches threshold |
| 4 | Magnetic pull $F_m$ overcomes spring force | Armature is pulled axially against the friction lining |
| 5 | Frictional contact established | Torque transferred via $\mu N$ (Coulomb friction) |
| 6 | Steady state | Slip $\to 0$, locked transmission, current held constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the time constant matters:** The coil is an **RL circuit**. The current cannot rise instantaneously. KTU questions frequently test the **engagement delay** $t_{eng} \approx 3\tau$ (time to reach ~95% of rated current). For an automotive A/C clutch, $\tau$ is typically **15 – 50 ms**.

## 2.3 Magnetic Circuit & Magnetic Pull Force

The core of every electromagnetic clutch is a **simple magnetic circuit** containing an air gap. Using **Ampere's Law** and the **B-H characteristic of ferromagnetic materials**, the **magnetomotive force (MMF)** is:

$$\mathcal{F} = NI = \oint \mathbf{H} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = H_{iron}\,\ell_{iron} + H_{gap}\,\ell_{gap}$$

Because $\mu_r \gg 1$ for the iron path, almost all the MMF is dropped across the air gap:

$$NI \approx H_{gap}\,\ell_{gap} = \frac{B_{gap}}{\mu_0}\,\ell_{gap}$$

Solving for flux density $B$:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \, N I}{\ell_{gap}}$$

The **magnetic attraction force** on each pole face is derived from **Maxwell's stress tensor** (or, equivalently, the energy derivative $F = -dW/dx$):

$$\boxed{\,F_m = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2\,\ell_{gap}^{\,2}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Maxwell Magnetic Pull Equation** — the single most important formula in Module 2. The force is **proportional to the square of the current** and **inversely proportional to the square of the air-gap length**. Halving the air gap quadruples the pull force.

## 2.4 Torque Transmission in a Friction-Type EM Clutch

Once the armature is in contact, torque is transmitted by **kinetic/static friction**:

$$\boxed{\,T = \mu \, F_m \, r_{mean} \, n_{surfaces} = \mu \, F_m \, r_{mean} \, N_s\,}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (≈ **0.25–0.4** for organic linings, **0.4–0.6** for ceramic)
- $F_m$ = magnetic clamping force (N)
- $r_{mean}$ = mean effective radius of the friction surface (m)
- $N_s$ = number of friction surfaces (1 for single-plate, multiple for multi-plate)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_m = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Maxwell magnetic pull force | Engagement force calculation |
| 2 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{\ell_g}$ | Air-gap flux density | Magnetic circuit design |
| 3 | $T = \mu F_m r_m N_s$ | Friction torque (dry clutch) | Plate-type EM clutch |
| 4 | $\tau = L/R$ | RL electrical time constant | Engagement delay analysis |
| 5 | $I(t) = \dfrac{V}{R}\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$ | Coil current build-up | Clutch response time |
| 6 | $T_{eddy} = k\, s\, \omega_s$ | Eddy-current clutch torque | Smooth slip coupling |
| 7 | $P_{slip} = T \cdot \omega_{slip} = T (\omega_s - \omega_r)$ | Heat generated in slip | Thermal design |
| 8 | $V_{induced} = B\,\ell\, v$ | Motional EMF in eddy disc | Eddy-current principle |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in coil | Switching transients |
| 10 | $s = \dfrac{\omega_s - \omega_r}{\omega_s}$ | Slip ratio | All slip couplings |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When asked for *clutch torque*, students often confuse $F_m$ (axial pull) with $T$ (rotational torque). Always multiply $F_m$ by $\mu$, $r_{mean}$, and $N_s$ to convert the linear magnetic force into rotational torque.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive HVAC:** Electromagnetic clutch on A/C compressor pulley.
- **Engine cooling fans:** Viscous + electromagnetic fan clutches in trucks.
- **Industrial drives:** Soft-start couplings for pumps, conveyors, mixers.
- **Hybrid vehicles:** MG1/MG2 in Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) — an electromagnetic planetary e-CVT.
- **Electric vehicles:** Tesla and Lucid use a single-speed electromagnetic reduction drive.
- **Aerospace:** Electromagnetic brakes in landing gear and flap actuators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell Magnetic Pull Force

**Starting point:** Energy stored in the magnetic field of an air gap of volume $V = A \cdot \ell_g$ is:

$$W_{field} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot V = \frac{B^2 A \ell_g}{2\mu_0}$$

When the armature moves a tiny distance $dx$ *toward* the rotor, the gap decreases, so the stored field energy decreases. This energy is converted to mechanical work:

$$F_m \, dx = -dW_{field} = -\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{B^2 A x}{2\mu_0}\right) dx$$

Treating $B$ as approximately constant (constant-current assumption):

$$F_m = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{B^2 A x}{2\mu_0}\right) = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

**Substituting** $B = \mu_0 N I / \ell_g$:

$$F_m = \frac{A}{2\mu_0} \left(\frac{\mu_0 N I}{\ell_g}\right)^{2} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 \ell_g^{\,2}}$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_m &= \frac{A}{2\mu_0} \cdot \frac{\mu_0^{2}\, N^{2}\, I^{2}}{\ell_g^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\mu_0\, N^{2}\, I^{2}\, A}{2\,\ell_g^{2}} \quad \text{(after one }\mu_0 \text{ cancels)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})\, N^{2}\, I^{2}\, A}{2\,\ell_g^{2}} \quad \text{N (numerical form)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Force scales as $I^2$ (square-law) and $1/\ell_g^2$ (inverse-square). Designers therefore use a small air gap to maximize pull, but the gap cannot be zero — it is limited by **axial run-out** and **thermal expansion** of the rotor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Engagement Time of the Coil

The electromagnetic coil, modelled as a series **R-L** circuit, obeys the differential equation:

$$V = R\, I(t) + L\,\frac{dI}{dt}$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearranging:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}\, I = \frac{V}{L}$$

With the initial condition $I(0) = 0$, the integrating-factor solution is:

$$\boxed{\,I(t) = \frac{V}{R}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr), \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{L}{R}\,}$$

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \frac{V}{L} - \frac{R}{L}I \\[4pt]
e^{Rt/L}\,\frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}\,e^{Rt/L}I &= \frac{V}{L}\,e^{Rt/L} \\[4pt]
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(I\,e^{Rt/L}\right) &= \frac{V}{L}\,e^{Rt/L} \\[4pt]
I\,e^{Rt/L} - 0 &= \frac{V}{L}\cdot \frac{L}{R}\bigl(e^{Rt/L} - 1\bigr) \\[4pt]
I(t) &= \frac{V}{R}\bigl(1 - e^{-Rt/L}\bigr) = \frac{V}{R}\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

**Engagement time** (time to reach ~98% of $V/R$, i.e., $t \approx 4\tau$):

$$t_{eng} \approx 4\tau = \frac{4L}{R}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-typical):** A clutch coil has $L = 0.5\ \text{H}$ and $R = 20\ \Omega$.

$$\tau = \frac{0.5}{20} = 0.025\ \text{s} = 25\ \text{ms}, \qquad t_{eng} \approx 4 \times 25 = 100\ \text{ms}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque and Slip Heat in an Eddy-Current Clutch

In an **eddy-current coupling**, a multi-pole electromagnet rotates at synchronous speed $\omega_s$. The stationary conductive drum experiences a **time-varying flux** $B(t) = B_0 \cos(\omega_s t)$. Faraday's law gives an induced EMF in the drum:

$$e_{induced} = -\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = B_0\,A\,\omega_s \sin(\omega_s t)$$

The induced current density is $J = \sigma\, e_{induced}$ (Ohm's law for the moving conductor). The **Laplace / Lorentz force** on the induced eddy current is:

$$F_{Laplace} = J \times B$$

Averaging over a full electrical cycle yields the **mean tangential force**, hence the **transmitted torque**:

$$T_{eddy} = k\,(\omega_s - \omega_r) = k\, s\, \omega_s$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on drum conductivity, pole area, and field strength. The **slip power** (heat dissipated in the drum) is:

$$\boxed{\,P_{slip} = T_{eddy}\,(\omega_s - \omega_r) = k\,s^{2}\,\omega_s^{2}\,}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{slip} &= \omega_s - \omega_r \\[4pt]
s &= \frac{\omega_{slip}}{\omega_s} = \frac{\omega_s - \omega_r}{\omega_s} \\[4pt]
P_{slip} &= T_{eddy}\cdot\omega_{slip} = (k\,s\,\omega_s)(s\,\omega_s) = k\,s^{2}\,\omega_s^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Because slip converts into heat, eddy-current couplings are **inherently inefficient during starting**. They are used in applications where smooth, wear-free start-up is more important than energy efficiency — e.g., large industrial fans, dynamometers, and **hybrid vehicle torque-coupling during launch**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of EM Transmission Types

| Feature | Single-Plate EM Clutch | Multi-Plate EM Clutch | Magnetic Powder Clutch | Eddy-Current Clutch |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Engagement | Axial magnetic pull | Axial magnetic pull | Field-stiffened fluid | Induced eddy current |
| Contact type | Friction (dry) | Friction (dry/wet) | Shear (particle suspension) | No contact (inductive) |
| Torque density | Medium | **High** | Medium-High | Low-Medium |
| Wear | Moderate | Moderate-High | **Very low** | **Zero** |
| Heat dissipation | Air-cooled | Oil-cooled (wet) | Self-circulating | Drum fins / water-cooled |
| Response time | 50 – 200 ms | 30 – 100 ms | 10 – 50 ms | 50 – 300 ms |
| Slip control | Poor (on/off) | Limited | **Excellent (linear)** | **Excellent (smooth)** |
| Typical use | A/C compressor, pumps | Heavy vehicle auxiliaries | Tension control, robotics | Dynamometers, soft-start drives |
| Torque equation | $T = \mu F_m r_m$ | $T = \mu F_m r_m N_s$ | $T = \tau_{shear}\,A_{shear}$ | $T = k\,s\,\omega_s$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Electromagnetic Clutch System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] -->|PWM Signal| DRV[Driver Stage Transistor]
    DRV -->|Coil Current I| COIL[Energizing Coil on Rotor]
    COIL -->|Magnetic Flux Phi| GAP[Air Gap Field]
    GAP -->|Pull Force Fm| ARM[Armature Pressure Plate]
    ARM -->|Friction Torque T| OUT[Output Shaft Transmission Input]
    SPG[Return Springs] -.->|Restoring Force| ARM
    SPD[RPM Sensor] --> ECU
    TMP[Thermal Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU -->|Diagnostics| DLC[OBD-II Data Link]
```

## 4.2 Magnetic Circuit Equivalent — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MMF[Magnetomotive Force F = N times I] --> CORE[Ferromagnetic Core Rotor]
    CORE --> GAP1[Air Gap 1 - Working Gap]
    GAP1 --> ARM[Armature Disc]
    ARM --> GAP2[Air Gap 2 - Return Path]
    GAP2 --> CORE
    GAP1 -->|Maxwell Pull| FORCE[Fm = B squared A over 2 mu0]
    FORCE --> FRIC[Friction Torque T = mu Fm rm]
```

## 4.3 Engagement Sequence — Time-Domain Control Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Driver
    participant E as ECU
    participant C as Clutch Coil
    participant A as Armature
    participant S as Output Shaft
    D->>E: A/C switch ON
    E->>C: Apply 12 V DC via MOSFET
    Note over C: Current rises I = V over R times 1 minus exp of negative t over tau
    C->>A: Magnetic flux builds in air gap
    A->>A: Fm exceeds spring preload
    A->>S: Frictional lock - torque transmitted
    Note over S: Slip s tends to 0
    D->>E: A/C switch OFF
    E->>C: Coil de-energized
    C->>A: Flux collapses
    A->>S: Return springs disengage armature
```

## 4.4 Eddy-Current Coupling — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Prime Mover Engine] --> F[Field Rotor Multi-Pole Electromagnet]
    F -->|Rotating Flux at omega_s| D[Drum Conductive Non-Magnetic]
    D -->|Induced Eddy Currents| F2[Laplace Force on Drum]
    F2 -->|Torque Teddy| L[Load Driven Shaft at omega_r]
    D -.->|Slip Heat Ps| H[Dissipation Fins or Water Jacket]
    CTRL[Excitation Current Controller] -->|Sets Iexcite| F
```

## 4.5 Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) E-CVT — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] --> P1[Planetary Carrier]
    P1 --> PG[Planetary Gear Set]
    PG --> RING[Ring Gear]
    PG --> SUN[Sun Gear]
    SUN --> MG1[MG1 Motor Generator acts as CVT]
    MG1 --> BAT[HV Battery via Inverter]
    RING --> OUT[Motor Output Shaft]
    MG2[MG2 Traction Motor] --> OUT
    OUT --> DIFF[Differential to Wheels]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** In the THS architecture, MG1 *electromagnetically* controls the sun-gear speed, effectively making the planetary gear behave as a continuously variable transmission. The ICE's mechanical path and MG2's electrical path are blended through the same ring gear.
