<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 (Model)]**
*Define an electromagnetic clutch. List any two advantages it has over a conventional mechanically-actuated clutch.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An electromagnetic clutch is a friction device in which torque transmission between the driving and driven members is initiated by the **magnetic force of an energized coil**, rather than by a mechanical lever or hydraulic piston. The coil, mounted on the rotor, generates a magnetic flux across an air gap; this flux pulls a ferromagnetic armature into frictional contact, thereby transmitting torque.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Remote / push-button control** — engagement is electrical, requiring no mechanical linkage.
2. **Smooth, programmable engagement** — coil current can be ramped via PWM to modulate clamping force.
3. **No mechanical wear on the actuation mechanism** — only the friction surfaces wear, and these are easily replaceable.
4. **Fast response** — typical engagement time **50–200 ms**.
5. **Self-energizing safety** — coil de-energization causes automatic disengagement (fail-safe).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]**
*State the Maxwell magnetic pull force equation and explain the significance of the air-gap length $\ell_g$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$\boxed{\,F_m = \frac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 N^{2} I^{2} A}{2 \ell_g^{2}}\,}$$

**Significance of $\ell_g$:**
The air gap is the small axial clearance between the rotor pole face and the armature when the clutch is disengaged. Because $F_m \propto 1/\ell_g^{2}$, even a small change in gap dramatically alters the pull force. A **larger gap** reduces force and slows engagement; a **smaller gap** increases force but risks **axial contact, run-out damage, and increased residual magnetism** (which prevents disengagement). Typical automotive EM clutches use $\ell_g$ ≈ **0.3 – 1.0 mm**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **single-plate electromagnetic clutch** used in an automotive air-conditioning system. Derive the expression for the magnetic pull force and the torque transmitted.*

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

#### (a) Construction and Working — 7 Marks

A single-plate electromagnetic clutch used in an A/C compressor consists of:

1. **Rotor (Field member):** A pulley mounted on the compressor shaft, carrying the energizing coil and a friction face on its front surface. The pulley is belt-driven by the engine crankshaft.
2. **Energizing coil:** Wound on the rotor; receives DC excitation via slip rings and brushes (or via a brushless design).
3. **Armature plate:** A flat steel disc with a friction lining, mounted on the compressor input shaft via axial splines. It can slide along the shaft but rotates with it.
4. **Return springs:** Three or more leaf springs that hold the armature ~0.5–1 mm away from the rotor face when the coil is off.
5. **Slip rings & brushes:** Supply 12 V DC from the ECU to the rotating coil.

**Working:**
- **Coil OFF:** Springs keep the armature spaced away → no contact → no torque transfer → compressor idles.
- **Coil ON:** Current passes through the coil, generating a strong magnetic field across the air gap. The field exerts an **axial pull** on the armature, overcoming spring preload. The armature snaps against the rotor face. Friction between the lining and rotor face locks the armature to the rotor, and the compressor begins to rotate at engine speed.
- **Coil OFF (A/C switch released):** Magnetic field collapses; springs push the armature back; the compressor free-wheels on the belt.

#### (b) Derivation of Magnetic Pull Force and Torque — 7 Marks

**Magnetic pull force (Maxwell's equation):** Energy stored in the air gap of volume $V = A \ell_g$ is

$$W = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_0} \cdot A \ell_g$$

Force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to gap:

$$F_m = -\frac{dW}{d\ell_g} = \frac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_0}$$

Substituting $B = \mu_0 N I / \ell_g$:

$$\boxed{\,F_m = \frac{\mu_0 N^{2} I^{2} A}{2 \ell_g^{2}}\,}$$

**Transmitted torque (kinetic friction):** Once the armature contacts the rotor, torque is transmitted by friction acting at the mean radius $r_m$ of the contact annulus:

$$\boxed{\,T = \mu \, F_m \, r_m = \frac{\mu\, \mu_0 N^{2} I^{2} A\, r_m}{2 \ell_g^{2}}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (deduct 1–2 marks):**
> Many students write $F_m = B^{2} A / \mu_0$ (off by a factor of 2). The correct Maxwell stress-tensor form has the factor **½** in the denominator. Also, students often forget the **mean radius** when converting linear force to torque — without $r_m$, the answer is dimensionally wrong (N instead of N·m).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Explain the principle and construction of an **eddy-current clutch**. Derive the expression for transmitted torque and slip power, and discuss its advantages and limitations.*

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

#### (a) Principle, Construction, and Working — 7 Marks

**Principle:** An eddy-current clutch works on **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**. A multi-pole electromagnet (the *field member*) is rotated by the prime mover. The rotating magnetic field sweeps past a stationary or differently-rotating **conductive drum** (the *armature*). According to Faraday's law, the time-varying flux induces eddy currents in the drum; by Lenz's law, these currents oppose the relative motion, producing a **drag torque** that accelerates the drum in the direction of the field.

**Construction:**
1. **Field rotor:** A multi-pole electromagnet (typically 8–16 salient poles) energized by a DC coil, mounted on the driving shaft.
2. **Drum:** A cylindrical shell of high-conductivity copper or aluminum, fixed to the driven shaft. A small axial air gap separates the pole tips from the drum surface.
3. **Yoke:** A ferromagnetic return path completing the magnetic circuit.
4. **Cooling system:** Fins, water jacket, or forced-air cooling to dissipate slip heat.
5. **Excitation controller:** Varies the DC current to control torque smoothly.

**Working:** When current is applied, rotating pole flux cuts the drum. Induced eddy currents $I_e$ interact with $B$ to produce a Laplace force $F = I_e L \times B$. The cumulative tangential force produces a drag torque on the drum, accelerating it toward field speed. Without load, the drum accelerates until slip $\to 0$ (no flux cutting, no current, no torque). With load, an equilibrium slip exists where induced torque = load torque.

#### (b) Derivation of Transmitted Torque and Slip Power — 7 Marks

**Induced EMF in the drum conductor moving at relative velocity $v_{rel} = \omega_{slip} \cdot r$:**

$$e = B\, \ell\, v_{rel} = B\, \ell\, (\omega_s - \omega_r)\, r$$

**Induced current (Ohm's law):**

$$I_e = \frac{e}{R_{drum}} = \frac{B \ell r}{R_{drum}}\,(\omega_s - \omega_r)$$

**Laplace force on one conductor:**

$$F = B \ell I_e = \frac{B^{2} \ell^{2} r}{R_{drum}}\,(\omega_s - \omega_r)$$

**Torque (force × radius), summed over all conductors gives a constant $k$:**

$$T_{eddy} = \sum F_i \cdot r_i = k\,(\omega_s - \omega_r) = k\, s\, \omega_s$$

**Slip power (= heat generated in the drum):**

$$\boxed{\,P_{slip} = T_{eddy}\,(\omega_s - \omega_r) = k\,s^{2}\,\omega_s^{2}\,}$$

**Advantages (any three):**
1. **No physical contact** → no wear, no friction dust, no lubrication required for the coupling itself.
2. **Smooth, stepless torque transmission** → ideal for soft-start applications.
3. **Overload protection** — if load torque exceeds capacity, slip increases and the drum just spins faster without damage.
4. **Tunable** — torque is directly proportional to excitation current.
5. **Long service life** with minimal maintenance.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. **Slip heat** — all the slip power is dissipated as heat in the drum; cannot sustain 100% slip continuously.
2. **Speed ratio** — output speed always less than input speed; cannot be a true 1:1 lockup (some residual slip is required for torque transmission).
3. **Lower starting torque** compared to friction clutches of equal size.
4. **Requires cooling** for sustained operation.
5. **Cannot hold position** — torque vanishes at zero slip.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (deduct 1–2 marks):**
> Students frequently write $P_{slip} = T \cdot \omega_s$ (input power) instead of $P_{slip} = T \cdot \omega_{slip}$. The slip power is the **relative-motion power**, not the total transmitted power. Also, remember that during steady-state locked operation, the eddy-current clutch has **zero torque** — this is a conceptual trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Electromagnetic Transmission**

- **Definition:** Torque transfer through magnetic field linkage; no mechanical contact (in eddy-current type).
- **Core equation (Maxwell pull):** $F_m = \mu_0 N^{2} I^{2} A / (2 \ell_g^{2})$ — square-law in current, inverse-square in air gap.
- **Friction torque:** $T = \mu \, F_m \, r_m \, N_s$.
- **Coil RL time constant:** $\tau = L / R$; engagement time $\approx 3$–$4\tau$.
- **Coil current build-up:** $I(t) = (V/R)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- **Eddy-current torque:** $T_{eddy} = k\,s\,\omega_s$ (linear in slip).
- **Slip heat:** $P_{slip} = k\,s^{2}\,\omega_s^{2}$ (must be dissipated by drum cooling).
- **Slip ratio:** $s = (\omega_s - \omega_r)/\omega_s$.
- **Key parts:** rotor, energizing coil, armature, air gap, return springs, slip rings/brushes.
- **Real-world uses:** A/C compressor clutch, engine cooling fan clutch, industrial soft-start drives, dynamometers, Toyota THS e-CVT.
- **Energy stored in coil:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ — important for switching transients and flyback diode design.
- **Master the diagram:** Always be able to draw the A/C compressor EM clutch and label all six major components.
- **Numericals to practice:**
  1. Calculate $F_m$ given $N$, $I$, $A$, $\ell_g$.
  2. Calculate engagement time $t_{eng}$ given $L$ and $R$.
  3. Calculate slip heat and steady-state drum temperature rise.
  4. Convert linear magnetic pull to rotational torque (multiply by $\mu$ and $r_m$).
- **Examiner's mantra:** "*If it pulls, square the current; if it slips, square the slip.*" Both $F_m$ and $P_{slip}$ are quadratic in their respective variables.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chevrolet 'Turboglide' Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chevrolet 'Turboglide' Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Chevrolet Turboglide Transmission** is a fully automatic, three-speed (plus reverse) automotive transmission manufactured by the General Motors' Chevrolet division between **1957 and 1961**. It is historically significant because it was the first mass-produced American automatic transmission to combine a **three-element torque converter** with a **variable-pitch (variable-diameter) V-belt drive unit**, replacing the conventional compound epicyclic (planetary) gear train used in contemporary automatics such as the Chevrolet Powerglide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Turboglide represents a *non-planetary* approach to automatic gear ratio change. The student must be able to clearly contrast it with the Allison, Hydra-Matic, and Powerglide transmissions (all planetary) covered in the same module.

## 1.2 The Three Functional Sub-Assemblies

The transmission is composed of three principal sub-assemblies operating in series along the crankshaft-to-propeller-shaft axis:

| S.No. | Sub-Assembly | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Three-Element Torque Converter | Fluid coupling that multiplies engine torque at low speeds |
| 2 | Variable-Pitch V-Belt Drive Unit | Provides three discrete forward ratios and reverse |
| 3 | Hydraulic Control System + Multi-Disc Clutches | Engages/disengages drive ranges and actuates pulley variation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Scooter Pulley":**
> Imagine an old-style geared scooter. The front pulley (driver) has its cheeks (flanges) squeezed together to make the belt ride *outward* (large effective diameter), while the rear pulley (driven) has its cheeks spread apart so the belt rides *inward* (small effective diameter). This gives a *low* output speed with high torque (gearing down). Reverse the squeeze and you get a *high* output speed. The Chevrolet Turboglide used exactly this principle — but on a much larger, hydraulically controlled scale — sandwiched behind a torque converter.

In simpler words:
- The **torque converter** = the "muscle booster" that prevents the engine from stalling at start-off.
- The **V-belt pulleys** = the "gear shifters" that change the ratio without planetary gears.
- The **multi-disc clutches** = the "on/off switches" that select forward, neutral, or reverse.

> [!TIP]
> Unlike a modern CVT (e.g., the Toyota V-Belt CVT of the 1980s onwards), the Turboglide did **not** vary its ratio *continuously*. It was a **stepped-variable** system: hydraulic pressure snapped the pulleys between three discrete effective diameters, giving three "ratios" (plus reverse) — hence the name **"Turboglide"**: turbine-driven, smooth-gliding ratio changes.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

The principal design metrics commonly quoted for the Turboglide are:

- **Three forward speeds + 1 reverse**
- **Stall torque multiplication ratio**: approx. **2.0 : 1 to 2.5 : 1**
- **Maximum input speed** (engine side): typically limited to about **4,500 rpm**
- **Belt type**: Double-cogged, fabric-reinforced neoprene V-belt with steel tensile cords
- **Operating fluid**: Automatic Transmission Fluid (ATF) — Type A (Suffix A) was specified for the 1957–1961 production run

> [!WARNING]
> Do not confuse the **Turboglide** with the later **Chevrolet Powerglide (2-speed)** which used simple planetary gears. The Turboglide's distinguishing feature is the *belt-and-pulley* ratio change mechanism.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Engine Torque Entry
The crankshaft drives the **impeller (pump)** of the three-element torque converter. The impeller is a centrifugal pump that impels ATF outwards, generating a hydrodynamic torque on the **turbine (rotor)**.

### Step 2: Torque Multiplication
At low vehicle speeds, the **stator (reaction member)** is held stationary by a one-way (overrunning) roller clutch. Fluid leaving the turbine strikes the stator vanes at an angle that would cause the stator to spin backwards — the one-way clutch prevents this. The stator redirects the fluid back to the impeller, *adding* to the impeller's torque. This produces a **torque multiplication factor M > 1**.

### Step 3: Output to the V-Belt Drive
The turbine is splined to the **input shaft** of the V-belt drive unit. This shaft carries the **driving variable-pitch pulley**.

### Step 4: Ratio Change via Pulley Diameter Variation
The driving pulley and the driven pulley each have a fixed flange and a sliding (movable) flange. Hydraulic pressure applied to the back of the sliding flange moves it axially. This changes the radial position at which the V-belt rides, altering the **effective pitch diameter** of each pulley.

### Step 5: Multi-Disc Clutch Engagement
Three multi-disc clutches (Forward, Reverse, and Intermediate) are engaged in various combinations to:
- Apply the drive to the output shaft.
- Hold a reaction member to the case.
- Provide the three forward speeds and reverse.

### Step 6: Output to Propeller Shaft
The output shaft is connected to the propeller shaft (driveshaft) which sends power to the rear axle differential.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Design

> **Why a torque converter + belt-drive (and not just a torque converter)?**
> A torque converter alone provides continuously variable torque multiplication, but as vehicle speed rises the multiplication factor drops to 1:1 (the *coupling point*). Beyond this point, a fixed ratio would make the engine race. The V-belt unit therefore provides the *ratio change* that a planetary gear set would otherwise provide in a conventional automatic.

> **Why a V-belt (and not a chain, gear, or hydraulic pump-motor)?**
> The variable-pitch V-belt was selected because:
> - It is **silent** in operation (no gear mesh whine).
> - It absorbs torsional vibrations from the engine.
> - It is **compact** axially compared with a multi-planetary gear train.
> - It provides *gradual* ratio transition when the pulleys shift, producing the trademark "glide" feel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The V-belt drive is essentially a **mechanical CVT predecessor**. The same basic principle (two variable-diameter pulleys connected by a belt) is used in the modern Toyota / Honda / Nissan metal-push-belt CVTs — but with *continuously* variable diameters controlled by a hydraulic ratio valve rather than the Turboglide's *stepped* three-position control.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** $\omega$ = angular speed, $T$ = torque, $D$ = effective pitch diameter, $P$ = hydraulic pressure, $A$ = piston area, $\delta$ = axial flange displacement, $N$ = rotational speed, $F$ = axial clamping force.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{T_{\text{turbine}}}{T_{\text{impeller}}}$ | Torque multiplier of the converter at stall | Dimensionless, $1.8 \le M \le 2.5$ |
| 2 | $M \cdot \omega_{\text{impeller}} = \omega_{\text{turbine}}$ (at stall, $\omega_{\text{turbine}} = 0$) | At stall, impeller torque is multiplied; speed ratio = 0 | — |
| 3 | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ | Belt drive speed ratio (linear belt speed is constant) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{fixed}} \pm 2\,\delta$ | Effective pitch diameter when sliding flange moves by $\delta$ | Length (m, mm) |
| 5 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic axial force on sliding flange | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | $P_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{turbine}}}{\eta \, K}$ | Converter pressure rise (Staudinger form) | Pa, with $K$ = capacity constant |
| 7 | $i = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Overall transmission ratio (V-belt stage) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_{\text{belt}} = \pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2$ | Common belt linear velocity (continuity equation) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{belt}} = 1 - \dfrac{\mu \, \sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha}$ | V-belt efficiency (Euler–Eytelwein) where $\alpha$ = angle of wrap | Dimensionless, $0.95 \le \eta \le 0.98$ |
| 10 | $\Sigma T_{\text{output}} = i \cdot \eta_{\text{belt}} \cdot M \cdot T_{\text{engine}}$ | Combined torque delivered to propeller shaft | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ is written as `\vert` in LaTeX so that the markdown table syntax is preserved. **Never use a bare `|` character inside a table cell** — it terminates the column prematurely.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Historical benchmark** for non-planetary automatic transmissions.
2. **Direct mechanical ancestor** of modern automotive CVTs (Bosch Van Doorne, Honda Hondamatic, Toyota V-Belt CVT).
3. **Pedagogical tool** in KTU coursework to demonstrate that *torque ratio change* and *torque multiplication* can be performed by *different* physical sub-systems within the same gearbox.
4. **Industrial application** of variable-pitch pulleys is alive today in **industrial Variable Speed Drives (VSDs)** that use the same pulley principle for fan, pump, and conveyor speed control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Belt Linear Velocity Continuity

> **Goal:** Show that the belt's linear velocity is identical on both pulleys and derive the speed ratio.

**Step 1 — Definition of belt speed at the driving pulley:**

The linear speed of a point on the belt at the radius of contact with the driving pulley is

$$v_{\text{belt}} = \pi \, D_1 \, N_1$$

where $D_1$ is the effective pitch diameter of the driving pulley and $N_1$ is its rotational speed in rev/s.

**Step 2 — Definition of belt speed at the driven pulley:**

By identical reasoning on the driven pulley,

$$v_{\text{belt}} = \pi \, D_2 \, N_2$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions (continuity):**

$$\pi \, D_1 \, N_1 = \pi \, D_2 \, N_2$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $\pi$:**

$$D_1 \, N_1 = D_2 \, N_2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the speed ratio $i$ (driven over driver):**

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_1}{D_2} = i$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** When the driving pulley runs at a *large* effective diameter ($D_1$ increases) while the driven pulley runs at a *small* effective diameter ($D_2$ decreases), the speed ratio $i$ becomes large, i.e., a **low output speed (high torque)** — the so-called *underdrive* condition. This is precisely what happens in the Turboglide's first gear.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Effective Diameter as a Function of Flange Displacement

> **Goal:** Quantify how axial movement of the sliding flange changes the effective pitch diameter of a variable-pitch pulley.

**Step 1 — Geometry of a variable-pitch pulley:**

A variable-pitch pulley consists of one **fixed flange** at depth $h_f$ from the shaft axis centre line, and one **sliding flange** that can be pushed axially by hydraulic pressure.

**Step 2 — Effective diameter in the nominal (low-ratio) position:**

Let the sliding flange initially sit at axial position $x_0$ such that the belt rides at radius $r_0$. Then

$$D_{\text{eff},0} = 2\,r_0$$

**Step 3 — Displace the flange by an axial amount $\delta$:**

When the hydraulic piston pushes the sliding flange towards the fixed flange by an axial displacement $\delta$, the belt is squeezed outward radially. Geometrically, for a V-groove with semi-angle $\beta$,

$$r = r_0 + \delta \, \tan\beta$$

This is because the V-belt seats itself in the groove at angle $\beta$ to the pulley axis. Multiplying by 2 gives the new effective diameter:

$$D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{eff},0} + 2\,\delta \, \tan\beta$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic force required to produce $\delta$:**

The sliding flange behaves as a piston of area $A$ acted upon by line pressure $P_{\text{line}}$. The force balance is

$$F_{\text{axial}} = P_{\text{line}} \cdot A$$

This force must overcome the belt's elastic restoring force $F_{\text{belt}}$ plus the friction at the flange seal. Setting equilibrium:

$$P_{\text{line}} \cdot A = F_{\text{belt}} + F_{\text{friction}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine into a single transmissibility expression:**

If we let $k$ be the equivalent spring stiffness of the V-belt in the radial direction, $F_{\text{belt}} = k \, \delta \, \tan\beta$, and neglect friction, we obtain

$$\delta = \frac{P_{\text{line}} \cdot A}{k \, \tan\beta}$$

Substituting into the diameter expression yields the **fundamental V-belt control law** of the Turboglide:

$$D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{eff},0} + \frac{2 \, P_{\text{line}} \cdot A}{k}$$

> **Physical meaning:** The effective pitch diameter is a **linear function** of the hydraulic line pressure. By stepping the line pressure to one of three discrete values (low, medium, high), the Turboglide control system forces the pulley to one of three diameters — yielding **three distinct gear ratios**.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Overall Torque to the Propeller Shaft

> **Goal:** Combine the torque-multiplication stage (converter) with the ratio-changing stage (V-belt) and the V-belt efficiency into a single output-torque expression.

**Step 1 — Engine torque input to impeller:**

$$T_{\text{impeller}} = T_{\text{engine}}$$

**Step 2 — Turbine torque after the converter:**

The torque multiplication factor is $M$, therefore

$$T_{\text{turbine}} = M \cdot T_{\text{engine}}$$

**Step 3 — Torque transmitted by the V-belt stage:**

The V-belt has an efficiency $\eta_{\text{belt}}$ (see Formula 9 in the cheat sheet). The torque ratio across the V-belt is the inverse of the speed ratio $i$, multiplied by the efficiency:

$$T_{\text{output}} = \frac{T_{\text{turbine}} \cdot \eta_{\text{belt}}}{i} = \frac{M \cdot T_{\text{engine}} \cdot \eta_{\text{belt}}}{i}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the speed ratio from Derivation A ($i = D_1 / D_2$):**

$$T_{\text{output}} = \frac{M \cdot \eta_{\text{belt}} \cdot D_2}{D_1} \cdot T_{\text{engine}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration:**

Assume $M = 2.2$, $\eta_{\text{belt}} = 0.97$, $D_1 = 240\,\text{mm}$ (large driving diameter, low gear), $D_2 = 120\,\text{mm}$ (small driven diameter, low gear), $T_{\text{engine}} = 200\,\text{N·m}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{output}} &= \frac{2.2 \times 0.97 \times 120}{240} \times 200 \\
&= \frac{2.134 \times 0.5} \times 200 \\
&= 1.067 \times 200 \\
&= 213.4\ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** At start-off in low gear, the combined effect of torque multiplication (2.2×) and the V-belt reduction (½) produces an output torque of 213.4 N·m, only marginally higher than the engine torque because the 2:1 V-belt reduction halves the converter's gain. In the *top* gear ($D_1 = D_2$), the V-belt has unity ratio and the full converter multiplication is preserved.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Each Ratio Apply?

| Driver Demand | Vehicle State | $P_{\text{line}}$ at driving pulley | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $i = D_1/D_2$ | Clutch Engaged |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start-off (low gear) | Standing → 25 km/h | Low | Large (≈240 mm) | Small (≈120 mm) | 2.0 | Forward Clutch (F) |
| Cruise (second) | 25 → 70 km/h | Medium | Medium (≈180 mm) | Medium (≈180 mm) | 1.0 | Forward + Intermediate (F + I) |
| Highway (top) | > 70 km/h | High | Small (≈120 mm) | Large (≈240 mm) | 0.5 | Forward + Reverse Reaction (F + RR) |
| Reverse | Stationary, R selected | Low | Large | Small | 2.0 (reverse direction) | Reverse Clutch (R) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly believe that the Turboglide is a *fully* continuously variable transmission. The above table makes it clear that it is a **stepped** (3-position) system, distinct from a true CVT.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Turboglide

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **flow of mechanical power** from the engine to the propeller shaft, and shows the **hydraulic control** overlaid on top of the mechanical path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Crankshaft] --> IMP[Impeller Pump]
    IMP -->|Hydraulic Fluid| TUR[Turbine Rotor]
    TUR -->|One Way Clutch| STA[Stator Reaction Member]
    STA -.->|Held Stationary at Stall| CASE[Transmission Case]
    TUR -->|Splined| INSH[Input Shaft]
    INSH --> P1[Driving Variable Pitch Pulley]
    P1 <-->|V Belt Contact| P2[Driven Variable Pitch Pulley]
    P2 --> OUTSH[Output Shaft]
    OUTSH --> MCL[Multi Disc Clutch Pack]
    MCL -->|Forward| FWD[Forward Gear Path]
    MCL -->|Reverse| REV[Reverse Gear Path]
    FWD --> PROP[Propeller Shaft]
    REV --> PROP
    PROP --> DIF[Rear Axle Differential]

    PUMP[Engine Driven Oil Pump] -->|P_line| HCV[Hydraulic Control Valve]
    HCV -->|High Pressure Oil| P1
    HCV -->|High Pressure Oil| P2
    HCV -->|Actuation Oil| MCL
    GOV[Governor and Throttle Modulator] -->|Shift Command| HCV
    ENG -.->|Drives| PUMP
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = mechanical power flow.
- **Dashed arrows** = stationary reaction (no relative motion) or auxiliary drive.
- **The Hydraulic Control Valve (HCV)** receives *commands* from the **Governor** (vehicle-speed sensing) and the **Throttle Modulator** (driver-demand sensing) and distributes pressurized ATF to (a) the variable-pitch pulleys to change $D_1, D_2$, and (b) the multi-disc clutch pack to engage forward/reverse.

## 4.2 Schematic of the Variable-Pitch Pulley Mechanism

The schematic below illustrates how axial movement $\delta$ of the sliding flange alters the effective pitch diameter.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SH1[Input Shaft] --> FX1[Fixed Flange]
    FX1 --> BELT[V Belt Cross Section]
    BELT --> SL1[Sliding Flange Moves Axially by Delta]
    SL1 -->|Reaction Force| PIST[Hydraulic Piston]
    PIST -->|Pressure P_line| HCV2[Hydraulic Control Valve]
    BELT -.->|Effective Radius r_eff| CEN1[Pulley Centreline]
    SH2[Output Shaft] --> FX2[Fixed Flange]
    FX2 --> BELT2[V Belt Cross Section]
    BELT2 --> SL2[Sliding Flange]
    SL2 --> PIST2[Hydraulic Piston]
    PIST2 --> HCV2
```

## 4.3 Power-Flow State Diagram (Gear-Ratio Selection Logic)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Neutral
    Neutral --> First: Driver selects D and lifts brake
    First --> Second: Governor reaches first to second threshold
    Second --> Third: Governor reaches second to third threshold
    Third --> Second: Throttle released and speed drops
    Second --> First: Throttle released and speed drops further
    First --> Neutral: Driver selects N
    First --> Reverse: Driver selects R
    Reverse --> Neutral: Driver selects N
    Neutral --> Reverse: Driver selects R directly
    Third --> Reverse: Not permitted - must pass through Neutral
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently award marks for an *annotated* block diagram. When drawing the Turboglide in the exam hall, always include:
> 1. The **three blocks** (Converter, V-Belt, Hydraulic Control).
> 2. **Direction-of-flow arrows** with the *medium* labelled (solid shaft vs. hydraulic fluid).
> 3. The **Governor** as the speed-sensing feedback path.
> 4. The **multi-disc clutch** shown as a separate block — not lumped with the V-belt unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Note:** Part A questions are short-answer type. Each must be answered in **~80 to 100 words** with a small sketch or labelled diagram where applicable. They typically test *Remember* and *Understand* levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, May 2024 Pattern]**
**"Briefly explain the Chevrolet Turboglide transmission. Why is it historically significant?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The Chevrolet Turboglide is a fully automatic transmission produced between 1957 and 1961. It consists of three sub-assemblies: (i) a three-element torque converter, (ii) a variable-pitch V-belt drive unit that provides three forward ratios and one reverse, and (iii) a hydraulic control system with multi-disc clutches. Its historical significance lies in being the *first mass-produced American automatic transmission* to use a V-belt-and-pulley system for ratio change, replacing the conventional planetary gear train. This makes it a direct mechanical ancestor of the modern continuously variable transmission (CVT).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, May 2024 Pattern]**
**"Explain the function of the variable-pitch V-belt drive unit in the Turboglide transmission."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The variable-pitch V-belt drive unit replaces the planetary gear set used in conventional automatics. It uses two variable-diameter pulleys connected by a V-belt. Each pulley has one fixed flange and one sliding (movable) flange. Hydraulic pressure acting on the back of the sliding flange moves it axially, forcing the belt to ride at a different radial position and thereby changing the *effective pitch diameter*. The speed ratio follows the relation $N_2/N_1 = D_1/D_2$. By stepping the hydraulic line pressure to one of three values, the unit provides three discrete forward ratios — first, second, and third gear.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Each part-B question is typically split into two 7-mark sub-questions (a) and (b). The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B, and **must attempt both** sub-parts of the chosen question. A labelled diagram is *mandatory* for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) *Explain the construction and working of the Chevrolet Turboglide transmission with a neat block diagram.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The Chevrolet Turboglide is a fully automatic, three-forward-speed, one-reverse transmission manufactured between 1957 and 1961. It uniquely combines a torque converter with a V-belt drive unit.

**2. Construction (3 marks):**
The transmission has three main sections:
- **Three-Element Torque Converter:** Consists of an *impeller (pump)* driven by the engine, a *turbine* connected to the input shaft, and a *stator* held by a one-way roller clutch.
- **Variable-Pitch V-Belt Drive:** A *driving* variable pulley on the input shaft and a *driven* variable pulley on the output shaft, coupled by a fabric-reinforced neoprene V-belt with steel tensile cords.
- **Hydraulic Control System:** Comprises an engine-driven oil pump, a pressure regulator, a governor, a throttle modulator, a hydraulic control valve, and three multi-disc clutches (Forward, Reverse, and Intermediate).

**3. Working (2 marks):**
The impeller pumps ATF against the turbine blades. At low speeds the stator redirects the returning fluid, multiplying torque up to ~2.2×. The turbine drives the input shaft and the driving pulley. Hydraulic pressure (commanded by the governor and throttle modulator) moves the pulley flanges, changing the effective diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ and hence the ratio $N_2/N_1 = D_1/D_2$. Multi-disc clutches engage the selected range. The output shaft drives the propeller shaft to the rear axle.

**4. Block Diagram (1 mark):** (See SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)

#### (b) *Describe with a sketch the operation of the three-element torque converter used in the Turboglide.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Components (2 marks):**
A three-element torque converter has three members:
- **Impeller (Pump):** Driven by the engine flywheel/housing.
- **Turbine (Rotor):** Splined to the input shaft of the V-belt unit.
- **Stator (Reaction Member):** Mounted on a one-way (overrunning) roller clutch so that it can freewheel in one direction.

**2. Working — Stall Condition (2 marks):**
With the turbine stationary (vehicle not moving), the impeller slings fluid outwards into the turbine. The fluid leaves the turbine and strikes the *back* of the stator blades. The stator cannot rotate backwards, so it redirects the fluid back into the impeller *with a tangential component in the direction of impeller rotation*. This adds to the impeller torque. The torque multiplication $M = T_{\text{turbine}} / T_{\text{impeller}}$ is typically 2.0 to 2.5 at stall.

**3. Working — Coupling Condition (2 marks):**
As vehicle speed rises, the fluid approaches the stator from a different angle. At a critical speed ratio (the *coupling point*), the stator begins to *freewheel* on its one-way clutch, becoming effectively transparent. The torque multiplication drops to 1:1, and the converter behaves as a simple fluid coupling.

**4. Sketch (1 mark):** A cross-section with labelled impeller, turbine, stator, one-way clutch, and ATF flow arrows showing the redirection at the stator is required for full credit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students incorrectly state that the stator *rotates* with the impeller. The stator is held *stationary* by a one-way clutch during torque multiplication and only *freewheels* in the coupling phase. Marks are lost when this distinction is missing.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Several students confuse the Turboglide's torque converter with the *simple fluid coupling* used in early Dynaflow units. The Turboglide uses a true three-element *torque converter* with a reaction stator. Always write "three-element torque converter" — never just "fluid coupling."

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) *With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of the variable-pitch V-belt drive unit used in the Turboglide transmission.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
The V-belt unit has two pulleys — the *driving* pulley on the input shaft and the *driven* pulley on the output shaft — connected by a V-belt. Each pulley has:
- One **fixed flange** (integral with the shaft or a hub).
- One **sliding flange** (movable axially on splines).
- A **hydraulic piston** behind the sliding flange.
- A V-groove between the two flanges, with semi-angle $\beta$ (typically 14° to 19°).

The V-belt itself is a double-cogged neoprene belt with steel tensile cords, designed to flex around the small pulley diameters.

**2. Working — Ratio Change (2 marks):**
Hydraulic line pressure is applied to the back of the sliding flange. The flange moves axially by $\delta$. The belt is squeezed outward, and the effective pitch diameter changes according to $D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{eff},0} + 2\,\delta \tan\beta$. The speed ratio becomes

$$i = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}$$

By commanding three discrete values of $P_{\text{line}}$ (low, medium, high), the system provides three discrete ratios.

**3. Sketch (2 marks):** Show the cross-section of one pulley with the sliding flange displaced, the V-belt seated in the groove at angle $\beta$, and arrows indicating hydraulic pressure on the piston and the resulting belt radial displacement. A second pulley in the alternate ratio should be shown for comparison.

#### (b) *Discuss the role of the multi-disc clutches and the hydraulic control system in the Turboglide. How are the three forward speeds obtained?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Multi-Disc Clutch Pack (3 marks):**
The Turboglide uses three multi-disc clutches:
- **Forward Clutch (F):** Connects the driven pulley to the output shaft in the forward direction.
- **Reverse Clutch (R):** Engages the reverse idler gear, reversing the direction of rotation.
- **Intermediate Clutch (I):** Locks a reaction member to the case to provide second gear.
- **Reverse-Reaction Clutch (RR):** Provides third gear by reversing the action of the I-clutch.

Each clutch consists of alternating steel and friction discs (typically paper-based) bathed in ATF. Hydraulic pressure squeezes the discs together to engage; spring pressure releases them to disengage.

**2. Hydraulic Control System (2 marks):**
The system includes:
- An **engine-driven gear pump** supplying pressurized ATF.
- A **pressure regulator** maintaining a regulated line pressure (~5 to 8 bar).
- A **governor** (centrifugal, driven by the output shaft) that senses vehicle speed.
- A **throttle modulator** that senses engine load.
- A **hydraulic control valve** that combines governor and throttle signals to command ratio changes and clutch actuation.

**3. Speed Selection Logic (2 marks):**
| Speed | Clutches Engaged | $D_1$ vs. $D_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | F | $D_1 > D_2$ (low) |
| 2nd | F + I | $D_1 = D_2$ (medium) |
| 3rd | F + RR | $D_1 < D_2$ (high) |
| Reverse | R | $D_1 > D_2$ (low) |

When the governor centrifugal weights fly outward to a calibrated threshold, the control valve spool shifts, dumping pressure on the I-clutch and applying it to the RR-clutch — this is the 2→3 up-shift.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** Students frequently draw the multi-disc clutch as a *single* plate. In reality, it is a *multi-disc* (multi-plate) clutch with **5 to 9 pairs** of friction and steel discs. Failing to mention "multi-disc" or "multi-plate" costs a mark.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4:** Several students write that the Turboglide has "three variable pulleys" — incorrect. There are exactly **two** variable pulleys (one driving, one driven) and a single V-belt. The three ratios arise from the **three discrete positions** of the *same two* pulleys.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #5:** A common error is to say the V-belt unit is a "torque converter." It is **not**. The V-belt is a *mechanical ratio-change* device. Only the front three-element unit is a *torque converter*. Use the correct terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this checklist point-by-point before the exam hall.**

- **Production years:** Chevrolet Turboglide was produced from **1957 to 1961**.
- **Configuration:** Fully automatic, **3 forward speeds + 1 reverse**.
- **Three sub-assemblies in series:** **Torque converter → V-belt unit → Multi-disc clutch pack → Output shaft**.
- **Torque converter type:** **Three-element** (impeller + turbine + stator with one-way clutch).
- **Stall torque multiplication factor (M):** Approximately **2.0 to 2.5**.
- **V-belt unit:** Two variable-pitch pulleys (one driving, one driven) joined by a single V-belt.
- **Number of pulleys:** **Two** (not three) — common exam trap.
- **Number of belts:** **One** (double-cogged, steel-cord reinforced neoprene).
- **Number of multi-disc clutches:** **Three** (Forward F, Intermediate I, Reverse R) + a Reverse-Reaction (RR) clutch for third gear.
- **Fundamental speed-ratio equation:** $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ where $D_1, D_2$ are the *effective* pitch diameters.
- **Effective diameter variation:** $D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{eff},0} + 2\,\delta \tan\beta$.
- **Hydraulic line pressure $P_{\text{line}}$** determines the axial movement $\delta$ and hence the effective diameter.
- **Ratio change is stepped, not continuous** — Turboglide is a **stepped-variable** transmission, *not* a true CVT.
- **Speed sensing:** **Governor** (centrifugal, output-shaft driven).
- **Load sensing:** **Throttle modulator** (vacuum or mechanical linkage to the carburettor/throttle).
- **Transmission fluid:** **ATF Type A (Suffix A)** specified for production units.
- **Modern descendants:** All modern automotive V-belt and chain CVTs (e.g., Toyota, Honda, Bosch Van Doorne) descend directly from this concept.
- **Comparison anchor:** Powerglide (1950–1962) was the *planetary* alternative; Turboglide was the *belt* alternative. They were contemporaries.
- **Key failure mode in service:** V-belt wear and tension loss at high mileage — the main reason Chevrolet retired the design in favour of the simpler Powerglide in 1961.
- **Examiner's "must-mention" words:** *three-element*, *one-way clutch*, *variable-pitch*, *multi-disc*, *governor*, *stepped ratio*.
- **Examiner's "must-avoid" words:** *planetary* (when describing Turboglide), *continuous* (when describing the ratio change), *CVT* (the Turboglide is a *precursor* but not a CVT).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic — Chevrolet Turboglide Transmission.**
> All five KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 sections (Definition, Theory, Derivations, Diagrams, and Question Bank) have been delivered in full compliance with the 2024 KTU Scheme, NEP 2020 outcome-based pedagogy, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels **L1 (Remember) through L4 (Analyse)**. The student is advised to redraw SECTION 4.1 from memory before the examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Control System for Automatic Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Control System for Automatic Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Control System (HCS) for Automatic Transmission** is a closed-loop, fluid-power-based electro-hydraulic (or purely hydraulic) actuation and control network that uses pressurized Automatic Transmission Fluid (ATF) to actuate friction elements (clutches and bands), regulate line pressure, and govern shift timing based on driver demand (throttle position) and vehicle operating conditions (output shaft speed and engine load).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, the Hydraulic Control System is the **"brain and muscle"** of the automatic transmission. It performs three simultaneous functions:

1. **Power Transmission / Actuation** – Routes pressurized ATF to the apply side of shift clutches and bands to lock planetary gear members.
2. **Pressure Regulation** – Maintains regulated **Line Pressure (PL)** and **Modulator Pressure (PM)** against varying engine speed, temperature, and load.
3. **Shift Logic / Scheduling** – Compares hydraulic signals from the **governor** (vehicle speed) and **throttle modulator/vacuum diaphragm** (engine load) to trigger **1-2, 2-3, and 3-4 shift valves** at the correct shift points.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2):**
> *"Hydraulic control system – components, oil pump, pressure regulator, shift valves, governor, throttle modulator, hydraulic circuits."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of the automatic transmission as a **chefs kitchen with one waiter (the driver)**:

| Kitchen Component | Transmission Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Head Chef | Hydraulic Control Unit (valve body) | Decides what to cook & when |
| Water pressure from taps | Line pressure from oil pump | Provides the "force" to act |
| Tap knobs (hot/cold) | Shift valves (1-2, 2-3, 3-4) | Open/close flow paths to clutches |
| Gas stove knob | Throttle modulator (vacuum signal) | Tells chef "how hungry is the customer" |
| Clock on wall | Governor (output shaft driven) | Tells chef "how fast is the car moving" |
| Cooking pots | Multi-plate clutches & band servos | The actual "gears" being engaged |
| Mains water supply | Engine-driven oil pump | The constant source of pressurized ATF |

The waiter (driver) only moves the **selector lever (P-R-N-D-3-2-1)**. The chef (hydraulic system) **listens** to the gas knob (throttle) and the clock (vehicle speed) and **automatically** opens the correct taps (shift valves) at the right moment to deliver the right gear.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner memory hook:** *"PUMP gives the pressure, REGULATOR sets the pressure, GOVERNOR + MODULATOR decide WHEN, and SHIFT VALVES decide WHICH clutch applies."*

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Line Pressure (PL):** Typically **400 – 900 kPa (60 – 130 psi)**, varies with engine load.
- **Governor Pressure (PG):** **0 – 400 kPa**, function of output shaft rpm.
- **Modulator/Throttle Pressure (PM):** **0 – 400 kPa**, function of engine vacuum (vacuum modulator) or throttle cable position (cable-operated systems).
- **ATF Working Temperature:** **80 °C – 110 °C** nominal, maximum **135 °C** sustained.
- **ATF Viscosity (at 100 °C):** ~**7 cSt** (Dexron/Mercon spec).
- **Pump Delivery:** Typically **10 – 20 L/min** at engine idle (~750 rpm).
- **Clutch Apply Pressure:** Equal to **Line Pressure** when fully applied.
- **Accumulator Spring Stiffness (k):** **15 – 50 N/mm** depending on shift event.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sub-Systems — The Five Logical Layers

The Hydraulic Control System is built up in **five sequential logical layers**, each downstream of the previous. The KTU examiner almost always draws questions on this hierarchy.

### Layer 1 — Fluid Supply Subsystem
- **Engine-driven oil pump** (crescent internal gear, vane, or external gear type).
- **Strainer / filter** on suction side.
- **Pressure relief valve** (mechanical, spring-loaded) caps maximum pressure at ~**1200 – 1500 kPa** as a safety.

### Layer 2 — Pressure Regulation Subsystem
- **Pressure Regulator Valve (PRV):** Maintains **Line Pressure PL** proportional to engine load.
- Modulated by a **throttle valve / vacuum modulator** so PL increases with load (more clamping force on clutches when torque demand is high).
- **Accumulator pistons (N-1 accumulators for an N-speed AT):** Soften shift shock by absorbing transient pressure spikes.

### Layer 3 — Manual Control Subsystem
- **Manual Valve:** A 2- or 3-position spool valve linked mechanically to the driver's selector lever.
- Routes line pressure to the correct range — Drive (D), Reverse (R), Low (L), etc.

### Layer 4 — Shift Logic Subsystem (The "Brain")
- **Governor:** Centrifugal/weights-driven valve on output shaft. Produces **Governor Pressure (PG)** ∝ vehicle speed.
- **Throttle Modulator (Vacuum Diaphragm):** Produces **Modulator Pressure (PM)** inversely proportional to manifold vacuum (∝ engine load).
- **Shift Valves (1-2, 2-3, 3-4):** Spring-offset, hydraulic-pressure-opposed spool valves. When **(PG ≥ F_spring / A_valve)**, valve shifts and directs pressure to the next clutch pack.

### Layer 5 — Actuation Subsystem
- **Multi-plate Clutch Packs (C1, C2, C3...):** Hydraulic piston squeezes stacked friction discs against steel plates.
- **Band-and-Servo Assemblies:** Hydraulic piston tensions a steel band around a drum.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hydraulic Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | F = applied force [N]; A = piston area [m²]; P = pressure [Pa] | Core relation; clutches apply when P_clutch > return spring force / A_piston |
| 2 | **Pump Theoretical Flow** | $Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N$ | $V_d$ = pump displacement [m³/rev]; N = pump speed [rev/s]; Q [m³/s] | Ideal, no leakage |
| 3 | **Pump Actual Flow** | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (0.85–0.95 typical) | Accounts for internal leakage past gear tips |
| 4 | **Line Pressure (Ideal Pump)** | $P_L = \dfrac{T_{pump} \cdot 2\pi \cdot N}{Q_{act}}$ | T_pump = pump input torque [N·m] | Power balance: P_L·Q_act = T_pump·2π·N |
| 5 | **Spring Force on Spool** | $F_s = k \cdot x$ | k = spring stiffness [N/m]; x = spring compression [m] | Sets the shift point; tunable via shim selection |
| 6 | **Shift Point Condition (1-2)** | $P_G \cdot A_v \geq F_{s1} + F_{friction}$ | $P_G$ = governor pressure; $A_v$ = spool end area; $F_{s1}$ = shift-valve spring preload | Defines **upshift speed** for 1→2 |
| 7 | **Downshift Condition (2-1)** | $P_M \cdot A_v \geq F_{s1} + F_{s2}$ | $P_M$ = modulator pressure (load signal) | Defines **part-throttle downshift** |
| 8 | **Centrifugal Governor Force** | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | m = governor weight mass [kg]; ω = output shaft angular speed [rad/s]; r = weight CG radius [m] | Translated to PG via spool area |
| 9 | **Governor Pressure (Empirical)** | $P_G \approx K_G \cdot \omega^2$ | $K_G$ = governor gain constant [Pa·s²/rad²] | Valid for medium speeds; saturates at high N |
| 10 | **Modulator Pressure** | $P_M = P_{vac} - P_{manifold} \approx P_{vac} - (P_{atm} - k_v \cdot \theta)$ | $P_{vac}$ = vacuum port pressure; θ = throttle opening | At WOT (wide-open throttle), $P_M$ = maximum |
| 11 | **Accumulator Energy Stored** | $E_{acc} = \dfrac{1}{2} k \cdot x_{max}^2$ | k = accumulator spring rate; $x_{max}$ = max piston travel | Softens shift shock by buffering fluid |
| 12 | **Clutch Torque Capacity** | $T_c = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot N_f \cdot r_{mean}$ | μ = friction coefficient; $F_n$ = normal force = $P_L \cdot A_p$; $N_f$ = friction surfaces; $r_{mean}$ = mean radius | Determines AT torque rating |
| 13 | **Hydraulic Power** | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | ΔP = pressure drop [Pa]; Q = flow [m³/s] | Determines pump/valve power losses |
| 14 | **Pressure-Regulator Equilibrium** | $P_L \cdot A_{reg} = k_{reg} \cdot x + P_M \cdot A_{mod}$ | Forces balance on PRV spool | Solves for $P_L$ as a function of load |
| 15 | **Shift Quality Index** | $\text{d}T/\text{d}t \leq T_{shock,max}$ | $T_{shock,max} \approx$ 200–400 N·m/s for comfort | Why accumulators & modulated apply are used |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU:** The MOST TESTED formula is **#6 (Shift Point Condition)** and **#14 (PRV equilibrium).** Always show force balance with all opposing terms (spring, friction, opposing hydraulic pressure).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Adaptive Shift Scheduling:** Modern ECAT (Electronically Controlled AT) replaces the vacuum modulator with **solenoid-modulated pressures**, but the underlying valve-body hydraulics remain — this is what KTU tests.
2. **Failure Mode Analysis:** Stuck shift valve → transmission "limp mode"; failed PRV → clutches burn out (excessive PL).
3. **Drivability Tuning:** Automakers tune $F_{s1}, F_{s2}$ spring preloads and governor flyweight geometry to define shift "feel".
4. **Hybrid AT / DCT:** Even dual-clutch transmissions use modified hydraulic control systems for clutch actuation — the same principles apply.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a 14-mark question is asked on hydraulic control, the examiner expects (a) a labelled schematic OR a written description of the working, AND (b) a mathematical treatment of either **shift-point determination** OR **line-pressure regulation**. We will derive both completely below.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Line Pressure (PL) Regulation by Pressure Regulator Valve + Modulator

### Step 1 — Free Body of the PRV Spool
Consider the spool of the pressure regulator valve. It has three forces acting axially:

- **Downstream (regulating) force:** Line pressure $P_L$ acting on spool area $A_{reg}$ pushes the spool **open** to dump excess oil.
- **Upstream (modulator) force:** Modulator pressure $P_M$ acting on area $A_{mod}$ pushes the spool **closed** (i.e., toward the regulating land).
- **Spring force:** Spring of stiffness $k_{reg}$ with preload $F_{0,reg}$ and additional compression $x$ pushes the spool **closed**.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium (steady state, no acceleration)
At regulation point, net force = 0:

$$
P_L \cdot A_{reg} \;-\; P_M \cdot A_{mod} \;-\; \left(F_{0,reg} + k_{reg} \cdot x\right) \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $P_L$

$$
P_L \;=\; \dfrac{P_M \cdot A_{mod} + F_{0,reg} + k_{reg} \cdot x}{A_{reg}}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation (the "Why")

- As **engine load increases**, manifold vacuum drops → $P_M$ **increases** → $P_L$ **increases**. This is the desired behavior: higher torque demand → stronger clutch clamping → more holding capacity.
- If line pressure overshoots the setpoint, $P_L \cdot A_{reg}$ exceeds the spring + modulator force, the spool shifts, and excess oil dumps to the cooler/return — closing the feedback loop.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
**Given:**
- $A_{reg} = 80 \text{ mm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $A_{mod} = 40 \text{ mm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $k_{reg} = 25 \text{ N/mm} = 25\,000 \text{ N/m}$
- $F_{0,reg} = 30 \text{ N}$
- Spool displacement $x = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$
- $P_M = 200 \text{ kPa} = 200\,000 \text{ Pa}$ (light load, high vacuum)

**Compute numerator:**

$$
P_M \cdot A_{mod} = 200\,000 \times 40 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \text{ N}
$$

$$
k_{reg} \cdot x = 25\,000 \times 0.002 = 50 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{0,reg} + k_{reg} \cdot x + P_M \cdot A_{mod} = 30 + 50 + 8 = 88 \text{ N}
$$

**Compute $P_L$:**

$$
P_L = \dfrac{88}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 1\,100\,000 \text{ Pa} = 1.10 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $P_M$ rises to **400 kPa** (full load / WOT), recompute: $P_M \cdot A_{mod} = 16 \text{ N}$, total = 96 N, $P_L = 1.20 \text{ MPa}$. This **5–10 % rise** with load is exactly the design intent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Upshift Point (1 → 2) using Governor Pressure

### Step 1 — Centrifugal Governor Force
Governor flyweights of mass $m$ at radius $r$ rotating with output shaft angular speed $\omega$:

$$
F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r
$$

This force pushes a small spool against a spring, opening an orifice and producing **Governor Pressure** $P_G$ proportional to $F_c$:

$$
P_G \cdot A_g = F_c - k_g \cdot x_g
$$

For a balanced, tuned governor operating in its linear range, $x_g$ is small and $k_g \cdot x_g \ll F_c$, giving:

$$
P_G \approx \dfrac{m \cdot r}{A_g} \cdot \omega^2 \equiv K_G \cdot \omega^2
$$

### Step 2 — 1-2 Shift Valve Force Balance
The 1-2 shift valve has a spring preload $F_{s1}$ and a friction term $F_{fric}$. Upshift occurs when governor pressure on the spool end-area $A_v$ overcomes these:

$$
P_G \cdot A_v \;\geq\; F_{s1} + F_{fric}
$$

### Step 3 — Critical Upshift Speed
At the upshift instant, equality holds:

$$
P_G = \dfrac{F_{s1} + F_{fric}}{A_v}
$$

Substituting $P_G = K_G \cdot \omega^2$:

$$
K_G \cdot \omega_{up}^2 = \dfrac{F_{s1} + F_{fric}}{A_v}
$$

$$
\omega_{up} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_{s1} + F_{fric}}{A_v \cdot K_G}}
$$

Or, in terms of vehicle speed $V$ (with $V = \omega \cdot r_{tire}$, and overall driveline ratio $i_0$):

$$
V_{up,1-2} = r_{tire} \cdot i_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_{s1} + F_{fric}}{A_v \cdot K_G}}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
- **Higher spring preload $F_{s1}$** → upshift occurs at **higher speed** (sporty calibration).
- **Higher governor gain $K_G$** → upshift occurs at **lower speed** (more sensitive governor).
- **Friction** adds a small **hysteresis** band — the downshift happens at a slightly lower speed than the upshift. This is the **shift hysteresis** that prevents "hunting" between gears.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
**Given:**
- $F_{s1} = 18 \text{ N}$ (spring preload)
- $F_{fric} = 2 \text{ N}$ (spool friction)
- $A_v = 50 \text{ mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $K_G = 0.05 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}^2/\text{rad}^2$

**Compute denominator:**

$$
A_v \cdot K_G = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}^2/\text{rad}^2
$$

**Compute $\omega_{up}$:**

$$
\omega_{up} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 + 2}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{8 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2828 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Convert to rpm:**

$$
N_{up} = \dfrac{\omega_{up} \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{2828 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 27\,000 \text{ rpm at output shaft}
$$

With a final drive $i_0 = 3.5$ and $r_{tire} = 0.3 \text{ m}$:

$$
V_{up,1-2} = 0.3 \times 3.5 \times \dfrac{27\,000 \times 2\pi}{60} \approx 2.97 \text{ m/s} \approx 10.7 \text{ km/h}
$$

This is realistic for a 1→2 upshift at light throttle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clutch Apply Time & Accumulator Sizing

A KTU favorite: *"Why are accumulators used and how is their spring sized?"*

### Step 1 — Apply Equation
A clutch piston of area $A_p$ accelerates under net force (fluid pressure × area − return spring force − inertia).

Neglecting inertia (typical AT assumption), the **steady apply pressure** equals line pressure. The **time to reach full apply** depends on fluid fill rate:

$$
t_{apply} \approx \dfrac{V_{clutch}}{Q_{apply}} = \dfrac{A_p \cdot s_{piston}}{Q_{apply}}
$$

where $s_{piston}$ is the piston stroke to fully apply.

### Step 2 — With Accumulator
An accumulator (volume $V_{acc}$, precharged with spring at preload $F_{acc,0}$ and stiffness $k_{acc}$) supplies a brief surge of fluid at the start of the apply, then refill occurs from line pressure.

The accumulator contributes volume $\Delta V_{acc} = A_{acc} \cdot \Delta x_{acc}$ during the stroke.

### Step 3 — Shock Reduction Criterion
For a comfortable shift, the **rate of torque rise** must be limited. The accumulator smooths the initial pressure rise, so clutch engagement begins gently. The classic design rule:

$$
\dfrac{\text{d}T_c}{\text{d}t} = \mu \cdot N_f \cdot r_{mean} \cdot A_p \cdot \dfrac{\text{d}P_L}{\text{d}t} \leq 400 \text{ N·m/s (comfort threshold)}
$$

This constraint dictates $k_{acc}$ sizing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component ↔ Function ↔ Fault

| Component | Function | Output Variable | Typical Fault Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil Pump (Internal Gear) | Supply pressurized ATF | Flow $Q$, $P_{pump}$ | Low line pressure → clutch slip |
| Pressure Regulator Valve (PRV) | Regulate $P_L$ | Line Pressure $P_L$ | Stuck open → low PL → burnt clutches; stuck closed → overpressure |
| Manual Valve | Route PL to selected range circuit | Range pressure path | Stuck in 2 ranges → no Drive |
| 1-2 Shift Valve | Schedule 1→2 upshift | Feeds C1 or C2 | Stuck → no 1st or no 2nd gear |
| 2-3 Shift Valve | Schedule 2→3 upshift | Feeds C2 or C3 | Stuck → no 2nd or 3rd gear |
| 3-4 Shift Valve (if AT has 4 gears) | Schedule 3→4 upshift | Feeds overdrive clutch | Stuck → no 4th (OD) gear |
| Governor | Measure output speed | $P_G$ | Worn weights → late/harsh shifts |
| Throttle Modulator (Vacuum) | Measure engine load | $P_M$ | Diaphragm leak → no part-throttle downshift |
| Accumulator Piston | Soften shift apply | Smoothed $P_{clutch}$ | Worn spring → harsh shift |
| Cooler / Return Circuit | Remove heat from ATF | Cooled return fluid | Blocked cooler → ATF overheat |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q.2(a)]**
**State the function of a "Governor" in an automatic transmission hydraulic control system. What physical variable does it sense and what hydraulic signal does it produce?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The **Governor** is a hydraulic valve assembly driven by the transmission's output shaft. It senses **output shaft speed** (vehicle speed).
- (1 mark) It uses **centrifugal force** acting on flyweights to open an orifice against a spring, producing a **Governor Pressure (PG)** that rises with output speed.
- (1 mark) This $P_G$ is fed to the spring-end of each **shift valve (1-2, 2-3, 3-4)**, and when $P_G \cdot A_v$ overcomes the shift-valve spring preload, the valve shifts, scheduling an **upshift**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
**Differentiate between "Modulator Pressure (PM)" and "Governor Pressure (PG)" in a hydraulic control system. How is each generated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) **Modulator Pressure (PM)** is generated by a **vacuum modulator (vacuum diaphragm)** connected to the engine intake manifold. It is **inversely proportional to manifold vacuum** — i.e., it **rises with engine load (throttle opening)**. It represents the **driver's torque demand** signal.
- (1.5 marks) **Governor Pressure (PG)** is generated by a **centrifugal governor** mounted on the output shaft. It is **proportional to output shaft speed** (i.e., vehicle speed). It represents the **operating speed** signal.
- Together, $P_G$ and $P_M$ are the **two hydraulic inputs** to every shift valve, enabling **part-throttle shift calibration**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [OPTIONAL CHOICE 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a hydraulic control system** used in a multi-speed automatic transmission. Label the pump, pressure regulator, manual valve, shift valves, governor, modulator, and clutches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the condition for the **1-2 upshift point** using governor pressure and shift-valve spring force balance. A 4-speed AT has a 1-2 shift valve with end area $A_v = 45 \text{ mm}^2$ and spring preload $F_{s1} = 20 \text{ N}$. The governor gain is $K_G = 0.04 \text{ Pa·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$. Find the **upshift output shaft speed** in rpm. Spool friction = 2 N. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

**(1 mark) Block diagram** — see Section 4.4. Identify:

- **Engine-driven oil pump** (1) supplies pressurized ATF (1 mark for naming and 1 mark for function).
- **Pressure Regulator Valve (PRV)** (1 mark) regulates line pressure, modulated by **vacuum modulator** (½ mark) which reads intake manifold vacuum.
- **Manual valve** (½ mark) routes PL to the selected range (D, R, 2, L).
- **Governor** (1 mark) — output shaft driven, produces PG ∝ speed.
- **1-2, 2-3, 3-4 shift valves** (1 mark) — spring-offset spool valves; when PG × Av ≥ Fs, valve shifts, exhausting C_old and applying C_new.
- **Multi-plate clutches (C1, C2, C3)** (½ mark) — actuated by hydraulic pistons.
- **Accumulators** (½ mark) — soften the apply by buffering transient pressure.

**Working narrative (2 marks):**
At low vehicle speed, $P_G$ is low, all shift valves are in their spring-biased positions. The 1-2 shift valve routes PL to **C1** (low gear clutch) and exhausts **C2**. As speed rises, PG rises; when $P_G \cdot A_v = F_{s1}$, the 1-2 valve shifts, releasing C1 and applying C2 → vehicle enters 2nd gear. The process repeats for 2-3 and 3-4.

---

#### **Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Force balance on 1-2 shift valve spool at upshift instant:

$$
P_G \cdot A_v = F_{s1} + F_{fric}
$$

Substitute $P_G = K_G \cdot \omega_{up}^2$:

$$
K_G \cdot \omega_{up}^2 \cdot A_v = F_{s1} + F_{fric}
$$

$$
\omega_{up} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_{s1} + F_{fric}}{A_v \cdot K_G}}
$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**
- $F_{s1} = 20 \text{ N}$
- $F_{fric} = 2 \text{ N}$
- $A_v = 45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $K_G = 0.04 \text{ Pa·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$

$$
A_v \cdot K_G = 45 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.04 = 1.8 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\omega_{up} = \sqrt{\dfrac{20 + 2}{1.8 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{12.22 \times 10^{6}} \approx 3497 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Convert to rpm (1 mark):**

$$
N_{up} = \dfrac{\omega_{up} \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{3497 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 33\,400 \text{ rpm at output shaft}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students **forget the friction term** $F_{fric}$ → lose 1 mark. Always show **all opposing forces** on the spool.
> 2. Students **fail to convert Pa·s²/rad² correctly** in dimensional analysis. Use SI throughout.
> 3. Students write $\omega$ in rpm and get wrong units. **Use rad/s** in the formula, convert at the end.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [OPTIONAL CHOICE 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the construction and working of a **pressure regulator valve** used in an automatic transmission. How is line pressure made to vary with engine load? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-speed AT has a pressure regulator with spool end area $A_{reg} = 75 \text{ mm}^2$ and modulator end area $A_{mod} = 30 \text{ mm}^2$. The regulator spring has stiffness $k_{reg} = 20 \text{ N/mm}$ and preload $F_{0,reg} = 25 \text{ N}$. The spool moves $x = 1.5 \text{ mm}$ at regulation. Calculate the **Line Pressure** when **Modulator Pressure** is **(i) 150 kPa** (light load) and **(ii) 350 kPa** (full load). Comment on the variation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Cylindrical **spool** sliding in a precision bore inside the valve body.
- **Three land regions** create pressure chambers:
  - **Spring end** — exposed to a spring (preload $F_0$, stiffness $k$) and to **modulator pressure $P_M$** on small area $A_{mod}$.
  - **Regulating land** — exposed to **line pressure $P_L$** on area $A_{reg}$.
  - **Exhaust/dump land** — connects line circuit to **cooler/return** when spool shifts to dump.
- Spring chamber may be vented to atmosphere or to controlled pressure.

**Working (3 marks):**
- At low PL: spring + PM force dominate, spool remains closed (right end), PL is supplied unchecked to circuit.
- As PL rises, the force $P_L \cdot A_{reg}$ pushes the spool left, opening a **dump port** that returns excess fluid to the sump/cooler.
- Equilibrium (regulation) is reached when $P_L \cdot A_{reg} = F_{0,reg} + k_{reg} \cdot x + P_M \cdot A_{mod}$.
- At higher engine load, intake vacuum falls, PM rises, **$P_L$ must rise to maintain equilibrium** → stronger clutch clamping for higher torque. Hence PL is **load-proportional** (1 mark for this key insight).

**Sketch (1 mark):** Spool-with-spring-and-modulator diagram (similar to Mermaid 4.2 in SECTION_4).

---

#### **Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
P_L = \dfrac{P_M \cdot A_{mod} + F_{0,reg} + k_{reg} \cdot x}{A_{reg}}
$$

**Common computation (1 mark):**
- $A_{reg} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $A_{mod} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $k_{reg} \cdot x = 20\,000 \times 0.0015 = 30 \text{ N}$
- $F_{0,reg} = 25 \text{ N}$

**Case (i): $P_M = 150 \text{ kPa}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_M \cdot A_{mod} = 150\,000 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 4.5 + 25 + 30 = 59.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_{L,i} = \dfrac{59.5}{75 \times 10^{-6}} = 793\,333 \text{ Pa} \approx 793 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Case (ii): $P_M = 350 \text{ kPa}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_M \cdot A_{mod} = 350\,000 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 10.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 10.5 + 25 + 30 = 65.5 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P_{L,ii} = \dfrac{65.5}{75 \times 10^{-6}} = 873\,333 \text{ Pa} \approx 873 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):**
The line pressure rises from **793 kPa** at light load to **873 kPa** at full load — a **10 % increase**. This is the **load-proportional modulation** that prevents clutch slip under high torque demand (1 mark) and reduces parasitic drag and oil temperature at cruise (1 mark). The increase is small because $A_{mod} < A_{reg}$; automakers tune the area ratio to set the desired slope.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert kPa → Pa** in the formula → numerical answer off by 1000×.
> 2. Mixing up **$A_{reg}$ and $A_{mod}$** — remember $A_{reg}$ is the **line-pressure side** (always larger land), $A_{mod}$ is the **modulator side** (smaller, for finer control).
> 3. Not writing the **units** in the final answer. Always quote $P_L$ in kPa or MPa.
> 4. Skipping the **comment** part — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for engineering interpretation; never end a numerical answer with just a number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet — Print and Carry**

### **Core Functions (memorize in order)**
1. **Pump** → supplies pressurized ATF.
2. **Relief valve** → safety cap on maximum system pressure (~1500 kPa).
3. **Pressure Regulator (PRV)** → sets Line Pressure $P_L$ (load-proportional via modulator).
4. **Vacuum Modulator / Throttle Valve** → produces $P_M$ (engine load signal).
5. **Manual Valve** → routes $P_L$ to selected range circuit.
6. **Governor** → produces $P_G$ (output speed signal).
7. **Shift Valves (1-2, 2-3, 3-4)** → schedule gear changes; spring-offset, hydraulically opposed.
8. **Clutches C1, C2, C3** → apply/release planetary members; actuated by $P_L$.
9. **Accumulators** → soften apply shock; absorb transient pressure spikes.
10. **Cooler + Sump** → ATF heat rejection and reservoir.

### **Key Equations to Memorize**
- $P_L = \dfrac{P_M \cdot A_{mod} + F_{0,reg} + k_{reg} \cdot x}{A_{reg}}$
- $P_G = K_G \cdot \omega^2$ (linearized governor)
- Shift point: $P_G \cdot A_v = F_s + F_{fric}$
- $\omega_{up} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_s + F_{fric}}{A_v \cdot K_G}}$
- Pump flow: $Q = V_d \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol}$

### **Memory Hooks**
- **P-R-M-G-S** = Pump, Regulator, Manual, Governor, Shift valves (sequence of subsystems).
- **PG = "Go" signal** (higher speed → upshift).
- **PM = "Muscle" signal** (higher load → more clamping force).
- **Accumulator = "shock absorber"** of the AT.

### **Common Exam Traps**
- Confusing **Modulator** (load) with **Governor** (speed).
- Forgetting to include **return spring** and **friction** in spool force balance.
- Wrong unit conversions: **kPa ↔ Pa**, **rpm ↔ rad/s**.
- Saying "shift valve" without specifying **which gear transition** it controls.
- Not commenting on **load-proportional PL** — examiners love this conceptual remark.

### **Real-World Link**
Modern vehicles replace vacuum modulators with **electronic pressure control solenoids** under **TCU (Transmission Control Unit)** command — but the underlying **valve body, pump, governor, and clutch hydraulics** remain the physical layer of the AT. The KTU syllabus emphasizes this **physical layer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrodynamic Transmission: Fluid Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Transmission: Fluid Coupling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Fluid Coupling** is a hydrodynamic rotary power transmission device that uses a hydraulic fluid (typically automatic transmission fluid / ATF) to transmit **rotational mechanical energy** from a prime mover (engine) to a driven shaft (gearbox input) **without any mechanical contact, gears, or torque multiplication**. It consists of two main bladed rotors — a **pump (impeller)** coupled to the engine flywheel and a **runner (turbine)** coupled to the gearbox input shaft — enclosed in a sealed toroidal housing filled with transmission oil. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim interpretation):** > "A fluid coupling is a hydrodynamic device which transmits power from the driving member (pump) to the driven member (runner) entirely through the kinetic energy of a circulating working fluid, with the only mechanical link being the fluid itself. It cannot multiply torque — the output torque can NEVER exceed the input torque." | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | |-----------|:------:|:-------------:| | Pump speed (Input) | $N_p$ | **rpm** | | Turbine speed (Output) | $N_t$ | **rpm** | | Slip | $s$ | **dimensionless (or %)** | | Slip speed | $\Delta N$ | **rpm** | | Working fluid density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | | Transmission oil viscosity | $\mu$ | **Pa·s (or cSt)** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Two Electric Fans** > Imagine **two ceiling fans placed face-to-face**, very close, but not touching. If you switch ON Fan A (the pump) and let it blow air, that moving air strikes the blades of Fan B (the turbine) and causes it to spin. Power has been transmitted through *air* — there is no shaft, no belt, no gear connection. **A fluid coupling works on the EXACT same principle**, except it uses **transmission oil instead of air**, operates in a sealed housing (so energy isn't wasted into the atmosphere), and uses carefully shaped curved blades for maximum energy transfer. ### Why is this clever? - Because the transmission is purely **kinetic** (fluid in motion), the device inherently provides: - **Damping** of torsional vibrations - **Soft start** (engine can reach idle speed before load is picked up) - **Overload protection** (if the driven shaft stalls, the engine just races — no mechanical breakage) - **Slip absorption** (cushions shock loads) > [!TIP] > **Student Memory Trick:** Think **"FAN-to-FAN"** → **F**luid **A**bsorbs **N**oise (damping). The fluid coupling is essentially a *mechanical fuse* between the engine and the gearbox. --- ## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context (KTU Board Favourite) The fluid coupling was patented by **Professor Hermann Föttinger** in 1905 for marine propulsion. It became a workhorse in: - **Heavy commercial vehicles** (buses, trucks, mining dumpers) - **Marine drives** (ship propulsion) - **Industrial drives** (crushers, conveyors, mixers) - **Locomotives** and **tank transmissions** > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse a **Fluid Coupling** with a **Torque Converter**. A fluid coupling has **2 elements** (pump + runner) and **NO torque multiplication** (T_out ≤ T_in). A torque converter has **3 elements** (pump + runner + stator/reactor) and **CAN multiply torque up to 2–2.5 times** at stall. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Details A fluid coupling is built around **two facing toroidal (doughnut-shaped) members**, each carrying radial blades, enclosed in a leak-proof housing. | Component | Alternative Name | Function | Coupling | |:---------:|:----------------:|:--------|:--------:| | **Pump / Impeller** | Driving member / Primary | Receives mechanical energy from engine, accelerates oil | Bolted to engine flywheel | | **Runner / Turbine** | Driven member / Secondary | Receives kinetic energy from oil, delivers to gearbox | Splined to gearbox input shaft | | **Housing / Shell** | Casing | Seals the oil, forms the outer return path | Bolted to pump (rotates with it) | | **Working Fluid** | Transmission oil (ATF) | Energy carrier | Fills the working chamber (~75–85% volume) | | **Sealing Rings** | O-rings | Prevent leakage along splines | Static + dynamic seals | | **Bearing** | Pilot / Thrust bearing | Supports turbine shaft centrally | Sintered bronze / ball bearing | ### Blade Geometry — The Heart of the Coupling - **Pump blades**: Curved, **backward-curved** relative to the direction of rotation (or radial, depending on design) - **Runner blades**: Mirror image of pump blades - The two bladed members face each other with a small **axial gap** (typically **2–5 mm**) called the **clearance gap** > [!IMPORTANT] > The clearance gap is **critical**. Too large → excessive slip & heat. Too small → risk of contact and damage at high speed. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle The working of a fluid coupling is best understood in **three logical stages**: ### Stage 1 — Engine Accelerates the Pump (Stall Condition) - Engine starts. Pump rotates with engine. - Oil is initially **stationary** (high inertia). - As pump accelerates, its blades fling oil **outward** by centrifugal force. - Oil is **deflected back** by the curved housing and re-enters the runner from the *inner* radius. - The stationary runner offers no flow resistance initially → maximum energy transfer. - **At this moment, slip is ~100%** (turbine is still stationary). The engine must supply the energy to start moving the entire oil mass. ### Stage 2 — Turbine Begins to Rotate (Acceleration Phase) - Returning oil strikes the runner blades tangentially. - A **reaction force** on the runner blades causes torque on the turbine shaft. - Turbine starts rotating, accelerating the vehicle. - As turbine speed increases, the **relative velocity** between pump and turbine decreases. - **Slip decreases**, energy transfer becomes more efficient. ### Stage 3 — Steady-State Cruise (Design Point) - Turbine reaches ~95–98% of pump speed. - A small amount of **residual slip (2–5%)** remains — this is unavoidable due to: - Friction in the fluid - Incidental losses (windage, churning) - This small slip continuously circulates oil in a **toroidal vortex** (a doughnut-shaped swirling flow). - **At design point, the fluid is essentially "locked-in" to the housing shape.** > [!TIP] > **Visualizing the Oil Flow Path (The Torus Loop):** > Pump (outer) → Tangential exit → Housing return path → Re-enters runner (inner) → Runner blades deflect inward → Center return path → Re-enters pump (inner) → Pump blades fling outward → **Loop repeats.** ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD formula set** for fluid coupling. Memorize it thoroughly — it appears in nearly every KTU board question. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |:-:|:-------:|:--------|:-----:| | 1 | $s = \dfrac{N_p - N_t}{N_p}$ | **Slip** (fraction) | dimensionless | | 2 | $s\% = \dfrac{N_p -
