- **Hybrid/Electric Vehicles** — Modern series hybrids (e.g., the failed but technically interesting BMW i3 REx concept, Chevrolet Volt development prototypes) explored hydrostatic drives as a series-hybrid coupler.
- **Wind Turbines and Machine Tools** — Industrial applications for high-torque, low-speed drives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not in passenger cars?** Hydrostatic drives in passenger cars face three major challenges: (a) noise from the pump, (b) limited efficiency (85–92% vs 96–98% for modern automatic gearboxes), and (c) heat dissipation issues. They are therefore mainly used in **low-speed, high-torque, stop-and-go** applications where their advantages dominate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Pump Hydraulic Power Output

**Given:** A positive-displacement pump with displacement $V_p = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ rotating at $N_p = 1800 \text{ RPM}$ against a system pressure of $P_p = 30 \text{ MPa}$.

**Find:** The theoretical hydraulic power delivered by the pump.

---

**Step 1: Convert units to SI base.**

$$V_p = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

$$N_p = 1800 \text{ RPM} = \frac{1800}{60} \text{ rev/s} = 30 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$P_p = 30 \text{ MPa} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the pump's volumetric flow rate.**

Using the fundamental pump equation $Q_p = V_p \cdot N_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= V_p \times N_p \\
Q_p &= (50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}) \times (30 \text{ rev/s}) \\
Q_p &= 1500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
Q_p &= 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** The pump displaces 50 cm³ of fluid for each of its 30 revolutions per second, yielding 1500 cm³ of fluid per second, which equals 1.5 liters per second.

**Step 3: Calculate the theoretical hydraulic power.**

Using the power-flow relation $\dot{W}_p = P_p \cdot Q_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_p &= P_p \times Q_p \\
\dot{W}_p &= (30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}) \\
\dot{W}_p &= 45 \times 10^3 \text{ W} \\
\dot{W}_p &= 45 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** Each second, 1.5 liters of fluid is pushed out of the pump at 30 MPa pressure. The work done per second (power) is pressure × flow rate, giving 45,000 Joules per second = 45 kW.

**Result:** The pump delivers **45 kW of theoretical hydraulic power**.

---

### Derivation 2: Motor Torque and Output Speed

**Given:** The pump above drives a fixed-displacement hydraulic motor with $V_m = 80 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$. Assume line losses are negligible, so $P_m \approx P_p = 30 \text{ MPa}$. Assume motor volumetric efficiency $\eta_{\text{vol},m} = 0.96$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_{\text{mech},m} = 0.94$.

**Find:** (a) Theoretical motor speed, (b) Actual motor speed, (c) Output torque, (d) Output power.

---

**Step 1 (Part a): Calculate the theoretical motor speed.**

Using $N_m = \dfrac{V_p \cdot N_p}{V_m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_m &= \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 30}{80 \times 10^{-6}} \\
N_m &= \frac{1500 \times 10^{-6}}{80 \times 10^{-6}} \\
N_m &= 18.75 \text{ rev/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_m = 18.75 \times 60 = 1125 \text{ RPM}$$

**Logical Explanation:** The motor requires 80 cm³ per revolution. The pump supplies 1500 cm³/s, so the motor turns at 1500/80 = 18.75 revolutions per second.

**Step 2 (Part b): Apply volumetric efficiency to get the actual motor speed.**

Internal leakage in the motor reduces the effective flow. The relationship is:

$$N_{m,\text{actual}} = N_m \times \eta_{\text{vol},m}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{m,\text{actual}} &= 18.75 \times 0.96 \\
N_{m,\text{actual}} &= 18.0 \text{ rev/s} \\
N_{m,\text{actual}} &= 1080 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** 4% of the flow leaks internally inside the motor, so the motor receives less effective fluid per revolution, reducing its speed by 4%.

**Step 3 (Part c): Calculate the theoretical motor torque.**

Using $T_m = \dfrac{P_m \cdot V_m}{2\pi}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \frac{(30 \times 10^6) \times (80 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi} \\
T_m &= \frac{2400}{6.2832} \\
T_m &= 381.97 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Explanation:** The motor generates torque proportional to pressure and displacement. At 30 MPa with 80 cc/rev, the theoretical torque is 381.97 N·m.

**Step 4 (Part d): Apply mechanical efficiency to get the actual output torque.**

$$T_{m,\text{actual}} = T_m \times \eta_{\text{mech},m}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{m,\text{actual}} &= 381.97 \times 0.94 \\
T_{m,\text{actual}} &= 359.05 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the actual output power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_{\text{out}} &= T_{m,\text{actual}} \times \omega_{m,\text{actual}} \\
\omega_{m,\text{actual}} &= 2\pi \times 18.0 = 113.10 \text{ rad/s} \\
\dot{W}_{\text{out}} &= 359.05 \times 113.10 \\
\dot{W}_{\text{out}} &= 40,604 \text{ W} \approx 40.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate overall efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{out}}}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} \\
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= \frac{40.6}{45.0} \\
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= 0.902 \text{ or } 90.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Parameter | Theoretical | Actual |
|---|---|---|
| Motor Speed | 1125 RPM | 1080 RPM |
| Motor Torque | 381.97 N·m | 359.05 N·m |
| Output Power | 45.0 kW | 40.6 kW |
| **Overall Efficiency** | **100%** | **90.2%** |

---

### Derivation 3: Variable Displacement Speed Control — Infinitely Variable Transmission

**Given:** A hydrostatic drive with pump displacement variable from $V_{p,\min} = 0$ to $V_{p,\max} = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, motor displacement $V_m = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ (fixed), and pump speed $N_p = 2000 \text{ RPM}$ (constant).

**Find:** The minimum and maximum motor speeds, demonstrating infinitely variable transmission.

---

**Step 1: Maximum motor speed (at maximum pump displacement).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{m,\max} &= \frac{V_{p,\max} \cdot N_p}{V_m} \\
N_{m,\max} &= \frac{60 \times 10^{-6} \times (2000/60)}{75 \times 10^{-6}} \\
N_{m,\max} &= \frac{60 \times 33.33}{75} \\
N_{m,\max} &= \frac{2000}{75} \\
N_{m,\max} &= 26.67 \text{ rev/s} = 1600 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Minimum motor speed (at zero pump displacement).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{m,\min} &= \frac{V_{p,\min} \cdot N_p}{V_m} = \frac{0 \times 33.33}{75} = 0 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Speed range ratio.**

$$
\text{Speed Ratio} = \frac{N_{m,\max}}{N_{m,\min}} = \frac{1600}{0} \rightarrow \infty
$$

**Logical Conclusion:** With variable pump displacement from 0 to 60 cc/rev, the motor speed can be continuously varied from **0 to 1600 RPM** — this is the **infinitely variable transmission (IVT)** characteristic, the defining feature of a hydrostatic drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Note:** Although the speed ratio is technically infinite (zero minimum speed), practical considerations (pump volumetric efficiency drops to zero at zero displacement, fluid heating, control stability) limit the effective speed range to about **20:1** in real automotive systems. Always quote this practical figure in design problems.

---

### Derivation 4: Bi-Directional Operation (Closed-Loop Reversal)

**Given:** A closed-loop hydrostatic drive with a bi-directional variable-displacement axial piston pump (swashplate angle $\alpha$ controllable from $-20°$ to $+20°$) and a fixed motor.

**Find:** Demonstrate the principle of reversing the motor direction.

---

**Step 1: Establish the swashplate-flow relationship.**

The pump displacement is proportional to the swashplate angle:

$$V_p = V_{p,\max} \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

**Step 2: For positive swashplate angle (forward).**

When $\alpha = +20°$:

$$V_p = V_{p,\max} \cdot \sin(20°) = 0.342 \cdot V_{p,\max}$$

Flow is directed from port A (high pressure) to port B (low pressure). The motor rotates in the **forward direction**.

**Step 3: At neutral swashplate (zero flow).**

When $\alpha = 0°$:

$$V_p = V_{p,\max} \cdot \sin(0°) = 0$$

The pump produces no flow, so the motor stops. The vehicle is stationary, but the engine is still running.

**Step 4: For negative swashplate angle (reverse).**

When $\alpha = -20°$:

$$V_p = V_{p,\max} \cdot \sin(-20°) = -0.342 \cdot V_{p,\max}$$

The negative sign indicates that flow is now directed from port B (now high pressure) to port A (now low pressure). The motor rotates in the **reverse direction**.

**Logical Conclusion:** The bi-directional swashplate pump allows smooth, continuous transition from forward to reverse without any clutches, gears, or synchronizers — a major advantage of closed-loop hydrostatic drives for off-highway vehicles.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Open-Loop HST | Closed-Loop HST | Mechanical Gearbox |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed Control | Infinitely variable | Infinitely variable | Discrete steps |
| Speed Ratio Range | Up to 40:1 | Up to 100:1 | 4–10 ratios |
| Max Efficiency | 75–85% | 85–92% | 96–98% |
| Direction Reversal | External valve | Swashplate reversal | Reverse gear + synchronizer |
| Dynamic Braking | Poor | Excellent (regen) | Moderate (engine brake) |
| Heat Generation | High | Moderate | Low |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Noise Level | High | Moderate | Low |
| Typical Application | Older tractors, mowers | Modern construction, military | Passenger cars, trucks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Closed-Loop Hydrostatic Drive — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Internal Combustion Engine] --> PMP[Variable Displacement Axial Piston Pump]
    PMP -- High Pressure Port A --> MOT[Fixed Displacement Hydraulic Motor]
    MOT -- Low Pressure Port B --> PMP
    MOT --> WH1[Drive Shaft to Wheels]
    PMP --> CHG[Charge Pump and Filter]
    CHG --> RSV[Hydraulic Reservoir]
    RSV --> CHG
    CHG --> PMP
    PMP --> RV1[Relief Valve High Pressure 42 MPa]
    PMP --> RV2[Relief Valve Low Pressure 2.5 MPa]
    MOT --> BRK[Dynamic Braking Mode Selector]
    BRK --> COOL[Heat Exchanger / Charge Cooler]
    COOL --> RSV
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **engine** drives the **variable displacement pump** at constant speed.
- The pump sends pressurized fluid (Port A, ~35 MPa) to the **hydraulic motor**.
- Return fluid (Port B, ~2 MPa) flows back to the pump, completing the closed loop.
- A separate **charge pump** (typically a small fixed gear pump) supplies make-up fluid to compensate for internal leakage and maintain loop pressure.
- The **relief valves** protect the system from over-pressure transients.
- During braking, the **dynamic braking mode selector** redirects the motor's pumped output through a **heat exchanger** to the reservoir, dissipating kinetic energy as heat (regenerative braking is possible if an accumulator is included).

---

### Diagram 2: Open-Loop Hydrostatic Drive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENG1[Engine Prime Mover] --> PMP1[Fixed or Variable Pump]
    PMP1 -- Discharge at 25 MPa --> DCV[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3]
    DCV -- Forward Path --> MOT1[Hydraulic Motor]
    DCV -- Reverse Path --> MOT1
    DCV -- Neutral Position --> OFF[Flow to Tank]
    MOT1 --> OUT1[Output Shaft to Load]
    MOT1 -- Return Line at Low Pressure --> RSV1[Hydraulic Reservoir]
    RSV1 -- Suction Line --> PMP1
    FILT[Inline Filter 10 micron] --> RSV1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- This is a simpler, cheaper topology where fluid from the motor returns to the **reservoir** rather than back to the pump.
- The **directional control valve** (DCV) selects forward, reverse, or neutral — at neutral, the pump flow is unloaded back to the tank at near-zero pressure.
- The **filter** removes contaminants before fluid re-enters the pump suction.

---

### Diagram 3: Speed and Torque Control Strategy in HST

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRV[Driver Input Accelerator Pedal] --> ECU[Electronic Control Unit]
    ECU --> SWP[Swashplate Actuator on Pump]
    SWP -- Changes alpha from minus 20 to plus 20 --> VDP[Variable Displacement Pump]
    VDP -- Flow Q proportional to sin alpha --> MOT2[Fixed Displacement Motor]
    MOT2 --> WHL[Vehicle Wheels]
    WHL -- Load Feedback --> LD[Load Sensor Pressure Transducer]
    LD --> ECU
    ECU --> MON[Dashboard Display]
    ECU --> LIM[Pressure Limiter]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **driver's accelerator pedal** sends a signal to the **ECU**.
- The ECU commands a **swashplate actuator** to tilt the pump's swashplate to the required angle.
- The pump generates the corresponding flow, and the motor spins at the demanded speed.
- A **load sensor** (pressure transducer) on the high-pressure line monitors the actual load and feeds this back to the ECU for closed-loop control.
- The system includes a **pressure limiter** to prevent system over-pressure.

---

### Diagram 4: Power Flow in a Hydrostatic Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mechanical Power in Pin times omega in] -->|Pump| H1[Hydraulic Power P times Q]
    H1 -->|Line Losses 2 to 5 percent| H2[Hydraulic Power at Motor]
    H2 -->|Motor| M2[Mechanical Power out Tout times omega out]
    H2 -->|Motor Losses 8 to 12 percent| HL[Heat Loss to Reservoir]
    M1 -->|Pump Losses 5 to 10 percent| PL[Heat Loss in Pump]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- A typical hydrostatic drive converts about **85–92%** of the input mechanical power to output mechanical power.
- The remaining 8–15% is lost as heat, distributed between the pump, motor, and connecting lines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Define a hydrostatic drive. List any four advantages of a hydrostatic drive over a mechanical gearbox.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hydrostatic drive** is a power transmission system that uses a pressurized hydraulic fluid in a closed or open loop, converting mechanical energy to hydraulic energy via a positive-displacement pump and reconverting it to mechanical energy via a positive-displacement motor.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Infinitely variable speed control** — output speed can be varied continuously from zero to maximum, without discrete steps.
2. **Self-overload protection** — if the load exceeds capacity, the system pressure simply rises to the relief valve setting, preventing mechanical damage.
3. **Smooth, clutchless direction reversal** — achieved by simply tilting the pump swashplate through neutral.
4. **Dynamic/regenerative braking** — the motor can act as a pump, providing built-in braking force.
5. *(Bonus)* Compact, modular design suitable for off-highway vehicles.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any four advantages)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**What is meant by 'volumetric efficiency' of a hydraulic pump? State the typical volumetric efficiency of an axial piston pump at rated pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

**Volumetric efficiency** ($\eta_{\text{vol}}$) is the ratio of the actual flow delivered by a pump to its theoretical (geometric) flow, expressed as a percentage or fraction:

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{loss}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

The losses $Q_{\text{loss}}$ are due to internal leakage of fluid from the high-pressure side back to the low-pressure side through clearances between pistons, valves, and seals.

**Typical value:** An axial piston pump at rated pressure of **35 MPa** has a volumetric efficiency of **95–98%**.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for typical value)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Module Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram, the working of a **closed-loop hydrostatic transmission system** used in an off-highway vehicle. Discuss its components, working principle, and the role of the swashplate in controlling speed and direction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A variable displacement pump has a maximum displacement of $V_p = 70 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and is driven at a constant speed of $N_p = 1500 \text{ RPM}$. It supplies a fixed displacement hydraulic motor with $V_m = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$. The system operates at a working pressure of $P = 28 \text{ MPa}$. Assuming the pump volumetric efficiency is 96% and overall efficiency is 88%, calculate: **(7 marks)**

  (i) The pump's actual flow rate in LPM (liters per minute).
  (ii) The motor's theoretical output speed in RPM.
  (iii) The motor's theoretical torque in N·m.
  (iv) The motor's actual output power in kW.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Block Diagram and Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Diagram:**

```
[Engine] → [Variable Displacement Pump (Swashplate)] ⇄ [Fixed Displacement Motor] → [Wheels]
                ↓ (Charge Circuit)                                ↑
                [Charge Pump] → [Reservoir] → [Filter] ← [Charge Cooler]
                ↑                                                          ↑
                [Relief Valve H.P. 42 MPa]              [Relief Valve L.P. 2.5 MPa]
```

**Working Principle:**

1. The **internal combustion engine** runs at a relatively constant speed and drives the **variable-displacement axial piston pump**.
2. The pump's swashplate is tilted by an **electrohydraulic actuator** commanded by the vehicle's ECU. The swashplate angle $\alpha$ determines the pump displacement $V_p = V_{p,\max} \cdot \sin(\alpha)$.
3. Pressurized fluid (typically at 25–35 MPa) flows from the pump's high-pressure port through a rigid line to the **hydraulic motor**.
4. The motor converts the fluid's pressure energy into **rotational mechanical energy** at its output shaft, which is connected to the vehicle's wheels via a final drive.
5. Fluid leaving the motor's low-pressure port (at ~2 MPa) returns directly to the pump's low-pressure port, completing the **closed loop**.
6. A small **charge pump** (gear or vane pump, ~10–20 cc/rev) draws fluid from the **reservoir**, filters it, and pressurizes the low-pressure side to ~2 MPa. This compensates for internal leakage and maintains loop integrity.
7. **Relief valves** protect the system from pressure surges. The low-pressure relief typically opens at 2.5 MPa; the high-pressure relief at 42 MPa.
8. **Direction reversal** is achieved by tilting the swashplate through neutral to the opposite side, reversing the pump's flow direction.

**Role of the Swashplate:**

- **Speed control:** Steeper swashplate angle → higher displacement → more flow → higher motor speed.
- **Direction reversal:** Positive angle = forward; negative angle = reverse; zero angle = neutral (no movement).
- **Torque limiting:** Swashplate angle also limits the maximum theoretical torque the motor can deliver.

*(Mark allocation: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for swashplate role)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**

$V_p = 70 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $N_p = 1500 \text{ RPM}$, $V_m = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $P = 28 \text{ MPa}$, $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 0.96$, $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.88$.

**(i) Actual pump flow rate in LPM:**

Theoretical flow:

$$Q_{\text{theory}} = V_p \times N_p = 70 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} \times 1500 \text{ rev/min} = 105,000 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} = 105 \text{ LPM}$$

Actual flow (with volumetric efficiency):

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = Q_{\text{theory}} \times \eta_{\text{vol}} = 105 \times 0.96 = 100.8 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Answer (i): 100.8 LPM** *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(ii) Motor's theoretical output speed:**

$$
N_{m,\text{theory}} = \frac{V_p \times N_p}{V_m} = \frac{70 \times 1500}{50} = 2100 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Answer (ii): 2100 RPM** *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(iii) Motor's theoretical torque:**

First, convert pressure to SI: $P = 28 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $V_m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$.

$$
T_{m,\text{theory}} = \frac{P \times V_m}{2\pi} = \frac{(28 \times 10^6) \times (50 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi}
$$

$$
T_{m,\text{theory}} = \frac{1400}{6.2832} = 222.82 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Answer (iii): 222.82 N·m** *(2 marks)*

---

**(iv) Actual output power in kW:**

The theoretical hydraulic power transmitted:

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{theory}} = P \times Q_{\text{theory}} = 28 \times 10^6 \times \frac{105,000 \times 10^{-6}}{60} \text{ W}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{theory}} = 28 \times 10^6 \times 1.75 \times 10^{-3} = 49,000 \text{ W} = 49 \text{ kW}
$$

Actual output power (using overall efficiency):

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{actual}} = \dot{W}_{\text{theory}} \times \eta_{\text{overall}} = 49 \times 0.88 = 43.12 \text{ kW}
$$

**Answer (iv): 43.12 kW** *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Alternative Module Question]**

**(a)** Compare **open-loop and closed-loop hydrostatic drive systems** based on the following criteria: (i) circuit complexity, (ii) heat dissipation, (iii) dynamic response, (iv) direction reversal mechanism, (v) typical efficiency, and (vi) one example application each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrostatic drive consists of a fixed displacement pump with $V_p = 40 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ rotating at $N_p = 2400 \text{ RPM}$ and a variable displacement motor with maximum displacement $V_{m,\max} = 80 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$. The system pressure is $P = 25 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate: **(7 marks)**

  (i) The flow rate in LPM.
  (ii) The maximum motor speed and the corresponding minimum motor speed when $V_m$ is at its minimum of $20 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$.
  (iii) The maximum motor torque at $V_{m,\max} = 80 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$.
  (iv) The maximum output power.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Hydrostatic Drive Comparison (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Criteria | Open-Loop HST | Closed-Loop HST |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Circuit Complexity** | Simple; pump, motor, tank, valves | Complex; includes charge pump, filters, dual relief valves |
| 2 | **Heat Dissipation** | High; fluid passes through tank every cycle | Moderate; fluid stays in loop, only leakage goes to tank |
| 3 | **Dynamic Response** | Slower; requires tank refill between cycles | Faster; fluid is always available at high pressure |
| 4 | **Direction Reversal** | External 4/3 directional control valve | Swashplate reversal on the pump |
| 5 | **Typical Efficiency** | 70–85% | 85–92% |
| 6 | **Application Example** | Older agricultural tractors, lawn mowers, log splitters | Modern skid-steer loaders, military vehicles, in-wheel drives |

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark per row × 6 rows + 1 mark for diagram or conclusion)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_p = 40 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $N_p = 2400 \text{ RPM}$, $V_{m,\max} = 80 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $V_{m,\min} = 20 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $P = 25 \text{ MPa}$.

**(i) Flow rate in LPM:**

$$
Q = V_p \times N_p = 40 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} \times 2400 \text{ rev/min} = 96,000 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} = 96 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Answer (i): 96 LPM** *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(ii) Maximum and minimum motor speeds:**

Maximum motor speed (at $V_{m,\min} = 20$ cc/rev):

$$
N_{m,\max} = \frac{V_p \times N_p}{V_{m,\min}} = \frac{40 \times 2400}{20} = 4800 \text{ RPM}
$$

Minimum motor speed (at $V_{m,\max} = 80$ cc/rev):

$$
N_{m,\min} = \frac{V_p \times N_p}{V_{m,\max}} = \frac{40 \times 2400}{80} = 1200 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Answer (ii): Max = 4800 RPM, Min = 1200 RPM** *(2 marks)*

---

**(iii) Maximum motor torque (at $V_{m,\max} = 80$ cc/rev):**

$P = 25 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $V_{m,\max} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$.

$$
T_{m,\max} = \frac{P \times V_{m,\max}}{2\pi} = \frac{(25 \times 10^6) \times (80 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi} = \frac{2000}{6.2832} = 318.31 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Answer (iii): 318.31 N·m** *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(iv) Maximum output power:**

At $V_{m,\max}$: $N_{m,\min} = 1200 \text{ RPM} = 20 \text{ rev/s}$, $\omega = 2\pi \times 20 = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$.

$$
\dot{W}_{\max} = T_{m,\max} \times \omega_{m,\min} = 318.31 \times 125.66 = 40,000 \text{ W} = 40 \text{ kW}
$$

Cross-check: $\dot{W} = P \times Q = 25 \times 10^6 \times \frac{96,000 \times 10^{-6}}{60} = 25 \times 1.6 = 40 \text{ kW}$ ✓

**Answer (iv): 40 kW** *(2 marks)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Hydrostatic Drive Problems**
> 1. **Unit confusion (loses 1 mark):** Always convert cc/rev to m³/rev and RPM to rev/s before applying the SI formula $T = PV/(2\pi)$. Students often mix cm³ and m³, losing the factor of $10^6$ silently.
> 2. **Missing the $2\pi$ factor (loses 1 mark):** The factor $2\pi$ converts one full revolution (in radians, $2\pi$ rad) to the angular sweep. Skipping it gives torque values that are too high by a factor of $2\pi \approx 6.28$.
> 3. **Forgetting efficiency (loses 2 marks):** Whenever the problem says "actual," you must apply either $\eta_{\text{vol}}$ (for flow/speed) or $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ (for torque) or $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ (for power). Theoretical values alone will be marked down.
> 4. **Confusing pump and motor formulas (loses 1 mark):** Pump torque is the **input** torque (from the engine); motor torque is the **output** torque (to the load). Don't swap them in the energy balance.
> 5. **Wrong direction of swashplate angle in closed-loop (loses 1 mark):** Positive $\alpha$ = forward, negative $\alpha$ = reverse. State this explicitly when describing direction reversal.
> 6. **Missing the charge pump (loses 1 mark on diagram questions):** A complete closed-loop HST diagram MUST show the charge pump, reservoir, filter, and dual relief valves. Skipping the charge circuit is a common error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hydrostatic Drive (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** A power transmission system using pressurized hydraulic fluid, a positive-displacement pump, and a positive-displacement motor, providing infinitely variable speed and torque.
>
> ✅ **Three Energy Stages:** Mechanical → Hydraulic → Mechanical, governed by Pascal's Law.
>
> ✅ **Two Architectures:** Open-loop (fluid returns to tank) and Closed-loop (fluid circulates in a sealed circuit).
>
> ✅ **Key Components:** Variable-displacement pump, fixed/variable motor, reservoir, charge pump, filter, relief valves, heat exchanger, swashplate actuator, ECU.
>
> ✅ **Speed Formula:** $N_m = (V_p \cdot N_p)/V_m$. Speed ratio $= V_m/V_p$.
>
> ✅ **Torque Formula:** $T_m = (P \cdot V_m)/(2\pi)$. Always include $2\pi$ in the denominator.
>
> ✅ **Power Formula:** $\dot{W} = P \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$.
>
> ✅ **Standard Working Pressure:** 25–35 MPa (peak 42 MPa) for modern automotive HSTs.
>
> ✅ **Efficiencies:** Volumetric $\eta_{\text{vol}} = Q_{\text{actual}}/Q_{\text{theory}}$; Mechanical $\eta_{\text{mech}} = T_{\text{actual}}/T_{\text{theory}}$; Overall $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}} = 85$–$92\%$.
>
> ✅ **Direction Reversal:** Achieved by tilting the pump swashplate through neutral in a closed-loop system.
>
> ✅ **Dynamic Braking:** Motor acts as a pump; energy dissipated as heat or stored in accumulator.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Agricultural tractors, construction equipment (skid-steers, excavators), forklifts, military vehicles, in-wheel drives.
>
> ✅ **Why not in passenger cars:** Lower efficiency (85–92% vs 96–98% for AT), higher noise, heat management challenges.
>
> ✅ **Advantages:** Infinitely variable, self-overload protection, smooth reversal, dynamic braking, modular, compact.
>
> ✅ **Disadvantages:** Lower efficiency than mechanical gearboxes, noise, fluid leakage risk, heat dissipation needs, higher cost.
>
> ✅ **KTU Mantra:** Always quote **two numbers** in numerical problems — one theoretical (100% efficiency) and one actual (with $\eta$ applied). Examiners love this discipline.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Types of Hydrostatic Systems - Principles of Hydrostatic Drive System, Advantages and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Types of Hydrostatic Systems - Principles of Hydrostatic Drive System, Advantages and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrostatic Drive System** (also termed *Hydrostatic Transmission* or *Hydraulic Transmission*) is a power transmission arrangement in which a **prime mover** (typically an internal combustion engine or electric motor) drives a **hydraulic pump** that converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy in the form of a pressurized, flowing incompressible fluid. This pressurized fluid is then directed to a **hydraulic motor** (or hydraulic cylinder), which reconverts the hydraulic energy back into mechanical energy (rotary motion) to drive the load.

Unlike hydrodynamic drives, which depend on momentum transfer between fluid masses (kinetic energy exchange — as in torque converters), hydrostatic drives rely purely on **pressure–flow energy conversion** governed by Pascal's Law and Bernoulli's principle for incompressible fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The hydrostatic drive system is the **backbone of modern off-highway and agricultural vehicles** (e.g., tractors, bulldozers, excavators, combine harvesters) because it offers infinitely variable speed control, reverse capability without gear shifting, and inherent overload protection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you have a **bicycle** (the prime mover / engine) and a **water-filled garden hose** connected between two water wheels. As you pedal the bicycle, a small pump at one end pushes water through the hose at high pressure. The water strikes a paddle wheel at the other end, making it rotate, which in turn rotates a grinding stone (the load).

- If you **pedal faster** → more water is pumped → grinding stone spins faster.
- If you **squeeze the hose** (throttle) → less water flows, but pressure rises → torque on the wheel changes.
- You can connect the **other end of the hose** to send water back to the paddle wheel and reverse the rotation — no gear shifting needed!

This is exactly how a hydrostatic drive works:

- The **bicycle + pump** = Engine + Hydraulic Pump
- The **garden hose** = High-pressure hydraulic lines
- The **paddle wheel** = Hydraulic Motor
- The **grinding stone** = Vehicle's wheels / driven load

The "fluid" inside does the work, transmitting power without any direct mechanical contact — only pressure and flow.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used in Hydrostatic System Analysis**
> - **Standard operating pressure** in mobile hydrostatic drives: **350 bar (35 MPa)** to **420 bar (42 MPa)**
> - **Density of hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46)** ≈ **870 kg/m³** at 40 °C
> - **Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil** ≈ **1.4 × 10⁹ to 1.7 × 10⁹ Pa** (governs stiffness/compressibility)
> - **Kinematic viscosity** of typical hydraulic oil at 40 °C: **46 cSt (mm²/s)**
> - **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa** (SI conversion)

## 1.4 Hydrostatic vs. Hydrodynamic Transmission — A Critical Distinction

| Feature | Hydrostatic Drive | Hydrodynamic Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Energy transfer mode | Pressure (potential energy) | Momentum (kinetic energy) |
| Working fluid state | Pressurized, low-velocity | High-velocity, low-pressure |
| Output torque at standstill | **Yes — full stall torque available** | **No — torque drops to zero at stall** |
| Efficiency at part load | High (with variable displacement) | Poor (torque converter slip) |
| Example components | Hydraulic pump + motor pair | Torque converter, fluid coupling |

This distinction is a **favourite KTU question topic** — examiners frequently ask students to compare the two.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Pressure–Flow–Torque" Trinity

The hydrostatic drive system operates on three fundamental energy relationships. Every design, sizing, and selection problem on this topic hinges on these three equations.

### Step 1 — Pressure Generation
The hydraulic pump draws fluid from the reservoir and discharges it at a pressure determined by the **system load resistance**.

$$p = \frac{T_{\text{load}} \cdot 2\pi}{D_m \cdot \eta_{hm}}$$

where $p$ = system pressure (Pa), $T_{\text{load}}$ = resisting torque (N·m), $D_m$ = motor displacement (m³/rev), $\eta_{hm}$ = hydraulic-mechanical efficiency of motor.

### Step 2 — Flow Generation
The pump displacement and speed determine the theoretical flow delivered:

$$Q_{\text{theoretical}} = D_p \cdot N_p$$

where $D_p$ = pump displacement (m³/rev), $N_p$ = pump speed (rev/s). Actual delivered flow is reduced by volumetric leakage:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = Q_{\text{theoretical}} \cdot \eta_{v,p} = D_p \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_{v,p}$$

### Step 3 — Speed & Torque at the Motor
The motor's rotational speed is governed by the flow it receives:

$$N_m = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{D_m \cdot \eta_{v,m}}$$

The motor's output torque is governed by the pressure acting across it:

$$T_m = \frac{p \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_{hm,m}}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Golden Rule" of Hydrostatics**
> - **Speed is a function of FLOW.**
> - **Torque is a function of PRESSURE.**
> - **Power = Pressure × Flow (with appropriate units).**
> This decoupling of torque and speed control is what makes hydrostatic drives inherently *infinitely variable*.

## 2.2 Power Flow in the System

The mechanical input power at the pump:

$$P_{\text{in,pump}} = 2\pi \cdot N_p \cdot T_p$$

The hydraulic power transmitted through the fluid (the "shaftless" power):

$$P_{\text{hydraulic}} = p \cdot Q = p \cdot D_p \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_{v,p}$$

The mechanical output power at the motor:

$$P_{\text{out,motor}} = 2\pi \cdot N_m \cdot T_m$$

The **overall efficiency** of the hydrostatic drive:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{hm,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m} \cdot \eta_{hm,m}$$

For a typical mobile hydrostatic transmission, $\eta_{\text{overall}}$ ranges from **75 % to 88 %** at optimal operating points.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical under this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Typical Mobile Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical flow from pump | $Q_t = D_p \cdot N_p$ | m³/s | 50 – 300 L/min |
| 2 | Actual flow (with leakage) | $Q_a = Q_t \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | — |
| 3 | Hydraulic power | $P_h = p \cdot Q$ | W (or kW) | 50 – 250 kW |
| 4 | Input power (shaft) | $P_{\text{in}} = 2\pi N_p T_p$ | W | — |
| 5 | Output torque of motor | $T_m = \dfrac{p \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_{hm}}{2\pi}$ | N·m | 200 – 1500 N·m |
| 6 | Output speed of motor | $N_m = \dfrac{Q_a}{D_m \cdot \eta_v}$ | rev/s | 0 – 4000 rpm |
| 7 | Motor output power | $P_{\text{out}} = 2\pi N_m T_m$ | W | — |
| 8 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_{v,p} \eta_{hm,p} \eta_{v,m} \eta_{hm,m}$ | — | 0.75 – 0.88 |
| 9 | Power in practical units | $P\,[\text{kW}] = \dfrac{p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot Q\,[\text{L/min}]}{600}$ | kW | — |
| 10 | Torque in practical units | $T\,[\text{N·m}] = \dfrac{p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot D_m\,[\text{cm³/rev}]}{2\pi \cdot 100} \cdot \eta_{hm}$ | N·m | — |

> **Note on units:** $1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 0.1\,\text{MPa}$. To convert $D$ in cm³/rev to m³/rev, divide by $10^6$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pressure–flow–torque decoupling principle is exploited in:
- **Combine harvesters**: Independent reel, threshing drum, and ground-drive speeds set by operator.
- **Skid-steer loaders**: Two independent hydrostatic loops (one per track) enable zero-radius turning.
- **Hydraulic excavators**: Swing, boom, arm, and bucket motions all driven by independent hydraulic motors.
- **Main battle tanks** (e.g., Leopard 2): Full hydrostatic regenerative steering at 0–30 km/h plus mechanical drive at higher speeds.
- **Aircraft ground-support equipment** (tug vehicles): Infinitely variable control with high starting torque.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Power from First Principles

**Statement to Prove:**
$$P_{\text{hydraulic}} = p \cdot Q$$

**Given:**
- Pressure $p$ is force per unit area: $p = F/A$ ⇒ $F = p \cdot A$
- Volume flow rate $Q$ is volume per unit time crossing a section: $Q = V/t$
- Work done by pressure force moving fluid a distance $L$ across area $A$:

$$\text{Work} = F \cdot L = p \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Express the swept volume $V$ in terms of cross-sectional area $A$ and the distance the fluid column moves $L$:

$$V = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V = A \cdot L$ into the work equation:

$$\text{Work} = F \cdot L = p \cdot A \cdot L = p \cdot V$$

**Step 3:** Power is work done per unit time, so divide both sides by time $t$:

$$P = \frac{\text{Work}}{t} = \frac{p \cdot V}{t}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $V/t$ is the volumetric flow rate $Q$:

$$P = p \cdot Q$$

**Hence proved.** This is the **single most important equation** in fluid power engineering.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power in Practical Units (kW, bar, L/min)

**Statement to Prove:**
$$P\,[\text{kW}] = \frac{p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot Q\,[\text{L/min}]}{600}$$

**Given:** $P = p \cdot Q$ in SI units (W, Pa, m³/s).

**Step-by-Step Unit Conversion:**

**Step 1 — Convert Pa to bar:**
$$1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{Pa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p\,[\text{Pa}] = p\,[\text{bar}] \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Convert L/min to m³/s:**
$$1\,\text{L/min} = \frac{10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3}{60\,\text{s}} = \frac{1}{60\,000}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q\,[\text{m}^3/\text{s}] = \frac{Q\,[\text{L/min}]}{60\,000}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply pressure and flow in SI units:**

$$P\,[\text{W}] = \bigl(p\,[\text{bar}] \times 10^5\bigr) \cdot \left(\frac{Q\,[\text{L/min}]}{60\,000}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant:**

$$P\,[\text{W}] = p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot Q\,[\text{L/min}] \cdot \frac{10^5}{60\,000}$$

$$\frac{10^5}{60\,000} = \frac{100\,000}{60\,000} = \frac{10}{6} = \frac{5}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Convert W to kW (divide by 1000):**

$$P\,[\text{kW}] = p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot Q\,[\text{L/min}] \cdot \frac{5}{3 \times 1000} = \frac{p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot Q\,[\text{L/min}]}{600}$$

**Hence proved.** This is the form used in 95 % of all mobile-equipment datasheets.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:**
A hydrostatic drive system for a tractor uses a variable-displacement axial piston pump with maximum displacement $D_p = 75\,\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$ driven by a diesel engine at $N_p = 2400\,\text{rpm}$. The pump supplies a fixed-displacement hydraulic motor of displacement $D_m = 60\,\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$ which drives the rear wheels. The system operating pressure is $p = 350\,\text{bar}$. Assume volumetric efficiency of pump $\eta_{v,p} = 0.97$ and motor $\eta_{v,m} = 0.95$, and hydraulic-mechanical efficiency of pump $\eta_{hm,p} = 0.92$ and motor $\eta_{hm,m} = 0.94$.

**Calculate:**
(a) The theoretical and actual flow from the pump.
(b) The speed of the hydraulic motor.
(c) The output torque of the motor.
(d) The hydraulic power transmitted and the output mechanical power.
(e) The overall efficiency of the hydrostatic drive.

**Step (a) — Flow from the pump:**

Theoretical flow:

$$Q_t = D_p \cdot N_p = 75 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{rev} \times \frac{2400}{60}\,\text{rev/s}$$

$$Q_t = 75 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 3000 \times 10^{-6} = 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_t = 180\,\text{L/min}$$

Actual flow (accounting for pump leakage):

$$Q_a = Q_t \cdot \eta_{v,p} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.97 = 2.91 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 174.6\,\text{L/min}$$

**Step (b) — Motor speed:**

The actual flow reaching the motor (after motor's own internal leakage is accounted for in motor speed formula):

$$N_m = \frac{Q_a}{D_m \cdot \eta_{v,m}}$$

$$N_m = \frac{2.91 \times 10^{-3}}{60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.95} = \frac{2.91 \times 10^{-3}}{5.7 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$N_m = 51.05\,\text{rev/s} = 3063\,\text{rpm}$$

**Step (c) — Motor output torque:**

Convert pressure to Pa: $p = 350\,\text{bar} = 350 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 3.5 \times 10^7\,\text{Pa}$

$$T_m = \frac{p \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_{hm,m}}{2\pi}$$

$$T_m = \frac{3.5 \times 10^7 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.94}{2\pi}$$

$$T_m = \frac{3.5 \times 10^7 \times 5.64 \times 10^{-5}}{6.2832}$$

$$T_m = \frac{1974}{6.2832} = 314.2\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step (d) — Hydraulic power and output mechanical power:**

Hydraulic power transmitted through the fluid:

$$P_h = p \cdot Q_a = 3.5 \times 10^7 \times 2.91 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_h = 101\,850\,\text{W} = 101.85\,\text{kW}$$

Alternatively using practical units: $P_h = \dfrac{350 \times 174.6}{600} = 101.85\,\text{kW}$ ✓

Output mechanical power at the motor shaft:

$$P_{\text{out}} = 2\pi \cdot N_m \cdot T_m$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = 2\pi \times 51.05 \times 314.2 = 100\,752\,\text{W} = 100.75\,\text{kW}$$

**Step (e) — Overall efficiency:**

Input power at the pump (shaft power from engine):

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{P_h}{\eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{hm,p}} = \frac{101.85}{0.97 \times 0.92} = \frac{101.85}{0.8924} = 114.13\,\text{kW}$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{100.75}{114.13} = 0.8828 = 88.28\,\%$$

Cross-check using individual efficiencies:

$$\eta_o = 0.97 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.94 = 0.8828 = 88.28\,\% \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The drive transmits 100.75 kW to the wheels while consuming 114.13 kW of engine power. The 13.38 kW loss appears as heat in the fluid, which must be removed by the hydraulic oil cooler.

This four-quadrant capability is unique to hydrostatic drives among all automotive transmission types.
