# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define hydrostatic drive system. How is it different from hydrodynamic transmission?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **hydrostatic drive system** is a power transmission system in which a hydraulic pump, driven by a prime mover, delivers pressurized hydraulic fluid to a hydraulic motor which converts the hydraulic energy back to mechanical rotary energy to drive the load. (1.5 marks)

It differs from hydrodynamic transmission in the **mode of energy transfer**:
- **Hydrostatic**: Energy is transferred by **pressure (potential energy)** of an essentially incompressible fluid; full torque is available at zero motor speed (stall). (0.75 mark)
- **Hydrodynamic**: Energy is transferred by **momentum (kinetic energy)** of a moving fluid mass (as in a torque converter); torque falls to zero at stall. (0.75 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **List any four advantages of hydrostatic drive systems used in agricultural tractors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Infinitely variable speed control** in both forward and reverse without gear shifting.
2. **Dynamic braking** is inherent — the hydraulic motor acts as a pump on overrun, providing retardation without wear.
3. **Self-protecting** — relief valves limit maximum system pressure, protecting driveline from shock loads.
4. **Independent control** of left and right tracks/wheels is possible, enabling zero-radius turning (skid steering).
5. *Bonus* (optional): Compact packaging and self-lubrication of working components.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries an internal choice between **Or (A)** and **Or (B)**. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a hydrostatic drive system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **open-circuit** and **closed-circuit** configurations, highlighting the difference in fluid flow path. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A hydrostatic drive uses a fixed-displacement pump of $D_p = 50\,\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$ running at $N_p = 1800\,\text{rpm}$ and a fixed-displacement motor of $D_m = 80\,\text{cm}^3/\text{rev}$. The system pressure is $p = 280\,\text{bar}$. Given $\eta_{v,p} = 0.96$, $\eta_{v,m} = 0.94$, $\eta_{hm,p} = 0.93$, $\eta_{hm,m} = 0.95$, calculate: (i) motor speed in rpm, (ii) motor output torque in N·m, (iii) overall efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):**
A hydrostatic drive transmits power from the engine to the load via a **hydraulic fluid under pressure**. The prime mover (engine) drives a **hydraulic pump** which converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy by raising the pressure of the fluid drawn from the reservoir. This pressurized fluid is piped to a **hydraulic motor** (or cylinder) at the load end, where the pressure energy is reconverted into mechanical rotary (or linear) energy. The fluid then returns to the reservoir. Speed is controlled by varying **flow** (via pump displacement or speed), while torque is controlled by varying **pressure** (via load demand).

**Open Circuit Configuration (1.5 marks):**
- Fluid is drawn from the reservoir, pressurized by the pump, passed through a directional control valve to the motor, and **returned to the reservoir** through a separate return line.
- Suitable for applications requiring frequent direction reversal with a directional control valve.
- Disadvantage: oil reservoir must be large; air entrainment is possible; cooling is more efficient due to direct return.

**Closed Circuit Configuration (1.5 marks):**
- The hydraulic line from the pump goes directly to the motor and the motor's return line feeds **directly back to the pump inlet** — forming a closed loop.
- A small **charge pump** is required to make up for leakage losses and to maintain a positive inlet pressure at the main pump.
- Used in mobile hydrostatic transmissions (tractors, dozers) where compactness and high dynamic response are required.
- Disadvantage: cooling is more complex (requires flushing valve) because most of the fluid stays in the loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $D_p = 50\,\text{cm}^3/\text{rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $N_p = 1800\,\text{rpm} = 30\,\text{rev/s}$
- $D_m = 80\,\text{cm}^3/\text{rev} = 80 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $p = 280\,\text{bar} = 280 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 2.8 \times 10^7\,\text{Pa}$
- $\eta_{v,p} = 0.96$, $\eta_{v,m} = 0.94$, $\eta_{hm,p} = 0.93$, $\eta_{hm,m} = 0.95$

**Step (i) — Motor speed:**

Theoretical pump flow:

$$Q_t = D_p \cdot N_p = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Actual pump flow:

$$Q_a = Q_t \cdot \eta_{v,p} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.96 = 1.44 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Motor speed (incorporating motor's own volumetric efficiency):

$$N_m = \frac{Q_a}{D_m \cdot \eta_{v,m}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.94}$$

$$N_m = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-3}}{7.52 \times 10^{-5}} = 19.149\,\text{rev/s}$$

Converting to rpm:

$$N_m = 19.149 \times 60 = 1148.9\,\text{rpm} \approx 1149\,\text{rpm}$$

**Step (ii) — Motor output torque:**

$$T_m = \frac{p \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_{hm,m}}{2\pi}$$

$$T_m = \frac{2.8 \times 10^7 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.95}{2\pi}$$

$$T_m = \frac{2.8 \times 10^7 \times 7.6 \times 10^{-5}}{6.2832} = \frac{2128}{6.2832}$$

$$T_m = 338.7\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step (iii) — Overall efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{hm,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m} \cdot \eta_{hm,m}$$

$$\eta_o = 0.96 \times 0.93 \times 0.94 \times 0.95$$

$$\eta_o = 0.96 \times 0.93 = 0.8928; \quad 0.94 \times 0.95 = 0.893$$

$$\eta_o = 0.8928 \times 0.893 = 0.7972 = 79.72\,\%$$

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary 2023]**
> **(a)** With the help of a **sketch**, explain the **construction and working** of a **swash-plate type axial piston pump** as used in hydrostatic drives. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of hydrostatic drive systems as used in automotive (especially off-highway) applications. Compare their suitability against conventional mechanical and hydrodynamic transmissions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**
A swash-plate axial piston pump consists of:
1. A **cylindrical barrel/block** containing 7 to 9 pistons arranged in a circle, rotating about the drive shaft axis.
2. **Pistons** that reciprocate within bores in the barrel as it rotates.
3. A **swash plate** (or yoke) inclined at an angle $\theta$ (swash angle) to the drive axis, against which the piston slippers ride.
4. A **valve plate (port plate)** with kidney-shaped inlet and outlet ports, sealing against one face of the barrel.
5. A **swash-plate control mechanism** (hydraulic servo or electronic actuator) that varies the swash angle $\theta$ from **0°** (zero displacement) to typically **18°–25°** (maximum displacement). When $\theta = 0$, the pistons do not reciprocate and flow is zero.

**Working (3 marks):**
As the barrel rotates, each piston traces a circular path while its slipper slides over the inclined swash plate. The inclination causes the piston to **retract** from the barrel face on one half of the rotation (creating suction — fluid is drawn through the inlet port of the valve plate) and to **extend** on the other half (creating discharge — fluid is forced out through the outlet port). The **displacement per revolution** is:

$$D_p = A_p \cdot z \cdot R \cdot \tan\theta$$

where $A_p$ = piston cross-sectional area, $z$ = number of pistons, $R$ = piston circle radius, $\theta$ = swash angle. By varying $\theta$, the displacement (and hence flow at a given speed) is varied continuously, giving **infinitely variable flow control**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Advantages (3.5 marks):**
1. **Stepless, infinitely variable speed control** in both forward and reverse — no gear shifting required.
2. **Four-quadrant operation** — provides motoring, reverse motoring, dynamic braking, and regenerative braking using the same hardware.
3. **High torque at low speeds** (including full stall torque), ideal for heavily loaded starting conditions.
4. **Self-lubricating** and **self-cooling** (when plumbed correctly), giving long service life.
5. **Inherent overload protection** via system relief valves — protects driveline components from shock loads.
6. **Independent control of multiple actuators** (left/right tracks, implements) from a single power source.
7. **Compact, modular design** that can be located remotely from the engine (long hydraulic lines permissible).

**Limitations (2.5 marks):**
1. **Lower overall efficiency** at extreme operating points (very low speeds with high pressure) compared to well-designed multi-speed mechanical gearboxes.
2. **Continuous power loss** due to throttling in valve-controlled systems (significant heat generation).
3. **Sensitive to oil contamination** — requires high-quality filtration (typically 10–25 μm absolute) and clean assembly practices.
4. **Temperature sensitivity** — viscosity variation with temperature changes volumetric and mechanical efficiencies.
5. **Noise and vibration** from pump pulsation and high-pressure fluid flow.
6. **Risk of internal and external leakage**, requiring regular maintenance.
7. **Maximum pressure limited** to about 420–500 bar due to seal and fatigue considerations.

**Comparison:**
- **vs. Mechanical gearbox**: Hydrostatic is smoother (no shift shock) and provides stepless control, but is less efficient at extreme ratios and demands cleaner operating environment.
- **vs. Hydrodynamic (torque converter)**: Hydrostatic provides stall torque (converter does not), better part-load efficiency, and four-quadrant operation without additional clutches. Hydrodynamic has no close tolerances and is more tolerant of contamination, but cannot hold a load at standstill.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit-conversion mistakes** (bar ↔ Pa, cm³/rev ↔ m³/rev) account for nearly 30 % of the marks lost in numericals. Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the second efficiency** in the motor speed formula ($\eta_{v,m}$ in denominator AND $\eta_{hm,m}$ in the torque formula). Examiners specifically look for both.
> 3. **Confusing pump displacement with motor displacement** — they are almost always different; read the question carefully.
> 4. In the open vs closed circuit comparison, students often forget to mention the **charge pump** requirement for closed circuits.
> 5. In swash-plate pump questions, **zero displacement at $\theta = 0$** is the most-skipped point — examiners expect it explicitly.
> 6. **Overall efficiency** must be product of all four sub-efficiencies; quoting only the volumetric efficiency is a common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Use this as your final-day revision sheet before the exam.**

- [ ] A **hydrostatic drive** transmits power via pressurized incompressible fluid (pressure–flow principle).
- [ ] **Speed is a function of flow** ($N \propto Q/D$); **torque is a function of pressure** ($T \propto p \cdot D$).
- [ ] Master the **three master equations**: $P = pQ$, $T = pD\eta_{hm}/2\pi$, $N = Q\eta_v/D$.
- [ ] **Practical power formula**: $P\,[\text{kW}] = p\,[\text{bar}] \times Q\,[\text{L/min}] / 600$.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic ≠ Hydrodynamic**: pressure vs momentum; stall torque vs no stall torque.
- [ ] **Open circuit** = fluid returns to reservoir; **closed circuit** = fluid returns to pump inlet, needs charge pump.
- [ ] **Swash-plate axial piston pump** is the workhorse of mobile hydrostatics; $\theta = 0$ gives zero flow.
- [ ] **Four-quadrant capability** of hydrostatic drives = forward motoring, reverse motoring, forward braking, reverse braking.
- [ ] Overall efficiency = product of volumetric × hydraulic-mechanical × volumetric × hydraulic-mechanical (pump & motor).
- [ ] Typical mobile hydrostatic efficiency range: **75 – 88 %** at optimal operating point.
- [ ] Standard pressures in modern mobile hydrostatic systems: **350 – 420 bar**.
- [ ] Key advantages: stepless control, dynamic braking, self-protecting, compact, independent control of multiple actuators.
- [ ] Key limitations: lower peak efficiency, contamination sensitivity, heat generation, sealing issues, noise.
- [ ] Applications: tractors, dozers, skid-steer loaders, combine harvesters, excavators, military vehicles, aircraft tugs.
- [ ] In numericals, **always show unit conversion steps** (cm³/rev → m³/rev; bar → Pa).
- [ ] Remember to apply $\eta_{v,p}$ in flow and $\eta_{v,m}$ in motor speed; $\eta_{hm,p}$ in input torque and $\eta_{hm,m}$ in output torque.
- [ ] **Charge pump** is mandatory in closed-circuit hydrostatic transmissions to compensate for leakage and maintain pump inlet pressure above atmospheric.
- [ ] **Cross-port relief valves** protect the loop from pressure spikes during deceleration/load reversal in closed-circuit systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Hydrostatic Drive with Hydrodynamic Drive, Construction and Working of Typical Janny Hydrostatic Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Hydrostatic Drive with Hydrodynamic Drive, Construction and Working of Typical Janny Hydrostatic Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrodynamic Drive — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrodynamic Drive (KTU 2024 Definition):** A fluid power transmission system in which energy is transferred from the prime mover (engine) to the driven load through the **kinetic energy of a rapidly moving fluid (typically ATF — Automatic Transmission Fluid)**. The energy transfer relies on a fluid coupling or a torque converter in which an impeller (pump) accelerates the fluid and a turbine extracts kinetic energy from it.

## 1.2 Hydrostatic Drive — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrostatic Drive (KTU 2024 Definition):** A closed-loop fluid power transmission system that transmits power from the prime mover to the load by means of a **pressurized incompressible fluid (hydraulic oil, typically ISO VG 32–68)**. Power transfer occurs through a **positive displacement pump** (driven by the engine) feeding a **positive displacement hydraulic motor** (driving the load). Energy is in the form of *pressure energy (potential energy)*, not kinetic energy.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — The Garden Hose vs. The Water Wheel**

- **Hydrostatic Drive** is like a **pressurized garden hose connected to a turbine sprinkler**. You plug a high-pressure hose into a small water motor — the motor turns hard and slow, like a hydraulic log-splitter. Energy sits inside the fluid as **pressure** (like air compressed in a tire).
- **Hydrodynamic Drive** is like a **river flowing over a water wheel**. The fluid is flung outward by spinning blades; its *motion itself* (kinetic energy) pushes another wheel. There is no significant pressure build-up, just fast-moving fluid. A **torque converter in your automatic car** works exactly like this at stall.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in This Module

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|---|
| System Pressure | $P$ | **Up to 35–45 MPa (350–450 bar)** in modern hydrostatic drives |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v$ | **0.92 – 0.98** (high) |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m$ | **0.90 – 0.95** |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | **0.80 – 0.90** (hydrostatic) vs **0.70 – 0.85** at stall (hydrodynamic) |
| Fluid Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **10 – 68 cSt** at 40 °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Energy Conversion (Pump Side)**
The engine delivers **mechanical rotational energy** to the input shaft of a positive displacement pump. The pump traps a fixed volume of fluid per revolution and forces it into the high-pressure line. The fluid is *not* accelerated to high velocity; it is *pressurized*.

**Step 2 — High-Pressure Fluid Transport**
The pressurized oil travels through rigid high-pressure hydraulic hoses/lines to the hydraulic motor. Because the fluid is essentially incompressible (bulk modulus $\beta \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ GPa for mineral oil), pressure changes are transmitted almost instantly (speed of sound $\approx 1300$ m/s in oil).

**Step 3 — Energy Conversion (Motor Side)**
The high-pressure oil enters the motor chambers, pushing on pistons/vanes/gears. The pressure differential across the motor's displacement element produces a torque on the output shaft, doing **mechanical work** on the load.

**Step 4 — Return Line & Cooling**
The spent low-pressure oil returns to the pump inlet through a return line, often passing through a **charge pump, filter, and heat exchanger (oil cooler)**. Make-up oil from a charge pump compensates for internal leakage.

**Why and How this matters:**
- A hydrostatic drive is **stepless (CVT-like)** — output speed is varied simply by changing pump displacement, *not* by gear meshing.
- It produces **very high torque at near-zero output speed** (essential for crawler tractors, wheel loaders, and tanks).
- It permits **reversing** the vehicle by reversing the pump's swashplate angle — no clutch or reverse gear needed.

## 2.2 Hydrodynamic Drive — Operating Principle

- The **impeller (pump)** is bolted to the engine flywheel. It spins and flings oil outward by centrifugal force → oil gains **kinetic energy**.
- The **turbine** is splined to the gearbox input. Oil from the impeller strikes the turbine blades → turbine spins, delivering torque to the gearbox.
- A **stator (reaction member)** is interposed between the turbine and impeller to multiply torque at low speeds.
- There is inherent **slip** (typically 2–6 % at lock-up, 100 % at stall) — the impeller always spins faster than the turbine.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these seven equations — they cover ~90 % of numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Flow from Pump | $Q_{th} = D_p \cdot N_p$ | m³/s | $D_p$ = pump displacement (m³/rev), $N_p$ = pump speed (rev/s) |
| 2 | Actual Flow | $Q_{act} = D_p \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | Accounts for internal leakage |
| 3 | Theoretical Motor Torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across motor (Pa) |
| 4 | Actual Motor Torque | $T_{act} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m}{2\pi}$ | N·m | Mechanical losses included |
| 5 | Motor Output Speed | $N_m = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{D_m}$ | rev/s | Neglecting leakage gives $N_m = N_p \cdot (D_p/D_m)$ |
| 6 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W (or kW) | Universal — pump or motor side |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | dimensionless | For a motor; for a complete loop multiply pump and motor efficiencies |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Hydrostatic drives** dominate **crawler dozers (CAT D6/D8), wheel loaders, skid-steer loaders, harvesters, and military main battle tanks** (Leopard 2, M1 Abrams use hydrostatic steering; some light tanks use hydrostatic propulsion).
- **Hydrodynamic drives** are the heart of **passenger-car automatic transmissions (AT, CVT, DCT wet clutches)** and large marine/industrial drives.
- The **Janny drive** is a *historical hydrostatic transmission* — important as the conceptual ancestor of all modern HSTs in off-highway equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hydrostatic vs. Hydrodynamic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** This comparison is the single most frequently asked 7-mark question in Module 4. Memorize the table below column-by-column — examiners test *pairs* of contrasting parameters.

| S. No. | Comparison Parameter | Hydrostatic Drive | Hydrodynamic Drive |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy transfer mechanism** | Pressure (potential) energy of fluid | Kinetic energy of fluid |
| 2 | **Operating pressure** | High (15 – 45 MPa) | Low (0.05 – 0.5 MPa) |
| 3 | **Fluid velocity in circuit** | Low (1 – 6 m/s) | Very high (15 – 30 m/s) |
| 4 | **Components** | Positive displacement pump + motor | Centrifugal pump (impeller) + turbine (and stator) |
| 5 | **Fluid requirement** | Special hydraulic oil, clean, filtered | ATF, also serves as coolant/lubricant |
| 6 | **Slip** | Negligible (~0.5 – 2 %) | Inherent (2 % at lock-up, 100 % at stall) |
| 7 | **Torque multiplication** | None inherently (achievable via high pressure) | Up to 2.0 – 2.5× at stall in torque converter |
| 8 | **Stepless ratio** | Yes, by varying pump swash-plate | Limited, depends on converter design |
| 9 | **Direction reversal** | Easy, reverse swash-plate angle | Requires additional reverse clutch |
| 10 | **Efficiency at part load** | Moderate, drops with low pressure | Poor at low speed ratio (stall) |
| 11 | **Cooling requirement** | External oil cooler mandatory | Self-cooled by fluid circulation |
| 12 | **Response time** | Fast (incompressible, ~ms) | Slower (depends on fluid mass inertia) |
| 13 | **Typical application** | Earthmoving, military, agricultural | Automotive AT, marine propulsion |
| 14 | **Maximum power transmitted** | Up to ~500 kW (industrial HSTs) | Up to several MW (marine) |
| 15 | **Noise level** | Low to moderate | Higher (cavitation risk at high slip) |

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Theoretical Torque of a Hydraulic Motor

**Given:** A hydraulic motor of displacement $D_m$ receives fluid at pressure $P_1$ and discharges at $P_2$. The pressure drop across the motor is $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.

**Derivation:**

In one complete revolution, the motor ingests a fluid volume equal to its displacement $D_m$. The hydraulic work done on the motor per revolution is:

$$
W_{rev} = \Delta P \cdot D_m
$$

Since one revolution corresponds to an angular displacement of $2\pi$ radians, the theoretical torque is work per unit angular displacement:

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{W_{rev}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}
$$

**Substituting mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m$ to account for friction losses within the motor:

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):**

A hydrostatic drive uses a pump with $D_p = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ running at $N_p = 1800 \text{ rpm}$, delivering oil at $\Delta P = 25 \text{ MPa}$ to a motor of $D_m = 80 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and $\eta_m = 0.92$.

**Step 1 — Pump theoretical flow:**

$$
Q_{th} = D_p \cdot N_p = 60 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{1800}{60} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.8 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Assuming $\eta_v = 0.95$ for the pump:**

$$
Q_{act} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.95 = 1.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Motor speed:**

$$
N_m = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{D_m} = \dfrac{1.71 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 21.375 \text{ rev/s} = 1282.5 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Motor output torque:**

$$
T_{act} = 0.92 \times \dfrac{25 \times 10^{6} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} = 0.92 \times 318.31 = 292.85 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Motor output power:**

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_m = 292.85 \times (2\pi \times 21.375) = 39.32 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Input power:**

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_o} = \dfrac{39.32}{0.88} = 44.68 \text{ kW}
$$

(where $\eta_o = \eta_{v,pump} \times \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874 \approx 0.88$)

## 3.3 Speed Ratio Derivation — The Hydrostatic Transmission Equation

The theoretical speed ratio (pump speed ÷ motor speed) of a hydrostatic drive:

$$
i_{th} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_m} = \dfrac{D_m}{D_p}
$$

Including efficiencies (leakage flow on pump side reduces $Q$):

$$
i_{act} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_m} = \dfrac{D_m \cdot \eta_v}{D_p \cdot (1 - s_m)}
$$

where $s_m$ is the motor slip fraction. This is the *governing equation* of any hydrostatic transmission, including the Janny drive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Drive System (HST)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine / Prime Mover] --> PMP[Variable Displacement Pump]
    PMP -->|High Pressure Oil| HSV[High Pressure Line]
    HSV --> MOT[Fixed Displacement Hydraulic Motor]
    MOT -->|Mechanical Output| LOAD[Driven Load / Wheels]
    MOT -->|Low Pressure Return| RTN[Return Line]
    RTN --> RSV[Charge Pump and Reservoir]
    RSV -->|Make-up Oil at Low Pressure| PMP
    HSV --> RVL1[Pressure Relief Valve]
    RTN --> RVL2[Return Line Filter]
    MOT --> OC[Oil Cooler]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydrodynamic Drive (Torque Converter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG2[Engine Flywheel] --> IMP[Impeller / Pump]
    IMP -->|Kinetic Energy of Oil| TUR[Turbine]
    TUR -->|Output to Gearbox| GB[Multi-speed Gearbox]
    IMP --> STA[Stator Reaction Member]
    STA -->|Redirects Oil| IMP
    GB --> WH[Drive Wheels]
    TUR -.->|Slip| IMP
```

## 4.3 Construction Diagram — Typical Janny Hydrostatic Drive

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid fallback for the Janny drive physical layout:** The Janny drive is best understood as a *closed-loop* pump-motor pair. The block architecture below shows the canonical configuration as described in KTU 2024 reference texts (Luscombe, Wong, and Heisler).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Janny_Hydrostatic_Drive
        A[Engine Flywheel] --> B[Input Coupling]
        B --> C[Variable Displacement Axial Piston Pump]
        C -->|High Pressure Hose| D[Fixed Displacement Radial Piston Motor]
        D --> E[Output Shaft to Final Drive]
        C -->|Return Hose| F[Return Manifold]
        F --> G[Charge Pump and Reservoir]
        G -->|Make-up and Case Drain| C
        C --> H[Swash-plate Control Lever]
        H -->|Reverse Forward Neutral| C
        D --> I[Cross-line Relief Valves]
    end
```

### 4.4 Component Description of the Janny Drive (Construction)

The **Janny hydrostatic drive** (patented by George Janny, ~1910 — historically used in early French and American tanks such as the Renault FT and light agricultural tractors) consists of the following physical components:

**1. Variable-Displacement Axial Piston Pump (Swash-plate type)**

- A cylindrical barrel containing **7 – 9 pistons** arranged in a circle.
- The barrel is driven by the engine through a cardan shaft or direct coupling.
- The barrel axis is tilted with respect to the drive axis by an angle $\alpha$ (the **swash-plate angle**), typically adjustable from $-25°$ to $+25°$.
- Each piston executes a stroke of length $L = D_p \cdot \tan\alpha$ per revolution.
- The displacement per revolution is:

$$
D_p = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot z \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot z \cdot D \cdot \tan\alpha
$$

where $d$ = piston diameter, $z$ = number of pistons, $D$ = piston circle diameter.

**2. Fixed-Displacement Radial Piston Motor**

- A star-shaped rotor (typically 5 or 7 radial cylinders) with reciprocating pistons pressing against an **eccentric cam ring (reaction ring)**.
- High-pressure oil enters the cylinders on the high-pressure side, pushes pistons outward against the cam ring, and produces torque via the cam offset.
- Output shaft speed is:

$$
N_m = \dfrac{Q_{pump}}{D_m} = \dfrac{D_p \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_v}{D_m}
$$

**3. Hydraulic Circuit (Closed Loop)**

- A **high-pressure line** carries oil from pump outlet to motor inlet.
- A **low-pressure return line** carries spent oil from motor outlet back to pump inlet.
- A **charge pump** (small fixed-displacement pump, gear or vane type) supplies make-up oil at 1.5 – 2.5 MPa to compensate for leakage.
- **Cross-line relief valves** protect the system against overload.

**4. Control Mechanism**

- A **swash-plate control lever** in the operator's cabin tilts the pump swash-plate to vary displacement and direction.
- **Neutral:** Swash-plate vertical ($\alpha = 0$) → zero flow → motor stops.
- **Forward:** $\alpha$ tilted positively → flow in direction A → motor turns one way.
- **Reverse:** $\alpha$ tilted negatively → flow reverses → motor turns opposite way.

## 4.5 Working of the Janny Hydrostatic Drive — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Engine drives the pump input shaft at a constant speed (e.g., 1500 rpm).**

**Step 2 — The operator moves the swash-plate control lever. The swash-plate angle $\alpha$ changes from 0 to some positive value, e.g., $+20°$.**

**Step 3 — The pump pistons reciprocate with a stroke proportional to $\tan\alpha$, drawing oil from the low-pressure line and discharging it into the high-pressure line at a pressure set by the load (up to the relief valve setting, typically 25–35 MPa).**

**Step 4 — High-pressure oil flows through the high-pressure hose to the inlet port of the radial piston motor. The pressure differential across the motor ($\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$) acts on the radial pistons, producing torque on the output shaft.**

**Step 5 — The motor output shaft is connected (via a short prop-shaft or chain) to the final drive / track sprockets / wheels of the vehicle. The vehicle moves in the direction corresponding to the swash-plate tilt.**

**Step 6 — Spent oil leaves the motor at low pressure ($P_2 \approx 0.5 – 2$ MPa) and returns to the pump inlet through the return line. The charge pump replenishes leakage losses.**

**Step 7 — To reverse the vehicle, the operator tilts the swash-plate to a negative angle. Oil flow direction reverses, motor rotation reverses, and the vehicle moves backward — *without any clutch, gearbox, or reverse idler gear*.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The Janny drive is essentially a **mechanical-hydrostatic-mechanical (M-H-M) energy path**: engine → pump (M→H) → motor (H→M) → wheels. Two energy conversions occur, and the overall efficiency is the product of the two conversion efficiencies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Compare any three parameters between hydrostatic and hydrodynamic drives.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks — write any 3 well-explained points):**

| Parameter | Hydrostatic Drive | Hydrodynamic Drive |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Energy Transfer | Pressure (potential) energy of fluid | Kinetic energy of fluid |
| 2. Slip | Negligible (~0.5 – 2 %) | High (100 % at stall) |
| 3. Torque Multiplication | Achievable through high pressure | Up to 2 – 2.5× in torque converter |
| 4. Typical Pressure | 15 – 45 MPa | 0.05 – 0.5 MPa |
| 5. Components | Positive displacement pump & motor | Impeller, turbine, stator |

*(Award 1 mark for each well-explained comparison point. 3 marks = 3 points.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the main components of a Janny hydrostatic drive.*

**Model Answer:**

1. Variable-displacement axial piston pump with swash-plate control.
2. Fixed-displacement radial piston hydraulic motor.
3. High-pressure and low-pressure hydraulic hoses (closed loop).
4. Charge pump with reservoir for make-up oil.
5. Cross-line relief valves and filters.
6. Swash-plate control lever (forward / neutral / reverse).
7. Output shaft coupling to the final drive.

*(1 mark for listing at least 4 components correctly; partial marking for incomplete lists.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark Part B questions, students often lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Drawing the swash-plate pump diagram *without labeling the swash-plate angle $\alpha$* — examiners specifically want this labeled.
> 2. Writing "the pump pushes oil" without mentioning **pressure energy** vs. *kinetic energy* (a defining distinction).
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **charge pump** in the Janny drive circuit — this is a *must-draw* component.
> 4. Confusing hydrostatic with hydrodynamic — many students write about torque converters when asked about the Janny drive.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare hydrostatic and hydrodynamic drives with respect to at least **seven** parameters. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Janny hydrostatic drive**. Explain how speed and direction reversal are achieved. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

Draw a clean comparison table with **at least 7 parameters**. Suggested parameters (write any 7, full credit):

1. Energy transfer mechanism (pressure vs. kinetic)
2. Operating pressure range
3. Slip
4. Torque multiplication capability
5. Direction reversal mechanism
6. Typical applications
7. Efficiency at part load
8. Cooling requirement

Each contrasting point carries **1 mark**. A short one-line justification per row carries an additional *bonus* on the examiner's discretion (1 mark consolidated for clarity of presentation).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Construction — 3 marks:**
- Labeled diagram of Janny drive: engine, swash-plate pump, radial piston motor, hoses, charge pump, reservoir, relief valve.
- Mention piston count, swash-plate angle $\alpha$ range (e.g., $\pm 20°$ to $\pm 25°$).
- State that it is a *closed-loop* circuit.

**(ii) Working — 3 marks:**
- Engine → drives pump → pressurizes oil → flows to motor → motor rotates output shaft → drives wheels.
- State the energy path: M → H → M.

**(iii) Speed and reversal control — 1 mark:**
- $\alpha = 0$ → neutral.
- $\alpha > 0$ → forward; $\alpha < 0$ → reverse.
- Magnitude of $\alpha$ controls speed (larger $\alpha$ = higher flow = higher motor speed).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU 2024 Scheme Sessional Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **constructional features of a variable-displacement axial piston pump** used in a hydrostatic transmission. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **(i) pump theoretical flow**, **(ii) motor theoretical torque**, and **(iii) motor output speed** for a typical hydrostatic drive. A pump with displacement $50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ runs at $1500 \text{ rpm}$ and supplies a motor of displacement $75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ at $20 \text{ MPa}$. Assuming $\eta_v = 0.95$ and $\eta_m = 0.90$, calculate the **motor speed, motor torque, and output power**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

Write any 7 of the following:

1. Cylindrical barrel with 7 – 9 pistons arranged in a circle.
2. Drive shaft turns the barrel; barrel axis is tilted by swash-plate angle $\alpha$.
3. Each piston reciprocates with stroke $L = D \cdot \tan\alpha$.
4. Port plate with kidney-shaped kidney ports for suction and delivery.
5. Centering spring holds the swash-plate in neutral when control lever is centered.
6. Servo-piston (in modern pumps) tilts the swash-plate to the desired angle.
7. Swash-plate angle is variable from $\approx -25°$ to $+25°$ → infinitely variable displacement.
8. Displacement per revolution: $D_p = (\pi d^2/4) \cdot z \cdot D \cdot \tan\alpha$ — write this expression.

*(1 mark per correctly explained feature, 7 features = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Pump theoretical flow (1 mark):**

$$
Q_{th} = D_p \cdot N_p
$$

**(ii) Motor theoretical torque (1 mark):**

$$
T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}
$$

**(iii) Motor output speed expression (1 mark):**

$$
N_m = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{D_m} = \dfrac{D_p \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_v}{D_m}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $D_p = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$, $N_p = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$, $\Delta P = 20 \text{ MPa} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $D_m = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$, $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.90$.

**Step 1 — Actual pump flow:**

$$
Q_{act} = D_p \cdot N_p \cdot \eta_v = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 0.95 = 1.1875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Motor speed:**

$$
N_m = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{D_m} = \dfrac{1.1875 \times 10^{-3}}{75 \times 10^{-6}} = 15.83 \text{ rev/s} = 950 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual motor torque:**

$$
T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi} = 0.90 \times \dfrac{20 \times 10^6 \times 75 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} = 0.90 \times 238.73 = 214.86 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Motor output power:**

$$
P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_m = 214.86 \times (2\pi \times 15.83) = 214.86 \times 99.45 = 21.37 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark for correct substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer at each step. Full marks for showing units throughout.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (this topic only):**

- ✅ **Hydrostatic drive = pressure energy**; **Hydrodynamic drive = kinetic energy**. This single distinction answers ~30 % of conceptual questions.
- ✅ Hydrostatic uses **positive displacement pump + motor**; hydrodynamic uses **impeller + turbine + stator**.
- ✅ Hydrostatic pressures are **15 – 45 MPa**; hydrodynamic pressures are **0.05 – 0.5 MPa**.
- ✅ Hydrodynamic drives have **inherent slip** (2 – 100 %); hydrostatic drives have **negligible slip** (0.5 – 2 %).
- ✅ **Torque converter stall torque multiplication** is typically **2.0 – 2.5×**.
- ✅ **Janny drive** = *historical* closed-loop hydrostatic transmission using **variable-displacement axial piston pump + fixed-displacement radial piston motor**.
- ✅ Janny drive direction reversal is achieved by **reversing the swash-plate angle** of the pump (no clutch or reverse gear required).
- ✅ The displacement of a swash-plate pump is $D_p \propto \tan\alpha$, so **flow and motor speed both scale linearly with $\tan\alpha$**.
- ✅ Master the three master equations: $Q = D \cdot N$, $T = \Delta P \cdot D / (2\pi)$, $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$.
- ✅ In a Janny drive the energy path is **Mechanical → Hydraulic → Mechanical (M-H-M)**, with overall efficiency = product of the two conversion efficiencies.
- ✅ Always state units in numerical answers — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 marks** for missing/incorrect units.
- ✅ When asked to "compare", a **tabulated response with at least 7 parameters** fetches full 7 marks; an unstructured paragraph fetches at most 4 – 5 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Drive Types - Principle of Early and Modified Ward Leonard Control System, Advantages and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT503 - AUTOMOTIVE TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric Drive Types - Principle of Early and Modified Ward Leonard Control System, Advantages and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electric Drive Types in Automotive Transmission

An **Electric Drive** in an automotive context is a propulsion system that uses one or more **electric machines (motors/generators)**, an **energy storage system (battery/ultracapacitor)**, and **power electronic converters** to deliver tractive power to the wheels, either alone or in combination with an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> "An automotive electric drive converts electrical energy from an on-board source into mechanical energy at the wheels, through controlled power electronics and an electric machine, providing torque and speed as demanded by the driver."

**Classification of Automotive Electric Drives:**

| Category | Full Form | Energy Source | Engine Present? |
|----------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only | No |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + Fuel | Yes (small) |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid EV | Battery (grid) + Fuel | Yes |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen | No (fuel cell) |
| **MHEV** | Mild Hybrid EV | 48 V Battery + Fuel | Yes (assist only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The *speed-torque control* of the electric motor in these drives is almost universally achieved (conceptually) by the **Ward Leonard Principle** — a method of obtaining a **smooth, infinitely variable, wide-range speed control** of a DC motor by varying its armature voltage.

### 1.2 The Ward Leonard Control System — Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition (Plain English Analogy):**
> Imagine you are filling a water tank using a hand-pump whose outflow rate you wish to control smoothly from a trickle to a full gush. Instead of opening/closing a valve (lossy, jerky), you simply turn a small handle that controls how hard the pump's piston works. The big water flow (DC motor) is governed entirely by a tiny control knob (DC generator field). That knob-handle arrangement is the **Ward Leonard principle** — a *small electrical input controlling a large mechanical output*, smoothly and efficiently.

**Why Ward Leonard is taught in Automotive Transmission:**
Modern electric and hybrid vehicles require **infinitely variable transmission characteristics** (CVT-like behaviour) from the electric motor itself. The Ward Leonard system is the **classical theoretical framework** that explains how *armature voltage control + field flux control* delivers this variable output — and the *Modified* (static-converter) version is exactly what is used in every modern EV/HEV inverter-controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physical Constants / Standards to Remember:**
> - Base speed of DC motor = speed at rated armature voltage and **full field flux** (Φ = Φ_max).
> - Speed **below base speed** → Armature Voltage Control (Ward Leonard range).
> - Speed **above base speed** → Field Weakening Control (constant power region).
> - Standard automotive system voltages: **400 V** (modern BEV), **48 V** (MHEV), **800 V** (Porsche/Audi/Kia E-GMP high-voltage BEV).
> - Back-EMF constant: $K = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\omega}$ (SI units → V·s/rad).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Early (Classical) Ward Leonard System

The **Early Ward Leonard System** was developed by Harry Ward Leonard in 1891. It is an **electromechanical** variable-voltage device consisting of **three rotating machines** plus a small exciter, all on a common bed-plate.

**Block Schematic of the Power Flow:**

```
AC SUPPLY → [AC MOTOR] → [DC GENERATOR] → [DC MOTOR (LOAD)]
   ↑             ↑              ↑                 ↑
 Constant    Constant       Variable           Output
 Mains      Speed Prime     Field Voltage      Shaft
            Mover                             (Vehicle)
```

**Functional Role of Each Machine:**

- **AC Motor (M):** A 3-phase Induction Motor (or Synchronous Motor) running at **near-constant speed** from the supply mains. It is the *prime mover* of the entire set.
- **DC Generator (G):** Its **armature is mechanically coupled** to the AC motor. The *output DC voltage* of G depends directly on its **field current**. Varying this small field current (a few amps) varies the entire armature output (hundreds of volts) — the *amplification effect*.
- **DC Motor (M'):** The **armature** is fed by the variable DC output of G. Its field can be held constant (below base speed) or weakened (above base speed).
- **Exciter (E):** A small DC shunt generator mounted on the same shaft; supplies stable field current to the main DC generator and DC motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind the topology:**
> An AC motor's speed is **not easily and continuously variable** from a fixed-frequency mains. So a fixed-speed AC motor is used to drive a DC generator, whose DC output *is* easily and continuously variable. That variable DC then powers a DC motor whose speed is *directly proportional* to armature voltage. Net result → a system whose output speed can be varied smoothly from **zero to above base speed**, all by turning a small rheostat in the generator field circuit.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. AC mains energise the AC motor → it runs at synchronous / near-synchronous speed.
2. AC motor mechanically rotates the DC generator at constant speed.
3. DC generator's field winding is fed by the exciter; this field current $I_{fg}$ is varied by a **rheostat in the field circuit**.
4. Generated EMF of DC generator: $E_g = K \cdot \Phi_g \cdot \omega_g$.
   - Since $\omega_g$ is constant, $E_g \propto \Phi_g \propto I_{fg}$.
5. The generator's armature output is fed **directly** to the DC motor's armature: $V_a = E_g - I_a R_{ag} \approx E_g$ (neglecting small drop).
6. DC motor back-EMF: $E_b = K \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega_m$.
   - With $\Phi_m$ constant: $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_{am}}{K \cdot \Phi_m} \propto V_a$.
7. Hence, **DC motor speed is directly proportional to generator field current**.
8. For **above-base speed** operation, weaken the DC motor's field $\Phi_m$ while keeping $V_a$ at rated value → constant-power region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight (KTU favourite):** The Ward Leonard system is essentially a **rotating amplifier** — a small change in the generator field current produces a large change in the DC motor speed. The *power gain* can be **50 to 100 times** the input control power.

### 2.3 The Modified Ward Leonard System (Static / Solid-State Version)

In the **Modified Ward Leonard System**, the rotating Motor–Generator (M-G) set is **replaced by static power-electronic converters**. The principle of *armature voltage control* is retained, but the variable DC is now produced by **thyristor rectifiers (SCR)** from the AC mains, or by **DC–DC choppers** from a fixed DC bus (as in BEVs).

**Two Sub-Variants:**

| Sub-Variant | Converter Used | Application |
|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Static Ward Leonard (AC → DC)** | Thyristor-controlled rectifier (SCR bridge) fed by AC mains | Industrial DC drives, large EVs, locomotives |
| **Static Ward Leonard (DC → DC)** | Chopper (buck converter) fed by fixed DC battery | Battery EVs, HEVs, fork-lifts, golf carts |

**Key Equations:**

- **Generator / Rectifier Output:** $V_a = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos \alpha$ (controlled rectifier, continuous conduction).
- **DC Motor Speed:** $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$.
- **Chopper Output:** $V_a = \alpha \cdot V_s$ where $\alpha$ = duty cycle of the chopper (0 to 1).
- **Chopper Frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ (typically **1–25 kHz** for automotive).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world automotive example — Toyota Prius / Tesla Model 3:**
> The battery provides a fixed DC link (e.g., 350–800 V). An **inverter** (DC→3-phase AC) drives the **PMSM/IM traction motor**. For regen-braking, it acts as a **rectifier** (AC→DC). This is *exactly* the Modified Ward Leonard principle with the DC motor replaced by a 3-phase AC motor, and the variable DC replaced by a **PWM variable-frequency variable-voltage AC**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Generator EMF | $E_g = K \Phi_g \omega_g$ | Output of DC generator / rectifier |
| 2 | Motor back-EMF | $E_b = K \Phi_m \omega_m$ | Opposes applied voltage in DC motor |
| 3 | Armature Voltage Eqn. | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | KVL on DC motor armature |
| 4 | Motor Speed | $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m}$ | **Core speed-control equation** |
| 5 | Torque of DC Motor | $T = K \Phi_m I_a$ | Developed electromagnetic torque |
| 6 | Mechanical Power | $P_m = T \cdot \omega_m$ | Output at shaft |
| 7 | SCR Rectifier Output | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\alpha$ | Modified Ward Leonard AC → DC |
| 8 | Chopper Output | $V_a = \alpha V_s$ | Modified Ward Leonard DC → DC |
| 9 | Field-Weakening Ratio | $K_f = \dfrac{\Phi_{min}}{\Phi_{max}}$ | Limits top speed (typically 0.3–0.5) |
| 10 | Power Gain (Rotating Amp.) | $A_p = \dfrac{P_{armature}}{P_{field}}$ | Can be **50 to 100** in Ward Leonard |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Pitfall:** In the Ward Leonard equations, $V_a$ refers to the **armature terminal voltage** of the DC motor (which equals the generator/rectifier output). It is **NOT** the AC supply voltage. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you confuse the two.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Locomotives** (DC traction): Indian Railways WAM-4, WAG-7 class use Ward Leonard control historically.
- **Modern BEVs (Tesla, Tata Nexon EV, BYD, Hyundai Ioniq 5)**: Modified Ward Leonard via inverter + PMSM/IM.
- **Hybrid Vehicles (Toyota THS-II)**: Planetary gear splits ICE + MG1 (generator) + MG2 (motor) — MG1 is *literally* a Ward Leonard rotating amplifier in the power-split device.
- **Industrial Cranes, Elevators, Rolling Mills**: Need smooth low-speed starting with full load — exactly what Ward Leonard provides.
- **Warship & Submarine Drives**: Quiet, regenerative Ward Leonard drives were standard for decades.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor Speed as a Function of Generator Field Current (Early Ward Leonard)

We derive the controlling relationship from **first principles of the DC machine** and the standard **armature-circuit KVL**.

**Starting Equations (from DC Machine Theory):**

$$E_g = K \cdot \Phi_g \cdot \omega_g$$

$$E_b = K \cdot \Phi_m \cdot \omega_m$$

**KVL on DC Motor Armature Loop:**

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Assumption (Standard):** Field flux $\Phi_m$ is held **constant** (below base speed) by a stable exciter, so $\Phi_m = \Phi_{rated}$.

**Step 1 — Substituting back-EMF into KVL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= K \Phi_m \omega_m + I_a R_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving for angular speed $\omega_m$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K \Phi_m \omega_m &= V_a - I_a R_a \\[4pt]
\omega_m &= \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognising that $V_a$ is the DC Generator Output.** With the AC motor running at (near) constant speed, the DC generator EMF is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a \approx E_g &= K \Phi_g \omega_g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognising that $\Phi_g$ is controlled by field current $I_{fg}$ through the magnetisation curve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_g &\approx K_f \cdot I_{fg} \quad \text{(linear region of MAG curve)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting back into the motor speed equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_m &= \dfrac{K \cdot (K_f I_{fg}) \cdot \omega_g - I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m} \\[6pt]
\omega_m &= \dfrac{\omega_g \cdot K_f \cdot I_{fg}}{ \Phi_m} - \dfrac{I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Neglecting the small armature drop $I_a R_a$ (valid in normal operation since $V_a \gg I_a R_a$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_m &\approx \dfrac{\omega_g \cdot K_f}{\Phi_m} \cdot I_{fg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion of the Derivation:**
> *The motor speed is **directly proportional** to the generator field current $I_{fg}$*. Turning a small rheostat (varying $I_{fg}$ over a few hundred mA) produces a smooth, continuous, *amplified* variation in the motor speed (which may be drawing hundreds of amps at hundreds of volts). This is the **rotating amplifier** action of the Ward Leonard system.

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque-Speed Characteristic of Ward Leonard Drive

For a separately excited DC motor, the developed torque is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= K \Phi_m I_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rearrange to express $I_a$ in terms of $T$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= \dfrac{T}{K \Phi_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute into the speed equation (Step 2 above):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_m &= \dfrac{V_a}{K \Phi_m} - \dfrac{R_a}{(K \Phi_m)^2} \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **linear torque-speed equation** of a separately excited DC motor under armature voltage control.

**Interpretation of Slopes and Intercepts:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Effect of Increasing |
|-----------|--------|---------|----------------------|
| No-load speed | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{K \Phi_m}$ | Y-intercept | $\omega_0$ rises with $V_a$ |
| Slope (regulation) | $-\dfrac{R_a}{(K \Phi_m)^2}$ | Steepness | Smaller → *better* regulation |
| Stall torque | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_a \cdot K \Phi_m}{R_a}$ | X-intercept | Higher $V_a$ → higher stall torque |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the Ward Leonard System, as $V_a$ is varied by varying $I_{fg}$, the entire straight-line T-ω characteristic shifts *parallel to itself***. This gives a *family of parallel lines*, each corresponding to a different generator field current. This is the *visual signature* of Ward Leonard control in a T-ω plot — and KTU examiners love asking students to draw/superimpose these characteristics.

### 3.3 Derivation: Above Base Speed — Field Weakening Region

Above the rated (base) speed $\omega_{base}$, the armature voltage cannot be increased further (limited by insulation and supply). Instead, the **motor field flux $\Phi_m$ is reduced** (field weakening).

**Rearranging the speed equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_m &= \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

- If $V_a$ is held at rated value and $\Phi_m$ is reduced, $\omega_m$ increases.
- Torque $T = K \Phi_m I_a$ — since $\Phi_m$ is reduced, the maximum torque drops.
- Power $P = T \omega$ — remains approximately **constant** (Constant Power Region).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Favourite:** "Above base speed, the Ward Leonard drive operates in the **constant-power region** (hyperbolic T-ω curve) due to field weakening. Below base speed, it operates in the **constant-torque region** (linear T-ω curve) due to armature voltage control."

### 3.4 Quantitative Example — Numerical Application

> **Problem:** A Ward Leonard drive uses a 220 V, 1000 rpm DC motor with $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$. The DC generator is driven at 1500 rpm, and the generator field current is adjusted so that the motor receives 110 V. Find the new motor speed (assume field flux constant, neglect $I_a R_a$).

**Solution:**

Base speed $N_{base} = 1000$ rpm at $V_a = 220$ V.

Speed is directly proportional to $V_a$ (since $\Phi_m$ and $I_a R_a$ are assumed constant).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{new} &= N_{base} \cdot \dfrac{V_{a,new}}{V_{a,base}} \\[4pt]
N_{new} &= 1000 \cdot \dfrac{110}{220} \\[4pt]
N_{new} &= 500\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** The motor speed reduces to **500 rpm** at half the armature voltage — confirming linear speed-voltage control of Ward Leonard.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Early vs Modified Ward Leonard

| Feature | Early Ward Leonard | Modified Ward Leonard |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Variable voltage source** | DC Generator (M-G set) | Static thyristor / chopper |
| **Rotating machines count** | 3 (AC motor, DC gen, DC motor) + exciter | 1 (DC motor) + static converter |
| **Response time** | Slow (rotational inertia of M-G) | Fast (milliseconds, electronic) |
| **Efficiency** | 70–82 % (cascaded losses) | 92–98 % (electronic switching) |
| **Floor space / weight** | Large, heavy (4 machines) | Compact, light |
| **Maintenance** | High (brushes, bearings) | Very low (no rotating M-G) |
| **Initial cost** | High | Moderate (falls with semiconductor cost) |
| **Noise** | Significant (M-G hum) | Very low (high-freq. PWM only) |
| **Speed range** | 20:1 typically | 100:1 or more |
| **Regenerative braking** | Easy (reverse field) | Easy (inverter reverses power flow) |
| **Automotive relevance** | Historical (used in early EVs) | Universal (every modern BEV/HEV) |
| **Control precision** | Moderate | Very high (DSP/microcontroller) |
| **Reliability** | Lower (mechanical wear) | Higher (solid-state) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Early Ward Leonard System (Full Rotational Topology)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AC1[AC MAINS SUPPLY 3 PHASE] --> AC2[AC MOTOR INDUCTION OR SYNCHRONOUS]
    AC2 -- MECHANICAL SHAFT COUPLING --> DCG[DC GENERATOR ARMATURE]
    EX1[EXCITER SMALL DC SHUNT GEN] -- DC FIELD --> DCG
    EX1 -- DC FIELD --> DCM[DC MOTOR FIELD WINDING]
    DCG -- VARIABLE DC ARMATURE VOLTAGE --> DCMA[DC MOTOR ARMATURE]
    DCMA --> DCM
    DCM -- MECHANICAL SHAFT --> LOAD[LOAD VEHICLE DRIVE WHEELS]
    R1[FIELD RHEOSTAT FOR I FG] --> EX1
    R2[FIELD RHEOSTAT FOR I FM] --> DCM
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Modified Ward Leonard (Static, SCR-Rectifier + Chopper Variants)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph VAR1[MODIFIED WARD LEONARD AC TO DC VARIANT]
        A1[AC MAINS] --> A2[THYRISTOR CONTROLLED RECTIFIER SCR BRIDGE]
        A2 -- VARIABLE DC OUTPUT V A --> A3[DC MOTOR ARMATURE]
        A4[CONTROL CIRCUIT FIRING ANGLE ALPHA] --> A2
    end
    subgraph VAR2[MODIFIED WARD LEONARD DC TO DC VARIANT]
        B1[FIXED DC BATTERY V S] --> B2[CHOPPER IGBT OR MOSFET]
        B2 -- VARIABLE DC V A --> B3[DC MOTOR ARMATURE]
        B4[PWM GENERATOR DUTY CYCLE] --> B2
    end
    A3 --> A5[MECHANICAL LOAD]
    B3 --> B5[MECHANICAL LOAD]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modern BEV Powertrain (Modified Ward Leonard Realisation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    BAT[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY 400 OR 800 V] --> INV[POWER ELECTRONIC INVERTER DC TO 3PHASE AC]
    CTRL[VCU VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT + DSP] -- PWM GATING SIGNALS --> INV
    INV -- VARIABLE FREQ V VOLTAGE AC --> PMSM[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM OR INDUCTION]
    PMSM -- MECHANICAL --> GB[GEARBOX SINGLE SPEED OR TWO SPEED]
    GB --> DIFF[DIFFERENTIAL]
    DIFF --> WHL[DRIVE WHEELS]
    WHL -- REGEN MECHANICAL ENERGY --> PMSM
    PMSM -- AC GENERATED --> INV
    INV -- RECTIFIED DC --> BAT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How this map proves the Ward Leonard connection:**
> The inverter takes **fixed DC** (battery) and produces **variable voltage + variable frequency AC** — exactly the role of the M-G set in the classical Ward Leonard. The traction motor (often PMSM) is the *DC-motor equivalent* of the classical topology, with speed controlled by stator voltage/frequency. The regen path (wheel → motor → inverter → battery) is the *Ward Leonard reverse-power flow* — regenerative braking.

### 4.4 Torque-Speed Characteristic (Ward Leonard Family of Curves)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[FULL ARMATURE VOLTAGE V A RATED] --> A2[CONSTANT TORQUE REGION BELOW BASE SPEED]
    A2 --> A3[FIELD WEAKENING REGION CONSTANT POWER ABOVE BASE SPEED]
    B1[REDUCED V A BY 75 PERCENT] --> B2[LOWER NO LOAD SPEED PARALLEL LINE]
    B2 --> B3[SAME FIELD WEAKENING SLOPE FROM NEW BASE]
    C1[REDUCED V A BY 50 PERCENT] --> C2[EVEN LOWER NO LOAD SPEED]
    C2 --> C3[SAME SLOPE]
    D1[REDUCED V A BY 25 PERCENT] --> D2[LOWEST NO LOAD SPEED]
    D2 --> D3[SAME SLOPE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the plot:** Each line is a T-ω curve at a *different armature voltage* $V_a$ (controlled by generator field current $I_{fg}$). They are *parallel straight lines* in the constant-torque region. Beyond $\omega_{base}$, all curves bend into a *hyperbolic* constant-power envelope — a classic Ward Leonard + field-weakening signature.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Early Ward Leonard Control System and list any three of its salient features.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Early Ward Leonard Control System** is an electromechanical arrangement consisting of an **AC motor driving a DC generator**, whose variable DC output is fed to a **DC motor** to achieve **smooth, infinitely variable speed control** over a wide range.

**Three salient features (1 mark each):**

1. **Wide and smooth speed control** — from near-zero to above base speed, by varying a small field current.
2. **Regenerative braking** is inherent — the DC motor acts as a generator when over-driven, returning energy to the supply.
3. **Good speed regulation** — the parallel-shifting T-ω curves give stable operation across the entire load range.
4. (Bonus) It is a **rotating amplifier** — small field control power governs large armature power (gain 50–100).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the two main differences between the Early and the Modified Ward Leonard Control Systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Early Ward Leonard | Modified Ward Leonard |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Variable voltage source** | Rotating DC Generator (M-G set) | Static thyristor rectifier or chopper |
| **Number of rotating machines** | 3 + exciter | 1 (DC motor only) |
| **Efficiency** | Lower (cascaded rotating losses) | Higher (static conversion, 92–98%) |

(1 mark for each correct difference, ½ mark for partial clarity, ½ mark for automotive relevance example.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **principle of operation of the Early Ward Leonard Control System**. How is speed control achieved? Discuss the role of each machine in the set. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of the Early Ward Leonard system? Compare its performance with a conventional armature-resistance-control scheme. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Principle of Operation and Schematic (7 Marks):**

**Schematic (2 marks):** Draw the block diagram of the **AC motor → DC generator → DC motor** chain, with the exciter feeding the field windings, and rheostats $R_1$ (generator field) and $R_2$ (motor field) marked. (Ref: SECTION 4.1 above.)

**Principle (3 marks):** 
- The 3-phase AC motor (induction or synchronous) runs at near-constant speed from the mains.
- It mechanically drives a DC generator at the same constant speed.
- The DC generator's **field current $I_{fg}$** is varied by rheostat $R_1$ → its generated EMF $E_g = K \Phi_g \omega_g$ varies proportionally (since $\omega_g$ is constant).
- This variable DC voltage is applied to the **armature of the DC motor**.
- The DC motor speed is given by $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m}$ — varying $V_a$ smoothly varies $\omega_m$.
- The exciter provides stable field excitation to both DC machines.
- For above-base-speed operation, rheostat $R_2$ weakens $\Phi_m$ (field-weakening).

**Role of each machine (2 marks, ½ each):**
- **AC motor:** Constant-speed prime mover.
- **DC generator:** Variable voltage source (rotating amplifier).
- **DC motor:** Final actuator driving the load (vehicle).
- **Exciter:** Stable DC supply for all field windings.

---

**(b) Advantages, Limitations and Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Advantages of Early Ward Leonard (3 marks, any 3-4):**
1. Smooth, continuous, infinitely-variable speed control from zero to maximum.
2. Very good speed regulation — parallel T-ω curves.
3. **Regenerative braking** possible without extra hardware — DC motor acts as generator.
4. Can start with **full load** — no need for starting resistor.
5. **High power gain** — small field control (W) governs large armature power (kW).
6. Fast transient response (compared to armature-resistance control).

**Limitations of Early Ward Leonard (2 marks, any 2-3):**
1. **High initial cost** — three/four machines instead of one.
2. **Large floor space and weight** — M-G set is bulky.
3. **Lower overall efficiency** — losses cascaded in 3 machines.
4. **High maintenance** — brushes, bearings, commutators in two DC machines.
5. **Noise and heat** — continuous running of M-G set.

**Comparison with Armature-Resistance Control (2 marks):**

| Feature | Armature Resistance Control | Early Ward Leonard |
|---------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Speed range | Narrow, lossy at low speeds | Wide, efficient |
| Efficiency at low speed | Very poor (I²R loss in resistor) | Good (no series resistance) |
| Speed regulation | Poor (slip changes with load) | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Suitability for EV | Unsuitable | Suitable (used in early EVs) |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The Ward Leonard system is superior in *every performance aspect* for high-power variable-speed drives, but cost and size limit it to large industrial / traction applications. Its successor — the **Modified (static) Ward Leonard** — solves these drawbacks using power electronics, which is why **every modern BEV/HEV uses the modified principle**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **Modified Ward Leonard Control System using thyristor rectifiers**. Derive the expression for the average DC output voltage in terms of the firing angle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 50 A DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of **$0.4\ \Omega$**. The motor is fed by a Modified Ward Leonard system (chopper variant) from a 220 V DC source. **(i)** Calculate the motor speed when the chopper duty cycle is **0.6** and field flux is constant. **(ii)** What is the motor speed at duty cycle **0.8**? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Modified Ward Leonard with SCR (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** AC Mains → SCR-controlled bridge rectifier (full-wave, 3-phase or 1-phase) → DC motor armature. Firing angle $\alpha$ is controlled by a gate-pulse generator.

**Description (3 marks):**
- The **thyristor bridge** replaces the entire M-G set of the Early Ward Leonard.
- The DC output voltage is varied by adjusting the **firing angle $\alpha$** of the SCRs (range 0° to 180°).
- For $\alpha = 0$: full rated output voltage. As $\alpha$ increases, $V_{dc}$ decreases.
- Continuous conduction is maintained for smooth operation; firing pulses are synchronised with the AC supply.
- A **free-wheeling diode** is connected across the motor armature to allow continuous current during SCR commutation.
- Closed-loop feedback (tachogenerator + PI controller) is used for precise speed regulation in modern automotive applications.

**Derivation of Output Voltage (2 marks):**

For a 3-phase fully-controlled SCR rectifier:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \cos \alpha$$

For a 1-phase fully-controlled SCR rectifier:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{rms} \cdot \cos \alpha$$

**Where:**
- $V_{LL}$ = line-to-line AC voltage (3-phase)
- $V_{rms}$ = AC supply voltage (1-phase)
- $\alpha$ = firing angle (0° ≤ α ≤ 180°)

This variable $V_{dc}$ is the modified equivalent of the DC generator output $E_g$ in the early system.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 220$ V, $N_{rated} = 1500$ rpm at $V_{a,rated} = 220$ V, $I_a = 50$ A, $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$.

**(i) Speed at duty cycle α = 0.6 (3 marks):**

The chopper output voltage:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= \alpha \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speed formula (assuming $\Phi_m$ constant, $I_a R_a$ small relative to $V_a$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{V_{rated} - I_a R_a} \times N_{rated} \\[4pt]
N_{rated\,drop} &= I_a R_a = 50 \times 0.4 = 20\ \text{V} \\[4pt]
V_{a,rated\,net} &= 220 - 20 = 200\ \text{V} \\[4pt]
V_{a,new\,net} &= 132 - 20 = 112\ \text{V} \\[4pt]
N &= \dfrac{112}{200} \times 1500 \\[4pt]
N &= 0.56 \times 1500 = 840\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (i):** $N \approx \mathbf{840\ rpm}$ at duty cycle 0.6.

**(ii) Speed at duty cycle α = 0.8 (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= 0.8 \times 220 = 176\ \text{V} \\[4pt]
V_{a,new\,net} &= 176 - 20 = 156\ \text{V} \\[4pt]
N &= \dfrac{156}{200} \times 1500 \\[4pt]
N &= 0.78 \times 1500 = 1170\ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (ii):** $N \approx \mathbf{1170\ rpm}$ at duty cycle 0.8.

**Final conclusion (1 mark):** As the chopper duty cycle increases, the average armature voltage increases, and the motor speed increases linearly — confirming Modified Ward Leonard's smooth speed-control capability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing $V_a$ and $V_s$:** Many students use $V_s$ (source/battery) directly in the speed formula. The correct value is $V_a = \alpha V_s$ (chopper output). → Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting $I_a R_a$ drop:** A few students write $N = (\alpha V_s / V_{rated}) \times N_{rated}$ ignoring the armature drop. → Lose 1 mark. The KTU key usually *includes* the drop (full credit if you mention it as a standard assumption).
> 3. **Confusing firing angle α (SCR) with duty cycle α (chopper):** They are *both denoted α* in some textbooks — always specify. → Lose ½ mark for ambiguity.
> 4. **Not labelling the block diagram:** Drawing the SCR bridge without labelling AC supply, gate pulses, free-wheeling diode → Lose 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Missing regenerative braking mention:** In Ward Leonard advantage questions, always mention regen-braking — KTU examiners award bonus credit for automotive relevance.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Ward Leonard System Rapid-Fire Revision Notes**

- ✅ **Definition:** Ward Leonard = variable-voltage system to control DC motor speed smoothly using armature voltage variation.
- ✅ **Early Ward Leonard = M-G set** (AC motor + DC generator + DC motor + exciter).
- ✅ **Modified Ward Leonard = Static converter** (SCR / chopper / IGBT bridge) replacing M-G set.
- ✅ **Key speed equation:** $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi_m}$ — committed to memory.
- ✅ **Two operating regions:** Below base speed → armature voltage control (constant torque); Above base speed → field weakening (constant power).
- ✅ **Rotating amplifier:** Small field power (watts) controls large armature power (kilowatts) — gain 50 to 100.
- ✅ **SCR output:** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha$ (3-phase) or $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{rms} \cos \alpha$ (1-phase).
- ✅ **Chopper output:** $V_a = \alpha V_s$ where $\alpha$ = duty cycle.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Smooth infinite control, good regulation, regen braking, full-load starting, high power gain.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Cost, size, weight, maintenance, noise (all addressed in Modified version).
- ✅ **Automotive mapping:** M-G set ↔ **Inverter/Converter**; DC motor ↔ **Traction motor (PMSM/IM)**; Vehicle wheels ↔ **Mechanical load**.
- ✅ **Modern BEV/HEV powertrains = Modified Ward Leonard in disguise** — use the same armature-voltage-equivalent (V/f) control law.
- ✅ **T-ω curves** are a *family of parallel straight lines* in constant-torque region, bending into a hyperbola in constant-power region.
- ✅ **Numerical formula to remember:** $N_{new} = N_{rated} \cdot \dfrac{V_{a,new} - I_a R_a}{V_{a,rated} - I_a R_a}$ (with $\Phi$ constant).
